### Step 4 - Build two throwaway resources to test order safely
_Goal: You have a provider and a consumer that report whether the order worked._

Never test order by restarting `ox_lib`, a framework, or a paid script on a live server, you can kick players and break everything downstream. Instead build two tiny local resources: no players, no data, no risk.

Create this layout inside your `resources` folder. The `[local]` bracket folder keeps your own test scripts grouped:

```text
resources/[local]/qu_dep_provider/
fxmanifest.lua
server.lua
resources/[local]/qu_dep_consumer/
fxmanifest.lua
server.lua
```

`qu_dep_provider/fxmanifest.lua`:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

`qu_dep_provider/server.lua`:

```lua
exports('Ping', function()
return 'pong'
end)

print('[qu_dep_provider] export ready') -- this text appears in the txAdmin Live Console when the resource starts
```

`qu_dep_consumer/fxmanifest.lua`:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

`qu_dep_consumer/server.lua`:

```lua
CreateThread(function()
local ok, result = pcall(function()
return exports.qu_dep_provider:Ping()
end)

if ok and result == 'pong' then
print('[qu_dep_consumer] provider found: ' .. result)
else
print('[qu_dep_consumer] provider MISSING (wrong ensure order)')
end
end)
```

Notice neither manifest has a `lua54` line. Lua 5.4 is the only runtime in 2026 (Lua 5.3 was removed in June 2025), so the `lua54` setting is deprecated and you do not need to enable it. Leave it out.

### Step 5 - Run the order test, then break it on purpose
_Goal: You watch one console line flip when the order is wrong._

Make a backup copy of `server.cfg` first. Then add both resources in dependency-first order, near the bottom, and save:

```text
ensure qu_dep_provider
ensure qu_dep_consumer
```

Open txAdmin in your browser, click **Live Console** in the left sidebar, then type each command into the input box at the bottom of that page and press Enter (one line at a time). Stop both resources first, then start the provider and consumer in that order:

```text
stop qu_dep_consumer
stop qu_dep_provider
start qu_dep_provider
start qu_dep_consumer
```

The consumer found the provider because it loaded second. Your exact lines may print in a slightly different order, and may carry a resource-name prefix the console adds, but the meaning is what matters (illustrative):

Expected output:

```
[qu_dep_provider] export ready
[qu_dep_consumer] provider found: pong
```

Note: a resource's exports only become callable after FXServer's first scheduler tick, which is why the consumer waits inside CreateThread before calling the provider.

Now break it. Swap the two lines so the consumer is ensured **first**, save, then stop both and start them in the wrong order. Merely restarting the consumer while the provider is still running would not test startup order.

```text
ensure qu_dep_consumer
ensure qu_dep_provider
```

```text
stop qu_dep_consumer
stop qu_dep_provider
start qu_dep_consumer
start qu_dep_provider
```

The consumer ran before the provider's export existed, so it reports the failure, the exact problem a real dependency hits when it loads too early (illustrative; line order and any console prefix may vary):

Expected output:

```
[qu_dep_consumer] provider MISSING (wrong ensure order)
[qu_dep_provider] export ready
```

Put the lines back in provider-first order and restore your backup if you changed anything else. You proved the rule from Step 3 with your own eyes.

### Step 6 - Check a real script's dependencies before you install it
_Goal: You can predict the right ensure position without restarting anything live._

For real paid scripts, inspect, do not restart. Open the resource's `fxmanifest.lua` and read its `dependencies` block and any shared scripts it pulls in:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'

dependencies {
'ox_lib',
'oxmysql',
'es_extended'
}
```

That `@ox_lib/init.lua` line is not a hint, it is the mechanism. The `@resource/file.lua` syntax tells FXServer to run another resource's file inside this one before its own scripts. So `shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'` runs ox_lib's init file here, and that init is what defines the global `lib` table this script then calls. If `ox_lib` has not started yet, the `@ox_lib/init.lua` include has nothing to pull in, `lib` is nil, and the script errors the instant it touches `lib.`. That is the real reason `ox_lib` must be `ensure`d above any script that uses it.

The `dependencies` block says the same thing in plain words: this script needs `ox_lib`, `oxmysql`, and the framework `ensure`d above it. Confirm each one already sits higher in `server.cfg`, then add the script below them. No live restart required.

> **The resources folder, at a glance**
> Bracket folders like `[local]`, `[jobs]`, or `[cars]` are organizers, FiveM still sees the resources inside them, and `ensure [cars]` starts every resource in that bracket. Your tree usually looks like `resources/[standalone]/ox_lib`, `resources/[qb]/qb-core`, `resources/[local]/qu_dep_provider`, and so on.

> **Go deeper**
> For a complete production server.cfg with every line and section explained, see [A complete server.cfg, annotated](/lessons/server-cfg-annotated). For folder layout and load order, see [Organize your server](/lessons/organize-server-folders).

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Couldn't find resource qu_dep_provider` -> The folder name and the ensure name must match exactly. Check for a typo or a wrapping zip folder, the resource folder must hold fxmanifest.lua directly.
- `provider MISSING when you expected found` -> The consumer was ensured above the provider. Put ensure qu_dep_provider first, save, then restart the provider before the consumer.
- `No such export Ping` -> The provider failed to start or has a typo in exports('Ping', ...). Restart qu_dep_provider and read the line above the error in the Live Console.
- `Access denied for user / Unknown database 'fivem'` -> Your mysql_connection_string is wrong or the database does not exist yet. Fix the connection string and create the database before ensuring oxmysql-dependent scripts.
- `The command is not recognized` -> Type server commands like restart in the txAdmin Live Console, not in the in-game F8 client console.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Read server.cfg top to bottom and explain what every important line does, including the two that hold secrets.
- Order an ensure block from each resource's declared dependencies, putting every named dependency above its dependent.
- Build a throwaway provider and consumer to prove ensure order without risking a live server.
- Open any script's fxmanifest.lua, read its dependencies and shared_script lines, and place it correctly in server.cfg by inspection alone.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Resource loads but says "dependency missing" | The dependency is below it in server.cfg, or not installed | Move `ensure your_script` BELOW `ensure the_dependency` in server.cfg |
| "Could not find resource X" | The resource folder name doesn't match | Folder names are case-sensitive. `my_resource` ≠ `My_Resource`. Match exactly |
| Server starts but no resources load | server.cfg has a typo or missing line | Check every `ensure` line matches an actual resource folder name |
| `sv_licenseKey` not recognized | Wrong format or missing quotes | Must be: `sv_licenseKey "licensekey:cfxk_..."` with quotes |
| Two resources both need each other (circular dependency) | One must start first, or they need restructuring | Move shared code to a third resource that both depend on |

## Try it yourself

Open one installed resource, read its `fxmanifest.lua`, and write down every dependency it names (the `dependencies` block and any `@resource/init.lua` shared scripts). Then state, in one sentence, which line must sit above it in `server.cfg` and why.

## References
- Next lesson · server.cfg annotated line by line: /lessons/server-cfg-annotated
- Cfx.re docs · Server commands: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- Cfx.re docs · Resource manifest: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- oxmysql documentation: https://overextended.dev/oxmysql
- ox_lib documentation: https://overextended.dev/ox_lib

title: Step 05 · The txAdmin tour · Quasar Academy
ai:
txAdmin is the cockpit for your server. It ships **bundled inside FXServer**, so there is nothing extra to install. Six panels do 95% of the work: **Live Console**, **Players**, **Resources**, **Recipes**, **Settings**, and the in-game **txAdmin menu** (opened with `/tx` or your assigned txAdmin menu keybind). We'll click through them all so nothing feels mysterious, and you'll send a live `status` command before the end.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll do: Open every txAdmin panel, identify what each one is for, and use the Live Console to send a real command.
- Time: ~15 minutes
- You need: Step 04 done. txAdmin running on http://localhost:40120 with a green badge.
- You'll learn: Live Console -> Players panel -> Resources tab -> Recipes -> Settings (CFG Editor, Game Mode, Privacy) -> in-game txAdmin menu

### Before you start
- txAdmin is open in my browser at http://localhost:40120.
- The top-left badge is green (Server Online).
- I'm logged in with the admin account I created in Step 03.
- I have a free Cfx.re license key from portal.cfx.re in my server.cfg.

> **What txAdmin actually is, in one minute**
> txAdmin is a web app that wraps your FXServer process. The server runs as a child of txAdmin: it watches the server, restarts it on crash, and gives you a dashboard so you never have to dig through a raw console window. Think of it as the nurse station outside the kitchen. The kitchen (FXServer) does the cooking. txAdmin tells you who is at the table, what is burning, and lets you send orders without walking in. Do one step, read the proof, then move on.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Open the dashboard and read the heartbeat
_Goal: You can send a command to your running server and read its reply._

Open your browser to the dashboard:

```text
http://localhost:40120
```

Confirm the top-left badge is green and says **Server Online**. In the left sidebar, click **Live Console**. You'll see a black terminal scrolling text: every line FXServer prints, you read here. At the bottom is an input box. Type this and press Enter:

```bash
status
```

The `rconlog` resource provides `status`. It prints the connected-player list (primary identifier, server ID, name, endpoint, and ping). With no players connected, the list is empty. That is your server answering live.

Expected output:

```
# Illustrative — exact columns and spacing vary by build. With no players connected you see no player rows.
# Each connected player prints one line: server ID, primary identifier, name, endpoint, ping.
```

Color codes matter: white is info, yellow is a warning, red is an error. If you see red, copy the line and search it. About 90% of red lines are typos in `server.cfg` or a bad `fxmanifest.lua`.

### Step 2 - Find a player in the Players panel
_Goal: You can locate any connected player and read their identifiers._

In the left sidebar, click **Players**, then the **Online** tab. It lists everyone connected right now (including you, once you join in Step 06). Click any name to open the **Player Modal**, where day-to-day moderation lives:

```text
Info -> identifiers: fivem:, steam:, discord:, license:
History -> past warnings, kicks, bans
IDs -> hardware IDs, for ban-evasion checks
Actions -> Warn / Kick / Ban / DM, each with a reason field
```

The **Info** tab is the one you'll use most. When you connect to your own server later, click your own name and copy the `fivem:` value. That is what you paste into the admin line in `server.cfg`:

```text
add_principal identifier.fivem:1234567 group.admin
```

### Step 3 - Restart one resource from the Resources tab
_Goal: You can flip a single resource without restarting the whole server._

In the left sidebar, click **Resources**. Each row is one resource folder. A green dot means running, grey means stopped, red means crashed. Hover a row to reveal **Start**, **Stop**, and **Restart**. The button at the top of the page, **Refresh List**, re-scans the `resources/` folder for new files you dropped in.

The core developer loop is: edit a script, save, click **Restart** on that one resource, then alt-tab back into the game. No full server restart needed. You can also do it from the Live Console. Restart the built-in `chat` resource to see it:

```bash
restart chat
```

Expected output:

```
# Illustrative — you'll see lines confirming chat stopped then started again.
# Exact wording varies by FXServer build; the point is the console echoes the restart.
```

### Step 4 - Understand Recipes (and when to use one)
_Goal: You know what a recipe is and why you almost never run a second one._

Click **Recipes** in the sidebar. A recipe is a file that tells txAdmin: download this list of resources, drop them in `resources/`, and add the matching `ensure` lines to `server.cfg`. One click builds a working server preset. You saw a recipe run in Step 03.

Recipe availability changes. ESX Legacy, QBCore, and Qbox each publish official sources; Qbox describes itself as a QBCore successor and publishes an official txAdmin recipe. Choose from the current recipe list by checking the framework docs and the compatibility requirements of the resources you intend to install.

Recipes are one-time scaffolds. Use one to **create a new server**, not to update an existing one. Running a second recipe on a working server overwrites your `server.cfg` and you lose your customizations.

### Step 5 - Edit server.cfg from the browser with the CFG Editor
_Goal: You can edit server.cfg in-browser, with highlighting and a save button._

Click **Settings**, then the **CFG Editor** tab. Your full `server.cfg` opens in a Monaco editor, the same engine VS Code uses. Scroll to your admin line and confirm your resources are ensured:

```text
ensure chat
ensure qu_hello
```

Edit, then click **Save**. If a change needs a full restart, txAdmin tells you and you click the **Server Restart** button at the top-right. This editor is the cleanest way to edit a server that runs on a remote box: no SSH, no FileZilla, a password-protected web tab.

Expected output:

```
Saved server.cfg. Some changes may require a server restart.
```

### Step 6 - Set Privacy to Hidden, then open the in-game txAdmin menu
_Goal: You hide your dev server from the public list and know how to open the txAdmin menu in-game._

Still in **Settings**, open the **Game Mode & Privacy** area. While you're learning, set **Server Privacy** to **Hidden**. A hidden server is not listed in the FiveM browser, so players reach it only through **Direct Connect**. Flip to **Public** when you're ready to advertise. The **Game Mode** label (Roleplay, Drift, Sandbox) is the tag shown next to your server in the browser. Click **Save**.

Once you connect (Step 06), you can open the in-game txAdmin menu two ways. Either type `/tx` or `/txadmin` in the chat box, or bind a key for it: in the FiveM client open **Settings > Key Bindings > FiveM** and set **(txAdmin) Menu: Open Main Page** to a key of your choice. The txAdmin menu has **no default key**, so it is command-only (`/tx`) until you bind one. The menu gives you teleport, a vehicle spawner, weather and time, player actions, and a Live Editor for placing objects, and it is enabled by default. Access is gated by txAdmin's own account system, **not** by ACE: only someone who has a txAdmin admin account with their **Discord or Cfx.re identifier linked** (Settings > Admin Manager) can open it. If neither identifier is attached to your txAdmin account, the menu will not open for you. This account gate is separate from the `server.cfg` `add_principal`/`group.admin` ACE, which controls console and chat-command permissions, not this menu.

Expected output:

```
Privacy saved. Your server is now hidden from the public list.
```

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Browser shows ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED at localhost:40120` -> FXServer is not running. Re-launch it (run.bat or FXServer), watch its console for crash lines, and reload the page.
- `The Live Console input box is greyed out` -> Your txAdmin account lacks command rights. Log in as the master admin you created in Step 03, or promote your account in Settings -> Admin Manager.
- `status replies with red License key error` -> Add a free license key from portal.cfx.re to server.cfg as sv_licenseKey, then restart. The key is required even for a local test server.
- `Players panel is empty but I'm in the server` -> Refresh the page. The panel polls every few seconds and the first load can miss your join.
- `Typing /tx does nothing in-game` -> Two common causes. (1) The menu is disabled: txAdmin's menu is on by default (txAdmin-menuEnabled), and changing that setting needs a server restart. (2) Your account is not linked: you need a txAdmin admin account with your Discord or Cfx.re identifier attached in Settings > Admin Manager. If you see a red authentication message and you ARE registered, type /txAdmin-reauth in chat to retry authentication.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Open txAdmin at http://localhost:40120 and confirm the green Server Online badge.
- Send live commands from the Live Console; status is your is-it-alive check.
- Find any player's fivem: identifier in the Players panel and paste it into add_principal.
- Restart one resource from the Resources tab instead of the whole server.
- Edit server.cfg in the CFG Editor and set Privacy to Hidden while you develop.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Can't log into txAdmin | Forgot the username/password you set on first boot | Delete `txData/<profile>/admins.json` and restart — txAdmin asks you to create a new admin |
| "Server Offline" even though FXServer.exe is running | The server crashed but the process didn't exit | Check the Live Console for red error lines. Fix the error, then restart |
| Resources tab shows nothing | No resources are in the resources folder or server.cfg | Check your resources folder exists and has subfolders. Check server.cfg has `ensure` lines |
| CFG Editor changes don't save | You didn't click "Save" or the server rejected the change | Click the Save button (floppy disk icon). If it fails, the console will say why |
| Live Console is blank | The console buffer was cleared or server just started | Restart the server or wait for it to print new messages. Old messages are in `txData/logs/` |

## Try it yourself

Without joining the game, do three things in txAdmin: run `status` in the Live Console and read the reply, click **Restart** on `chat` in the Resources tab and watch the console echo it, then set **Privacy** to **Hidden** in Settings and Save. Write down the exact `status` output you saw.

## References
- CFX docs · Setting up a server using txAdmin: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-server-txadmin/
- CFX Portal · get your free license key: https://portal.cfx.re/
- txAdmin · official GitHub repo and changelog: https://github.com/citizenfx/txAdmin
- Qbox framework docs: https://docs.qbox.re/

title: Step 06 · Connect, restart, read console · Quasar Academy
ai:
This is the last 15 minutes of Track 0. You'll join your own server, learn the three reload commands every developer memorizes, and start reading the server console with confidence. You already have a server running because **txAdmin ships bundled inside FXServer** and opened on `http://localhost:40120` the first time you booted it. After this lesson you are ready for Lua.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll do: Direct-connect to your own server with localhost, open the in-game console (F8), run tx, learn ensure / restart / refresh, and read a real server log line by line.
- Time: ~15 minutes
- You need: Steps 03, 04, 05 done. txAdmin online (green badge) on http://localhost:40120, and the FiveM client installed.
- You'll learn: F8 console -> connect localhost -> the tx command -> ensure vs restart vs refresh -> reading info / warn / error lines -> where logs live on disk

### Before you start
- txAdmin shows a green "Server Online" badge at http://localhost:40120.
- The FiveM client is installed (Step 01) and I have a Cfx.re license key from portal.cfx.re in server.cfg.
- My server.cfg has no red error lines in the Live Console.
- I can keep VS Code and the txAdmin Live Console open side by side.

> **Two consoles, two jobs**
> You are about to use two different consoles. The **server console** is txAdmin's Live Console (or the window where `FXServer` runs); it prints what the *server* is doing and you read it to debug script crashes. The **client console** is in-game, opened with `F8`; it prints what your *client* sees and is where you type `connect`. Think of the server console as the kitchen pass and the client console as your own receipt. Different views of the same server.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Connect to your own server
_Goal: You spawn in-game on your own localhost server._

Your server is already running through txAdmin. Now join it from the game client:

1. Open the FiveM client.
2. Top tab, click **Play**.
3. Right side, click **Direct Connect**.
4. Type the address below in the bar, then click **Connect**.

```text
localhost
```

The first connect downloads any resources your server added, so the loading screen takes a moment. `localhost` means "this same computer" because you run the server and the client on one PC. Real players join later with your public IP or a Cfx.re join code. Security note: your Cfx.re license key in `server.cfg` is a secret. Never paste it in chat, screenshot it, or commit it to Git. And exposing your public IP / opening the game port (default 30120) means strangers can attempt to connect, so only do that behind a firewall once you understand it.

Expected output:

```
Loading screen progresses, then you spawn in-game on the default map.
```

### Step 2 - Open F8 and run tx
_Goal: The txAdmin in-game menu opens from the console._

While in-game, press `F8`. A black console drops down from the top. Type this and press Enter:

```text
tx
```

`tx` opens the txAdmin admin menu. In the F8 console you type commands without a leading slash, so `tx` here is the same registered command as `/tx` (or `/txadmin`) typed in the chat box, or with a key you assign yourself under Game Settings > Key Bindings > FiveM ('(txAdmin) Menu: Open Main Page'). txAdmin sets no default keybind. Press `F8` again to close the console. F8 is also where you run client-side commands later, like your own `RegisterCommand` callbacks.

Expected output:

```
The txAdmin admin menu opens on screen (identical to running /tx or to your assigned txAdmin Menu keybind).
```

### Step 3 - Learn ensure vs restart vs refresh
_Goal: You can pick the right reload command without guessing._

Three commands, three jobs. Run them from the **txAdmin Live Console or the FXServer server console**. They are server commands and do not run from a normal player's F8 client console.

```text
ensure my-resource
```

`ensure` starts the resource if it is stopped, and restarts it if it is already running. This is the line you put in `server.cfg` for every resource. It is the safe re-bounce.

```text
restart my-resource
```

`restart` stops then starts a running resource in one single step (it never leaves the resource off in between). Use it after you edit a Lua or JS file. Do not type `stop` then `start` as two separate commands; that leaves a window where the resource is dead.

```text
refresh
```

`refresh` re-scans the `resources/` folder for new sub-folders. It does not start anything on its own. After you drop in a brand-new resource folder, run `refresh` first, then `ensure my-resource`.

The one distinction that trips up beginners: `ensure` is what you write in `server.cfg` to bring a resource up at boot, and `restart` is what you type at runtime to reload it after an edit. Same family, different moment. When in doubt at runtime, `ensure my-resource` also works because it restarts an already-running resource.

Try it on a resource that already ships with your server:

```text
restart chat
```

Expected output:

```
Two lifecycle lines appear (exact text and spacing vary by artifact version, illustrative):
[ info] Stopping resource chat
[ info] Started resource chat
```

### Step 4 - Read the server console one line at a time
_Goal: You can label any console line as info, warn, or error._

Open txAdmin, then Live Console. The sample lines below are illustrative (your real lines will name your own resources and players); read them top to bottom:

```text
[ info] Console: Resource scoreboard started.
[ info] Player [Kishi] connected from 127.0.0.1.
[ warn] CFX: dependency 'es_extended' not found, scoreboard may misbehave
[ error] SCRIPT ERROR: @scoreboard/cl_score.lua:42: attempt to index a nil value (global 'PlayerData')
stack traceback:
@scoreboard/cl_score.lua:42: in function <@scoreboard/cl_score.lua:40>
```

How to read them:

- `info` (white) is FYI. A resource started, a player joined, nothing is wrong.
- `warn` (yellow) means "you should know". The server still runs, but something is off, such as a missing dependency or a deprecated API.
- `error` (red) means something broke. It almost always names the file and line number. Open that file, read that line, fix it.
- `stack traceback` is the call chain. The top line is where it actually broke; the lines under it are who called it.

Triage rule: red before yellow before white. One error often cascades into many warnings, so fix the topmost red line first and the warnings frequently disappear with it.

Expected output:

```
You can point at each line above and say which is info, warn, or error.
```

### Step 5 - Find the logs on disk
_Goal: You know where to look when the Live Console cannot show you something._

The Live Console only keeps the most recent output. Older history is written to disk by txAdmin under `txData/<profile>/` (the `<profile>` part is the name of your txAdmin data profile, which is `default` unless the server owner set a custom one). The log folders are:

```text
txData/<profile>/logs/fxserver/ full FXServer console rolling log
txData/<profile>/logs/admin/ admin actions: kick, ban, restarts
txData/<profile>/logs/server/ server-side script and event logs
```

If you started txAdmin with a different profile or moved your data folder, the path shifts. The reliable move is to search for it. On Windows, run this in Command Prompt (it is `cmd.exe` syntax, not bash):

```text
dir /s /b txData\*\logs
```

On macOS or Linux:

```bash
find . -type d -path "*txData*/logs*"
```

Expected output:

```
The search prints the logs folder under txData/<profile>/logs.
```

### Step 6 - Run the edit, restart, observe loop
_Goal: You complete the loop you will repeat thousands of times._

Every script change from now on follows the same three beats. Practice it once on the `chat` resource:

1. **Edit** one of your own resource's Lua files in VS Code and save with `Ctrl`+`S`. Do not edit the bundled `chat` resource for this exercise.
2. **Restart** the resource. Two ways:

```text
restart chat
```

Or in txAdmin, open the Resources tab and click **Restart** on the row.

3. **Observe**. Watch the Live Console for the lifecycle lines, then trigger the feature you changed to confirm it works. If you only want to practise the command safely, restart `chat` without editing it.

Recover from a bad edit: if a red `error` line appears after you restart, your last edit broke something. Undo it in VS Code with `Ctrl`+`Z`, save again with `Ctrl`+`S`, and run `restart my-resource` once more. The error should clear.

Keep VS Code on the left half of your screen and the Live Console on the right half. You edit on the left, save, and watch the right side react with no alt-tab.

Expected output:

```
[ info] Stopping resource chat
[ info] Started resource chat
```

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Connection failed: timed out` -> Your server is on a different port than expected. Check the Live Console for the UDP listener port and connect with localhost:PORT instead of plain localhost.
- `F8 does not open the console` -> Your keyboard layout may map F8 elsewhere. In FiveM go to Settings -> Key Bindings, search "console", and re-bind it.
- `could not find resource` -> Folder name typo, or you skipped refresh after dropping in a new folder. Run refresh first, then ensure my-resource. Names are case-sensitive on Linux.
- `I edited the file but the server still runs old code` -> Saving does not reload anything. Run restart my-resource so FXServer drops the cached version.
- `FXServer window vanished or the server crashed` -> Open the newest log under txData/<profile>/logs/fxserver; the bottom of the file shows the crash. Comment out suspect ensure lines in server.cfg, restart, and isolate the bad resource.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Direct-connect to your own server with localhost from the FiveM client.
- Open F8 for the client console and run tx to reach the txAdmin menu.
- Pick ensure for server.cfg, restart for a hot reload after editing, and refresh for a brand-new folder.
- Label every console line as info, warn, or error and fix the topmost red line first.
- Find persistent logs under txData/<profile>/logs, and search for them if the path moved.
- Run the edit -> restart -> observe loop that every later lesson assumes.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| "connect localhost" does nothing (stays on the F8 screen) | Server isn't running or the FiveM client can't reach it | Check txAdmin shows "Server Online" (green). If not, start FXServer.exe |
| "Timed out" or "Connection failed" | Firewall is blocking the connection | Allow FXServer.exe and FiveM.exe in Windows Firewall. Check port 30120 is open |
| `ensure my_resource` says "Could not find resource" | Folder name doesn't match, or folder is in the wrong place | Check `resources/my_resource/fxmanifest.lua` exists. Folder name must match exactly |
| `restart` works but changes don't appear in game | Client cache is showing the old version | Clear `%localappdata%/FiveM/FiveM.app/data/nui-storage/` and reconnect |
| F8 console won't open | Keybind conflict or F8 is locked by another program | Try pressing F8 while holding the Fn key. Close other programs that use F8 (OBS, Discord overlay) |

## Try it yourself

Run the loop once end to end: direct-connect to `localhost`, open `F8` and run `tx` to confirm the menu opens, then switch to the **txAdmin Live Console**, run `restart chat`, and find the `Started resource chat` line. Write down which console accepted each command.

## References
- CFX docs: server commands (start, stop, restart, ensure, refresh): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- CFX docs: setting up a server with txAdmin: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-server-txadmin/
- Cfx.re Portal (free license keys): https://portal.cfx.re/
- CFX docs: Lua scripting runtime (next track): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/

title: Lesson 07 · Anatomy of a resource · Quasar Academy

toc.label: Build it, one file at a time

ai:
A FiveM resource is nothing more than a folder with a label card inside it. The label card is a file called `fxmanifest.lua`. It tells the server three things: what this folder is, which files belong to it, and which side each file runs on. In this lesson you build that folder by hand, one file at a time, and watch it start.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A working resource named qu_hello: a folder with a manifest and three script files that each print on the correct side.
- Time: ~15 minutes
- You need: Your server's resources folder open, txAdmin running, and the F8 console ready in game.
- You'll learn: What fxmanifest.lua is, every line it needs, and how FiveM decides which file runs where.

### Before you start
- I can find my server's resources folder.
- I have a server I can restart (txAdmin or a console).
- I can open the in game console with F8.

> **Before you touch anything**
> Find your server's `resources` folder first. Everything below happens inside it. With txAdmin it is usually `.../txData/your-server/resources/`.

## Build it, one file at a time

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: An empty folder the server can find._

Inside your `resources` folder, make a new folder named exactly:

```text
resources/qu_hello
```

The folder name *is* the resource name. Keep it lowercase with no spaces. You type this same name in `server.cfg` later, so spell it the same everywhere.

### Step 2 - Create the manifest file
_Goal: FiveM now recognises the folder as a resource._

Inside `qu_hello`, create a new file named exactly:

```text
fxmanifest.lua
```

This is the label card. Without it, FiveM ignores the folder. The name must be exactly `fxmanifest.lua`, not `manifest.lua`, and not the old `__resource.lua`.

### Step 3 - Write the manifest, line by line
_Goal: The server knows which files to load and where._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and type this. Each line is explained underneath.

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

author 'you'
description 'My first resource'

shared_script 'config.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

- `fx_version 'cerulean'`: the manifest format version. Always use the latest, `cerulean` is current.
- `game 'gta5'`: which game this runs on. For FiveM it is always `gta5`.
- `shared_script`: runs on *both* the server and every client. Use it for config and constants only.
- `client_script`: runs on each player's PC. Drawing, key presses, NUI.
- `server_script`: runs once on the server. Database, money, anything players must not control.

### Step 4 - Create the three script files
_Goal: Each side has one file with one print._

Still inside `qu_hello`, create three more files and put one print in each, so you can see where it runs.

```lua
-- config.lua  (shared)
print('[qu_hello] shared loaded')
```

```lua
-- client.lua
print('[qu_hello] hello from your CLIENT')
```

```lua
-- server.lua
print('[qu_hello] hello from the SERVER')
```

Your folder now looks like this:

```text
resources/qu_hello/
  fxmanifest.lua
  config.lua
  client.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 5 - Tell the server to start it
_Goal: The resource starts when the server boots._

Open your `server.cfg` file (it sits next to the `resources` folder). Add this line near the other `ensure` lines:

```text
ensure qu_hello
```

`ensure` means "start this resource, and restart it if it was already running". The name after it must match the folder name exactly.

### Step 6 - Restart and read the proof
_Goal: You see your three prints split across two consoles._

Save every file. Then, in the live server console (or the txAdmin Live Console, reached from the txAdmin web panel on port 40120), type:

```text
ensure qu_hello
```

Use `ensure` here rather than `restart`: `ensure` starts the resource if it is stopped and restarts it if it is already running, so it works whether or not you rebooted the server after editing server.cfg. (`restart qu_hello` also works, but only once the resource is already running.)

Now watch both consoles. The client lines only appear once you are actually in the game world: join your own server first (in the FiveM client, open F8 and type `connect 127.0.0.1:30120`, or use Direct Connect to the same address). If you are not connected in-game, only the two server-console lines will show, and that is expected — there is simply no client running yet.

Expected output:

```
In the server console (your real console will also show a timestamp and may interleave other lines — the part that matters is these two appearing):

[qu_hello] shared loaded
[qu_hello] hello from the SERVER

In your F8 game console:

[qu_hello] shared loaded
[qu_hello] hello from your CLIENT
```

The shared line prints on *both* sides. The server line prints only on the server. The client line prints only on your screen. That split is the whole point of the three script types.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_hello doesn't exist` -> The folder name and the ensure line don't match, or there is no fxmanifest.lua. Check the spelling and the file name.
- `Could not load resource qu_hello` -> fxmanifest.lua has a typo. A missing quote or a stray character breaks the whole manifest.
- `Nothing prints at all` -> You added ensure but didn't restart, or you edited the wrong server.cfg. Run restart qu_hello and look again.
- `Only the server line shows, and I am NOT connected in-game` -> That is expected. The client line only appears in the F8 console once you have joined your own server (connect 127.0.0.1:30120). Connect, then restart qu_hello again.
- `I AM connected in-game but the F8 client line never appears` -> client.lua is not being loaded or errored. Confirm the manifest has the line client_script 'client.lua', the filename matches exactly, and check the F8 console for a red Lua error from qu_hello.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Make a resource folder and a fxmanifest.lua by hand.
- Read every manifest line and say what it controls.
- Choose shared, client, or server for a file on purpose.
- Start a resource from server.cfg and prove it ran.

## Try it yourself

Add a fourth print to `server.lua` that shows the resource name, using the native `GetCurrentResourceName()`:

```lua
print('[qu_hello] my name is ' .. GetCurrentResourceName())
```

Restart and confirm it prints `qu_hello`. You just read a value FiveM handed you instead of hard coding it.

## References
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- Manifest: script loading order: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Lesson 08 · Client, server, shared · Quasar Academy
ai:
Open almost any serious FiveM resource and you see the same three folders: `client/`, `server/`, and `shared/`. They are not decoration. Each one runs in a different place, and putting a line in the wrong folder is how beginners leak secrets, crash the client, or wonder why a value is `nil`. This lesson builds the split for real and then teaches the rule for deciding where every line you write belongs.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A three-folder resource where a shared config table is read by both the client and the server, proven across the network with one command.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM practice setup you can restart, a text editor, and the ability to read both txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- You'll learn: The client / server / shared split, the decision rule for what goes where, why shared runs on both sides, glob imports, and what must never live in shared

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- I have built at least one single-file resource before (Lesson 07).

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a working FiveM resource named `qu_client_server_shared` with real `client/`, `server/`, and `shared/` folders. One shared config table is read by both sides, and a command proves it crosses the network. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_client_server_shared
```

### Step 2 - Create the three folders and their files
_Goal: The three-folder convention exists on disk._

Create this exact layout. The folder names are the convention you will see everywhere:

```text
resources/qu_client_server_shared/
  fxmanifest.lua
  shared/
    config.lua
  server/
    main.lua
  client/
    main.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM loads each folder into the right runtime._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_scripts {
    'shared/*.lua'
}

server_scripts {
    'server/*.lua'
}

client_scripts {
    'client/*.lua'
}
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is the only Lua runtime now, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the shared config
_Goal: One config table that both sides will read._

Open `shared/config.lua` and paste this:

```lua
Config = {}
Config.Greeting = 'three folders, one resource'
Config.MaxDistance = 25.0
```

### Step 5 - Write the server code
_Goal: The server reads shared config and fires an event across the network._

Open `server/main.lua` and paste this:

```lua
print('[qu_client_server_shared] server read Config.Greeting: ' .. Config.Greeting)

RegisterCommand('splitproof', function(src)
    print('[qu_client_server_shared] server fired splitproof for src ' .. src)
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_client_server_shared:proof', src, Config.MaxDistance)
end, false)
```

### Step 6 - Write the client code
_Goal: The client reads the same shared config and receives the server's event._

Open `client/main.lua` and paste this:

```lua
print('[qu_client_server_shared] client read Config.Greeting: ' .. Config.Greeting)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_client_server_shared:proof', function(maxDistance)
    print('[qu_client_server_shared] client received event, Config.MaxDistance = ' .. maxDistance)
end)
```

### Step 7 - Start and test it
_Goal: The shared value appears on both sides, and the event crosses the network._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_client_server_shared
```

    Save. Because this resource is brand new and has never started, do NOT use `restart` yet — `restart` only works on a resource the server is already running. In the server console (the txAdmin Live Console you opened in the prerequisites), first make the server notice the new files, then start the resource:

    ```text
refresh
ensure qu_client_server_shared
```

    `refresh` rescans the `resources` folder so FiveM sees the files you just created; `ensure` then starts the resource (and from now on, after you edit a file, `restart qu_client_server_shared` is the command you reuse because the resource is already running).

    Join the server, open the in-game chat box with T, and run:

    ```text
/splitproof
```

    The command uses `false` because this proof action only prints and sends a number back to the player who ran it. Running it as a connected player gives the handler a real player source such as `1`. The server console has source `0`, which is not a connected client and therefore cannot receive `TriggerClientEvent`.

Expected output:

```
Server: [qu_client_server_shared] server read Config.Greeting: three folders, one resource
Server: [qu_client_server_shared] server fired splitproof for src 1   (your number may differ — it is your player id, not always 1)
F8: [qu_client_server_shared] client read Config.Greeting: three folders, one resource
F8: [qu_client_server_shared] client received event, Config.MaxDistance = 25.0
```

## How it works

It worked, so now take it apart. The whole point of this lesson is one idea that beginners get wrong constantly: a FiveM resource is not one program. It is two programs that happen to ship in the same folder, plus a shared layer that gets copied into both. Once you see it that way, the folder split stops being a style choice and becomes the map of where your code actually runs.

### Two runtimes, not one

When the resource starts, FiveM spins up two separate Lua virtual machines. One runs on the server machine. A separate copy runs inside every connected player's game. They do not share memory. A variable you set on the server is invisible to the client unless you deliberately send it across the network.

That is why the proof printed in two different places. The server's print landed in txAdmin Live Console because `server/main.lua` only ever runs on the server. The client's print landed in F8 because `client/main.lua` runs inside your game. Same resource, two consoles, because two runtimes.

### What shared_script actually does

Here is the part that trips people up. `Config` is not a synced object. The `shared/config.lua` file is loaded into BOTH Lua VMs, so the code runs twice, once per side, and `Config` becomes a plain global on each side independently.

Read that again, because it is the core mental model. There is no magic link between the server's `Config` and the client's `Config`. They are two separate tables that happen to start identical because they were built from the same source file. If the server later does `Config.MaxDistance = 99.0`, the client's copy is still `25.0`. Shared means "the same starting code runs on both sides," not "the same live value lives on both sides."

That is exactly why config belongs in shared. Both sides need to agree on constants like a max interaction distance or a label. Putting those constants in one shared file means both VMs read the same numbers without an event round-trip on startup. One source of truth, zero network traffic to read it.

### The decision rule for where a line goes

Stop guessing per file. Use this rule for every line you write:

- **client/** if the line touches the local player: the ped, the camera, drawing on screen, NUI, key presses, anything that only makes sense for one person's game. Natives like `PlayerPedId()`, `DrawMarker`, `SetNuiFocus` live here.
- **server/** if the line touches authority: the `source` of an event, money, inventory, the database, bans, or anything a cheating client must not be allowed to decide. This side is the referee. It is the only side a player cannot edit.
- **shared/** only for data and helpers that are safe on both sides: a number, a label, a list of job names, or a function that turns cents into a money string. No secrets, no authoritative mutable state, and no native unless the official reference marks it available on both client and server.

When you are unsure, ask one question: does this line decide something a player could cheat? If yes, it goes in server. The server is the only side you control; the client runs on the player's machine and they can read and change it.

### What must never go in shared

Two things will burn you if they end up in `shared/`.

The first is secrets. Database connection strings, API keys, admin passwords. Anything in `shared/` is shipped to every client and a determined player can dump it out of their game memory. A secret in shared is a secret you have handed to the public. Secrets belong in `server/` or, better, in convars set in `server.cfg`.

The second is side-specific natives. Natives like `GetPlayerIdentifiers` or `DropPlayer` only exist in the server runtime. If a shared file calls one, it loads fine on the server but throws `attempt to call a nil value` when the client runs that same file. Some Cfx natives are available on both sides, so check the API-set badge in the official native reference rather than applying a blanket "no natives" rule.

### Why the glob import works and why load order matters

Look back at the manifest. You wrote `shared_scripts { 'shared/*.lua' }`, not a single filename. The `*` is a glob: FiveM expands it to every matching `.lua` file in that folder. Add `shared/jobs.lua` tomorrow and the manifest already includes it. The official manifest reference does not promise an alphabetical order for glob matches, so list files explicitly whenever one shared file depends on another.

The curly-brace list also matters for a second reason: order. FiveM loads `shared_scripts` before `client_scripts` and `server_scripts`. That ordering is the reason your prints worked. By the time `server/main.lua` and `client/main.lua` run, `shared/config.lua` has already run on that side, so `Config` exists. Flip the order, load a script before the shared file that defines `Config`, and you get `attempt to index a nil value (global 'Config')`. Shared first is not a suggestion; it is what makes the config available when the rest of your code reads it.

One more detail on the manifest key for the future. When this resource depends on another (say `oxmysql` or `ox_lib`, the community Overextended libraries you will meet later), the canonical key is the plural `dependencies { 'oxmysql' }`. The singular `dependency 'oxmysql'` is tolerated but the plural braced form is the one to learn.

### Check yourself: You set Config.MaxDistance = 25.0 in shared. On the server you later write Config.MaxDistance = 99.0. What does the client print when the event fires, and why?

The client still prints `25.0`. The server and the client each loaded their own copy of `shared/config.lua`, so they hold two independent `Config` tables. Editing the server's copy does nothing to the client's copy. Shared means the same starting code ran on both sides, not that the live value is synced. If you genuinely needed the new value on the client, you would have to send it, which is exactly what the `TriggerClientEvent` line does: it pushes `Config.MaxDistance` across the network as the event payload, and the client reads what arrived in its handler, not its own table.

### When you reach for this split

You reach for it on the very first file of every resource, not as cleanup later. The moment a resource has both a server rule and a client display, you split it. A shop: the client draws the marker and menu, the server validates the purchase and moves the money, and shared holds the item list and prices both sides reference. A job: client handles the on-screen prompts, server tracks who is clocked in, shared lists the job names. Set the three folders up first and the decision rule answers "where does this go" for you every time you add a line.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'Config')` -> A client or server file ran before shared loaded, or the file is in the wrong folder. Confirm config.lua is inside shared/ and the manifest lists shared_scripts before client_scripts and server_scripts.
- `Could not find dependency / no script files matched 'shared/*.lua'` -> The folder name or glob is wrong. The path is case sensitive and relative to the resource root. Confirm the folder is named shared (not Shared) and the file ends in .lua.
- `Server console prints, but F8 shows nothing` -> The client print and event live in client/main.lua, which only runs in-game. You must join the server, not just restart it from the console. Read F8 while connected.
- `attempt to call a nil value when the resource starts` -> A shared file is calling a native that is unavailable on one side. Check the native's API-set badge and move the call into server/ or client/ unless it is documented for both.
- `splitproof prints source 0 but nothing reaches F8` -> You ran the command from txAdmin. Source 0 is the server console, not a player. Join the server and run /splitproof in chat, or type splitproof without a slash in F8.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Lay out a resource as client/, server/, and shared/ folders and point the manifest at each with a glob.
- Explain that a resource is two separate Lua VMs, so server and client never share memory unless you send data across the network.
- State that shared_script runs the same file on both sides as two independent globals, not one synced value.
- Apply the decision rule: local player and UI go in client, authority and data go in server, pure config and stateless helpers go in shared.
- Name the two things that must never go in shared: secrets and server-only natives, and say why each one breaks.
- Explain why shared loads first and what error you get when load order is wrong.

## Try it yourself

Prove the "two independent copies" claim with your own eyes. In `server/main.lua`, set `Config.Greeting = 'server edited this'` on the line right after the existing server print, then add a second command:

```lua
RegisterCommand('checkconfig', function(src)
    print('[qu_client_server_shared] server Config.Greeting is now: ' .. Config.Greeting)
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_client_server_shared:checkconfig', src)
end, false)
```

In `client/main.lua`, add the matching handler:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_client_server_shared:checkconfig', function()
    print('[qu_client_server_shared] client Config.Greeting is still: ' .. Config.Greeting)
end)
```

Restart `qu_client_server_shared`, join, and run `/checkconfig` in chat. The server prints `server edited this` while the client still prints `three folders, one resource`. You just watched the two VMs disagree, which is the whole lesson. Now move that `Config.Greeting = ...` edit into `shared/config.lua` instead and confirm both sides agree again, because now both copies start from the changed source.

## References
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- FiveM scripting: understanding scopes: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/introduction/
- FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Lesson 09 · Start, restart, stop, your daily dev loop · Quasar Academy
ai:
FiveM development is mostly one small loop: edit, save, `ensure`, read the console, test. Beginners often think they need more tricks than that. They do not. This lesson makes that loop feel normal, and teaches the four server commands that drive it so you always know which one to reach for.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A repeatable workflow: edit -> save -> ensure -> read console -> test.
- Time: ~15 minutes
- You need: The qu_hello resource from Lessons 07 and 08, and a running FiveM server.
- You'll learn: The four commands -> reading the server console -> the dev loop -> server.cfg -> common error patterns

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds one server-only resource named `qu_dev_loop` that prints a counter every time you restart it. That counter is the proof that your dev loop actually reloaded the code. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section explains the four commands and the console so you know which one to reach for.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_dev_loop
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_dev_loop/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
print('[qu_dev_loop] resource started')

RegisterCommand('dev_loop', function()
    print('[qu_dev_loop] command fired')
end, false)
```

There are two prints on purpose. The first one is at the top level of the file, so it runs the moment the resource starts. That is what proves your restart actually reloaded the code. The second one lives inside a command handler, so it only runs when you type the command.

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the server console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

```text
ensure qu_dev_loop
```

Save the file. Because this folder is brand new, the running server has not scanned it yet, so first tell it to look, in the txAdmin Live Console (the server console):

```text
refresh
```

Then start the resource, also from the server console:

```text
ensure qu_dev_loop
```

Use `ensure`, not `restart`, the first time: the resource has not started yet this session, and `restart` only reloads a resource that is already running, so it would do nothing here. (Once the resource is already up, `restart` is the command you use after each edit.) You should see the start line print immediately. Now run the command, also from the server console:

```text
dev_loop
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_dev_loop] resource started
[qu_dev_loop] command fired
```

## How it works

You restarted the resource and saw the start line. You typed the command and saw the second line. That is the entire dev loop in two prints. Now take it apart so you stop guessing which server command to run and start choosing on purpose.

### The two prints, and why one fires on restart

```lua
print('[qu_dev_loop] resource started')

RegisterCommand('dev_loop', function()
    print('[qu_dev_loop] command fired')
end, false)
```

When you run `restart qu_dev_loop`, FiveM reloads the resource and reads `server.lua` from the top, once. The first `print` is at the top level of the file, so it runs right then. That single line is your loop confirmation: if you see it, the new code is live. If you change the file, save, restart, and the start line does not change, the server is still running the old code and your edit never reached it.

The second `print` is inside the `RegisterCommand` handler, so reading the file does not run it. FiveM only stores the handler and waits. When you type `dev_loop`, the handler runs and the second line prints. Registering and running are two separate moments. That gap is why you need both a restart step (reload the code) and a test step (fire the code) in every loop.

### The four commands, and when each one is used

A FiveM server controls resources with a small set of console commands. The whole dev loop is choosing the right one. Here is what each does and when you reach for it.

The trap beginners fall into is creating a new folder and immediately typing `start qu_dev_loop`, getting `Resource qu_dev_loop does not exist`, and assuming their code is broken. It is not. The server has not scanned the folder yet. The fix is `refresh` first, then `ensure`. Once the resource is known and listed in `server.cfg`, your day-to-day loop never touches `refresh` again. You just edit, save, and `restart`.

### Why ensure is the one you put in server.cfg

`ensure` is the useful idempotent default: if the resource is stopped it starts it, and if it is already running it restarts it. `start` only starts a stopped resource and does not reload one that is already running. Both commands are valid in `server.cfg`; modern templates usually use `ensure` because the same line also works during manual recovery when the resource's state is uncertain.

`restart` versus `refresh` is the other distinction worth locking in. `restart qu_dev_loop` reloads the code of one resource that already exists. `refresh` does not restart anything. It only rescans the folder so the server learns about new resources and manifest changes. New folder: `refresh`, then `ensure`. Changed code in an existing resource: just `restart`.

### The restricted flag on RegisterCommand

```lua
RegisterCommand('dev_loop', function()
    print('[qu_dev_loop] command fired')
end, false)
```

The third argument is the restricted flag, and it is the reason your command either works or silently does nothing.

- With `false`, the command is open. Anyone can run it, including you from the server console (type the bare name `dev_loop`) and any connected player from their chat box (press T, then type it with a leading slash: `/dev_loop`). For a teaching command you want to fire freely while testing, `false` is correct. That is what this lesson uses.
- With `true`, the command is restricted. It requires an ace permission, which is a rule in the server config that grants a specific identifier the right to run it. The server console always has every ace, so a restricted command still works from the console. But a beginner who sets `true`, then tries to run the command from anywhere without an ace, sees nothing print and assumes the code is broken.

The old version of this lesson used `true` and never explained aces, so the proof line could silently fail to appear. You use `false` here on purpose: the command runs cleanly whether you type it in the server console or test it later in chat, and there is no hidden permission gate to debug. You will meet aces and restricted commands properly when you build admin actions, where keeping players out is the entire point.

### Check yourself: You created a brand new resource folder, added `ensure my_thing` to server.cfg, and typed `start my_thing` in the console. It says the resource does not exist. What is wrong, and what two commands fix it?

The running server has not scanned the new folder, so it does not know the resource exists yet. Run `refresh` to load new resource manifests, then `ensure my_thing` to start it. `start my_thing` would also start it after the refresh; `ensure` is preferred here because it works whether the resource is stopped or already running.

### Reading the server console

The last step of the loop is read the console, and that only helps if you can tell a good line from a bad one. After `ensure qu_dev_loop` or `restart qu_dev_loop`, the server prints a status line. A clean start looks like this:

```text
Started resource qu_dev_loop
[qu_dev_loop] resource started
```

The first line is the server telling you it brought the resource up. The second is your own top-level `print` confirming your code ran. Seeing both is the signal that the loop closed: new code is live.

A failed start looks different. The server prints an error instead of `Started resource`, and your own start line never appears:

```text
Error parsing script @qu_dev_loop/server.lua in resource qu_dev_loop: server.lua:4: '<eof>' expected near 'end'
Failed to load script server.lua.
```

When you see that, do not retype commands hoping it fixes itself. Read the line. It names the file, the line number, and what the parser expected. Open that file, go to that line, fix the syntax, save, and `restart` again. The console is not noise to scroll past. It is the feedback half of your loop, and learning to read it is the whole skill.

This is also why `qu_dev_loop` is a server-only resource. There is no `client_script`, so nothing runs on a player's game and there is no F8 client output to check. Every line for this lesson lands in the server console. When a resource does have client code, the split matters: server `print` goes to the server console, client `print` goes to F8 in the game.

### When you reach for this loop

This is not a beginner ritual you outgrow. It is the loop you run thousands of times.

- **After every code change.** Edit, save, `restart qu_dev_loop`, read the console, test. The tighter you make this loop, the faster you build.
- **When a resource misbehaves on a live server.** `restart` it to reload it clean, watch the console for the start line and any errors, then reproduce the problem.
- **When you add a new resource to a running server.** `refresh` once so the server sees it, then `ensure` it, then add the `ensure` line to server.cfg so it survives the next reboot.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_dev_loop does not exist` -> You created the folder but the server has not scanned it. Run refresh first, then ensure qu_dev_loop. Check the folder is directly inside resources and the name matches exactly.
- `Error parsing script ... '<eof>' expected near 'end'` -> A quote, comma, paren, or end is missing in server.lua. The error names the line number. Open that line, fix the syntax, save, and restart qu_dev_loop.
- `The start line prints but never changes after I edit` -> The server is still running the old code. You edited the file but did not restart, or you restarted a different resource. Save, then run restart qu_dev_loop and watch the start line.
- `dev_loop prints nothing` -> Confirm the resource started (you saw the start line) and that you typed dev_loop, not the resource name. With the restricted flag set to false the command runs from the server console with no permission setup.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Run the full loop: edit server.lua, save, restart qu_dev_loop, read the console, then run the command.
- Choose the right command on purpose: ensure to bring a resource up in any state, start for a stopped one, restart to reload changed code, stop to take one offline, refresh to make the server see a brand new folder.
- Explain why ensure is the idempotent default in server.cfg while start only starts a stopped resource.
- Tell a clean Started resource line apart from an Error parsing script line, and act on the line number instead of retyping commands.
- Say what the RegisterCommand restricted flag does and why a teaching command uses false.

## Try it yourself

Turn the proof into a restart counter that survives restarts, so the start line itself shows your loop working. FiveM gives every resource a tiny key-value store on disk. Read a number, add one, save it back, and print it on start:

```lua
local count = GetResourceKvpInt('restart_count') + 1
SetResourceKvpInt('restart_count', count)
print('[qu_dev_loop] restart number ' .. count)

RegisterCommand('dev_loop', function()
    print('[qu_dev_loop] command fired')
end, false)
```

Save, then run `restart qu_dev_loop` three times in the server console. Confirm the printed number climbs to 1, then 2, then 3. Each climb is undeniable proof the new code reloaded, because the old code could not have known the new count. The `..` operator joins the text and the number into one string. You just built a loop you can trust at a glance instead of squinting at whether anything changed.

## References
- FiveM server commands reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- FiveM RegisterCommand reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x5FA79B0F
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: Bonus · Version control with GitHub Desktop · Quasar Academy
ai:
This is the lesson that saves your afternoon. You have a working resource from Module 02. One careless edit, one corrupted file, one "I'll just try this", and it's gone. **Git is the safety net.** The mental model is trivial: git takes snapshots of your folder, and each snapshot is called a `commit`. GitHub Desktop is the button-based way to start using git right now, no terminal required.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A git repository for qu_hello, a .gitignore tuned for FiveM, two commits, a feature branch, and a published private repo on github.com.
- Time: ~35 minutes
- You need: Your qu_hello resource from Module 02 and a free github.com account.
- You'll learn: Install & sign-in -> init repo -> .gitignore for FiveM -> first commit -> branches -> revert -> publish -> the daily rhythm

### Before you start
- I have a working qu_hello folder with fxmanifest.lua, client/main.lua, and server/main.lua.
- I have a free github.com account with my email verified.
- I have GitHub Desktop installed and signed in to that account.
- I will not edit unrelated files while working through this lesson.

> **Git is a camera for your folder**
> Forget every intimidating thing you have read about git. It takes a snapshot of your folder; each snapshot is a commit; you can jump back to any of them at any time. Branches, merges, and pushes are just plumbing on top of that, and GitHub Desktop hides the plumbing behind buttons. Do one step, read the proof, then move to the next.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Install GitHub Desktop and sign in
_Goal: GitHub Desktop is open, signed in, and ready to receive your first repository._

Go to [github.com/apps/desktop](https://github.com/apps/desktop) (the old desktop.github.com link still redirects here) and click **Download now** to get the installer for your operating system (Windows or macOS). Run it and accept the defaults.

On first launch it asks you to sign in. Click **Sign in to GitHub.com**, authorise in the browser tab that opens, then return to the app. When it asks for your name and email, use anything you like; these become the author shown on your commits.

You land on the home screen with three buttons: **Clone a repository**, **Create a new repository**, and **Add an existing repository**. The third one is the one you want next.

Expected output:

```
Welcome to GitHub Desktop
Signed in as <your-username>
```

### Step 2 - Turn qu_hello into a repository
_Goal: GitHub Desktop tracks every file inside qu_hello and lists them ready to commit._

Use either safe path to add your folder:

```text
Home screen:  Add an existing repository from your hard drive
Menu:         File -> Add local repository
```

Browse to your `resources/qu_hello` folder. GitHub Desktop says the directory does not appear to be a git repository and offers a **create a repository** link. Click it, then fill the dialog:

```text
Name:                   qu_hello        (must match the folder name)
Description:            my first FiveM resource
Local path:             the parent of qu_hello (already filled in)
Initialize with README: ticked
Git Ignore:             None            (you write a custom one in Step 3)
License:                None
```

Click **Create repository**. On the main screen the left pane lists every file inside `qu_hello`, all ticked, ready for the first snapshot. The README that the init step just generated is in that list too, which is fine: you write the FiveM `.gitignore` in the next step before you ever commit, so nothing you want excluded gets locked into history first.

> **One resource, one repo**
> The FiveM community convention is that each resource is its own git repository. Do not put every resource inside one mega-repo. This is also how you install community scripts (clone the repo into `resources/`), so you are adopting the shape the whole ecosystem already uses.

Expected output:

```
Changes (4)
  README.md
  fxmanifest.lua
  client/main.lua
  server/main.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write a .gitignore for FiveM
_Goal: Git stops tracking editor junk, logs, secrets, and large stream assets._

A `.gitignore` lists patterns git will not track. It is the difference between a clean repo and one full of `.DS_Store`, editor config, and streaming assets nobody wants in version control.

In GitHub Desktop click **Repository -> Open in Visual Studio Code** (or open the folder in your editor by hand). Create a new file in the root of `qu_hello` called `.gitignore` and paste this:

```bash
# Editor / OS cruft
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp
*.bak
*.tmp
*.log

# Secrets (never commit these)
*.env
config.secret.*

# Node / build artefacts, if a resource uses NUI tooling later
node_modules/
dist/
build/
*.tsbuildinfo

# FiveM things that do not belong in a resource repo
cache/
logs/

# Large streaming assets -> keep these in a separate asset-pack repo
stream/**/*.ytyp
stream/**/*.ydr
stream/**/*.ydd
stream/**/*.ytd
```

Save. Back in GitHub Desktop the changes list updates: `.gitignore` is now tracked, and anything matching its patterns disappears from the list.

> **Never commit an .env**
> Any file holding secrets (API keys, database passwords, license tokens) must be in `.gitignore` before you ever commit. If you accidentally commit a secret, rotate it immediately even after you delete the file, because the history still contains it. Your Cfx.re license key, your database password, and any Tebex secret all belong here.

Expected output:

```
Changes (4)
  .gitignore
  README.md
  fxmanifest.lua
  client/main.lua
  server/main.lua
```

### Step 4 - Make your first commit
_Goal: A snapshot of qu_hello is captured with a readable message._

Look at the bottom-left of GitHub Desktop. There are two fields: **Summary** (required, a one-line description under 70 characters in the imperative tense) and **Description** (optional longer prose). Skip the description for now and type this summary:

```text
feat: add /hello command and manifest
```

Click the blue **Commit to main** button. That is a snapshot. Open the **History** tab (top-left) and you see one entry: your commit, your name, the timestamp, and every file captured. Click any file to see exactly what was stored.

> **Quasar commit-message style**
> Use a type prefix: `feat:` new feature, `fix:` bug fix, `refactor:` code tidy-up, `docs:` docs only, `chore:` housekeeping like gitignore and configs. Example: `feat: add cooldown to /heal command`. One line, present tense, under 70 characters. Future-you reading the history will thank you.

Now make a second commit the right way. Open `server/main.lua` and add one line at the top:

```lua
print('qu_hello loaded')
```

Save. The **Changes** tab shows `server/main.lua` with exactly one green added line in the diff on the right. Commit it:

```text
chore: log when resource starts
```

Check **History**: two commits now. Click the new one and confirm only that single line was captured.

Expected output:

```
History
  chore: log when resource starts   <you>   just now
  feat: add /hello command and manifest   <you>   1 minute ago
```

### Step 5 - Branch, then revert
_Goal: You can experiment on a side branch and undo any bad commit in one click._

A **branch** is a parallel line of commits that does not affect `main` until you merge it. In the top bar click **Current branch: main**, click **New Branch**, type a new name in the "Create a Branch" dialog, and click **Create Branch**:

```text
feature/cooldown
```

You are now on the new branch. Add a line to the top of `server/main.lua` and commit it:

```lua
local lastHealAt = 0
```

```text
feat: scaffold heal cooldown
```

Switch back to `main` (branch dropdown -> pick **main**) and open `server/main.lua`. The line is gone. That is not a bug, that is branches working: `main` is untouched. Switch back to `feature/cooldown` and the line returns.

When a branch breaks something you cannot explain, you do not guess. Open **History**, find the commit where the code last worked, right-click it, and choose **Revert changes in commit**. That creates a new commit undoing the bad one, history intact. Inspect the diff first so you can see exactly which lines were the culprit.

> **The daily workflow**
> For every non-trivial change: branch off `main`, commit as you go, merge back when the change is proven (**Branch -> Merge into current branch**), then delete the feature branch (**Branch -> Delete...**). Six clicks. When something breaks mysteriously, open **History** and revert the most recent commit, then re-test. Revert is the beginner-safe button; you do not need destructive history tools in this course.

Expected output:

```
On main: line not present at top of server/main.lua
On feature/cooldown: local lastHealAt = 0 visible at line 1
```

### Step 6 - Publish to GitHub as a private repo
_Goal: Your repo lives on github.com, backed up and ready to sync between machines._

Right now the repo is local only. If your drive dies, your commits die with it. In the top bar click **Publish repository** and fill the dialog:

```text
Name:                    qu_hello        (pre-filled, leave it)
Keep this code private:  ticked
Organization:            None
```

Click **Publish Repository**. A few seconds later it is live at `github.com/<your-username>/qu_hello`, private, visible only to you. From now on: after any local commit click **Push origin** to sync up; before a session on another machine click **Fetch origin** then **Pull origin** to pull down.

> **Why private for now**
> Your first FiveM code will have bugs, security holes, and embarrassing comments. Keep the repo private until the resource is actually useful, then flip to public via **Settings -> Change repository visibility**. Convention: open-source finished resources, keep works-in-progress private, never commit anything with secrets in its history.

Expected output:

```
Successfully published repository
github.com/<your-username>/qu_hello (Private)
```

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `This directory does not appear to be a Git repository` -> That is expected for a fresh folder. Click the create a repository link in the same dialog to initialise git.
- `.gitignore changes nothing in the file list` -> Confirm the file is named exactly .gitignore (no .txt extension) and sits in the root of qu_hello, not in a subfolder.
- `The diff shows more than the one line you changed` -> Your editor normalised line endings or added a trailing newline. It is harmless; commit as-is, or add a .editorconfig later.
- `My file does not appear in Changes` -> Save it (check the editor dirty-dot), and confirm it lives inside the qu_hello folder, which is the repository boundary.
- `Publish repository button is missing` -> You must commit at least once first. Make your initial commit, then Publish repository appears in the top bar.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Turn any resource folder into its own git repository with GitHub Desktop, no terminal needed.
- Write a FiveM-tuned .gitignore that keeps secrets, editor junk, and stream assets out of history.
- Commit snapshots with clear feat / fix / chore messages and read the diff for any commit in History.
- Branch to experiment safely, revert a bad commit in one click, and publish a private backup to github.com.

## Try it yourself

Open your `qu_hello` repo in GitHub Desktop. Create a branch called `feature/test-exercise`, add the comment `-- exercise branch test` to the top of `server/main.lua`, and commit it. Switch back to `main` and confirm the comment is gone, then switch back to `feature/test-exercise` and confirm it reappears. Finally, delete the branch.

## References
- GitHub Desktop documentation: https://docs.github.com/en/desktop
- What is version control (Atlassian): https://www.atlassian.com/git/tutorials/what-is-version-control
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: Lesson 10 · Two worlds, one resource · Quasar Academy
ai:
This is one of the first big FiveM ideas that confuses beginners. Your resource
folder looks like one thing, but it actually runs as **two separate programs**:
one on the server, one on each player's PC. They do not share variables, and they
do not secretly sync. By the end you will be able to point at any line in a resource
and say which world it runs in, and how the two worlds talk to each other.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: Why a resource runs as two programs, and which code lives on each side.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- Prereqs: A running server and one resource you can edit.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
