- Explain that an event is a named message, and that resources use events because they cannot share variables directly.
- Register two handlers for one event name with AddEventHandler and predict that both run without relying on their order.
- Fire a local event with TriggerEvent and state that it stays on the same side.
- Explain why a command's args arrives as a table and what table.concat(args, ' ') rebuilds.
- State the rule: AddEventHandler for same-side events, RegisterNetEvent only for events that cross the network, and why mixing them up is a silent failure or a security hole.

## Try it yourself

Make the two handlers do different work so you can prove that each handler runs independently. Right now both just print. Change handler two so it reacts to the content of the message instead of echoing it:

```lua
AddEventHandler('qu_trigger_event:shout', function(message)
    print('[qu_trigger_event] handler one ' .. message)
end)

AddEventHandler('qu_trigger_event:shout', function(message)
    print('[qu_trigger_event] handler two heard ' .. #message .. ' characters')
end)
```

Restart `qu_trigger_event`, then run `/shout hello world` in chat or `shout hello world` in F8. Handler one should echo `hello world`, and handler two should report `11 characters`. The `#` operator on this ASCII string returns its byte length. You just proved the two handlers are independent: they receive the same value but do different things with it.

## References
- FiveM AddEventHandler reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/AddEventHandler/
- FiveM TriggerEvent reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/TriggerEvent/
- FiveM events introduction: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/working-with-events/listening-for-events/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Lesson 14 · TriggerServerEvent & TriggerClientEvent · Quasar Academy
ai:
Last lesson was about events on one side. This lesson is about sending a message across the wall. The client asks. The server checks. The server answers the player who asked, and can also tell everyone else. That small pattern sits underneath many real FiveM features.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: TriggerServerEvent -> TriggerClientEvent -> RegisterNetEvent -> the source variable -> one client vs all clients
- Time: ~28 minutes
- Prereqs: Lesson 13 (TriggerEvent) and Lesson 12 (trust & authority).
- Outcome: A working ping-pong feature: client asks, server answers the one player who asked, and broadcasts a notice to everyone, with validation and authority intact.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds TriggerServerEvent, RegisterNetEvent, source, and TriggerClientEvent as a working FiveM resource named `qu_trigger_server_client_event`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes the round trip apart so you know why each line is there.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_trigger_server_client_event
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_trigger_server_client_event/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:ping', function(message)
    local src = source
    if type(message) ~= 'string' or #message > 40 then return end

    print('[qu_trigger_server_client_event] server got ' .. message .. ' from ' .. GetPlayerName(src))

    -- Answer only the player who asked (a single server id targets one client).
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:pong', src, 'server received ' .. message)

    -- Tell everyone on the server (-1 broadcasts to all clients).
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:notice', -1, GetPlayerName(src) .. ' pinged the server')
end)
```

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('pingserver', function(_, args)
    TriggerServerEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:ping', table.concat(args, ' '))
end, false)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:pong', function(reply)
    print('[qu_trigger_server_client_event] pong: ' .. reply)
end)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:notice', function(text)
    print('[qu_trigger_server_client_event] notice: ' .. text)
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_trigger_server_client_event
```

    Save, then run:

    ```text
restart qu_trigger_server_client_event
```

    Join the server, open F8, and run this test:

    ```text
/pingserver hello
```

Expected output:

```
Server: [qu_trigger_server_client_event] server got hello from yourName
F8 pong (only you): [qu_trigger_server_client_event] pong: server received hello
F8 notice (every player): [qu_trigger_server_client_event] notice: yourName pinged the server
```

    The pong line lands only in your F8 because the server targeted your server id. The notice line lands in every connected player's F8 because the server passed `-1`. That difference is the whole point of this lesson, and the next section explains exactly how the server knows who to send to.

## How it works

You typed `/pingserver hello` on the client and three lines appeared in two different places. A value crossed the wall to the server, the server checked it, and the server sent two replies back across the wall: one private, one public. That round trip is the single most reused pattern in FiveM. Take it apart once, slowly, and you will recognize it inside almost every script you ever open.

### The round trip, in order

Here is the full path the word `hello` travels. Read it top to bottom, because every later subsection is just a zoom-in on one of these arrows.

1. Client: `/pingserver hello` runs the command handler, which calls `TriggerServerEvent('...:ping', 'hello')`. The value leaves the player's machine and goes to the server.
2. Server: `RegisterNetEvent('...:ping', ...)` is listening for that name. Its handler runs, with `source` set to the player who sent it and `message` set to `'hello'`.
3. Server: after the check passes, `TriggerClientEvent('...:pong', src, ...)` sends a reply back to that one player, and `TriggerClientEvent('...:notice', -1, ...)` sends a separate reply to everyone.
4. Client: `RegisterNetEvent('...:pong', ...)` and `RegisterNetEvent('...:notice', ...)` are listening on every player's machine. The matching handlers fire and print.

Two machines, four registrations, one value making a there-and-back journey. Nothing here is magic. Each piece is a named listener waiting for a named message.

### TriggerServerEvent and the network boundary

```lua
TriggerServerEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:ping', table.concat(args, ' '))
```

`TriggerServerEvent` is the client saying: fire an event named `...:ping` over there, on the server, and hand it this value. The first argument is the event name. Everything after it is payload, copied across the network to the server. This is fire-and-forget. The client does not wait for an answer and gets no return value. If you want an answer, the server has to send one back as its own event, which is exactly what the pong and notice lines do. When you need a true request-then-response in one call, you reach for a callback instead, which is the next lesson.

`table.concat(args, ' ')` is just glue. FiveM hands the command handler the typed arguments as a table, so `/pingserver hello there` arrives as `{ 'hello', 'there' }`. Joining them with a space rebuilds the sentence `hello there` before sending it. That detail is not the lesson; it is here so the payload is a single readable string.

### RegisterNetEvent: the listener that allows network traffic

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:ping', function(message)
```

This is the line that makes the wall passable. `RegisterNetEvent` declares that an event with this name is allowed to be triggered across the network, and attaches a handler to run when it arrives. You must declare it on the side that receives. The server registers `...:ping` because the server receives the ping. Each client registers `...:pong` and `...:notice` because clients receive those.

Here is the distinction that trips people up. `AddEventHandler` also attaches a handler to an event name, but it does not open the network gate. An event handled only with `AddEventHandler` can be fired locally with `TriggerEvent`, but a remote `TriggerServerEvent` or `TriggerClientEvent` aimed at it is rejected for safety. `RegisterNetEvent` is the one that says: this name is allowed to arrive from the other side. In current FiveM, `RegisterNetEvent` both opens the gate and registers the handler in one call, which is why you do not see a separate `AddEventHandler` line here. The rule to keep: if a value has to cross the wall to reach a handler, that handler's name was registered with `RegisterNetEvent` on the receiving side. Forget it on one side and that side simply never hears the event, with no error to point at it.

### local src = source, captured before anything else

```lua
local src = source
```

This is the most important line in the file and the easiest to write wrong. On the server, `source` is a special value that holds the server id of the player who triggered the current net event. It tells you who sent the ping, which is how the server knows who to answer privately.

The catch: `source` is only reliable on the very first line of the handler, before the code yields. Anything that pauses the handler, a `Citizen.Wait`, a database query you await, a callback, can let another event run in between and overwrite `source` with a different player. By the time your paused code resumes, `source` may now point at someone else, and you reply to the wrong player or write to the wrong account. Capturing it into a plain local the instant the handler starts freezes the correct id in a variable that nothing else can touch. From then on you use `src`, never the raw `source`. Even when a handler has no yield today, you capture `src` first out of habit, because the day you add a database call is the day the bug appears, and it is a brutal one to find.

### Check yourself: The handler runs print, which does not yield. So why bother capturing local src = source instead of just using source everywhere?

Because the habit has to survive the next edit, not just today's code. Right now the handler is synchronous, so `source` would still be correct on every line. But the moment you add a yield, a `Citizen.Wait`, an `oxmysql` query you await, a callback, another player's net event can run during the pause and overwrite `source`. Code that read `source` after the pause would then act on the wrong player. Writing `local src = source` on line one freezes the right id immediately, so when you later add the database call you do not have to remember to fix it, and you never ship the silent wrong-player bug.

### TriggerClientEvent and the target argument

```lua
TriggerClientEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:pong', src, 'server received ' .. message)
TriggerClientEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:notice', -1, GetPlayerName(src) .. ' pinged the server')
```

`TriggerClientEvent` is the server sending an event back across the wall to clients. Its shape is the mirror of `TriggerServerEvent`, with one extra argument wedged in second: the target. That target argument is the entire reason there are two lines here instead of one.

- A real server id, like `src`, sends the event to exactly that one client. The pong reply uses `src`, so only the player who pinged sees `pong:`. This is how you answer a request privately, or send a player their own inventory, their own money, their own menu.
- The number `-1` is the broadcast target. It sends the event to every connected client at once. The notice line uses `-1`, so every player on the server sees `notice:`. This is how you announce a restart, push a server-wide alert, or sync a shared world state change to the room.

Read it as: who is this for. One person, pass their id. Everyone, pass `-1`. The original version of this lesson only ever answered the one player and never showed the broadcast, which is why the second `TriggerClientEvent` line is here now. Seeing both in the same handler, side by side, is the clearest way to feel the difference.

### Why the server checks the payload

```lua
if type(message) ~= 'string' or #message > 40 then return end
```

The client sent this value, and the client cannot be trusted. A normal player typing `/pingserver hello` sends a short string. A malicious or modified client can call `TriggerServerEvent('...:ping', ...)` directly with anything: a number, a table, a forty-thousand-character string built to bloat your logs or break the code that concatenates it. The handler runs on your server with your server's authority, so whatever it does with the payload, it does for real.

So before the server uses the value, it states what it requires and bails out otherwise. `type(message) ~= 'string'` rejects anything that is not text. `#message > 40` rejects text longer than a sane ping. If either is true, `return` ends the handler immediately, no print, no reply, no harm. This is the trust-and-authority idea from Lesson 12 made concrete: the boundary between client and server is exactly where you validate, because it is the last point you still control. Every net event handler you write on the server should open by checking that its inputs are the shape and size you expected.

### When you reach for this pattern

Client asks, server checks, server answers, sits under a huge slice of real FiveM features. Three concrete cases:

- **A player action with a server-side effect.** Buy an item, pay a bill, accept a job. The client fires the request, the server validates and updates the truth, then sends the result back to that one player with a single server id.
- **A server-wide announcement.** A restart warning, an event start, a heist going live. The server fires `TriggerClientEvent('...', -1, ...)` and every client reacts at once.
- **The first half of a callback.** When you need the server's reply in the same breath as the question, you wrap this exact round trip in a callback helper. Knowing the raw events underneath is what makes the callback lesson click instead of feeling like magic.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `The server prints nothing when you run /pingserver` -> The ping name is not registered as a net event on the server, so the cross-wall trigger is rejected silently. Confirm server.lua uses RegisterNetEvent (not AddEventHandler) and that the event name matches the client's TriggerServerEvent name character for character.
- `Server prints, but no pong or notice appears in F8` -> The reply names are not registered on the client. Each TriggerClientEvent name must have a matching RegisterNetEvent in client.lua. A name typo on either side means that side never hears the event.
- `attempt to concatenate a nil value (local 'src')` -> source was read after a yield or assigned wrong. Capture local src = source on the first line of the handler, before anything else, and use src from then on.
- `Only you see the message and never the rest of the server` -> Both replies are going to a single id. The broadcast must pass -1 as the target: TriggerClientEvent('...:notice', -1, ...). A server id sends to one client, -1 sends to all.
- `Nothing happens and there is no error at all` -> The payload check returned early. You sent a value that is not a string or is longer than 40 characters, so the handler bailed by design. Send a short text ping like /pingserver hello.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Trace a full client-to-server-to-client round trip and name what each arrow does: TriggerServerEvent, RegisterNetEvent, TriggerClientEvent.
- Register a net event with RegisterNetEvent on the receiving side, and explain why AddEventHandler alone will not accept cross-wall traffic.
- Capture local src = source on line one of a server handler and say why reading source after a yield is a wrong-player bug.
- Choose the TriggerClientEvent target: a server id sends to one client, -1 broadcasts to all.
- Validate a client payload at the server boundary with a type and length check before trusting it.

## Try it yourself

Right now the server trusts that any text under 40 characters is fine. Add a rule the server enforces, and prove the broadcast obeys it. Extend the ping handler so that if the message is exactly the word `shout`, the server broadcasts an all-caps alert to everyone with `-1`, but for any other word it stays private and only pongs the sender.

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:ping', function(message)
    local src = source
    if type(message) ~= 'string' or #message > 40 then return end

    TriggerClientEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:pong', src, 'server received ' .. message)

    if message == 'shout' then
        TriggerClientEvent('qu_trigger_server_client_event:notice', -1, string.upper(GetPlayerName(src) .. ' is shouting'))
    end
end)
```

Restart `qu_trigger_server_client_event`, then run `/pingserver shout` and watch the notice reach every player in all caps. Run `/pingserver hello` and confirm only your own F8 gets a pong with no broadcast. You just moved the decision of who hears a message from a fixed rule into server-side logic, which is how real features gate public versus private replies.

## References
- FiveM TriggerServerEvent reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/TriggerServerEvent/
- FiveM TriggerClientEvent reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/TriggerClientEvent/
- FiveM events scripting manual: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/working-with-events/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Bonus · Latent events for big payloads · Quasar Academy
ai:
A regular `TriggerServerEvent` hands the whole payload to the network layer in one shot. For small messages that is fine. For big tables, image buffers, or character export blobs, that single burst can stall a player's connection long enough to time them out. Latent events stream the same payload at a controlled rate, in bytes per second, so the connection keeps breathing while the data trickles across.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: When to swap TriggerServerEvent for TriggerLatentServerEvent, what the bps parameter actually does, how to size it, and why broadcasting latent events to all players is a trap
- Time: ~14 minutes
- Prereqs: Lesson 14 (TriggerServerEvent / TriggerClientEvent)
- Outcome: You know the rule of thumb (small = regular, big = latent), you can size bps for any payload, and you can send latent events in both directions safely

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a round trip as a working FiveM resource named `qu_latent_events`. The client streams a large payload up with `TriggerLatentServerEvent`, the server measures it and streams an acknowledgement back down with `TriggerLatentClientEvent`. You see both natives, both directions, and the bps parameter that controls them. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_latent_events
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_latent_events/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: Both latent natives now run, one per direction._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_latent_events:upload', function(payload)
    -- `source` is the server id of the player who triggered this event.
    -- It is only valid right here, so copy it into a local before anything else.
    local src = source

    -- SECURITY: any connected client can fire this event with any data,
    -- not just the /latenttest command. Validate before you trust it.
    if type(payload) ~= 'string' then return end          -- reject non-string input
    local size = #payload
    if size > 6 * 1024 * 1024 then return end              -- cap the size you accept

    print('[qu_latent_events] server received ' .. size .. ' bytes from player ' .. src)

    -- Acknowledge back down to the client, also as a latent stream.
    TriggerLatentClientEvent('qu_latent_events:ack', src, 25000, size)
end)
```

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('latenttest', function()
    local payload = string.rep('A', 20000) -- 20,000 bytes of filler

    -- 25000 = stream this payload at 25,000 bytes per second.
    TriggerLatentServerEvent('qu_latent_events:upload', 25000, payload)
    print('[qu_latent_events] upload started (' .. #payload .. ' bytes at 25000 bps)')
end, false)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_latent_events:ack', function(size)
    print('[qu_latent_events] client got ack: server saw ' .. size .. ' bytes')
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct consoles._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_latent_events
```

    The `ensure` line makes the server start this resource every time it boots, but it only takes effect on the next boot. To load it now without rebooting, run this in your server console (the txAdmin Live Console, or the FXServer console window if you start the server directly):

    ```text
restart qu_latent_events
```

    Join the server, then open the chat box with T and run:

    ```text
/latenttest
```

Expected output:

```
F8 (client): [qu_latent_events] upload started (20000 bytes at 25000 bps)
Server (txAdmin): [qu_latent_events] server received 20000 bytes from player 1
F8 (client): [qu_latent_events] client got ack: server saw 20000 bytes
```

    The client line prints first. About one second later (20,000 bytes at 25,000 bps), the server line lands in txAdmin Live Console. The ack then streams back down and the second F8 line appears. Three lines, two consoles, one full round trip.

## How it works

The proof worked, but the point of this lesson is one number: `25000`. Everything else is plumbing you have seen before. So we take it apart in the order the data actually travels: out of the client, across the wire, into the server, and back.

### Why a regular event can time a player out

When you call `TriggerServerEvent('x', bigTable)`, FiveM serializes the whole payload and pushes it onto the player's reliable network channel as one unit. The channel will not move on to anything else until that whole unit is delivered. For a 200-byte table you never notice. For a multi-megabyte blob, that one event hogs the channel, every other packet for that player queues up behind it, and on a slow or lossy connection the client can stall past its timeout window and get dropped. The player did nothing wrong. Your event ate their connection.

Latent events fix this by chunking. Instead of one big push, FiveM breaks the payload into pieces and feeds them onto the channel at a rate you choose, leaving gaps for everything else. The payload still arrives whole, in order, and triggers the same handler. It just arrives as a polite trickle instead of a flood.

### The bps argument, the one number that matters

Compare the two calls. A regular event takes the name and the data:

```lua
TriggerServerEvent('qu_latent_events:upload', payload)
```

The latent version inserts one extra argument between them:

```lua
TriggerLatentServerEvent('qu_latent_events:upload', 25000, payload)
```

That `25000` is the **bytes-per-second stream rate**. It is not a size, not a timeout, not a chunk count. It is a speed limit. It tells FiveM: never push this payload onto the channel faster than 25,000 bytes every second. The data is metered out at that ceiling until it is all delivered.

Every latent native takes the rate in the same slot, right after the target arguments:

- `TriggerLatentServerEvent(eventName, bps, ...)` — client to server.
- `TriggerLatentClientEvent(eventName, target, bps, ...)` — server to client, where `target` is the player server id.

In our code the client streams up at `25000` and the server streams the ack back down at `25000`. Same number, same meaning, opposite directions.

### How to size bps for a payload

Once you know the rate is a speed limit, sizing it is just division. Transfer time is payload size divided by rate:

```text
transfer time (seconds) = payload size (bytes) / bps
```

Our 20,000-byte payload at 25,000 bps takes 20000 / 25000 = 0.8 seconds. That is why the server line lands about a second after the client line, not instantly.

Now you can reason about real numbers. A 5 MB character export (roughly 5,000,000 bytes) at 25,000 bps would take 200 seconds. That is almost certainly too slow, so you raise the rate. At 500,000 bps the same export finishes in 10 seconds. The trade is simple: a higher rate finishes faster but uses more of the channel, which is the exact congestion you were trying to avoid. A lower rate is gentler but slower. Pick the largest rate that still leaves headroom for normal gameplay traffic, and bias lower when the payload is huge or the players are far away. There is no single correct value. The number depends on your payload size and how much delay your feature can tolerate.

### Check yourself: A teammate sets bps to 20000 and expects a 20,000-byte payload to arrive instantly. Why does it take a full second, and what single change makes it arrive in a tenth of a second?

The bps argument is a speed limit, not a size, so a 20,000-byte payload at 20,000 bps is metered out over 20000 / 20000 = 1 second by design. The data is correct, it is just deliberately slow. To finish in roughly a tenth of a second, raise the rate to 200000 bps (20000 / 200000 = 0.1 seconds). Nothing else changes. The payload is identical and the handler is identical. The only knob is the rate, and a higher rate trades channel headroom for speed.

### The broadcast trap

This is the gotcha that bites people in production. With a regular client event you broadcast to everyone by passing `-1` as the target:

```lua
TriggerClientEvent('announcement', -1, 'Server restarting')
```

`TriggerLatentClientEvent` accepts `-1` too, and it looks harmless:

```lua
-- Dangerous on a full server with a big payload
TriggerLatentClientEvent('qu_latent_events:ack', -1, 25000, hugePayload)
```

But latent broadcasting does not share one 25,000 bps stream across the server. It opens a separate metered stream to every connected player, each at the full rate you named. On a 64-slot server that is 64 simultaneous streams at 25,000 bps, which is 1.6 MB per second leaving your server's uplink the moment you fire it. Send a large payload to `-1` and you can saturate the very channel latent events exist to protect, hitting every player at once.

The rule: latent broadcasts are for one target, or a small known list of targets. If you genuinely must push a big payload to everyone, stream it to one player at a time in a loop with a delay between sends, or rethink whether every player actually needs the data. Most of the time they do not. That is why our ack streams back to a single `src`, never to `-1`.

### When you reach for this pattern

Reach for a latent event the moment a payload is big enough that a single burst could stall a connection. Three honest cases:

- **Character or inventory exports.** A full character blob with appearance, inventory, and metadata can be hundreds of kilobytes. Stream it instead of bursting it.
- **Image or buffer uploads from NUI.** Screenshots, signatures, or generated images are large binary strings. Latent keeps the upload from freezing the player mid-send.
- **Server-to-client data sync on join.** Pushing a large config or world-state table to a freshly connected player is exactly when their connection is most fragile. Latent gives it time to land.

For everything small, a chat line, a notification, a single number, stay with the regular `TriggerServerEvent` and `TriggerClientEvent`. The rule of thumb holds: small is regular, big is latent.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_latent_events does not exist` -> Check the folder is inside resources and the ensure line uses the exact same name.
- `The /latenttest command does nothing in chat` -> The command is registered on the client, so it must be run from the chat box (press T) after you join the server, not from the txAdmin console. Confirm client.lua is listed in fxmanifest.lua and run restart qu_latent_events.
- `Client prints upload started but no server line ever appears` -> The server is not receiving the event. Confirm the event name matches exactly on both sides (qu_latent_events:upload) and that server.lua loaded without a manifest error.
- `Server line appears but no ack reaches the client` -> The ack is sent to src, so source must be captured at the top of the handler. Make sure you wrote local src = source before any Wait or other call, since source is only valid synchronously inside the handler.
- `Sending the payload lags or drops other players` -> Your bps is too high for your uplink, or you broadcast a large latent payload to -1. Lower the rate, or send to one target at a time instead of every player at once.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain why a regular TriggerServerEvent can time a player out on a large payload, and how latent events avoid it by metering the stream.
- Read the bps argument as a bytes-per-second speed limit, not a size or a timeout, and find it in the right slot of both latent natives.
- Size bps for any payload using transfer time = payload size / bps, and reason about the speed-versus-headroom trade.
- Send latent events in both directions: TriggerLatentServerEvent up, TriggerLatentClientEvent down.
- Avoid the broadcast trap by never streaming a large latent payload to -1, since that opens one full-rate stream per player.

## Try it yourself

Make the bps cost visible. Right now you cannot feel the difference between a fast rate and a slow one because the payload is tiny. Time a real transfer.

Capture the start time on the client, then have the server stamp arrival and the ack carry the duration back:

```lua
-- client.lua
local uploadStartedAt = 0

RegisterCommand('latenttest', function()
    local payload = string.rep('A', 500000) -- 500 KB now, not 20 KB
    uploadStartedAt = GetGameTimer()
    TriggerLatentServerEvent('qu_latent_events:upload', 25000, payload)
    print('[qu_latent_events] upload started (' .. #payload .. ' bytes at 25000 bps)')
end, false)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_latent_events:ack', function(size)
    local elapsed = GetGameTimer() - uploadStartedAt
    print('[qu_latent_events] round trip for ' .. size .. ' bytes took ' .. elapsed .. ' ms')
end)
```

Run `/latenttest` and read the elapsed time. A 500 KB upload at 25,000 bps should take roughly 20 seconds up, plus the ack stream back down. Now change only the bps number on the `TriggerLatentServerEvent` line to `250000` and run it again. The same payload should land in roughly a tenth of the time. You just measured the one parameter this lesson is about, instead of trusting the math on paper. Then ask yourself: at which rate did other gameplay traffic start to feel choppy? That is your real ceiling.

## References
- TriggerLatentClientEvent reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/TriggerLatentClientEvent/
- TriggerLatentServerEvent reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/TriggerLatentServerEvent/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Bonus · Cancelling events · Quasar Academy
ai:
Sometimes a handler needs to veto an engine action or report that an event was rejected. `CancelEvent()` sets that signal, but it does not stop other handlers from running. `WasEventCanceled()` or Lua's `TriggerEvent` return value lets the code that triggered a local event inspect the result.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: What CancelEvent() flags, why other handlers still run, how the caller checks cancellation, and the playerConnecting rejection pattern
- Time: ~12 minutes
- Prereqs: Lesson 13 (TriggerEvent) and 14 (TriggerServerEvent / TriggerClientEvent)
- Outcome: You can cancel an event from a handler, detect cancellation downstream, and pick the right tool between cancel, return, and defer

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a `CancelEvent` and `WasEventCanceled` handler chain as a working server-only FiveM resource named `qu_cancelling_events`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes the chain apart line by line so you know why a cancelled event still runs its second handler.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_cancelling_events
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_cancelling_events/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this. Two handlers listen for the same event, and a command fires it so you can watch the chain run:

```lua
AddEventHandler('qu_cancelling_events:check', function(name)
    print('[qu_cancelling_events] first handler checked ' .. name)
    if name == 'blocked' then
        CancelEvent()
        print('[qu_cancelling_events] event cancelled')
    end
end)

AddEventHandler('qu_cancelling_events:check', function(name)
    print('[qu_cancelling_events] second handler still ran for ' .. name)
end)

RegisterCommand('canceltest', function(_, args)
    local name = args[1] or 'allowed'
    print('[qu_cancelling_events] --- firing check for ' .. name .. ' ---')
    TriggerEvent('qu_cancelling_events:check', name)
    print('[qu_cancelling_events] caller saw cancelled = ' .. tostring(WasEventCanceled()))
end, true)
-- The final `true` is the "restricted" flag: it locks this command behind the
-- command.canceltest ACE permission. The server console (txAdmin Live Console
-- or the FXServer window) always has permission, so run `canceltest` THERE,
-- not from in-game chat, where a normal player would be silently denied.
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the server console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_cancelling_events
```

    Save, then type this into the server console (the txAdmin Live Console, or the FXServer.exe window if you launched it directly):

    ```text
restart qu_cancelling_events
```

    Run the blocked case first:

    ```text
canceltest blocked
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_cancelling_events] --- firing check for blocked ---
[qu_cancelling_events] first handler checked blocked
[qu_cancelling_events] event cancelled
[qu_cancelling_events] second handler still ran for blocked
[qu_cancelling_events] caller saw cancelled = true
```

    Then run the allowed case:

    ```text
canceltest open
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_cancelling_events] --- firing check for open ---
[qu_cancelling_events] first handler checked open
[qu_cancelling_events] second handler still ran for open
[qu_cancelling_events] caller saw cancelled = false
```

    The two handler lines may appear in a different order. The official contract is that cancellation does not prevent other handlers from running; do not depend on registration order. The caller's final cancellation result is the assertion that matters.

## How it works

Read the blocked output again, because it contains the single most counter-intuitive fact in the whole event system. You called `CancelEvent()` in one handler, yet the other handler still ran. If `CancelEvent()` stopped dispatch, that line could never appear. So what does it actually do?

### CancelEvent() raises a flag, it does not stop the chain

When you trigger `qu_cancelling_events:check`, FiveM dispatches the event to its registered handlers. `CancelEvent()` does not stop those other handlers. It records that the current event was cancelled so the code that triggered it, or the game for a cancellable built-in event, can inspect that outcome. Do not depend on handlers running in registration order; the documented contract does not promise one.

So in plain terms, this is the sequence for `canceltest blocked`:

- The command prints the `--- firing check ---` line and calls `TriggerEvent`.
- The checking handler sees `name == 'blocked'`, calls `CancelEvent()`, and prints `event cancelled`.
- The other handler still runs and prints its line.
- After `TriggerEvent` finishes, the command calls `WasEventCanceled()` and prints `true`.

That last point is the part beginners miss: the second handler runs even though the event was cancelled. For a custom local event, the caller decides what to do with the result. For a built-in cancellable event, such as `playerConnecting`, the game understands the flag and applies its documented effect.

### WasEventCanceled() is the caller's check

For the local event in this lesson, call `WasEventCanceled()` immediately after `TriggerEvent` returns. It reports whether any handler cancelled that dispatch. In Lua, `TriggerEvent` also returns the cancellation result, so this is equivalent:

```lua
local canceled = TriggerEvent('qu_cancelling_events:check', name)
print('cancelled = ' .. tostring(canceled))
```

Do not build a permission or security system around one event handler checking what another handler did. Keep the protected action and its validation together, and treat the event's final cancellation result as a signal to the caller.

### Check yourself: You add a third handler to qu_cancelling_events:check that prints 'third handler ran'. Will it run when another handler cancels the event?

Yes. `CancelEvent()` does not prevent the remaining handlers from running. Do not predict where its line appears relative to the other handlers, because handler order is not a documented guarantee. After all handlers finish, the caller still sees a final cancellation result of `true`.

### Cancellation is local: it does not cross the network

There is one more boundary that trips people up, and it is the reason this lesson is built server-only. `CancelEvent()` and `WasEventCanceled()` operate on the **local handler chain**, on the side where the event is running. They have no reach across the client and server boundary.

Concretely, you cannot have a server handler `CancelEvent()` a client event, and you cannot stop a `TriggerServerEvent` from the client by cancelling it on the server before the work runs in your own code. When a client does `TriggerServerEvent('something')`, by the time your server handler is executing, the event has already crossed the wire and arrived. Cancelling it only affects the rest of the server-side chain for that event, the same way you saw above. It does not reach back to the client. If you want the server to refuse something a client asked for, you do not cancel; you simply do not perform the action and you keep the authority on the server. Cancellation is for vetoing handlers on the same side, not for blocking a message in flight.

### The pattern that matters: vetoing playerConnecting

The toy chain above teaches the mechanics. Here is the production use the intro promised. `playerConnecting` is a built-in server event that fires while a player is still on the loading screen, before they are in the world. Cancelling it stops the join. This is exactly how ban systems and allowlists kick a player before any other resource even sees them.

```lua
AddEventHandler('playerConnecting', function(name, setKickReason, deferrals)
    local src = source
    local license

    for _, id in ipairs(GetPlayerIdentifiers(src)) do
        if id:find('license:') then
            license = id
            break
        end
    end

    if BANNED_LICENSES[license] then
        CancelEvent()
        setKickReason('You are banned from this server.')
    end
end)
```

> **Which identifier to ban on**
> This example matches on `license:` because it is platform-independent and easy to read. Be aware the `license` id can change if the player switches their Rockstar / Social Club account, so it is not a perfect ban key. For real ban lists Cfx recommends the `fivem` identifier (the player's Cfx.re user id). No single identifier is guaranteed always-present or unspoofable, so production ban systems usually store several.

Read it against everything above. `playerConnecting` runs a handler chain just like your test event did, and the engine itself checks `WasEventCanceled()` after the chain finishes: if any handler raised the flag, the connection is refused. `CancelEvent()` here raises that flag, and `setKickReason` is the callback FiveM hands you to attach the message the player sees. The `deferrals` argument is the third parameter and lets you hold the player on the loading screen while you run a slow check, for example a database lookup, before deciding; you call `deferrals.defer()` to start, `deferrals.update('message')` to show progress, and `deferrals.done()` or `deferrals.done('reason')` to admit or reject. The mechanics are identical to your two-handler toy: a flag, read downstream. The only difference is that downstream the reader is the engine, and the consequence is a real kick instead of a skipped print.

### When you reach for each tool

You now have three different ways to stop work, and they are not interchangeable:

- **CancelEvent()** when you are one handler in a chain for an event the engine or other resources also listen to, and you want to veto the shared outcome. `playerConnecting`, `populationPedCreating`, and other built-in cancellable events are the real targets.
- **return** when you simply want to exit your own handler early. It stops your code, nothing else. The second handler in your build used `return` to skip its own work after reading the flag.
- **deferrals** when you need to pause `playerConnecting` to run an async check before you decide. Cancel is instant; deferrals buy you time.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_cancelling_events does not exist` -> Check the folder is inside resources and the ensure line uses the exact same name.
- `Could not load resource qu_cancelling_events` -> Open fxmanifest.lua and fix the first quote, brace, or file name error the console reports.
- `The command does not print` -> Run restart qu_cancelling_events, then run canceltest in the txAdmin Live Console. This resource is server-only, so there is nothing in F8 to read.
- `Second handler runs even on canceltest blocked` -> That is correct behaviour, not a bug. CancelEvent only raises a flag. The second handler still runs; it only skips its work because it checks WasEventCanceled and returns.
- `Cancel seems to do nothing at all` -> Check handler order. The handler that calls CancelEvent must be registered before the handler that reads WasEventCanceled, or the reader checks the flag before it is set.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain that CancelEvent() raises a flag and does not stop the handler chain, so downstream handlers still run.
- Use WasEventCanceled() in a downstream handler to opt in to honouring a cancel, and return early when it is set.
- Predict how registration order decides which handlers can see a cancel, and place a vetoing handler after the deciders.
- Cancel playerConnecting with setKickReason to reject a banned player before any other resource sees the join.
- Say why CancelEvent() is local to one side and cannot stop a client event from the server, choosing return or server authority instead.

## Try it yourself

Prove the order rule with your own hands, then wire the real thing.

First, add a third handler to `qu_cancelling_events:check` that prints `[qu_cancelling_events] third handler ran` with no `WasEventCanceled` guard. Restart, run `canceltest blocked`, and confirm the third line prints even though the event was cancelled. Now move the *cancelling* handler to the bottom of the file so it registers last, restart, run `canceltest blocked` again, and watch the `allowed` line print before the cancel is ever raised. You have just demonstrated that cancellation is a flag and that order is everything.

Then convert the toy into the production pattern: add a second file `connect.lua`, register it as a `server_script` in the manifest, and write a `playerConnecting` handler that cancels the join and calls `setKickReason('blocked for testing')` when the connecting player's name contains the word `test`. Connect with a test name and confirm you are kicked with your message instead of spawning.

## References
- FiveM events & CancelEvent reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/events/
- playerConnecting and deferrals: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/events/list/playerConnecting/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: Lesson 16 · ox_lib, notifications & UI · Quasar Academy
ai:
So far you have built things the long way so you could see how they work. Now it is time to use the toolbox most modern FiveM servers reach for. `ox_lib` gives you ready-made pop-ups, menus, dialogs, and progress bars so you can focus on script logic instead of rebuilding UI every time. It is a community library from the Overextended team, not an official Cfx.re resource, but it is so widely used that knowing it is part of the job.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: lib.notify -> lib.registerContext / lib.showContext -> lib.inputDialog -> lib.progressCircle -> where the ox_lib docs live
- Time: ~25 minutes
- Prereqs: Module 04 complete. ox_lib installed and started before any qu_* resource.
- Outcome: One client file that shows a notification, opens a menu, asks for input, and runs a progress bar.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds an ox_lib notification, context menu, input dialog, and progress circle as a working FiveM resource named `qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes the code apart line by line so you know why it works.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Confirm ox_lib is installed and started
_Goal: The lib global will exist when your resource loads._

Your resource borrows the `lib` global from ox_lib, so ox_lib has to be running before your resource starts. In `server.cfg`, ox_lib must be ensured above your lesson resource:

```text
ensure ox_lib
ensure qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui
```

If you do not have ox_lib yet, download the latest release from the Overextended GitHub and drop the `ox_lib` folder into `resources`. Do not rename it: the `@ox_lib/init.lua` path in the next step depends on the folder being called exactly `ox_lib`.

### Step 2 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui
```

### Step 3 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 4 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load, and in what order._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'

dependencies {
    'ox_lib'
}
```

Older guides write the dependency as `dependency 'ox_lib'` (singular). That form is still tolerated, but the documented modern form is the plural table `dependencies { 'ox_lib' }`, so that is what you use here.

### Step 5 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
lib.registerContext({
    id = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui_menu',
    title = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui',
    options = {
        { title = 'Ask name', event = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui:ask' }
    }
})

RegisterCommand('oxdemo', function()
    lib.notify({
        title = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui',
        description = 'Notification works',
        type = 'success'
    })

    lib.showContext('qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui_menu')
end, false)

AddEventHandler('qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui:ask', function()
    local input = lib.inputDialog('Save a name', { 'Name' })
    if not input then return end

    lib.progressCircle({ duration = 1000, label = 'Saving' })

    print('[qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui] saved ' .. input[1])
end)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

With the two `ensure` lines from Step 1 already in `server.cfg`, save the file, then run:

```text
restart qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui
```

Join the server, then type this in the chat box (press T):

```text
/oxdemo
```

A green notification appears, a menu opens with one option, choosing it asks for a name, a progress circle runs for one second, and then the line below prints to your F8 client console. Type `Mike` when the dialog asks.

Expected output:

```
F8: [qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui] saved Mike
```

## How it works

You ran it and the four widgets fired in sequence. Now take the code apart so the next time you want a notification or a menu you are deciding which ox_lib call to use, not copying a block you do not understand. The whole resource is two pieces: a manifest that loads the library, and a client file that calls into it.

### Why the manifest line `@ox_lib/init.lua` comes first

```lua
shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
```

The `@` prefix means "reach into another resource and load this file from it." So `@ox_lib/init.lua` runs ox_lib's own init file inside your resource. That init file is what creates the `lib` global you call everywhere. Without this line, `lib` does not exist in your resource and the first `lib.notify` blows up with `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')`.

Order matters here. Manifest entries load top to bottom, so `@ox_lib/init.lua` is listed before `client.lua` on purpose: the library has to be in place before your code tries to use it. It is declared as a `shared_script` rather than a `client_script` because ox_lib runs on both the client and the server, and loading its init on both sides is what lets the same `lib` table work wherever you need it.

The `dependencies { 'ox_lib' }` line is a second, coarser guarantee. It tells the server not to start `qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui` at all until the `ox_lib` resource is already started. The `@` path handles loading the file; the dependency handles resource start order. You want both.

### Check yourself: The console says attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib') the moment you run /oxdemo. What is wrong?

The `lib` global was never created in your resource, which almost always means the `shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'` line is missing, misspelled, or placed below `client.lua` in the manifest. Check that the line is present, that the folder is named exactly `ox_lib` (the `@` path is case and name sensitive), and that ox_lib is ensured above your resource in `server.cfg`. Fix the manifest, save, then run `restart qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui`.

### lib.notify and the type field

```lua
lib.notify({
    title = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui',
    description = 'Notification works',
    type = 'success'
})
```

`lib.notify` takes one table of options, not a list of separate arguments. That table style is how almost every ox_lib call works: you pass a single `{ }` and set named keys inside it. Here `title` is the bold top line, `description` is the body text, and `type` controls the colour and icon. The common values are `'success'` (green), `'error'` (red), and `'inform'` (neutral blue). You chose `'success'` so the proof reads as a green toast. There are more keys available, such as `duration`, `position`, and `icon`. The full list lives in the ox_lib docs linked at the bottom of this lesson, and that is the page you will return to most.

### Why the context option uses `event=` and not the handler directly

```lua
lib.registerContext({
    id = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui_menu',
    title = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui',
    options = {
        { title = 'Ask name', event = 'qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui:ask' }
    }
})

lib.showContext('qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui_menu')
```

A context menu is built in two moves. `lib.registerContext` defines the menu and stores it under an `id`, and in this resource it runs once at the top of `client.lua` when the resource loads. `lib.showContext` then opens the menu by that same `id`, and in this resource it lives inside the `/oxdemo` command handler so the menu opens each time you run the command. Splitting definition from display means you register the menu once on load and reopen it as many times as you like.

Each entry in `options` is one row in the menu. Our row has a `title` and an `event`. When the player clicks it, ox_lib fires a local client event, which is why the code listens with `AddEventHandler`. Nothing crosses the network, so `RegisterNetEvent` would unnecessarily expose a local UI action as network-callable. The static context is registered once when the resource loads and the command only opens it, matching the ox_lib guidance not to re-register static menus repeatedly.

`event=` is one of two ways to wire a menu row. The other is `onSelect`, where you hand the option an inline function instead of an event name:

```lua
{ title = 'Ask name', onSelect = function() askForName() end }
```

`onSelect` is convenient when the action is small and local to this file. `event=` is the better choice when the same action needs to be triggerable from somewhere else too, since any code can `TriggerEvent('qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui:ask')` to reuse it. This lesson uses `event=` so you see the wiring explicitly.

### Why inputDialog returns a table and how the cancel case is handled

```lua
local input = lib.inputDialog('Save a name', { 'Name' })
if not input then return end
```

`lib.inputDialog` opens a form with a title and a list of fields. The second argument `{ 'Name' }` is a list of one field, so the dialog shows one text box labelled `Name`. Because a dialog can have many fields, ox_lib gives you the answers back as a table, indexed in the order the fields were declared. With one field, your answer is `input[1]`. If you had asked for three things, you would read `input[1]`, `input[2]`, and `input[3]`. That is why the print joins `input[1]` and not `input`: `input` is the whole table, `input[1]` is the first answer inside it.

The `if not input then return end` line handles the player pressing Escape or closing the dialog without submitting. In that case `lib.inputDialog` returns `nil` instead of a table. If you skipped this guard and went straight to `input[1]`, indexing `nil` would throw `attempt to index a nil value (local 'input')` every time someone cancels. Reading the cancel path first, before you touch the data, is the habit to keep: treat a cancelled dialog as a normal outcome, not an error.

### Why the progress circle runs after input is collected

```lua
lib.progressCircle({ duration = 1000, label = 'Saving' })
print('[qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui] saved ' .. input[1])
```

`lib.progressCircle` shows a circular timer and, importantly, it blocks until the timer finishes. `duration = 1000` means it waits one thousand milliseconds, one second, before the code continues. So the `print` on the next line does not run until the circle completes. This is why the order on screen is exactly: dialog closes, circle spins for a second, line prints. If you put the progress circle before `lib.inputDialog`, the player would watch a bar fill before you even asked them anything, which makes no sense. The pattern is collect the input first, then show progress while you pretend to do the work, then report the result. In a real script the gap during the progress bar is where you would actually save to a database or call the server.

> **Anything typed into inputDialog is untrusted client input**
> The value you read from `input[1]` comes from the player's own machine, so a player can send anything at all, not just what your dialog seems to allow. In this lesson it only goes to a local `print`, which is safe. The moment a real script sends that value to the server, the server must treat it as untrusted: check the type and length, reject what does not fit, and when it reaches a database use a parameterized query (the `?` placeholder form) rather than building the SQL string by gluing the value in. Trusting raw client input server-side is how servers get money duped, items spawned, and databases broken.

### Check yourself: A teammate moves the lib.progressCircle call above the lib.inputDialog call. What does the player experience, and why?

The progress circle runs first, blocking for a full second while the screen shows a spinner, and only after it finishes does the input dialog appear asking for the name. The behaviour is backwards because `lib.progressCircle` blocks until its `duration` elapses, so whatever you place after it waits. You want the bar to represent work done on data you already have, so the input has to be collected before the circle, not after.

### When you reach for this pattern

These four calls cover most of the UI a gameplay script needs, and you will combine them constantly:

- **lib.notify** for any quick confirmation or rejection. Bought an item, action failed, cooldown active.
- **lib.registerContext plus lib.showContext** whenever a player needs to pick from a short list. Shop categories, vehicle options, a job menu.
- **lib.inputDialog** when you need typed or selected values back. Naming something, entering an amount, choosing from a dropdown.
- **lib.progressCircle** to gate an action behind a visible timer. Lockpicking, crafting, repairing, anything that should not be instant.

The reason to use ox_lib instead of building these by hand, the way earlier lessons built things from scratch, is consistency and time. Every script on your server using the same library means one visual style and one set of behaviours your players already understand, and you write logic instead of UI plumbing.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')` -> ox_lib was not loaded into your resource. Confirm the manifest has shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua' above client_script, the folder is named exactly ox_lib, and ox_lib is ensured before this resource in server.cfg.
- `Resource qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui does not exist` -> The folder is not inside resources, or the ensure line does not match the folder name exactly. Names are case sensitive.
- `attempt to index a nil value (local 'input')` -> The cancel guard is missing. Add if not input then return end right after lib.inputDialog so an Escape or closed dialog does not try to read input[1].
- `/oxdemo does nothing and there is no notification` -> Run restart qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui, then trigger /oxdemo from the in-game chat box (press T). The command is restricted false, so it runs from chat, not the server console.
- `The menu opens but clicking the row does nothing` -> The option event name and AddEventHandler name must match exactly. Both must read qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui:ask.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain that @ox_lib/init.lua loads ox_lib's lib global into your resource, why it must sit above client.lua, and why ox_lib is ensured first.
- Show a notification with lib.notify and pick the right type of success, error, or inform.
- Build a context menu with lib.registerContext and open it with lib.showContext, and wire a row with event= versus onSelect.
- Read a value back from lib.inputDialog as input[1] and handle the cancel case with if not input then return end.
- Use lib.progressCircle as a blocking timer and place it after the input it depends on.
- Say that ox_lib is an Overextended community library and find its full options in the ox_lib docs.

## Try it yourself

Extend the dialog to collect two fields and gate the save behind a longer timer. Ask for both a name and an amount, then report both:

```lua
AddEventHandler('qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui:ask', function()
    local input = lib.inputDialog('Save a record', { 'Name', 'Amount' })
    if not input then return end

    lib.progressCircle({ duration = 2500, label = 'Saving record' })

    lib.notify({
        title = 'Saved',
        description = input[1] .. ' set to ' .. input[2],
        type = 'success'
    })
    print('[qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui] saved ' .. input[1] .. ' = ' .. input[2])
end)
```

Restart `qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui`, run `/oxdemo`, choose the menu row, and enter a name and an amount. Confirm the progress circle now runs for 2.5 seconds, a second notification reports both values, and F8 prints a line like `[qu_ox_lib_notifications_ui] saved Mike = 500`. You just read a second field as `input[2]`, proving the dialog hands answers back in field order.

## References
- ox_lib documentation (Overextended): https://overextended.dev/ox_lib
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/

title: Project · Player survey form with ox_lib · Quasar Academy
ai:
By the end of this lesson you type `/survey` in chat, fill in a three-question form that `ox_lib` draws for you, hit submit, and watch the validated answers print in the **server** console, not the client. You write zero HTML, JS, or CSS for the form.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: A /survey command that opens an in-game form, then sends the answers to the server, which re-checks them before logging.
- Time: About 30 minutes if you go slow.
- You'll learn: lib.inputDialog for a free NUI form, then why the client form is untrusted and the server has to re-validate every answer.
- Prereqs: Module 04 done. ox_lib installed and ensured before any qu_ resource. You have met lib.inputDialog and TriggerServerEvent.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- ox_lib is installed and ensured above my qu_ resources.

> **ox_lib is a community library, install it first**
> This lesson uses `ox_lib`, a utility library from Overextended. It is a community resource, not a built-in Cfx.re one, so it does not ship with your server. Download the latest `ox_lib` release, drop the folder into `resources` (keep the name exactly `ox_lib`), and `ensure` it before `qu_survey`. If `ox_lib` is missing or started after your resource, the global `lib` is nil and the first `lib.inputDialog` line errors with `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')`.

> **The form is free, the trust is not**
> `lib.inputDialog` hands you a finished NUI panel: labelled fields, validation, a submit and a cancel flow. That saves you the entire interface. But the dialog runs on the **player's machine**, so the answers it returns are player-controlled. The whole second half of this lesson is about getting those answers to the server and re-checking them there before you believe a single one.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_survey
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_survey/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load and that ox_lib must load first._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'ox_lib'
}
```

`fx_version 'cerulean'` is the current manifest format, so always use it. `game 'gta5'` says this resource targets GTA V. There is no `lua54 'yes'` line: that directive was removed in June 2025, Lua 5.4 is now the only runtime, so the line does nothing and you should not add it. The `shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'` line loads ox_lib's `lib` global into your resource, and `dependencies { 'ox_lib' }` blocks startup until ox_lib is running.

### Step 4 - Write the client form
_Goal: A command opens the dialog and sends the answers up to the server._

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('survey', function()
    local answers = lib.inputDialog('Player Feedback Survey', {
        { type = 'input',    label = 'What is your favorite in-game activity?', required = true, max = 100 },
        { type = 'number',   label = 'Rate the server performance (1-10)',      required = true, min = 1, max = 10 },
        { type = 'checkbox', label = 'Would you recommend this server?' },
    })

    if not answers then
        print('[qu_survey] survey cancelled')
        return
    end

    TriggerServerEvent('qu_survey:submit', {
        activity   = answers[1],
        rating     = answers[2],
        recommend  = answers[3],
    })
end, false)
```

### Step 5 - Write the server validator
_Goal: The server re-checks every answer before it trusts or logs it._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_survey:submit', function(data)
    local src = source

    if type(data) ~= 'table' then return end

    local activity  = data.activity
    local rating    = data.rating
    local recommend = data.recommend

    if type(activity) ~= 'string' or #activity == 0 or #activity > 100 then
        print('[qu_survey] rejected ' .. GetPlayerName(src) .. ': bad activity')
        return
    end

    rating = tonumber(rating)
    if not rating or rating < 1 or rating > 10 then
        print('[qu_survey] rejected ' .. GetPlayerName(src) .. ': rating out of range')
        return
    end

    local recommendText = (recommend == true) and 'Yes' or 'No'

    print('[qu_survey] ' .. GetPlayerName(src) .. ' | activity: ' .. activity
        .. ' | rating: ' .. rating .. ' | recommend: ' .. recommendText)
end)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: The validated answers print in the server console._

Make sure `ox_lib` starts first. Open `server.cfg` and add both lines in this order:

```text
ensure ox_lib
ensure qu_survey
```

Save, then run this in the **txAdmin Live Console** (the server console):

```text
restart qu_survey
```

Join the server, then run this from the in-game chat box (press T) (this is a client command):

```text
/survey
```

Type `Racing` for the activity, `8` for the rating, tick the recommend box, and submit.

Expected output:

```
Server: [qu_survey] YourName | activity: Racing | rating: 8 | recommend: Yes
```

The line appears in the **server** console, not F8, because the answers travelled up to `server.lua` to be validated before anything was logged.

## How it works

You typed one command, a form drew itself, you filled it in, and a clean validated line landed on the server. Three things happened in order: ox_lib drew a NUI form for free, the client packaged the answers and shipped them up, and the server treated every answer as a lie until it proved otherwise. Take each piece apart so you can build your own surveys, application forms, or report tools later.

### The dialog is a whole NUI layer you did not write

```lua
local answers = lib.inputDialog('Player Feedback Survey', {
    { type = 'input',    label = 'What is your favorite in-game activity?', required = true, max = 100 },
    { type = 'number',   label = 'Rate the server performance (1-10)',      required = true, min = 1, max = 10 },
    { type = 'checkbox', label = 'Would you recommend this server?' },
})
```

`lib.inputDialog` takes a heading and a list of field tables. Each `{ }` in the list is one row in the form. The `type` decides the control ox_lib renders: `input` is a text box, `number` is a numeric box with up/down arrows, `checkbox` is a tickbox. The `label` is the text the player reads above the field. `required = true` makes ox_lib refuse to submit until that field has a value, and the `min`/`max` on the number field draw a hint and stop the arrows going out of range. The `max = 100` on the text field caps its length in the UI.

The input dialog supports far more than these three types: `select` and `multi-select` dropdowns, `slider`, `color`, `date`, `date-range`, `time`, and `textarea`, each with its own options. The three used here keep the example readable. The full list lives in the ox_lib docs linked at the bottom, and that is the page you return to when you want a dropdown or a date picker.

The single most important fact about this call: it runs on the **client**. ox_lib NUI is the player's own browser layer rendered over their game. So the values that come back are values the player produced, on hardware you do not control. Hold that thought, it is the entire reason `server.lua` exists.

### Why the answers come back as an ordered table, and the cancel guard

```lua
if not answers then
    print('[qu_survey] survey cancelled')
    return
end
```

`lib.inputDialog` returns a table when the player submits, indexed in the order you declared the fields: `answers[1]` is the activity, `answers[2]` is the rating, `answers[3]` is the checkbox. If the player presses Escape or closes the dialog without submitting, it returns `nil` instead.

That is why the guard comes first. If you skipped it and went straight to `answers[1]`, indexing a `nil` value throws `attempt to index a nil value (local 'answers')` every single time someone cancels. A cancelled dialog is a normal outcome, not an error, so you handle it before you touch any data and quietly stop.

### Why the client hands answers off instead of acting on them

```lua
TriggerServerEvent('qu_survey:submit', {
    activity   = answers[1],
    rating     = answers[2],
    recommend  = answers[3],
})
```

This is the line that separates a toy from a real script. The naive version of this lesson stops at `print(answers[1])` on the client and calls it done. That prints to F8, on the player's own machine, where the player could already see and change everything anyway. It proves nothing and stores nothing.

Instead the client packages the three answers into one named table and fires `TriggerServerEvent('qu_survey:submit', ...)`. `TriggerServerEvent` sends an event up to the server, and FiveM attaches the sender's identity automatically. Naming the fields (`activity`, `rating`, `recommend`) instead of passing a bare list means the server reads `data.rating` by name and never has to guess what `[2]` was supposed to be. The client's job ends here: it collected input and forwarded it. It deliberately makes no decision about whether the answers are valid, because a cheating client would simply lie in its own favour.

### server.lua treats every answer as untrusted

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_survey:submit', function(data)
    local src = source

    if type(data) ~= 'table' then return end
```

`RegisterNetEvent` listens for the event the client just fired. `source` is a FiveM global that, inside a net event handler, holds the server id of the player who triggered it. You copy it into a local `src` right away, because `source` can be overwritten by any `await` or wait inside the handler, and you want a stable reference to who sent this.

The first check, `type(data) ~= 'table'`, is your front door. A normal client sends the table you built. A malicious client can call `TriggerServerEvent('qu_survey:submit', 'hello')` or send nothing at all, hand-crafting whatever payload it likes, because the event name is public the moment your resource ships. If `data` is not even a table, you bail before reading a field off it. This is the rule for every networked event: validate the shape of what arrived before you trust its contents.

```lua
if type(activity) ~= 'string' or #activity == 0 or #activity > 100 then
    print('[qu_survey] rejected ' .. GetPlayerName(src) .. ': bad activity')
    return
end
```

The client UI said `required = true` and `max = 100`, but those rules only ran in the player's browser. A crafted payload never opened the dialog, so it never obeyed them. On the server you re-check the same rules from scratch: it must be a string, not empty, and not longer than 100 characters. The `#activity` operator is Lua's length operator, so `#activity` is the character count of the string. If any check fails, you log who sent the bad data and stop. Notice the rules are duplicated on purpose, once in the UI for the honest player's convenience, once on the server because that copy is the one that actually counts.

```lua
rating = tonumber(rating)
if not rating or rating < 1 or rating > 10 then
    print('[qu_survey] rejected ' .. GetPlayerName(src) .. ': rating out of range')
    return
end
```

The number field had `min = 1, max = 10`, and again that range was only enforced in the UI. A forged event could send `rating = 9999` or `rating = "abc"`. `tonumber` converts the value to a number or returns `nil` if it cannot, so a non-numeric payload fails the `not rating` check. Then you re-assert the `1` to `10` range yourself. Never trust a client number field to stay between its `min` and `max`: re-check the range on the server, every time.

```lua
    local recommendText = (recommend == true) and 'Yes' or 'No'

    print('[qu_survey] ' .. GetPlayerName(src) .. ' | activity: ' .. activity
        .. ' | rating: ' .. rating .. ' | recommend: ' .. recommendText)
end)
```

The checkbox arrives as a boolean. `(recommend == true) and 'Yes' or 'No'` is Lua's idiom for a ternary: it reads as "if recommend is exactly true, use 'Yes', otherwise 'No'." Writing `recommend == true` rather than just `recommend` means a forged string like `recommend = 'maybe'` does not count as a yes, only a real boolean true does. With all three answers validated, the final `print` logs them to the server console. In a finished resource this is where you would write to a database with an oxmysql `MySQL.insert` using `?` placeholders, never string concatenation, but a print is enough to prove the validated data arrived.

### The mental model

Picture the dialog as a comment card a player fills out at the door, and the server as the manager inside who reads it. The card is printed nicely (ox_lib drew it), the player writes their answers, and a runner carries the card inside (`TriggerServerEvent`). The manager does not assume the card is honest just because it is on official paper. They re-read every line: is the name field actually filled in, is the rating a number between 1 and 10, is the recommend box a real tick. Only cards that pass get filed. Whenever data leaves a player's machine and you intend to store it, count it, or act on it, the validation that matters happens on the server, because that is the one side the player cannot edit. The client form is a convenience for honest players, not a security boundary.

### Check yourself: The number field already has min = 1, max = 10. Why does server.lua check the range again with tonumber and rating < 1 or rating > 10?

Because `min` and `max` are enforced only in the ox_lib UI, which runs on the player's own machine. A cheating client never has to open the dialog at all, it can fire `TriggerServerEvent('qu_survey:submit', { rating = 9999 })` directly with any payload it wants, so the UI rules never run for them. The server is the only side the player cannot tamper with, so the range check on the server is the one that actually protects your data. `tonumber` first guards against a non-numeric value (it returns `nil`, which fails `not rating`), then the `1` to `10` comparison re-asserts the range. The UI copy is for the honest player's convenience, the server copy is the real rule.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')` -> ox_lib is not loaded into your resource. Confirm the manifest has shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua' above client_script, the folder is named exactly ox_lib, and ensure ox_lib comes before ensure qu_survey in server.cfg.
- `Unable to load resource qu_survey (dependency ox_lib)` -> ox_lib is not installed or not running. Download ox_lib from Overextended, place it in resources, and ensure it. The dependencies block blocks startup until it loads.
- `attempt to index a nil value (local 'answers')` -> The cancel guard is missing. Add if not answers then ... return end right after lib.inputDialog, so pressing Escape or closing the dialog does not try to read answers[1].
