- `/survey does nothing and no form appears` -> This is a client command. Join the server and run it from the in-game chat box (press T), not the txAdmin console. If the dialog still never opens, check F8 for a lib error.
- `The form submits but nothing prints in the server console` -> The event names must match exactly. The client TriggerServerEvent and the server RegisterNetEvent must both read qu_survey:submit, character for character. Confirm server_script 'server.lua' is in the manifest and restart qu_survey.
- `rejected ... : rating out of range even though you typed a valid number` -> You are reading the wrong field index, or the field order in lib.inputDialog does not match the keys you pass to TriggerServerEvent. activity is answers[1], rating is answers[2], recommend is answers[3], in the order the fields are declared.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Draw a full in-game form with lib.inputDialog by passing a heading and a list of field tables, and pick the type, label, required, min, and max for each field.
- Read the answers back as an ordered table where answers[1] is the first field, and handle the cancel case with if not answers then return end before touching any value.
- Explain that the dialog runs on the client, so its required, min, and max rules only protect honest players and cannot be trusted.
- Forward the answers to the server with a named table and TriggerServerEvent instead of acting on them client-side.
- Re-validate every answer on the server: check the payload is a table, the string is non-empty and bounded, and the number is really a number inside its range with tonumber.
- State the rule that validation which matters happens on the server, because that is the only side the player cannot edit.

## Try it yourself

Add a fourth question with a fixed list of choices, and validate it on the server against that same list. A `select` field shows a dropdown, and you pass it an `options` list where each option has a `value` you get back and a `label` the player reads. In `client.lua`, add the select as a fourth field and forward its answer:

```lua
local answers = lib.inputDialog('Player Feedback Survey', {
    { type = 'input',    label = 'What is your favorite in-game activity?', required = true, max = 100 },
    { type = 'number',   label = 'Rate the server performance (1-10)',      required = true, min = 1, max = 10 },
    { type = 'checkbox', label = 'Would you recommend this server?' },
    { type = 'select',   label = 'How long have you played here?', required = true, options = {
        { value = 'new',     label = 'Less than a week' },
        { value = 'regular', label = 'A few weeks' },
        { value = 'veteran', label = 'Months or longer' },
    } },
})
-- ...keep the cancel guard, then add tenure = answers[4] to the table you send up
```

Then in `server.lua`, do not trust that the value is one of your three options just because the dropdown offered them, a forged event can send `tenure = 'admin'`. Re-check it against an allow-list:

```lua
local allowedTenure = { new = true, regular = true, veteran = true }
if not allowedTenure[data.tenure] then
    print('[qu_survey] rejected ' .. GetPlayerName(src) .. ': bad tenure')
    return
end
```

Restart `qu_survey`, run `/survey`, pick a tenure, and confirm the validated value prints on the server. Then prove the guard works: temporarily change the client to send `tenure = 'admin'` and confirm the server rejects it instead of logging it. You just learned that even a closed dropdown is still client input, and an allow-list on the server is how you pin a value to a known set.

## References
- ox_lib input dialog (Overextended): https://overextended.dev/ox_lib/Modules/Interface/Client/input
- ox_lib documentation home: https://overextended.dev/ox_lib
- FiveM events: triggering events: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/working-with-events/triggering-events/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Lesson 15 · Callbacks · Quasar Academy
ai:
Events are good when you just want to send a message and move on. But some situations need a reply. For example: "do I have enough money to buy this?" In that case the client asks a question, waits, and gets an answer back. That pattern is called a callback.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: When to use a callback -> lib.callback.register on the server -> lib.callback.await on the client -> sync vs async forms
- Time: ~25 minutes
- Prereqs: Lessons 13–14 complete. You know TriggerServerEvent, RegisterNetEvent, source.
- Outcome: A working "can I afford this?" check, client asks, server verifies against its own balance, client waits for the yes/no and branches.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a working callback as a FiveM resource named `qu_callbacks`. The client asks the server for a balance, waits for the answer, and prints it. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line so you know why the client gets one clean answer instead of two unrelated events.

> **ox_lib is a community library, install it first**
> Callbacks in this lesson use `ox_lib`, a utility library originally created by Overextended and now maintained by the community under the CommunityOx organization. It is a community resource, not a built-in Cfx.re one, so it does not ship with your server. Download the latest `ox_lib` release zip from https://github.com/CommunityOx/ox_lib/releases (the project, originally by Overextended, is now maintained under CommunityOx; the docs live at https://overextended.dev/ox_lib). Unzip it, rename the folder to exactly `ox_lib`, drop it in `resources`, and `ensure` it before `qu_callbacks`. If `ox_lib` is missing or started after your resource, the global `lib` is nil and every line below errors with `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')`.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_callbacks
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_callbacks/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load and that ox_lib must load first._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'ox_lib'
}
```

The `dependencies { 'ox_lib' }` block tells the server not to start `qu_callbacks` until `ox_lib` is running. The singular form `dependency 'ox_lib'` is tolerated, but the plural block is the canonical one, so use it. The `shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'` line is what actually loads the library and gives you the global `lib` on both the client and the server.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The client asks a question and the server answers it._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
lib.callback.register('qu_callbacks:getBalance', function(src)
    print('[qu_callbacks] server answering ' .. GetPlayerName(src))
    return 500
end)
```

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('mybalance', function()
    local balance = lib.callback.await('qu_callbacks:getBalance', false)
    print('[qu_callbacks] my balance is ' .. balance)
end, false)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Make sure `ox_lib` starts first. Open `server.cfg` and add both lines in this order:

```text
ensure ox_lib
ensure qu_callbacks
```

Save. Then, in the **txAdmin Live Console** (the same place you read server output — not the in-game chat and not your computer's terminal), type:

```text
restart qu_callbacks
```

Join the server, then run this test from the chat box (press T) (this is a client command):

```text
/mybalance
```

Expected output:

```
F8: [qu_callbacks] my balance is 500
```

You will also see `[qu_callbacks] server answering YourName` in the txAdmin Live Console, proving the request crossed the network and the server is the one that produced the number.

## How it works

You typed one command and got back one number that the server owns. That is the whole point of a callback: the client asks a question, the request travels to the server, the server computes an answer, and that exact answer comes back to the same line of client code that asked. Now take the round trip apart so you can build your own.

### The server registers an answer

```lua
lib.callback.register('qu_callbacks:getBalance', function(src)
    print('[qu_callbacks] server answering ' .. GetPlayerName(src))
    return 500
end)
```

`lib.callback.register` does on the server what `RegisterCommand` did for commands: it stores a named handler now and runs it later when something asks for it. The first argument, `'qu_callbacks:getBalance'`, is the name of the question. The `resource:thing` shape is a convention, not a rule, but it keeps names from colliding when twenty resources all register callbacks.

The second argument is the function that produces the answer. Two things make it different from a normal event handler:

- `src` is passed in for you automatically. `ox_lib` puts the server id of the player who asked as the first parameter, so you always know who is asking without reaching for the global `source`. Here you use it to look up that specific player's name.
- Whatever you `return` becomes the answer. A plain `TriggerClientEvent` sends a fire-and-forget message and returns nothing meaningful. A callback handler's `return` value is shipped back to the client as the result of its question. Return `500` here and the client receives `500`. In a real script you would replace `return 500` with a database read or a framework call like `return Player(src).money`.

This is the server side of trust. The balance is computed on the server, where the player cannot touch it. In a real framework resource, replace the fixed `500` with a documented server-side framework adapter or database read; `Player(src).money` is not a built-in FiveM API.

### The client asks and waits

```lua
RegisterCommand('mybalance', function()
    local balance = lib.callback.await('qu_callbacks:getBalance', false)
    print('[qu_callbacks] my balance is ' .. balance)
end, false)
```

`lib.callback.await` is where the magic lives. Read the middle line as a single sentence: "ask the server the `qu_callbacks:getBalance` question, then pause right here until the answer comes back, and put that answer in `balance`."

The function blocks. The line that prints does not run until the server's `return` has travelled all the way back across the network. This is why callbacks read like normal top-to-bottom code even though a network round trip is happening in the middle. You write it as if the answer is just sitting there, and `ox_lib` handles the waiting for you.

That pause is only possible because a command handler runs inside a coroutine, a FiveM thread that is allowed to yield and resume. `await` yields the thread while it waits and resumes it when the answer arrives. If you ever call `lib.callback.await` from code that is not inside a thread, you will get a "cannot resume" style error. Inside a `RegisterCommand` handler, a `CreateThread`, or an event handler, you are safe.

#### What the second argument actually does

```lua
lib.callback.await('qu_callbacks:getBalance', false)
```

That `false` is the most copied and least understood value in the whole lesson. It is the `delay`, in milliseconds: a re-trigger cooldown, not a response timeout. It controls the minimum time that must pass before the same callback name can be triggered again from this client.

- Pass a number, like `5000`, and you set a five second cooldown on this callback name. If you try to trigger `qu_callbacks:getBalance` again within that window, the new request is silently dropped, no answer comes back, and you do not even get a `nil`. Use this to rate-limit a callback a player could spam.
- Pass `false` and you set no cooldown. Every call is allowed through immediately. That is the right choice here, and the common one, because most callbacks do not need to be rate-limited.

Note what `delay` is not: it is not how long `await` waits for the answer. The response timeout is a separate global convar, covered below. If you want to throttle a spammable callback, pick a cooldown like `5000`; otherwise pass `false` and move on.

This delay is client-side convenience, not a security rate limit. A modified client can bypass it and call the underlying network event. Validate every callback argument and apply any real cooldown or rate limit on the server.

You should still guard the result before you trust it, because the server handler can return `nil`:

```lua
local balance = lib.callback.await('qu_callbacks:getBalance', false)
if balance == nil then
    print('[qu_callbacks] server returned no balance')
    return
end
print('[qu_callbacks] my balance is ' .. balance)
```

The real response timeout lives somewhere else entirely. It is a global convar named `ox:callbackTimeout`, set once for the whole server, not per call. Its default is `300000` (five minutes). You change it in `server.cfg` with a single line:

```text
setr ox:callbackTimeout 10000
```

When that limit is hit, `await` does not hand you `nil`, it raises an error like `callback event 'qu_callbacks:getBalance' timed out`. So a timeout surfaces as a thrown error you can see in the console, while a `nil` means the server handler ran but returned nothing useful.

### Check yourself: Why does the print line wait for the server instead of running immediately, and what would make balance come back as nil?

`lib.callback.await` blocks the handler's coroutine at that line. It yields the thread, sends the request to the server, and only resumes, letting the next line run, once the server's `return` value has arrived back. So the print cannot run with an empty `balance`, it runs with the real answer. `balance` comes back `nil` when the server handler returns `nil` or returns no value at all. The second argument, `false`, is a re-trigger cooldown, not a timeout, so it has nothing to do with `nil`. The actual response timeout is the global convar `ox:callbackTimeout`, and when it fires it raises an error rather than handing you `nil`.

### Why this beats two raw events

You could fake a callback with the tools from the previous lessons: the client fires `TriggerServerEvent('askBalance')`, the server fires back `TriggerClientEvent('balanceAnswer', src, 500)`, and a separate `RegisterNetEvent('balanceAnswer', ...)` on the client catches it. That works, but look at what you lost.

The answer arrives in a different place from where you asked. Your "what do I do with the balance" logic now lives in the event handler, far from the command that started everything. If you fire two balance requests close together, nothing tells you which answer belongs to which request, you have to hand-match them yourself with request ids. And there is no safety net for a dropped response, so a lost answer leaves the client waiting on an event that never comes, with no way to notice.

`lib.callback.await` solves all three. The answer comes back to the exact line that asked, each request is correlated to its own response, and the global `ox:callbackTimeout` convar makes an unanswered request error instead of hanging forever. This is why callback helpers are useful for client-to-server questions that need an answer.

### The async form, when you do not want to block

`await` is the blocking, synchronous form. There is a second form that does not freeze the thread. Instead of returning the value, you hand `lib.callback` a function that runs when the answer arrives:

```lua
RegisterCommand('mybalance_async', function()
    lib.callback('qu_callbacks:getBalance', false, function(balance)
        print('[qu_callbacks] async balance is ' .. balance)
    end)
    print('[qu_callbacks] this line runs before the answer arrives')
end, false)
```

Notice the call is `lib.callback` with no `.await`, and the last argument is a function. The request fires, the handler does not pause, the second print runs immediately, and the supplied function runs later when the server answers. Reach for the async form when you have other work to do while you wait, or when blocking the thread would freeze something time sensitive like a render loop. Reach for `await` when the next line genuinely needs the answer before it can do anything, which is most of the time. Both forms hit the same registered handler on the server, the only difference is whether the client thread waits.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')` -> ox_lib is not loaded. Confirm ensure ox_lib comes before ensure qu_callbacks in server.cfg, and that the manifest has shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'.
- `Unable to load resource qu_callbacks (dependency ox_lib)` -> ox_lib is not installed or not running. Download ox_lib from Overextended, place it in resources, and ensure it. The dependencies block blocks startup until it loads.
- `/mybalance does nothing and F8 stays empty` -> This is a client command, so join the server and run it from the in-game chat box (press T), not the txAdmin console. Check F8 for the client print.
- `balance is nil / attempt to concatenate a nil value` -> The server handler did not return a value (or returned nil). Make sure server.lua returns a value, and guard for nil before using balance.
- `attempt to yield from outside a coroutine` -> lib.callback.await must run inside a thread. Keep it inside the RegisterCommand handler, a CreateThread, or an event handler, never at the top level of the file.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain when to reach for a callback instead of an event: any time the client needs an answer back, not just to send a message.
- Register a server-side answer with lib.callback.register and return the value the client receives, using the auto-passed src to know who asked.
- Ask from the client with lib.callback.await and explain that it blocks the coroutine until the server's return arrives.
- Say what the second argument does: it is a cooldown (delay) — a number is the minimum time before the same callback can be re-triggered; false means no cooldown.
- Explain why one callback beats two raw events: the answer returns to the asking line, requests and responses are correlated automatically, and you get a timeout escape hatch.
- Choose between the blocking await form and the async lib.callback function form based on whether the next line needs the answer.

## Try it yourself

Make the callback answer a real yes/no question without trusting a client-supplied price. Let the client request an item name, then have the server look up the authoritative price:

```lua
local prices = { water = 25, bandage = 100 }

lib.callback.register('qu_callbacks:canAfford', function(src, itemName)
    if type(itemName) ~= 'string' then return false end
    local price = prices[itemName]
    if not price then return false end

    local balance = 500
    return balance >= price, price
end)
```

Change the client to send the item name and branch on the answer. The `5000` delay prevents accidental rapid repeats in this normal client, while the allow-list and server-side lookup enforce trust:

```lua
RegisterCommand('canibuy', function(_, args)
    local itemName = args[1] or ''
    local ok, price = lib.callback.await('qu_callbacks:canAfford', 5000, itemName)
    if ok then
        print(('[qu_callbacks] you can afford %s for %d'):format(itemName, price))
    else
        print('[qu_callbacks] unknown item or not enough money')
    end
end, false)
```

Restart `qu_callbacks`, then run `/canibuy water` and, after five seconds, `/canibuy supercar` in chat. The server accepts the allow-listed item and rejects the invented one. A real purchase would re-check the same server price and balance while performing the server-side debit; a positive UI callback alone must never grant the item.

## References
- ox_lib callback documentation (client): https://overextended.dev/ox_lib/Modules/Callback/Lua/Client
- ox_lib callback documentation (server): https://overextended.dev/ox_lib/Modules/Callback/Lua/Server
- ox_lib callback timeout source (ox:callbackTimeout default 300000): https://github.com/CommunityOx/ox_lib/blob/master/imports/callback/client.lua
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Bonus · State bags: Entity, Player, GlobalState · Quasar Academy
ai:
Half the time you reach for `TriggerClientEvent`, what you actually want is a **state bag**: a key/value blob attached to an entity, a player, or the whole server, automatically replicated across the network. Set the value once on the server; every client sees it. Hook a handler; you get notified when it changes.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: Entity state, Player state, and GlobalState; who can write each; and how to react to changes with AddStateBagChangeHandler
- Time: ~15 minutes
- Prereqs: Lesson 14 (events) and Lesson 12 (trust & authority)
- Outcome: You can replace ad-hoc broadcast events with state bags and pick the right scope (entity vs player vs global)

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a working FiveM resource named `qu_state_bags`. You set a value on the server, it replicates to every client, and a change handler on the client reads it back. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line and teaches the other two scopes the title promises.

> **OneSync must be on**
> State bags require state awareness (OneSync). Confirm your server profile has OneSync enabled before testing. If you manage `server.cfg` yourself, use the current `onesync on` setting supported by your artifact and perform a full server restart after changing it.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_state_bags
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_state_bags/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('setmood', function(src, args)
    local mood = args[1] or 'calm'
    Player(src).state:set('mood', mood, true)
    print('[qu_state_bags] set mood ' .. mood .. ' on player ' .. src)
end, false)
```

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local myBagName = ('player:%s'):format(GetPlayerServerId(PlayerId()))

AddStateBagChangeHandler('mood', myBagName, function(bagName, key, value)
    print('[qu_state_bags] my mood is now ' .. tostring(value))
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_state_bags
```

    Save. Then, in the txAdmin Live Console (or the FXServer console window), run:

    ```text
ensure qu_state_bags
```

    `ensure` starts the resource if it is not running yet and restarts it if it is, so it is the safe command for a resource you just added.

    Join the server so you are a real player, then run this in the in-game chat box:

    ```text
/setmood focused
```

Expected output:

```
Server: [qu_state_bags] set mood focused on player 1
F8: [qu_state_bags] my mood is now focused
```

    Your server slot may be a different number than `1`. The point is the same line appears on both sides: the server set it, the client received it.

## How it works

You set one value on the server and a different machine, the client, printed it back without you sending a single event. That is the whole promise of a state bag, and it is worth understanding exactly how the value crossed the network, because the same three lines unlock entity state and global state too.

### What a state bag actually is

A state bag is a **key/value store bolted onto something the engine already networks**: a player, an entity, or the server itself. You write `Player(src).state:set('mood', 'focused', true)` and FiveM stores `mood = "focused"` against that player and replicates it according to network scope. It is the opposite of an event in one important way. An event is fire-and-forget; a state bag holds the current value for as long as its owner exists. This is network state, not disk persistence: player/entity state disappears with that owner, and state bags do not replace MariaDB or the KVS for surviving a server restart.

Three scopes exist, and picking the right one is the skill the lesson title promises:

- **`Entity(handle).state`** belongs to a specific entity: a vehicle, a ped, a prop. The value travels with that entity. A common use is an engine-on flag or a locked flag on a car. When the entity is deleted, its bag goes with it.
- **`Player(serverId).state`** belongs to a player slot. Use it for player-level facts that should follow the person rather than their current body: a job, a stress level, an admin duty flag. This is what you built.
- **`GlobalState`** belongs to the whole server. There is exactly one, shared by everyone. Use it for world facts: the current weather, whether a heist is active, a server-wide event timer.

The rule of thumb: scope the data to the thing it describes. A car door does not belong on a player, and the weather does not belong on a car.

### The server line, argument by argument

```lua
Player(src).state:set('mood', mood, true)
```

`Player(src)` looks up the player by their **server id**, the slot number you saw printed as `1`. The `src` comes from the command handler, exactly as it did in the events lessons: it is the player who typed the command. `.state` is that player's state bag, the key/value store. `:set(...)` writes one key. Read its three arguments left to right:

- `'mood'` is the **key**, the name of the slot you are writing. You read it back later by this same name.
- `mood` is the **value**, here whatever word the player typed after the command. State bag values can be strings, numbers, booleans, or tables, so you could store `{ level = 3, color = 'red' }` just as easily. Note that `args[1]` is raw client input: a player controls exactly what they type, so for any state that grants power (a job, an admin flag) you must validate or restrict who can set it on the server rather than trusting the argument as-is.
- `true` is the **replicated flag**. State set by the server replicates by default, so omitting the third argument would also replicate; passing `true` here makes the lesson's intent explicit. Pass `false` to keep that write server-only.

### The client handler, argument by argument

```lua
local myBagName = ('player:%s'):format(GetPlayerServerId(PlayerId()))

AddStateBagChangeHandler('mood', myBagName, function(bagName, key, value)
    print('[qu_state_bags] my mood is now ' .. tostring(value))
end)
```

`AddStateBagChangeHandler` registers a function that fires whenever a matching bag changes. It takes three arguments:

- The first, `'mood'`, is the **key filter**: only changes to a key named `mood` wake this handler. Pass `nil` here and you would hear about every key on every matching bag.
- The second, `myBagName`, is the **bag filter**: which bag to watch. Player bag names use `player:<serverId>`, so building the name from your local player's server ID narrows this handler to your own bag. Pass `nil` only when you intentionally want to observe every matching bag.
- The third is the **callback**, and FiveM hands it `bagName`, `key`, and `value` when a change fires. `bagName` is a string like `player:1`, where `1` is the server id whose bag changed. `value` is the new value.

The bag name format is worth recognizing because the same shape shows up for other scopes. A player bag is `player:<serverId>`, an entity bag is `entity:<netId>`, and global state uses `global`. When you truly need all player bags, pass `nil` as the bag filter and resolve each bag deliberately; for an own-player handler, an exact bag filter is simpler and avoids mixing up server IDs, local player indexes, and not-found sentinel values.

### Check yourself: Why does this handler build `player:<serverId>` instead of comparing a resolved player value with `PlayerId()`?

A server ID, a client-local player index, and a state-bag name are three different identifiers. Building the exact bag name from `GetPlayerServerId(PlayerId())` lets FiveM filter before the callback runs and avoids ambiguous conversions. The result is one handler for exactly this client's player bag.

### When you reach for a state bag instead of an event

Reach for a state bag when the answer to "what is the value right now?" needs to be true for anyone who asks, including late joiners. Three concrete cases:

- **A flag that must survive.** A player goes on admin duty. With an event you would have to re-broadcast the flag every time someone new connects. With `Player(src).state:set('onDuty', true, true)` you set it once and every present and future client can read it.
- **A property of a thing, synced for free.** A vehicle's locked status. Put it on `Entity(veh).state` and every client that can see the car knows whether it is locked, without you tracking which clients to message.
- **A world fact.** A server-wide heist is active. `GlobalState:set('heistActive', true, true)` flips one switch and every script, on every client, can branch on `GlobalState.heistActive`.

If instead you only need a one-time "do this now" with no value worth remembering, an event is still the right tool. State bags are for state. Events are for moments.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_state_bags does not exist` -> Check the folder is inside resources and the ensure line uses the exact same name.
- `Server prints set mood but F8 shows nothing` -> Confirm the third argument of :set is true (or omitted for the server default), OneSync is enabled, and myBagName uses GetPlayerServerId(PlayerId()).
- `F8 prints other players' moods` -> Your bag filter is nil or built from the wrong ID type. Filter with ('player:%s'):format(GetPlayerServerId(PlayerId())).
- `Nothing replicates even with the true flag` -> State bags need OneSync. Confirm set onesync on is in server.cfg (the old onesync_enabled true form is deprecated), then restart the server, not just the resource.
- `F8 never opens or shows no output` -> You must be joined as a real player. The change handler runs on the client, so connect to the server first, then read F8.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain what a state bag is: a replicated key/value store on a player, entity, or the whole server, and how it differs from a fire-and-forget event.
- Name the three scopes and pick the right one: Entity(handle).state for a thing, Player(serverId).state for a person, GlobalState for the world.
- Set a value with Player(src).state:set(key, value, true) and explain that the third argument is the replicated flag that pushes it to clients.
- Read AddStateBagChangeHandler's three arguments: a key filter, a bag filter where nil means all bags, and a callback that receives bagName, key, and value.
- Build an exact player:<serverId> bag filter to react only to the local player's bag without mixing ID types.

## Try it yourself

Promote the mood from a player fact to a world fact. Add a second command, `/serverstatus`, that writes to GlobalState instead of a player, and a second handler that reads it on every client:

```lua
-- server.lua
RegisterCommand('serverstatus', function(_, args)
    local status = args[1] or 'calm'
    GlobalState.serverStatus = status
    print('[qu_state_bags] server status is now ' .. status)
end, true)
```

```lua
-- client.lua
AddStateBagChangeHandler('serverStatus', 'global', function(bagName, key, value)
    print('[qu_state_bags] world status changed to ' .. tostring(value))
end)
```

Restart `qu_state_bags`, run `serverstatus busy` from txAdmin Live Console, and confirm every connected client prints the new status, with no per-player guard needed. The command is ACE-restricted because a world-wide status is server authority, not something every player should set. Notice you passed `'global'` as the bag filter instead of `nil`, which narrows the handler to the server-wide bag.

## References
- FiveM state bags guide: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/networking/state-bags/
- AddStateBagChangeHandler native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x5BA35AAF
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: Lesson 17 · Frameworks: ESX, QBCore, Qbox & ox_lib · Quasar Academy
ai:
Real FiveM servers usually run a *framework*. A framework answers the same boring questions for every script: who is this player, how much money do they have, what job are they on, what inventory do they carry. This lesson reads like a translation guide. Learn the idea once, then see how ESX, QBCore, and Qbox spell it.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: What a framework is -> how the player object looks in each one -> money and job basics -> load events -> the ox_lib tools you will use most days
- Time: ~45 minutes
- Prereqs: Module 04 complete. A test server with any of ESX / QBCore / Qbox installed. ox_lib resource running.
- Outcome: You can read and compare scripts written for ESX, QBCore, and Qbox, and you can understand where ox_lib fits in.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a working FiveM resource named `qu_frameworks` that detects which framework is running and prints it through an `ox_lib` call. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Then the next section turns the detection into the real prize: a side-by-side translation guide for the player object, money, and jobs across all three frameworks.

## The frameworks at a glance

Each framework has its own docs and its own way of spelling the same ideas. Bookmark the one your server actually runs. Qbox describes itself as a QBCore successor and keeps a QB compatibility bridge; ESX Legacy and QBCore remain separate ecosystems. `ox_lib` is optional utility code, not a requirement of ESX or QBCore. The maintained 2026 `ox_lib`, `oxmysql`, `ox_target`, and `ox_inventory` repositories live under **Community Ox**. They are community resources, not Cfx.re platform features.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_frameworks
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_frameworks/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'ox_lib'
}
```

The canonical manifest key is the plural `dependencies { 'ox_lib' }`. The singular `dependency 'ox_lib'` is still tolerated, but show the plural so it reads the same as every modern resource. Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line; as of June 2025 that is deprecated and ignored, because Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime. Leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local function detectFramework()
    if GetResourceState('qbx_core') == 'started' then return 'qbox' end
    if GetResourceState('qb-core') == 'started' then return 'qbcore' end
    if GetResourceState('es_extended') == 'started' then return 'esx' end
    return 'none'
end

RegisterCommand('fwprobe', function()
    local fw = detectFramework()
    lib.print.info(('[qu_frameworks] detected framework: %s'):format(fw))
end, true)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line, after the line that ensures `ox_lib`:

```text
ensure qu_frameworks
```

Save, then run:

```text
restart qu_frameworks
```

Run this test from the txAdmin Live Console:

```text
fwprobe
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_frameworks] detected framework: none
```

On a bare practice server with no framework installed, the answer is `none`, and that is the correct, passing result. It means your detection works and correctly found nothing running. Install ESX, QBCore, or Qbox and ensure it before `qu_frameworks`, restart, and the same command prints `esx`, `qbcore`, or `qbox` instead. You will see why below.

## How it works

You ran it and a line appeared. The detection is the easy half. The valuable half is what the framework name unlocks: once you know which one is running, you know how to ask it who the player is, how much money they have, and what job they are on. That is the translation guide this whole lesson exists for. Take the code apart first, then learn the three idioms you will read every day.

### What GetResourceState actually returns

```lua
if GetResourceState('es_extended') == 'started' then return 'esx' end
```

`GetResourceState` is a FiveM *native* — a built-in function the game server itself provides (as opposed to a framework export or an ox_lib call), so it works the same on every server regardless of which framework is installed. You hand it a resource name as a string and it tells you the lifecycle state of that resource. The documented states are `missing`, `started`, `starting`, `stopped`, `stopping`, `uninitialized`, and `unknown`. The only state that means *this framework is live and its exports are callable* is `started`. So the probe is really asking, in order, is `qbx_core` started, then `qb-core`, then `es_extended`, and it returns the first match. The order matters: it checks Qbox first, then QBCore, then ESX, and returns the moment it finds one. On a server with none of them, every check fails and the function falls through to `return 'none'`. That is why a bare practice server prints `none`. Nothing is wrong; there is simply no framework resource in the `started` state to find.

The resource names are the real folder names each framework ships under, and they are easy to mix up:

- **ESX Legacy** runs under `es_extended`.
- **QBCore** runs under `qb-core` (with a hyphen).
- **Qbox** runs under `qbx_core` (with an underscore and an x).

If you check the wrong string, detection silently returns `none` even though the framework is running. Match the folder name exactly.

### Why the ox_lib dependency earns its place

In the old version of this lesson, `ox_lib` was declared as a dependency but never called, which is dead weight. Here the print goes through it on purpose:

```lua
lib.print.info(('[qu_frameworks] detected framework: %s'):format(fw))
```

`lib` is the global that `@ox_lib/init.lua` injects into your resource. The `shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'` line in the manifest is what loads it, and the `dependencies { 'ox_lib' }` line is what guarantees `ox_lib` starts first so `lib` exists by the time your code runs. `lib.print.info` is `ox_lib`'s leveled logger: it prints with an `[info]` tag and respects a per-resource log-level convar, so you can silence debug noise in production without deleting print lines. The `('...'):format(fw)` part is Lua string formatting, where `%s` is replaced by the value of `fw`. That is the same as writing `'... ' .. fw`, just cleaner when you have several values.

That single call is your first real `ox_lib` usage. The library also gives you the things you will reach for constantly: `lib.notify` for on-screen toasts, `lib.callback` for clean client to server request and response, `lib.registerContext` and `lib.showContext` for menus, `lib.progressBar` for timed actions, and `lib.callback.register` on the server. Every modern framework script you read leans on these instead of reinventing them.

### The translation guide: player, money, job

This is the part the title promised. The framework name you just detected tells you which dialect to speak. Every other line in this section depends on one move first: turning a server id into a player object. Get that one call right and the rest is just reading fields.

#### First, get the player object

You cannot read money or a job until you hold the player object. Each framework hands it to you a little differently, but the shape is identical: feed it the player's server id, get back a table you then read from. Here are the three calls side by side, nothing else.

Read it as one idea in three spellings. ESX grabs the shared object first, then calls `GetPlayerFromId`. QBCore grabs the core object, then calls `Functions.GetPlayer`. Qbox exposes the native `exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)` getter and separately provides a QB compatibility surface for older resources. In every case `src` is the server-side `source`, and the return value can be `nil` until the character has loaded. Always keep a nil guard before reading the object.

Here is each call on its own, with the line that proves you got a real object. These three blocks are illustrative: they only run on a server that actually has that framework installed and a player loaded, so do not expect them to print on the bare practice server from the Build it section.

```lua
-- ESX Legacy (server side)
local ESX = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()
local xPlayer = ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)
print(xPlayer.getName())                  -- proves the object exists
```

```lua
-- QBCore (server side)
local QBCore = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()
local Player = QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)
print(Player.PlayerData.charinfo.firstname) -- the character's first name; proves the object loaded
```

```lua
-- Qbox (server side)
local player = exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)
print(player.PlayerData.charinfo.firstname) -- same field in Qbox; proves the object loaded
```

That getter is the spine of the next lesson, [How a script talks to a framework](/lessons/how-scripts-talk-to-frameworks), which turns this one call into a full framework-agnostic resource that gets the player, gives them money, and reads their job on all three. Here you just need to recognize the call. There you build the whole thing.

Once you hold the object, reading money and job is the same job in each one. Here is the full pattern.

> **Security: never ship an unvalidated giveCash event**
> Any client can trigger a `RegisterNetEvent` handler. These giveCash snippets are illustrative of the money API only — never ship a net event that hands out money without server-side checks (who is allowed, how much, how often). An unvalidated giveCash event is a classic money-duplication exploit.

**ESX Legacy.** You grab the shared object, then ask it for a player by server id. The player object is usually called `xPlayer`.

```lua
local ESX = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()

RegisterNetEvent('myscript:giveCash', function()
    local src = source
    local xPlayer = ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)
    local cash = xPlayer.getAccount('money').money -- cash on hand (getMoney() also works but ESX docs mark it backwards-compat only)
    xPlayer.addMoney(500)                     -- add 500 cash
    local job = xPlayer.job.name              -- e.g. 'police'
    print(('%s has $%d, job %s'):format(GetPlayerName(src), cash, job))
end)
```

**QBCore.** You grab the core object, get the player, then read a `PlayerData` table. Money lives in `PlayerData.money`, a table keyed by account.

```lua
local QBCore = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()

RegisterNetEvent('myscript:giveCash', function()
    local src = source
    local Player = QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)
    local cash = Player.PlayerData.money.cash   -- cash account
    Player.Functions.AddMoney('cash', 500)
    local job = Player.PlayerData.job.name       -- e.g. 'police'
    print(('%s has $%d, job %s'):format(GetPlayerName(src), cash, job))
end)
```

**Qbox.** Qbox is a newer, ox-native fork of QBCore, so the shape is nearly identical to QBCore on purpose. The modern entry point is the `exports.qbx_core` getter, and the same `PlayerData` layout applies.

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('myscript:giveCash', function()
    local src = source
    local player = exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)
    local cash = player.PlayerData.money.cash
    exports.qbx_core:AddMoney(src, 'cash', 500, 'qu_frameworks example')
    local job = player.PlayerData.job.name
    print(('%s has $%d, job %s'):format(GetPlayerName(src), cash, job))
end)
```

Read those three side by side and the pattern is clear. Every framework gives you a way to turn a server id into a player object, a way to read and change money, and a `job` field. The verbs differ (`getMoney` vs `PlayerData.money.cash`), but the questions are the same four every time: who, how much money, what job, what inventory. Once you internalize the questions, learning a new framework is just looking up its spelling.

### Load events: why you wait

There is a trap here that bites every beginner. The player object does not exist the instant a player connects. The framework has to pull their character from the database, build the object, and hand it to the client. If your client code asks for the player object too early, it gets `nil`. So each framework fires a load event the moment the player is ready, and you do your setup inside it.

- **ESX Legacy:** the client event `esx:playerLoaded` (it hands you the `xPlayer` data), plus the server-side `esx:playerLoaded`.
- **QBCore:** the client event `QBCore:Client:OnPlayerLoaded`, and server-side `QBCore:Server:OnPlayerLoaded`.
- **Qbox:** keeps QBCore's `QBCore:Client:OnPlayerLoaded` (and server-side `QBCore:Server:OnPlayerLoaded`) for compatibility, and reuses much of the same event surface; its own events use a lowercase `qbx_core:` prefix, and it also exposes ox-style state for player data.

```lua
-- ESX client: only safe to read the player after this fires
RegisterNetEvent('esx:playerLoaded', function(xPlayer)
    print('Player is ready, job is ' .. xPlayer.job.name)
end)
```

The mental model: connecting and being loaded are two different moments, exactly like registering a command and running it are two different moments. You register a handler for the load event up front, and the framework runs it later, once the character is actually in the world. Reading money or job before that event is the number one cause of `attempt to index a nil value` in framework scripts.

### Check yourself: A teammate says their ESX script keeps erroring with 'attempt to index a nil value (local xPlayer)' right when a player joins. What is almost certainly wrong, and what is the fix?

They are calling `ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)` (or reading `xPlayer` on the client) before the framework has built the player object, so it returns `nil` and indexing it throws. The fix is to move the code inside the load event. On the client, wrap it in `RegisterNetEvent('esx:playerLoaded', function(xPlayer) ... end)` and use the `xPlayer` the event hands you. On the server, only call `ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)` after the player is loaded (inside the server `esx:playerLoaded`, or in response to an event the client only triggers after it is loaded). Connecting is not the same moment as being loaded; you must wait for the load event before the player object exists.

### When you reach for this pattern

Framework detection plus the translation idioms is the foundation under almost every script you will write or read on a real server:

- **Writing a framework-agnostic script.** You detect the framework once at startup, then branch your money and job calls so the same resource runs on ESX, QBCore, and Qbox. This is how published scripts support every framework from one download.
- **Reading someone else's resource.** When you open a script and see `exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()` you instantly know it is QBCore and you know where money and job live. The translation guide is a reading skill as much as a writing one.
- **Debugging on a live server.** A quick `fwprobe`-style command tells you what framework an unfamiliar server is actually running before you start guessing.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')` -> The @ox_lib/init.lua shared_script line is missing or ox_lib is not started. Add the shared_script line and ensure ox_lib before qu_frameworks in server.cfg.
- `Resource qu_frameworks does not exist` -> Check the folder is inside resources and the ensure line uses the exact same name.
- `Could not load resource qu_frameworks` -> Open fxmanifest.lua and fix the first quote, brace, or file name error the console reports. Confirm the dependencies block uses curly braces.
- `fwprobe always prints none even though a framework is running` -> Check the resource name string matches the real folder: es_extended for ESX, qb-core for QBCore, qbx_core for Qbox. A typo silently falls through to none.
- `The command does nothing when a player runs it in chat` -> The restricted flag is true, so only the server console may run it. Run fwprobe from the txAdmin Live Console, not the in-game chat box (press T).

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain what a framework gives you: a player object, money, job, and inventory behind one shared object.
- Detect the active framework with GetResourceState, and explain why started is the state that matters and why a bare server prints none.
- Read and write the player + money + job idiom in ESX (ESX.GetPlayerFromId, getMoney), QBCore (GetCoreObject, PlayerData.money), and Qbox (qbx_core getter).
- Wait for the correct load event (esx:playerLoaded, QBCore:Client:OnPlayerLoaded) before touching the player object, and recognize the nil-index error you get when you do not.
- Use a real ox_lib call (lib.print.info) and name the everyday ox_lib tools: lib.notify, lib.callback, lib.progressBar, lib.registerContext.
- Distinguish the three ecosystems without treating one framework as an automatic drop-in replacement for another.

## Try it yourself

Turn the probe into a real framework-agnostic money reader. Add a `mymoney` command that detects the framework, gets the player who ran it, reads their cash, and prints it. Branch on the detected framework so the same command works on ESX and QBCore or Qbox:

```lua
RegisterCommand('mymoney', function(src)
    if src == 0 then
        lib.print.warn('[qu_frameworks] run this in-game, not from the console')
        return
    end
    local fw = detectFramework()
    local cash
    if fw == 'esx' then
        local ESX = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()
        cash = ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src).getMoney()
    elseif fw == 'qbcore' then
        cash = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject().Functions.GetPlayer(src).PlayerData.money.cash
    elseif fw == 'qbox' then
        cash = exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src).PlayerData.money.cash
    else
        lib.print.warn('[qu_frameworks] no framework detected')
        return
    end
    lib.print.info(('[qu_frameworks] %s has $%d'):format(GetPlayerName(src), cash))
end, false)
```

Restart `qu_frameworks`, join the server, and run `/mymoney` from the chat box (press T) (this one is unrestricted because a real player triggers it). Confirm it prints your name and your cash on hand. You just wrote one command that speaks all three dialects, which is exactly how published scripts stay framework-agnostic.

## References
- ESX Legacy documentation: https://docs.esx-framework.org/
- Qbox documentation: https://docs.qbox.re/
- Community Ox ox_lib documentation: https://coxdocs.dev/ox_lib
- Community Ox ox_lib repository: https://github.com/communityox/ox_lib
- FiveM GetResourceState native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x4039B485
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/

title: Lesson 19 · /heal command, your first build · Quasar Academy
ai:
By the end of this lesson you will type `/heal` in chat and watch your character refill to full health, with this command's cooldown enforced on the server.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: A working /heal command that heals only the player who typed it, with a 60 second cooldown.
- Time: About 25 minutes if you go slow.
- You'll learn: How a chat command becomes code, and why the server is the one allowed to say yes.
- Prereqs: You can start a FiveM server and edit a file. That is enough.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then change it**
> This lesson builds a server-approved `/heal` command as a working FiveM resource named `qu_heal_command`. The server decides who is allowed to heal, and the client applies the health. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly, then read How it works to understand every line.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_heal_command
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_heal_command/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local cooldown = {}

RegisterCommand('heal', function(src)
    local now = os.time()
    if cooldown[src] and now - cooldown[src] < 60 then
        TriggerClientEvent('qu_heal_command:deny', src, 60 - (now - cooldown[src]))
        return
    end
    cooldown[src] = now
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_heal_command:heal', src)
    print('[qu_heal_command] approved heal for ' .. GetPlayerName(src))
end, false)
```

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_heal_command:heal', function()
    local ped = PlayerPedId()
    SetEntityHealth(ped, GetEntityMaxHealth(ped))
    print('[qu_heal_command] healed')
end)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_heal_command:deny', function(seconds)
    print('[qu_heal_command] wait ' .. seconds .. ' seconds')
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_heal_command
```

    Save. Now start the resource in the live console (txAdmin Live Console or the FXServer console window) by typing:

    ```text
ensure qu_heal_command
```

    Use `ensure`, not `restart`, the first time: the line you just added to `server.cfg` is only read when the whole server boots, so on an already-running server the resource has not started yet. `restart` only works on a resource that is already running, so it would do nothing here. `ensure` starts the resource if it is stopped and restarts it if it is already running, so it always works.

    Join your own server, wait until your character has spawned in, then open the in-game chat box (press the chat key, by default `T`) and type the command, then press Enter:

    ```text
/heal
```

Expected output:

```
Server: [qu_heal_command] approved heal for yourName
F8: [qu_heal_command] healed
```

## How it works

You just built a command that crosses the network twice. The chat input lands on the server, the server decides whether this resource's cooldown passed, and the answer travels back to the client to apply health. This protects the command rule. It does not stop a modified client from changing its own health by another route, so a server-approved heal command is not an anti-cheat. Here is what every line does.

### The manifest is just a list of files

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

`fx_version 'cerulean'` is the manifest format version. `cerulean` is the current one, so always use it. `game 'gta5'` says this resource runs on GTA V. The two `*_script` lines tell FiveM where each file runs. `server.lua` runs once on the server machine, `client.lua` runs on every connected player's PC. That split is the whole reason this command is safe. Notice there is no `lua54 'yes'` line. That directive was removed in June 2025. Lua 5.4 is now the only runtime, so the line does nothing and you should not add it.

### server.lua, the part that says yes

```lua
local cooldown = {}
```

This is a Lua table used as a lookup. The key is a player's server id, the value is the last time that player healed. Because it lives in `server.lua`, it exists once on the server, so no player can see it or tamper with it. A client variable would be reset or faked instantly by anyone running a cheat menu. Keeping the rule on the server is the entire point.

```lua
RegisterCommand('heal', function(src)
```

`RegisterCommand` tells FiveM that the text `/heal` should run this function. The first argument the function receives is `src`, the server id of the player who typed the command. On the server this number is trustworthy. The player cannot lie about who they are, because FiveM fills in `src` itself. That is why the heal logic belongs here and not on the client.

```lua
local now = os.time()
if cooldown[src] and now - cooldown[src] < 60 then
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_heal_command:deny', src, 60 - (now - cooldown[src]))
    return
end
```

`os.time()` returns the current time in whole seconds. The `if` reads as: if this player has healed before, and fewer than 60 seconds have passed since then, refuse. When it refuses, it fires `qu_heal_command:deny` back to that one player and passes the seconds remaining, then `return` stops the function so the heal never happens. The second argument to `TriggerClientEvent`, the `src`, is the target. The event goes only to the player who asked, not to everyone.

```lua
    cooldown[src] = now
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_heal_command:heal', src)
    print('[qu_heal_command] approved heal for ' .. GetPlayerName(src))
end, false)
```

If the code reaches here, the request is approved. It stamps the current time into `cooldown[src]` so the next attempt is measured from now, fires `qu_heal_command:heal` to that one player, and prints a server log so you can see the approval. The `false` at the very end is the `restricted` argument of `RegisterCommand`. `false` means any player may run the command. If you passed `true`, only players with an ace permission could run it, which is how you would build an admin only command later.

### client.lua, the part that does the work

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_heal_command:heal', function()
    local ped = PlayerPedId()
    SetEntityHealth(ped, GetEntityMaxHealth(ped))
    print('[qu_heal_command] healed')
end)
```

`RegisterNetEvent` listens for a networked event, here the approval the server just sent. `PlayerPedId()` returns the entity handle of your character, the body the camera follows. `GetEntityMaxHealth(ped)` reads the health ceiling for that ped, and `SetEntityHealth` writes it back, so the player refills to full no matter what the max is. Health natives like these only exist on the client because the ped is a client side entity. The server cannot call `SetEntityHealth` on your ped directly, which is exactly why the work is split. The server approves, the client acts.

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_heal_command:deny', function(seconds)
    print('[qu_heal_command] wait ' .. seconds .. ' seconds')
end)
```

This second listener handles the refusal. It receives the `seconds` the server calculated and prints them to F8. In a finished resource you would replace this print with a chat message or a notification, but as a proof a print is enough to confirm the cooldown path fired.

### The mental model

Picture two rooms. The server room holds the rulebook and never lets players in. The client room is the player's own PC, where the character actually lives. A command is a request slipped under the server door. The server reads its rulebook, the cooldown table, decides yes or no, and slides an answer back. Only on a yes does the client room refill the health. Whenever a script changes something a player should not be able to control, money, health, items, admin powers, the decision lives on the server and the visible effect lives on the client. Events are the slot under the door.

When would you reach for this pattern again? Any time you write a command that grants something. A `/giveweapon`, a `/repair`, a `/revive`, a shop purchase. They all share this shape: the server validates, then the client or the database applies. Learn it once here and you will reuse it for the rest of your scripting.

## Now revive someone else

Your `/heal` always acts on you, the person who typed it, so it never had to ask "who?". A `/revive` is the next rung up: an admin types `/revive 3` and player 3 stands back up. That one change, acting on someone other than the caller, forces two new jobs the heal command never needed. First, the command now carries an argument (the target id) that the player typed, and anything a player types is untrusted, so the server must read it and check it before acting. Second, because reviving another player is a power, not a self-service convenience, the command must be locked to admins. We will add a `/revive` to the same `qu_heal_command` resource and reuse the exact same spine: the server validates, then the client acts.

> **A targeted command is a privilege, not a feature**
> `/heal` healing yourself is harmless. `/revive <id>` lets one player change another player's state, which is grief bait if any player can run it. So this command is registered `restricted` (the third `RegisterCommand` argument is `true`), which means only an identity with the right ace permission may run it. The server console always bypasses the restriction, so you can test from the live console even before you grant yourself the ace.

Add this to the bottom of `server.lua`:

```lua
RegisterCommand('revive', function(src, args)
    local targetId = tonumber(args[1])
    if not targetId then
        print('[qu_heal_command] usage: /revive <id>')
        return
    end

    if GetPlayerName(targetId) == nil then
        print('[qu_heal_command] no player with id ' .. targetId)
        return
    end

    TriggerClientEvent('qu_heal_command:revive', targetId)
    print('[qu_heal_command] revived ' .. GetPlayerName(targetId))
end, true)
```

Add this to the bottom of `client.lua`:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_heal_command:revive', function()
    local ped = PlayerPedId()
    if IsEntityDead(ped) then
        SetEntityHealth(ped, GetEntityMaxHealth(ped))
        ClearPedTasksImmediately(ped)
        print('[qu_heal_command] revived')
    end
end)
```

In the live console (txAdmin Live Console or the FXServer window) type `ensure qu_heal_command` to reload the resource so both new blocks load. Then, from that same server console, type `revive 1` (use your own server id, which `status` will show in the live console). Because the server console bypasses the `restricted` flag, the revive runs without any ace grant.

Expected output:

```
Server: [qu_heal_command] revived yourName
F8: [qu_heal_command] revived
```

If you instead type `/revive 1` in the in-game chat box as a normal player, nothing happens and the server console prints an access-denied line. That is the `restricted` flag doing its job. To let a specific person run it from chat, add an ace in `server.cfg` (read at boot), for example `add_ace identifier.fivem:1234567 command.revive allow`, then `ensure qu_heal_command`.

### Why every new line earns its place

```lua
RegisterCommand('revive', function(src, args)
```

This time the function keeps a second parameter, `args`. FiveM splits whatever the player typed after `/revive` on spaces and hands it over as a Lua table (an array). `/revive 3` gives `args[1] == "3"`. `src` is still the trustworthy server id of the caller, filled in by FiveM, but `args` is raw player text and is trusted by nothing.

```lua
local targetId = tonumber(args[1])
if not targetId then
    print('[qu_heal_command] usage: /revive <id>')
    return
end
```

`args[1]` is always a string, or `nil` if the player typed `/revive` with nothing after it. `tonumber` converts `"3"` to the number `3`, and returns `nil` for anything that is not a number, including the empty case. So this one check catches both "they typed nothing" and "they typed garbage". When `targetId` is `nil` we print the usage hint to the server console and `return`, so the command never proceeds on bad input. This is the validate-at-the-boundary habit: the boundary is the moment untrusted text enters your server code.

```lua
if GetPlayerName(targetId) == nil then
    print('[qu_heal_command] no player with id ' .. targetId)
    return
end
```

A number that parses cleanly can still point at nobody, for example `/revive 99` when no player 99 is connected. `GetPlayerName` returns the player's name for a connected server id and `nil` for an id that is not in the game, so this is a reliable presence test you can trust. Do not use ping for this, an existence check on ping is a guess, not a fact. If the id is empty we print why and `return`, so the revive event is only ever sent to a real, connected target.

```lua
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_heal_command:revive', targetId)
    print('[qu_heal_command] revived ' .. GetPlayerName(targetId))
end, true)
```

Here is the whole point of a targeted command. The first heal command always passed `src` as the target, the caller. This one passes `targetId`, so the event lands on the player who was named, not the admin who typed the command. The trailing `true` is the `restricted` argument, the same slot that held `false` in `/heal`. `true` means an ace permission is required, which is why a state-changing command that affects other players must use it. The print goes to the server console, where your admin actions belong as an audit trail.

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_heal_command:revive', function()
    local ped = PlayerPedId()
    if IsEntityDead(ped) then
        SetEntityHealth(ped, GetEntityMaxHealth(ped))
        ClearPedTasksImmediately(ped)
        print('[qu_heal_command] revived')
    end
end)
```

This runs on the target's machine, because health and ped state are client-side, exactly as in the heal command. The new piece is the guard `IsEntityDead(ped)`: a revive only makes sense on a downed player, so we refill health and then `ClearPedTasksImmediately(ped)` to cancel the death animation and ragdoll so the body actually gets up instead of lying there at full health. `print` here goes to the target's F8 console, not the server console, because client scripts print to F8. If you want the admin to see confirmation, the server-side print above already gives them that.

### Check yourself: Why is the cooldown table in server.lua instead of client.lua?

Because a client-side cooldown can be reset by the player. Keeping it on the server means calls through this command receive a real yes or no. It does not prevent a modified client from healing outside this resource.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `approved heal prints in the server console but F8 shows nothing` -> You are reading the wrong console for that line, or you are not in game. The healed line only appears in the F8 client console. Join the server and run /heal again.
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'cooldown')` -> The local cooldown line is missing or sits below RegisterCommand. Put it on the first line of server.lua so the table exists before the command uses it.
- `Nothing happens and there is no deny message either` -> You ran /heal again within 60 seconds, so the cooldown blocked it, but the deny listener is not loading. Confirm client.lua is listed in fxmanifest.lua, restart, then wait the full minute.
- `Health refills but the server line never prints` -> An older client only heal script is also running and answering /heal first. Stop it, then restart qu_heal_command so only this resource owns the command.
- `Nothing happens at all: no server line, no F8 line, no deny` -> The resource never started. server.cfg is only read at server boot, so adding the ensure line does not start it on an already-running server. In the live console type ensure qu_heal_command (not restart, which only acts on an already-running resource), confirm you see the resource start, then run /heal again from in-game chat.
- `A normal player runs /revive in chat and nothing happens, with an access-denied line in the server console` -> That is the restricted flag working. The command needs an ace. Test from the live server console (it bypasses restrictions) or add_ace command.revive allow for that player's identifier in server.cfg, then ensure qu_heal_command.
- `Server prints 'no player with id N' even though that player is online` -> You passed the wrong number. /revive takes the server id, not a character name or Steam id. Run status in the live console to read the correct server id, then revive that number.
- `The target reaches full health in F8 but stays lying on the ground` -> ClearPedTasksImmediately is missing or ran before SetEntityHealth. The ped is still in its death task. Set health first, then clear tasks, and restart qu_heal_command.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Register a chat command with RegisterCommand and read the trusted src argument it hands you.
- Keep a rule, here a 60 second cooldown, in a server side table players cannot touch.
- Send an approval or a denial to one specific player with TriggerClientEvent and a target src.
- Apply health on the client with PlayerPedId, GetEntityMaxHealth, and SetEntityHealth, and explain why those natives must run client side.
- State the server decides, client acts pattern and name other commands that reuse it.

## Try it yourself

Right now a denied heal only prints to F8, so the player never sees why nothing happened. Make the refusal visible in chat. In `client.lua`, change the deny listener to send a chat message instead of a print:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_heal_command:deny', function(seconds)
    TriggerEvent('chat:addMessage', {
        args = { 'Heal', 'Wait ' .. seconds .. ' more seconds.' }
    })
end)
```

Restart `qu_heal_command`, run `/heal` twice within a minute, and confirm the second attempt shows a chat line counting down instead of healing. Bonus: add a matching `chat:addMessage` to the heal listener so a successful heal confirms itself in chat too.

## References
- RegisterCommand native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x5FA79B0F
- SetEntityHealth native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x6B76DC1F3AE6E6A3
- FiveM events: triggering events: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/working-with-events/triggering-events/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Lesson 20 · Teleport menu · Quasar Academy
ai:
By the end of this lesson you will type `/tpmenu`, click a place name, and watch your character arrive there with a smooth fade. The server approves destinations for this resource and can enforce permissions or write an audit entry. This is not an anti-teleport system: an exploited client can still call client natives directly.

### What this lesson covers
- You will build: qu_teleport_menu: a teleport menu with a server-checked destination list and a smooth fade
- Time: About 45 minutes the first time
- You should know: How to start a FiveM resource, the client and server split, and that ox_lib is installed on your server
- You will not need: Any prior teleport experience. Every term is defined the first time it is used.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- I have ox_lib installed and started on my server.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a teleport menu as a working FiveM resource named `qu_teleport_menu`. It uses three pieces you will meet often: an ox_lib context menu for the UI, a client to server to client round-trip so the server can approve the destination, and a fade so the move looks clean. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Then the next section takes the code apart line by line so you know why it works.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_teleport_menu
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_teleport_menu/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load, and loads ox_lib first._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'ox_lib'
}
```

Two things to know about this manifest. There is no `lua54 'yes'` line: as of June 2025 that directive is deprecated and ignored, because Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime. And `ox_lib` is a Community Ox library, not a built-in Cfx.re resource. You install it yourself and it must be running before this resource, which is what the `dependencies` block enforces.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The menu, the server check, and the fade all exist as runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local allowed = { legion = true, airport = true }

RegisterNetEvent('qu_teleport_menu:request', function(destination)
    local src = source
    if not allowed[destination] then
        print('[qu_teleport_menu] rejected source ' .. src .. ' for ' .. tostring(destination))
        return
    end
    print('[qu_teleport_menu] approved source ' .. src .. ' for ' .. destination)
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_teleport_menu:approved', src, destination)
end)
```

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local spots = {
    legion = vec3(215.76, -810.12, 30.73),
    airport = vec3(-1037.62, -2737.86, 20.17),
}

RegisterCommand('tpmenu', function()
    lib.registerContext({
        id = 'qu_teleport_menu_menu',
        title = 'Teleport',
        options = {
            { title = 'Legion Square', onSelect = function() TriggerServerEvent('qu_teleport_menu:request', 'legion') end },
            { title = 'Airport', onSelect = function() TriggerServerEvent('qu_teleport_menu:request', 'airport') end },
        },
    })
    lib.showContext('qu_teleport_menu_menu')
end, false)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_teleport_menu:approved', function(destination)
    local c = spots[destination]
    if not c then return end
    DoScreenFadeOut(500)
    Wait(600)
    SetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId(), c.x, c.y, c.z, false, false, false, false)
    DoScreenFadeIn(500)
    print('[qu_teleport_menu] moved to ' .. destination)
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line (after the line that ensures ox_lib):

```text
ensure qu_teleport_menu
```

Save, then run this in the server console (txAdmin Live Console or the FXServer console). Because this resource has never started yet, use `ensure`, which starts it if it is stopped (`restart` only works once a resource is already running):

```text
refresh
ensure qu_teleport_menu
```

After this first start, you can use `restart qu_teleport_menu` whenever you edit the code and want to reload it.

Join the server, then run this test in the chat box (press T):

```text
/tpmenu
```

Choose `Legion Square` from the menu. Your screen fades out, you arrive at Legion, and the screen fades back in.

Expected output:

```
F8: [qu_teleport_menu] moved to legion
Server console: [qu_teleport_menu] approved source 1 for legion
```

## How it works

You ran it and you teleported. Now take the code apart so the next time you build a menu-driven action you are deciding, not copying. Three ideas carry this lesson: the ox_lib context menu, the client to server to client round-trip that lets the server approve the move, and the fade timing. We will walk each one.

### The ox_lib context menu

ox_lib is a Community Ox utility library. It is not shipped by Cfx.re, so you install it yourself and start it before any resource that uses it. The `shared_script '@ox_lib/init.lua'` line in the manifest pulls ox_lib's helper table into your resource. From that point on, `lib` is a global you can call. If ox_lib is not started, `lib` is `nil` and every line that touches it errors. That is the most common failure in this lesson.

```lua
lib.registerContext({
    id = 'qu_teleport_menu_menu',
    title = 'Teleport',
    options = {
        { title = 'Legion Square', onSelect = function() TriggerServerEvent('qu_teleport_menu:request', 'legion') end },
        { title = 'Airport', onSelect = function() TriggerServerEvent('qu_teleport_menu:request', 'airport') end },
    },
})
lib.showContext('qu_teleport_menu_menu')
```

A **context menu** is a list of options the player clicks through. `lib.registerContext` describes the menu but does not display it. You hand it a table with an `id` (a unique name you choose so you can refer to it later), a `title` shown at the top, and an `options` array. Each option is its own table with a `title` the player reads and an `onSelect` function that runs when the player clicks that row. `lib.showContext` takes the `id` you registered and actually opens the menu on screen. Registering and showing are two separate calls on purpose: you can register a menu once at startup and show it many times.

Look closely at what `onSelect` does. It does **not** teleport.
