It calls `TriggerServerEvent('qu_teleport_menu:request', 'legion')`, which sends a message to the server saying "this player wants to go to legion." That word, `legion`, is the only thing that crosses the network. The client never decides the coordinates here. That sets up the whole point of the lesson.

### Why the server checks the destination

This round-trip controls what the **resource** will approve. It is useful when a destination requires an ACE, job, item, cooldown, or audit entry. It does not prove that the player's whole client is trustworthy: a modified client can call `SetEntityCoords` or locally trigger a client handler without asking this server event. Protect the valuable server-side result, such as entering a heist state or opening a stash, with its own server check.

```lua
local allowed = { legion = true, airport = true }

RegisterNetEvent('qu_teleport_menu:request', function(destination)
    local src = source
    if not allowed[destination] then
        print('[qu_teleport_menu] rejected source ' .. src .. ' for ' .. tostring(destination))
        return
    end
    print('[qu_teleport_menu] approved source ' .. src .. ' for ' .. destination)
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_teleport_menu:approved', src, destination)
end)
```

`allowed` is an **allow-list**: a table of the only destination names the server will accept. `RegisterNetEvent` registers a handler that runs on the server when a client fires `qu_teleport_menu:request`. Inside a server net event, the global `source` holds the server ID of the player who sent it, so the first line copies it into `src` before anything else can overwrite it. The guard `if not allowed[destination] then ... return end` is the gate: if the player sent a name that is not in the allow-list, the server logs a rejection and returns, and nothing happens. Only when the name passes does the server fire `TriggerClientEvent('qu_teleport_menu:approved', src, destination)`, which sends an approval back to that one player by their `src`.

Notice the shape of the trip. Client to server with `TriggerServerEvent`, then server back to one client with `TriggerClientEvent` plus the target `src`. That out-and-back is the round-trip. A normal client cannot make this resource approve a name outside `allowed`. A modified client can still teleport locally, so never use this table as the only protection for money, items, doors, stashes, or mission state.

### Check yourself: A player edits their client.lua to add a third menu option that sends 'bank_vault' to the server. Do they teleport into the vault?

This resource will not approve `bank_vault`: the server checks `allowed['bank_vault']`, gets `nil`, and returns. That does not stop an exploited client from moving its own ped by another route. Any reward, stash, mission state, or permission at the destination still needs a separate server-side check.

### The fade and the teleport

The approval comes back to the client, and only now does the actual move happen.

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_teleport_menu:approved', function(destination)
    local c = spots[destination]
    if not c then return end
    DoScreenFadeOut(500)
    Wait(600)
    SetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId(), c.x, c.y, c.z, false, false, false, false)
    DoScreenFadeIn(500)
    print('[qu_teleport_menu] moved to ' .. destination)
end)
```

`spots` is the client-side table that maps each approved name to a `vec3`, a value holding x, y, and z coordinates. The server sent the name; the client looks up the coordinates. The guard `if not c then return end` protects against a name with no matching spot.

The four lines after that are a small choreography, and the order matters:

- `DoScreenFadeOut(500)` fades the screen to black over 500 milliseconds. The number is the duration in ms.
- `Wait(600)` pauses this handler for 600 ms. Net event handlers run in a thread that can yield, so `Wait` is allowed here. This number is deliberately larger than the fade. You want the screen fully black **before** you move the player, so they never see the world snap. If you teleport at 400 ms while the fade is still finishing, the player catches a glimpse of the jump. Wait longer than the fade, always.
- `SetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId(), c.x, c.y, c.z, false, false, false, false)` does the move. `PlayerPedId()` returns the handle of the player's own character on this client. The three coordinates are the destination. The four `false` flags are, in order, `alive`, `deadFlag`, `ragdollFlag` (low-level physics flags the engine uses for dead and ragdolling peds; `alive` is effectively unused), and `clearArea` (when true, clears nearby entities at the destination). For a plain on-foot teleport, all four false is fine. (Some third-party docs mislabel the first three as `xAxis/yAxis/zAxis`; FiveM's native reference names them `alive/deadFlag/ragdollFlag`.)
- `DoScreenFadeIn(500)` fades back from black now that the player is standing in the new spot.

So the sequence reads: black out, wait until fully black, move, fade back in. The teleport hides inside the black frame, which is what makes it feel smooth instead of jarring.

### When you reach for this pattern

The client-asks, server-approves, server-answers round-trip is the backbone of almost every action a player can trigger that the server must police. You just built it once; here is where it shows up again:

- **Job and shop menus.** A player clicks "buy the rifle." The client sends the item id, the server checks the player can afford it and is allowed to buy it, then the server grants it. The client never sets its own money.
- **Admin teleports and noclip.** Same menu shape, but the server's allow-list is "is this player an admin" instead of a list of place names.
- **Any menu that grants something.** Garages, stashes, faction doors. The menu is just the UI. The decision always belongs on the server.

When the action is purely cosmetic and local, like opening a settings panel, you can skip the server trip. When it changes authoritative game state, route the request through the server and validate again at the protected action. A server-approved client teleport alone is not a security boundary.

## A second shape: the /tp [number] command

The menu you built constrains the player to options you defined, so the only thing that crosses the network is a name you chose. There is a second teleport shape you will see constantly, especially for quick solo testing and admin tools: a command that takes a typed argument, like `/tp 2`. Here the player types a value you did not pre-approve, so the lesson shifts from "click a safe option" to "parse and validate whatever they typed."

This variant is client-only on purpose. It is a builder/admin convenience, not a player-facing feature, so it skips the server round-trip. That trade is fine for a tool you gate behind a permission, and dangerous if you ever expose it to everyone. We will come back to that.

> **What changes versus the menu**
> The menu's `onSelect` already knew exactly which destination it meant, so there was nothing to parse. A command argument is raw text the player typed. Before you act on it you must turn it into a number and prove it points at a real destination. That parse-and-check step is the whole new idea in this section.

```lua
local teleportLocations = {
    { x = -75.0, y = -819.0, z = 326.0, name = 'Main Rooftop' },
    { x = -428.0, y = 1111.0, z = 327.0, name = 'Vinewood Hills' },
    { x = 402.0, y = -996.0, z = -99.0, name = 'Hidden Bunker' },
}

RegisterCommand('tp', function(source, args)
    local index = tonumber(args[1])
    if not index or not teleportLocations[index] then
        print('[qu_teleport_menu] /tp needs a number 1 to ' .. #teleportLocations)
        return
    end

    local spot = teleportLocations[index]
    DoScreenFadeOut(500)
    Wait(600)
    SetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId(), spot.x, spot.y, spot.z, false, false, false, true)
    DoScreenFadeIn(500)
    print('[qu_teleport_menu] moved to ' .. spot.name)
end, false)
```

Read it argument first. `RegisterCommand` hands your function two things: `source` (the server ID of whoever ran it; on the client this is your own player) and `args`, an array of the words typed after the command. When the player types `/tp 2`, `args[1]` is the **string** `"2"`, not the number `2`. That distinction is the trap. `tonumber(args[1])` converts that string to a number, and returns `nil` if the player typed something that is not a number, like `/tp rooftop`.

The guard `if not index or not teleportLocations[index] then` does two checks in one line. `not index` catches the case where `tonumber` returned `nil` (the player typed nonsense or nothing at all). `not teleportLocations[index]` catches a number that is out of range, like `/tp 9` when there are only three spots: indexing past the end of the array returns `nil`. Either failure prints a usage hint and returns, so an empty or garbage argument never crashes the handler. `#teleportLocations` is the length of the array, so the hint always names the real upper bound even after you add spots.

Notice the last `SetEntityCoords` flag here is `true`, not `false` like in the menu build. That final flag is `clearArea`: with `true` the engine clears nearby entities at the destination, which is handy when you teleport onto a rooftop or into a tight interior and do not want to land inside a parked car. The three flags before it are still `alive`, `deadFlag`, and `ragdollFlag`, unchanged from the menu build.

To try it, add this code to a client file in `qu_teleport_menu`, run `restart qu_teleport_menu` in the **server console** (txAdmin Live Console or the FXServer console), then in the **chat box** in game (press T) type `/tp 1`. Try `/tp 9` and `/tp banana` too, and watch the usage hint print to **F8** instead of the handler breaking.

Expected output:

```
F8 (after /tp 1): [qu_teleport_menu] moved to Main Rooftop
F8 (after /tp 9): [qu_teleport_menu] /tp needs a number 1 to 3
```

> **This shape has no server check**
> Because there is no `TriggerServerEvent` here, the server never sees or approves the move. That is acceptable for a builder tool you gate with a restricted command or an ACE, and unacceptable as a player feature: a player who can run `/tp` can reach any coordinate in your array, and an exploited client can reach any coordinate at all. If you want players to use numbered destinations, send `index` to the server and re-run the allow-list check there, exactly like the menu build did with a name. Convenience commands stay on the client only when you also restrict who can run them.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'lib')` -> ox_lib is not started. Add ensure ox_lib to server.cfg before the line that ensures qu_teleport_menu, then restart. The @ox_lib/init.lua import only works when ox_lib is running first.
- `The menu opens but choosing a place does nothing` -> The destination name in onSelect does not match a key in the server allowed table or the client spots table. Check that 'legion' and 'airport' are spelled the same in all three places.
- `Server console shows rejected source N for ...` -> That destination is not in the server allowed table. This is the server doing its job. Add the name to allowed on the server and to spots on the client if it is a real destination.
- `Screen fades but you do not move, or the world snaps without a clean fade` -> Confirm the Wait after DoScreenFadeOut is larger than the fade duration. With DoScreenFadeOut(500) the wait should be 600 or more so the screen is black before SetEntityCoords runs.
- `You teleport but fall through the map, land in the air, or spawn under the ground at a far-away or high destination` -> The destination's collision and ground have not streamed in yet, so the engine has nothing solid to put you on. Before the final move, request the collision and wait for the real ground height. Replace the bare SetEntityCoords with: first place the ped roughly with SetEntityCoords(ped, x, y, z, false, false, false, true), then RequestCollisionAtCoord(x, y, z), then loop a few frames calling local found, groundZ = GetGroundZFor_3dCoord(x, y, z + 5.0, false) with a Wait(0) each loop until found is true, then SetEntityCoords(ped, x, y, groundZ, false, false, false, true). The +5.0 starts the ground probe slightly above your target so it casts downward onto the surface. Note GetGroundZFor_3dCoord returns two values, the found boolean first and the height second.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Build an ox_lib context menu with lib.registerContext and open it with lib.showContext, and explain that ox_lib is a community library that must be started first.
- Send a player intent from the client to the server with TriggerServerEvent instead of acting locally.
- Check that intent against a server-side allow-list and reply to one player with TriggerClientEvent and their source.
- Explain why the destination is validated on the server and not the client, in terms of trust and authority.
- Sequence DoScreenFadeOut, a Wait longer than the fade, SetEntityCoords, and DoScreenFadeIn so the teleport looks smooth.

## Try it yourself

Add an admin-only destination that the menu offers to everyone but the server refuses unless the player is an admin. Add a third option `Police Armory` to the client menu that sends `armory`, and add `armory` to the client `spots` table with any coordinates you like. On the server, do not put `armory` in the plain `allowed` table. Instead, inside the request handler, check the player's permission before approving it:

```lua
if destination == 'armory' then
    if not IsPlayerAceAllowed(src, 'qu_teleport_menu.armory') then
        print('[qu_teleport_menu] denied armory to source ' .. src)
        return
    end
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_teleport_menu:approved', src, destination)
    return
end
```

Grant yourself the ace in server.cfg with `add_ace identifier.fivem:YOUR_ID qu_teleport_menu.armory allow`, restart, and confirm you reach the armory while a second account without the ace gets the denied line in the server console. You have now built a menu where the same UI gives different power to different players, with the server as the only judge.

## References
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- Community Ox context menu documentation: https://overextended.dev/ox_lib/Modules/Interface/Client/context
- FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/

title: Lesson 21 · SQL from zero: oxmysql · Quasar Academy
ai:
Every script you have built so far forgets everything on restart. Cooldowns, bank balances, notes -- all gone. A database is the fix. It is a separate program that stores rows of data on disk and gives them back when you ask. This lesson teaches you just enough SQL to make your FiveM scripts remember things forever, using oxmysql as the bridge between your Lua and the database.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: A working resource that creates a table, inserts a row with a safe parameterized query, reads it back, and updates it.
- Time: ~35 minutes
- You'll learn: What a database is -> install oxmysql -> CREATE TABLE -> INSERT with ? placeholders -> read one row -> UPDATE, and what .await actually does
- Prereqs: Modules 01-06 complete. A running FiveM server with MariaDB or MySQL installed, and the oxmysql resource downloaded.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console output.
- I have a MariaDB or MySQL database the server can reach.
- I have the oxmysql resource downloaded into resources.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds your first oxmysql table, INSERT, SELECT, and UPDATE as a working FiveM resource named `qu_sql_from_zero`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes the code apart line by line so you know why it works.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_sql_from_zero
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_sql_from_zero/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Point oxmysql at your database
_Goal: oxmysql knows how to reach MariaDB._

oxmysql does not guess your database. You give it a connection string once, in `server.cfg`, before any resource that uses it loads. Add this line near the top of `server.cfg` and fill in your own values:

```text
set mysql_connection_string "mysql://user:password@127.0.0.1:3306/database_name"
```

That single string carries the username, the password, the host and port the database listens on (MariaDB and MySQL default to `3306`), and the name of the database to use. If oxmysql cannot connect, every query in this lesson fails before it runs, so get this line right first. Security note: this line contains your database password in plain text. Never commit `server.cfg` to a public Git repo and never paste it unredacted into Discord or a forum when asking for help -- replace the password with `xxxx` first. If you ever do leak it, change the database password immediately.

### Step 4 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load and in what order._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies { 'oxmysql' }
```

The `@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua` line pulls oxmysql's `MySQL` API into your resource so the names `MySQL.insert`, `MySQL.single`, and `MySQL.update` exist. The `dependencies` line tells FiveM that oxmysql must already be started before this resource starts -- if it is not, your resource refuses to load. It does not start oxmysql for you, so oxmysql still needs its own `ensure oxmysql` line earlier in server.cfg.

### Step 5 - Create the table
_Goal: The database has a place to store rows._

A table is the shape your data lives in. Run this SQL once against your database (in HeidiSQL, phpMyAdmin, Adminer, or the `mysql` command line) before you start the resource:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_sql_from_zero_rows (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL,
  amount INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);
```

### Step 6 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('dbtest', function()
    local id = MySQL.insert.await(
        'INSERT INTO qu_sql_from_zero_rows (name, amount) VALUES (?, ?)',
        { 'lesson', 100 }
    )
    print('[qu_sql_from_zero] inserted row id ' .. id)

    local row = MySQL.single.await(
        'SELECT * FROM qu_sql_from_zero_rows WHERE id = ?',
        { id }
    )
    print('[qu_sql_from_zero] selected ' .. row.name .. ' ' .. row.amount)

    local affected = MySQL.update.await(
        'UPDATE qu_sql_from_zero_rows SET amount = ? WHERE id = ?',
        { 150, id }
    )
    print('[qu_sql_from_zero] updated ' .. affected .. ' row(s)')
end, true)
```

### Step 7 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the txAdmin Live Console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line below your other resources:

    ```text
ensure qu_sql_from_zero
```

    Save. Because this resource is brand new, the server does not know it exists yet. In the txAdmin Live Console, first rescan the resources folder, then start it:

    ```text
refresh
```

    ```text
ensure qu_sql_from_zero
```

    `refresh` makes the new folder available; `ensure` starts it (or restarts it on later runs). Use `restart qu_sql_from_zero` only after it has already been started once -- on a stopped resource `restart` does nothing.

    In the txAdmin Live Console (web panel sidebar -> Live Console) -- the same window where you ran `refresh` and `ensure`, NOT the in-game F8 console -- type:

    ```text
dbtest
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_sql_from_zero] inserted row id 1
[qu_sql_from_zero] selected lesson 100
[qu_sql_from_zero] updated 1 row(s)
```

    The first id is `1` on a fresh table. Run `dbtest` again and the id climbs to `2`, then `3`. That is `AUTO_INCREMENT` at work, and you will see why in the next section.

## How it works

You ran it and three lines appeared, each one a real round trip to the database and back. Now take the code apart so the next time you store data you are deciding, not copying. There are four ideas here: the table you created, the three oxmysql calls, what `.await` does, and why the `?` is the most important character on the page.

### The table you created

Before any Lua runs, the data needs a shape to live in. That shape is the table, and you defined it with `CREATE TABLE`. Read it column by column:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_sql_from_zero_rows (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL,
  amount INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);
```

- `IF NOT EXISTS` means run this once and never error if the table is already there. Safe to leave in.
- `id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` is the row's identity. `INT` is a whole number. `AUTO_INCREMENT` tells the database to fill this in for you, counting up by one with every insert, so the first row is `1`, the next `2`, and you never hand it a value yourself. `PRIMARY KEY` means this column uniquely identifies a row, and no two rows can share an id. This is why your INSERT can hand the new id straight back to you.
- `name VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL` is text up to 80 characters. `VARCHAR(80)` reserves only as much space as the text actually uses, up to that cap. `NOT NULL` means every row must have a name. Try to insert a row without one and the database refuses.
- `amount INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0` is a whole number that must be present, but `DEFAULT 0` means if you do not supply it, the database stores `0` instead of erroring. Defaults are how you give a column a sensible starting value.

### The three oxmysql calls

oxmysql gives you a `MySQL` object with one method per kind of query. You used three:

```lua
local id = MySQL.insert.await(
    'INSERT INTO qu_sql_from_zero_rows (name, amount) VALUES (?, ?)',
    { 'lesson', 100 }
)
```

`MySQL.insert.await` runs an `INSERT` and returns the `AUTO_INCREMENT` id of the row it just created. That is why `id` holds `1` on the first run. You did not type `1` anywhere; the database generated it and oxmysql handed it back. That returned id is the thread that ties the rest of the command together.

```lua
local row = MySQL.single.await(
    'SELECT * FROM qu_sql_from_zero_rows WHERE id = ?',
    { id }
)
```

`MySQL.single.await` runs a `SELECT` and returns exactly one row as a Lua table, or `nil` if nothing matched. Each column becomes a key, so `row.name` is the string `lesson` and `row.amount` is the number `100`. Use `single` when you expect at most one row, like a lookup by id. If you wanted many rows you would reach for `MySQL.query.await`, which returns an array of tables instead.

```lua
local affected = MySQL.update.await(
    'UPDATE qu_sql_from_zero_rows SET amount = ? WHERE id = ?',
    { 150, id }
)
```

`MySQL.update.await` runs an `UPDATE` and returns the number of rows it changed. Here it changes one row, so `affected` is `1`. That number is your proof the write actually landed. If your `WHERE` matched nothing, this would be `0`, which is a fast way to catch an UPDATE that silently did nothing.

### What .await actually does

Look at the `.await` on the end of every call. Without it, oxmysql gives you the result through a callback, a separate function that fires whenever the database replies, which scatters your logic across nested functions. `.await` flips that. A fiber is just FiveM's name for a running task that is allowed to pause and pick back up later. `.await` pauses the current task, lets the database do its work, and resumes your code on the very next line with the result already in hand. Your three queries read top to bottom like plain steps because `.await` is quietly waiting at each one.

This works because your code is running inside a command handler, which FiveM runs in a fiber that is allowed to yield. You do not have to wrap anything in `Citizen.CreateThread` here; `RegisterCommand` already gives you a context where `.await` is legal. The payoff is code you can read in order instead of a callback pyramid. (That trailing `true` is the `restricted` flag: it means a normal player typing `/dbtest` in chat is denied unless they hold the `command.dbtest` ace permission. The server console always bypasses that check, which is why your console test runs -- but keep it in mind before you expose a command to players.)

### Check yourself: The INSERT query has VALUES (?, ?) but no actual values in the SQL string. Where do 'lesson' and 100 come from, and why are they kept out of the query text?

They come from the second argument, the Lua table `{ 'lesson', 100 }`. oxmysql fills the first `?` with `'lesson'` and the second `?` with `100`, in order. They are kept out of the SQL string on purpose. The database receives the query shape and the values down two separate channels, so a value can never be read as SQL. That is a parameterized query, and it is what stops SQL injection. The moment you build a query by gluing user input into the string with `..`, you have opened the door to injection. Always pass values through the params table.

### Why the ? is the most important character here

The `?` placeholders are the entire point of doing SQL the right way. You never paste a value into the query string. You write a `?` where the value goes, then pass the real values as a table in the same order:

```lua
'INSERT INTO qu_sql_from_zero_rows (name, amount) VALUES (?, ?)',
{ 'lesson', 100 }
```

The first `?` becomes `'lesson'`, the second `?` becomes `100`. The database treats the params as data, never as commands, even if a value contains a quote, a semicolon, or a chunk of SQL. Compare that to the dangerous version, where you build the string yourself:

```lua
-- Never do this. The player's name becomes runnable SQL.
MySQL.insert.await("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('" .. playerName .. "')")
```

If `playerName` is something like `'); DROP TABLE users; --`, that string turns into a second command that deletes your table. With `?` placeholders that attack is impossible, because the value is delivered separately from the query. This is the same boundary rule you follow everywhere: validate and parameterize at the edge, never trust raw input inside a query. Make `?` a reflex now and you will never write an injectable query.

### MariaDB, MySQL, and oxmysql in 2026

A note on the pieces. MariaDB and MySQL are two compatible database engines that both speak SQL; in 2026 MariaDB is the one most FiveM servers run and the one txAdmin installs for you, so prefer it. oxmysql is the Lua library that connects your resource to that engine. It is a community resource from Overextended, the same group behind ox_lib and ox_inventory, not an official Cfx.re resource, so you download and `ensure` it like any other resource. It has replaced the older mysql-async and ghmattimysql wrappers as the standard, which is why every modern script you read uses the `MySQL.x.await` style you just wrote.

### When you reach for this pattern

INSERT, read back, UPDATE is the backbone of nearly every persistent feature in FiveM. Three real cases:

- **Player money or stats.** On connect you read the player's row; on a transaction you UPDATE it. The data survives restarts because it lives in the database, not in a Lua table that dies on reload.
- **Logs and records.** A purchase, a ban, a kill feed. Each event is an INSERT, and the returned id gives you a handle to that exact record if you need to amend it later.
- **Config and ownership.** Owned vehicles, houses, gang turf. A SELECT on join hydrates the player's world; an UPDATE saves their changes back. Every one of these is the loop you just built.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_sql_from_zero does not exist` -> Check the folder is inside resources and the ensure line uses the exact same name.
- `You typed dbtest and nothing happened / 'command not registered'` -> The resource never started. A brand-new resource needs `refresh` first, then `ensure qu_sql_from_zero`. `restart` alone does nothing on a resource that was never started.
- `Failed to execute query ... no connection / ECONNREFUSED` -> oxmysql cannot reach the database. Fix the mysql_connection_string in server.cfg: check the user, password, host, port, and that the database name exists and MariaDB is running.
- `Table 'qu_sql_from_zero_rows' doesn't exist` -> You skipped Step 5. Run the CREATE TABLE statement against the same database your connection string points at, then rerun dbtest.
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'MySQL')` -> The @oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua line is missing or oxmysql is not started. Confirm both server_script lines exist and that oxmysql is ensured before this resource.
- `attempt to index a nil value (local 'row')` -> The SELECT matched no row, so MySQL.single returned nil. This usually means the INSERT failed silently. Read the inserted row id print to confirm the insert ran first.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Point oxmysql at MariaDB with a mysql_connection_string in server.cfg.
- Wire @oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua and the dependencies line so the MySQL API exists and loads after oxmysql.
- Read a CREATE TABLE statement and explain AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, VARCHAR, NOT NULL, and DEFAULT.
- Use MySQL.insert.await to write a row and capture the new id it returns.
- Use MySQL.single.await to read one row back as a Lua table, and MySQL.update.await to change it and confirm the affected count.
- Explain why ? placeholders plus a params table stop SQL injection, and why .await lets the three calls read top to bottom.

## Try it yourself

Right now `dbtest` always inserts the same hard coded row. Make it store a real value you pass in. FiveM hands your command handler a table of arguments, so read the first one and insert it as the name, falling back to a default when none is given:

```lua
RegisterCommand('dbtest', function(source, args)
    local name = args[1] or 'lesson'
    local id = MySQL.insert.await(
        'INSERT INTO qu_sql_from_zero_rows (name, amount) VALUES (?, ?)',
        { name, 100 }
    )
    local row = MySQL.single.await(
        'SELECT * FROM qu_sql_from_zero_rows WHERE id = ?',
        { id }
    )
    print('[qu_sql_from_zero] stored ' .. row.name .. ' as id ' .. id)
end, true)
```

Restart `qu_sql_from_zero`, then run `dbtest banker` in the console. Confirm the print reads `stored banker as id N`. Notice the user supplied `banker` still travels through the `?` placeholder and the params table, never glued into the SQL string. You just took untrusted input and stored it the safe way, which is exactly how a real feature reads a player's input and persists it.

## References
- oxmysql documentation (Overextended): https://overextended.dev/oxmysql
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- MariaDB SQL statements reference: https://mariadb.com/kb/en/sql-statements/

title: Lesson 22 · Mini banking, capstone · Quasar Academy

toc.label: Connecting it to real money (ESX and QBCore)

ai:
By the end of this lesson you will run a server-console `/bank` command that changes a practice ledger in MariaDB. This is not yet a framework bank: it does not remove pocket cash on deposit or grant pocket cash on withdrawal. It isolates the database rule so you can prove validation before connecting it to real money.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: qu_bank: one balance table in MariaDB and one server file that runs /bank deposit, withdraw, and balance.
- Time: About 60 minutes if you have never used oxmysql before.
- Prereqs: Lessons 14 (events), 15 (callbacks), and 16 (ox_lib menus).
- Outcome: A server-only ledger demo that rejects invalid amounts and a withdraw larger than the stored balance.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I have oxmysql installed and a MariaDB database connection string set in server.cfg.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds the database half of a server-authoritative bank as a working resource named `qu_bank`. Follow the folder names, file names, SQL, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. The final section also names the framework money and concurrency work still required before this pattern is production-ready.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_bank
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_bank/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

This is a server-only resource. There is no client file and no NUI, so there is no F8 output to read. Every line you see will land in the txAdmin Live Console.

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'oxmysql'
}
```

Notice there is no `lua54 'yes'` line. As of June 2025 that directive is deprecated and ignored, since Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime. The `dependencies { 'oxmysql' }` block is the canonical plural form: it tells the server to start oxmysql before `qu_bank`, so the `@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua` import resolves. You may see the singular `dependency 'oxmysql'` in older code; it is tolerated, but the plural block is the form to learn.

### Step 4 - Create the balance table
_Goal: The database has one row per player, keyed so it cannot duplicate._

Run this SQL in your database before starting the resource. oxmysql works with both MySQL and MariaDB; MariaDB is the most common choice for FiveM servers and benchmarks fastest in oxmysql's own tests:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_bank_accounts (
  citizenid VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
  balance INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (citizenid)
);
```

The table keys on a character id, not an auto-increment row id. QBCore and Qbox call that value `citizenid`; ESX uses its active character `identifier` instead. The primary key makes one ledger row per active character id.

### Step 5 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The /bank command runs deposit, withdraw, and balance on the server._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
-- For the lesson we use one fixed account so you can test from the console.
-- In a real server this comes from the player's framework identity.
local TEST_CITIZEN = 'ABC12345'

-- Read the balance, creating the account at 0 on first touch.
local function getBalance(citizenid)
    local row = MySQL.single.await(
        'SELECT balance FROM qu_bank_accounts WHERE citizenid = ?',
        { citizenid }
    )
    if not row then
        MySQL.insert.await(
            'INSERT INTO qu_bank_accounts (citizenid, balance) VALUES (?, ?)',
            { citizenid, 0 }
        )
        return 0
    end
    return row.balance
end

RegisterCommand('bank', function(source, args)
    local action = args[1]
    local amount = tonumber(args[2])
    local citizenid = TEST_CITIZEN

    if action ~= 'balance' and (not amount or amount <= 0 or amount ~= math.floor(amount)) then
        print('[qu_bank] amount must be a positive whole number')
        return
    end

    local balance = getBalance(citizenid)

    if action == 'balance' then
        print('[qu_bank] balance for ' .. citizenid .. ' is ' .. balance)

    elseif action == 'deposit' then
        local newBalance = balance + amount
        MySQL.update.await(
            'UPDATE qu_bank_accounts SET balance = ? WHERE citizenid = ?',
            { newBalance, citizenid }
        )
        print('[qu_bank] deposited ' .. amount .. ', balance now ' .. newBalance)

    elseif action == 'withdraw' then
        if amount > balance then
            print('[qu_bank] REJECTED withdraw of ' .. amount .. ', balance is only ' .. balance)
            return
        end
        local newBalance = balance - amount
        MySQL.update.await(
            'UPDATE qu_bank_accounts SET balance = ? WHERE citizenid = ?',
            { newBalance, citizenid }
        )
        print('[qu_bank] withdrew ' .. amount .. ', balance now ' .. newBalance)

    else
        print('[qu_bank] usage: bank deposit|withdraw|balance <amount>')
    end
end, true)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the txAdmin console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_bank
```

    Save. If this is the first time you are starting the resource this session, type `ensure qu_bank` in the txAdmin Live Console to start it. After any later edit to the files, use `restart qu_bank` to reload it:

    ```text
ensure qu_bank
```

    Now run these four commands in order, from the txAdmin Live Console:

    ```text
bank balance
bank deposit 100
bank withdraw 250
bank withdraw 40
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_bank] balance for ABC12345 is 0
[qu_bank] deposited 100, balance now 100
[qu_bank] REJECTED withdraw of 250, balance is only 100
[qu_bank] withdrew 40, balance now 60
```

    The third line is the whole point. You asked to withdraw 250 from a balance of 100, and the server said no. The balance never went negative, and the fourth line proves the account still works afterward.

## How it works

You ran it and the practice ledger refused an over-withdraw. Now take the code apart so you understand where the stored truth lives and what must be added before this becomes a real economy feature.

### Why the balance lives only on the server

In FiveM, a `client_script` runs inside every player's own game, on their own machine. A `server_script` runs once, on the box you control. The single most important security rule in the whole platform follows from that split: never trust a number the client sends you, only trust what the server decides.

If the balance lived on the client, a cheater would edit it to a billion and you could not stop them, because they own that machine. So the balance lives in the database, which only the server can read and write. The client is allowed to *ask* for a deposit or a withdraw, but the server is the one that checks the rules and changes the number. That is what "server authority" means, and it is the reason this lesson is the capstone.

In this lesson the restricted command runs from the console, so you are testing the ledger half directly. A finished feature also needs a trusted source for each move. A deposit must remove the same amount from server-held cash, a withdrawal must add the same amount through the framework, and both changes need failure handling. Never expose this practice deposit as an unrestricted player event.

### The reject-if-larger check

```lua
if amount > balance then
    print('[qu_bank] REJECTED withdraw of ' .. amount .. ', balance is only ' .. balance)
    return
end
```

This is the guard that stops the practice ledger going negative. Before the ledger changes, the server compares the requested `amount` against the stored `balance`. If the request is too big, it returns before the `UPDATE`. Production code must additionally make the check-and-update atomic so two simultaneous withdrawals cannot both pass after reading the same old balance.

Notice the order: read the real balance first, then check, then write. If you checked against a number the client sent instead of the number the server read, you would have rebuilt the exact hole you were trying to close. The check is only worth anything because it runs against server-owned data.

### MySQL.single, insert, and update with .await

```lua
local row = MySQL.single.await(
    'SELECT balance FROM qu_bank_accounts WHERE citizenid = ?',
    { citizenid }
)
```

`MySQL` here is the oxmysql library. The maintained 2026 repository is under Community Ox; it is not an official Cfx.re component. The three calls you used each do one job:

- `MySQL.single` returns one row, or `nil` if nothing matched.
- `MySQL.insert` runs an INSERT and returns the new row's auto-increment id.
- `MySQL.update` runs an UPDATE and returns how many rows changed.

The `.await` on the end is the part worth slowing down on. A database query takes time, and you do not want to freeze the whole server while it runs. So oxmysql runs the query in the background. The `.await` suffix says: pause *this* command handler until the result comes back, then hand me the value and continue. The rest of the server keeps running the whole time. Without `.await` you would use a callback instead, passing a function that fires later with the result, and your code would read out of order. `.await` lets you write the query as a straight line and read the result on the very next line, which is far easier to reason about.

One rule comes with `.await`: it only works inside a coroutine. A FiveM command handler already runs inside one, so you are fine here. If you ever call `.await` at the top level of a file, outside any thread or handler, you will get an error, because there is no coroutine to pause. When that happens, wrap the call in `Citizen.CreateThread(function() ... end)`.

The `?` marks in the SQL are placeholders. The real values come from the table in the second argument, like `{ citizenid }`. Always pass values this way. Never glue a player's input straight into the SQL string, or you open the door to SQL injection.

### Why one row per player beats a generic table

```sql
PRIMARY KEY (citizenid)
```

The table keys on `citizenid` and stores exactly one balance per player. Compare that to a generic log table with an auto-increment `id` that just appends a row every time something happens. With the per-player design, reading a balance is one lookup by `citizenid`, and there is physically no way to end up with two conflicting balances for the same person, because the primary key forbids a duplicate. That guarantee is doing real work: it is the database enforcing "one account per player" for you, even if your code has a bug.

This is also why you would reach for an upsert in a fuller version. An upsert only detects duplicates because the character id is unique. This lesson keeps SELECT, INSERT, and UPDATE separate so each operation is visible, but that is not an atomic transfer design. Concurrent writes require a conditional update or transaction/locking strategy, and a two-account transfer must succeed or fail as one unit.

### Check yourself: A player edits their game memory and sends the server a withdraw request for 1,000,000 from an account holding 50. Why do they still get nothing?

Because the amount lives on the client, but the balance and the decision live on the server. The server reads the real balance (50) from its own database, compares the requested 1,000,000 against it, sees the request is larger, prints a rejection, and returns before any UPDATE runs. The player can lie about how much they want, but they cannot touch the number the server reads, so the check always runs against the truth. The only thing the client controls is the request, never the answer.

### When you reach for this pattern

This read, check, then write shape is the backbone of every economy feature you will ever build in FiveM. Three real cases:

- **Any money transfer.** Paychecks, shop purchases, player to player transfers. Each one reads a balance, checks a rule (enough funds, valid target), then writes. Skip the check and you have a money printer.
- **Limited inventory or stock.** A shop with five of an item left does the same dance: read the count, refuse if zero, then decrement. The item count is your balance.
- **Cooldowns and limits.** A heist a player may run once an hour reads the last-run time, checks it is old enough, then writes the new time. Same pattern, different number.

In every one of these, the rule is the same: the server reads its own data, decides, and only then writes. The client asks. It never decides.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_bank does not exist` -> Check the folder is inside resources and the ensure line uses the exact same name, qu_bank.
- `Failed to execute query ... near 'qu_bank_accounts'` -> You started the resource before running the CREATE TABLE SQL. Run the SQL from step 4, then restart qu_bank.
- `No such export oxmysql ... or MySQL is nil` -> oxmysql is not started before qu_bank. Confirm ensure oxmysql comes before ensure qu_bank in server.cfg, and that the dependencies block names oxmysql.
- `Connection refused / unable to connect to the database` -> Your MariaDB connection string is wrong or the server is not running. Check the mysql_connection_string convar in server.cfg points at a live MariaDB instance.
- `amount must be a positive whole number` -> Pass a positive integer, such as bank deposit 100. Zero, negative, fractional, and missing amounts are rejected before any query runs.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain why a balance must live on the server, and why the client may only request a change, never make one.
- Write a reject-if-larger check that reads the real balance first, then refuses an over-withdraw before any write runs.
- Use MySQL.single, MySQL.insert, and MySQL.update with .await, and explain that .await pauses the handler until the query returns and only works inside a coroutine.
- Design a one-row-per-player balance table keyed on citizenid, and say why that primary key prevents duplicate accounts and enables a safe upsert.
- Pass values through ? placeholders instead of concatenating user input into SQL.

## Try it yourself

Add a `transfer` action so a player can move money to another account, with the server enforcing every rule. Extend the command to accept `bank transfer <toCitizenid> <amount>`:

Because the command's opening guard runs `tonumber(args[2])` on every non-`balance` action, a transfer (where `args[2]` is the target id, not a number) is rejected before this branch runs. Add `transfer` to the guard exemption first, the same way `balance` is exempt:

```lua
-- change the top-level guard so transfer is not blocked by the amount check
if action ~= 'balance' and action ~= 'transfer' and (not amount or amount <= 0 or amount ~= math.floor(amount)) then
    print('[qu_bank] amount must be a positive whole number')
    return
end
```

```lua
elseif action == 'transfer' then
    local target = args[2]
    local moveAmount = tonumber(args[3]) or 0
    if moveAmount <= 0 or moveAmount ~= math.floor(moveAmount) then
        print('[qu_bank] transfer amount must be a positive whole number')
        return
    end
    if moveAmount > balance then
        print('[qu_bank] REJECTED transfer of ' .. moveAmount .. ', balance is only ' .. balance)
        return
    end
    local targetBalance = getBalance(target)
    MySQL.update.await('UPDATE qu_bank_accounts SET balance = ? WHERE citizenid = ?', { balance - moveAmount, citizenid })
    MySQL.update.await('UPDATE qu_bank_accounts SET balance = ? WHERE citizenid = ?', { targetBalance + moveAmount, target })
    print('[qu_bank] transferred ' .. moveAmount .. ' to ' .. target)
```

Restart `qu_bank`, run `bank deposit 200`, then `bank transfer XYZ99999 250` and confirm it is rejected, then `bank transfer XYZ99999 50` and confirm both balances move. Notice you added two server-side checks: the amount must be positive, and it cannot exceed the balance. A real transfer also needs the two updates to succeed or fail together as a transaction, which is the next thing to learn after this.

## Connecting it to real money (ESX and QBCore)

The ledger above is deliberately standalone so you could prove the validation rule in isolation. To make `/bank` move a player's **actual** pocket cash and bank balance, you stop storing your own balance and instead read and write the **framework's** money. The server-authority rule does not change: the server reads the real balance, validates, then moves it. Only the storage moves from your table to the framework.

Detect the framework once, then branch. Both blocks below run **server-side** and validate before moving anything:

```lua
-- ESX (es_extended) deposit: cash -> bank, validated on the server
local xPlayer = ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)          -- ESX.Player is the modern accessor
if not xPlayer then return end
local cash = xPlayer.getAccount('money').money     -- 'money' is the cash account in ESX
if amount > 0 and cash >= amount then
    xPlayer.removeAccountMoney('money', amount)
    xPlayer.addAccountMoney('bank', amount)
end
```

```lua
-- QBCore deposit: cash -> bank, validated on the server
local Player = QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)
if not Player then return end
local cash = Player.Functions.GetMoney('cash')     -- QBCore money types: cash, bank, crypto
if amount > 0 and cash >= amount then
    Player.Functions.RemoveMoney('cash', amount, 'bank-deposit')
    Player.Functions.AddMoney('bank', amount, 'bank-deposit')
end
```

The validation is identical to your ledger: positive amount, sufficient balance, decided on the server, never trusting a number the client sent. Withdraw is the mirror image (check `bank >= amount`, then `RemoveMoney('bank', ...)` and `AddMoney('cash', ...)`). The full Quasar University banking project wraps this in a NUI front-end with deposit, withdraw, and transfer, but the rule never changes: **the server is the only place a balance moves.** Reach for a real database table (the pattern above) only when you need balances the framework does not already track.

## References
- Community Ox oxmysql documentation: https://coxdocs.dev/oxmysql
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- FiveM events and server authority: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/working-with-events/triggering-events/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: Lesson 23 · Server-only banking with audit logs · Quasar Academy
ai:
The mini bank proved the server owns the practice balance. This lesson adds an audit record and resolves the connected player to the framework's active character id. It is still a teaching ledger, not a drop-in replacement for framework cash: a deposit is restricted to administrators, and production code must make the balance write and audit insert one atomic transaction.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: qu_audit_bank: two MariaDB tables and one server file where the real player triggers a deposit or withdraw, and every transaction writes an audit row.
- Time: ~50 minutes
- You'll learn: Resolve source to the active character -> validate an intent -> update a practice balance -> append an audit row -> understand the transaction work still required
- Prereqs: SQL from zero (oxmysql, parameterized queries) and the mini bank capstone (server-held balance, reject-if-larger) complete.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I have oxmysql installed and a MariaDB connection string set in server.cfg.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console output.
- I can join my own server so a command has a real player source.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a server-authoritative practice ledger with an audit trail as a working FiveM resource named `qu_audit_bank`. It is server-only: there is no client file and no NUI, so there is no F8 output. Every line lands in the txAdmin Live Console. Follow the folder names, file names, SQL, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line.

> **The two writes are not yet atomic**
> The example writes the balance and then the audit row as two visible steps. A process or database failure between them can leave a changed balance with no audit row, and two simultaneous requests can race after reading the same balance. Use this to learn the data flow. Before production, put the money change and audit record behind a transaction or a serialized account service, and use an atomic balance condition for withdrawals.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_audit_bank
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_audit_bank/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

This is a server-only resource. There is no client file and no NUI, so there is no F8 output to read. You will drive it with commands that you run from your own in-game chat, because a command typed in chat by a connected player carries a real player `source`, and the `source` is the heart of this lesson.

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'oxmysql'
}
```

There is no `lua54 'yes'` line. As of June 2025 that directive is deprecated and ignored, since Lua 5.4 is the only Lua runtime. The `dependencies { 'oxmysql' }` block is the canonical plural form and tells the server to start oxmysql before `qu_audit_bank`, so the `@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua` import resolves.

### Step 4 - Create the two tables
_Goal: The database has one balance per player and one append-only audit log._

A bank with a record needs two tables, not one. You run this SQL once in a database client connected to the same MariaDB database your `mysql_connection_string` in `server.cfg` points at. Use whatever client you have: HeidiSQL or DBeaver on Windows, or the database/SQL tab some panels expose. Open a new query window, paste both statements, and execute. oxmysql speaks to MariaDB, the recommended engine for FiveM in 2026:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_audit_balances (
  citizenid VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
  balance INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (citizenid)
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_audit_log (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  citizenid VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
  source INT NOT NULL,
  action VARCHAR(32) NOT NULL,
  amount INT NOT NULL,
  balance_before INT NOT NULL,
  balance_after INT NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

The balances table keys on `citizenid`: one row per player, and the primary key forbids a duplicate. The audit log is the opposite shape on purpose. It keys on an `AUTO_INCREMENT` id and is append-only, so every transaction adds a new row and nothing is ever overwritten. The `created_at` column fills itself in with the server time on every insert, so you never pass a timestamp by hand.

### Step 5 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: A real player's intent flows through a server event into a balance change plus an audit row._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
-- Resolve the active character id. A server source is only a temporary slot;
-- never use it as the durable account key.
local function citizenIdFor(source)
    if GetResourceState('qbx_core') == 'started' then
        local player = exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(source)
        return player and player.PlayerData.citizenid
    elseif GetResourceState('qb-core') == 'started' then
        local core = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()
        local player = core.Functions.GetPlayer(source)
        return player and player.PlayerData.citizenid
    elseif GetResourceState('es_extended') == 'started' then
        local esx = exports.es_extended:getSharedObject()
        local player = esx.GetPlayerFromId(source)
        return player and player.identifier
    end
end

-- Read the balance, creating the account at 0 on first touch.
local function getBalance(citizenid)
    local row = MySQL.single.await(
        'SELECT balance FROM qu_audit_balances WHERE citizenid = ?',
        { citizenid }
    )
    if not row then
        MySQL.insert.await(
            'INSERT INTO qu_audit_balances (citizenid, balance) VALUES (?, ?)',
            { citizenid, 0 }
        )
        return 0
    end
    return row.balance
end

-- The single trusted path. Every money move on the server goes through here.
-- This is a same-runtime handler fired by AddEventHandler below, so the player
-- is passed in explicitly as `src` (the magic `source` is only populated for a
-- real client -> server net event, which this is not).
AddEventHandler('qu_audit_bank:request', function(src, action, amount)
    local citizenid = citizenIdFor(src)
    if not citizenid then
        print('[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED: no loaded framework character for source ' .. src)
        return
    end

    -- Validate the intent. The client may only ask to deposit or withdraw a
    -- positive whole number. It never sends a balance.
    amount = tonumber(amount)
    if action ~= 'deposit' and action ~= 'withdraw' then return end
    if not amount or amount <= 0 or amount ~= math.floor(amount) then
        print('[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED bad amount from source ' .. src)
        return
    end

    -- This demo has no cash account to debit, so only an administrator may
    -- create ledger value with /deposit. A real ATM would remove framework
    -- cash here and continue only if that server-side removal succeeded.
    if action == 'deposit' and not IsPlayerAceAllowed(src, 'qu_audit_bank.deposit') then
        print('[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED unauthorized deposit from source ' .. src)
        return
    end

    local balanceBefore = getBalance(citizenid)
    local balanceAfter

    if action == 'deposit' then
        balanceAfter = balanceBefore + amount
    else -- withdraw
        if amount > balanceBefore then
            print('[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED withdraw of ' .. amount .. ', balance is only ' .. balanceBefore)
            return
        end
        balanceAfter = balanceBefore - amount
    end

    -- One safe UPDATE applies the change the server computed.
    MySQL.update.await(
        'UPDATE qu_audit_balances SET balance = ? WHERE citizenid = ?',
        { balanceAfter, citizenid }
    )

    -- One INSERT records the transaction. This runs for every action.
    MySQL.insert.await(
        'INSERT INTO qu_audit_log (citizenid, source, action, amount, balance_before, balance_after) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)',
        { citizenid, src, action, amount, balanceBefore, balanceAfter }
    )

    print('[qu_audit_bank] ' .. action .. ' ' .. amount ..
        ' for ' .. citizenid .. ': ' .. balanceBefore .. ' -> ' .. balanceAfter)
end)

-- Thin commands so you can fire the request as a real player from chat.
-- These commands are registered in a server_script, so their handlers run on
-- the server, where RegisterCommand hands you the player's server id as the
-- first argument. We pass that trusted source into the same-runtime event with
-- TriggerEvent (the correct same-runtime call; TriggerServerEvent is client-only).
RegisterCommand('deposit', function(source, args)
    TriggerEvent('qu_audit_bank:request', source, 'deposit', args[1])
end, false)

RegisterCommand('withdraw', function(source, args)
    TriggerEvent('qu_audit_bank:request', source, 'withdraw', args[1])
end, false)

-- Print the last few audit rows so you can read the trail.
RegisterCommand('auditlog', function()
    local rows = MySQL.query.await(
        'SELECT id, citizenid, source, action, amount, balance_before, balance_after FROM qu_audit_log ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 5',
        {}
    )
    for i = #rows, 1, -1 do
        local r = rows[i]
        print('[qu_audit_bank] #' .. r.id .. ' src=' .. r.source ..
            ' ' .. r.action .. ' ' .. r.amount ..
            ' (' .. r.balance_before .. ' -> ' .. r.balance_after .. ')')
    end
end, true)
```

### Step 6 - Start, join, and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the txAdmin Live Console._

Open `server.cfg` and add these lines. The ACE lets txAdmin administrators use the demo deposit; ordinary players cannot mint ledger value:

    ```text
ensure qu_audit_bank
add_ace group.admin qu_audit_bank.deposit allow
```

    Save, then run:

    ```text
restart qu_audit_bank
```

    Join with a loaded framework character and a txAdmin administrator account. Run `deposit` and `withdraw` from your in-game chat box (press T). The handler performs the explicit deposit ACE check. `auditlog` is registered as a restricted command (the `true` you passed to `RegisterCommand`), which means a normal player needs the `command.auditlog` ACE to run it. We never grant that ACE to anyone, so type `auditlog` in the txAdmin Live Console instead: the server console runs as the system principal that is allowed every ACE, so it bypasses the restriction. A player who types `/auditlog` in chat is silently denied, which is exactly what you want for an admin-only command:

    ```text
/deposit 100
/withdraw 250
/withdraw 40
```

    Then in the txAdmin Live Console:

    ```text
auditlog
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_audit_bank] deposit 100 for ABC12345: 0 -> 100
[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED withdraw of 250, balance is only 100
[qu_audit_bank] withdraw 40 for ABC12345: 100 -> 60
[qu_audit_bank] #1 src=1 deposit 100 (0 -> 100)
[qu_audit_bank] #2 src=1 withdraw 40 (100 -> 60)
```

    Your source number and character id will differ from the sample. QBCore/Qbox print the active `citizenid`; ESX prints the active character `identifier`. The rejected withdraw never reaches the transaction log because no balance moved.

## How it works

You ran it as a real player, the server moved your money, and a permanent record appeared. Now take the code apart so you understand the three things that make this different from a number on a screen: where `source` comes from, why the client can only send an intent, and why the audit row is written on the same trip as the balance change.

### source is the server's proof of who asked

```lua
RegisterCommand('deposit', function(source, args)
    TriggerEvent('qu_audit_bank:request', source, 'deposit', args[1])
end, false)

AddEventHandler('qu_audit_bank:request', function(src, action, amount)
    local citizenid = citizenIdFor(src)
```

The mini bank ran from the console, where there is no real player. Here `RegisterCommand` hands the server handler the connection's current server slot as `source`. The client cannot choose a different sender slot for that command, but `source` is not a durable identity: it is reused after disconnects. `citizenIdFor` therefore resolves the slot through the active framework player and returns QBCore/Qbox `citizenid` or the ESX character `identifier`.

Because everything happens in one runtime, the command passes `source` explicitly into the handler with `TriggerEvent`. The same-runtime handler receives it as `src`, resolves the loaded character, and rejects the request if no framework player exists. The player typed the amount; they did not supply the durable character key.

### The client sends an intent, never a balance

```lua
amount = tonumber(amount)
if action ~= 'deposit' and action ~= 'withdraw' then return end
if not amount or amount <= 0 or amount ~= math.floor(amount) then
    print('[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED bad amount from source ' .. src)
    return
end
```

Look at what crosses the wire from the client: an `action` string and an `amount`. That is the entire intent. The client is saying "I would like to deposit 100." It is not saying "my balance is now 100," and it has no way to. The server never reads a balance from the request, so there is nothing the client can lie about that touches the truth.

Because the intent is untrusted, the server validates it at the boundary before acting. The `action` must be exactly `deposit` or `withdraw`. The `amount` must be a number, must be positive, and must be a whole number, so `withdraw -500` (a deposit in disguise), `deposit 0`, and `deposit 10.5` are all refused before any query runs. This is the same edge-validation rule you follow with `?` placeholders: distrust input the moment it arrives, and only let clean values past the gate.

### Read, decide, write, in that order

```lua
local balanceBefore = getBalance(citizenid)
local balanceAfter

if action == 'deposit' then
    balanceAfter = balanceBefore + amount
else -- withdraw
    if amount > balanceBefore then
        print('[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED withdraw of ' .. amount .. ', balance is only ' .. balanceBefore)
        return
    end
    balanceAfter = balanceBefore - amount
end

MySQL.update.await(
    'UPDATE qu_audit_balances SET balance = ? WHERE citizenid = ?',
    { balanceAfter, citizenid }
)
```

The server reads the real `balanceBefore` from its own table, decides the `balanceAfter` itself, then writes it in a single UPDATE. The reject-if-larger check sits between the read and the write, comparing the requested `amount` against the balance the server just read, never against anything the client sent. A withdraw bigger than the balance prints a rejection and returns before the UPDATE, so the balance never goes negative.

Notice the deposit and withdraw both end at the same UPDATE and reuse the same computed `balanceAfter`. That prevents two formulas inside this handler. It does not make the UPDATE and following audit INSERT atomic; the production warning above still applies.

### The audit row is written on every action, on the same trip

```lua
MySQL.insert.await(
    'INSERT INTO qu_audit_log (citizenid, source, action, amount, balance_before, balance_after) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)',
    { citizenid, src, action, amount, balanceBefore, balanceAfter }
)
```

This INSERT is the lesson. Immediately after the balance changes, the server appends one row to the append-only log: who (`citizenid` and the player's `src`, stored in the `source` column), what (`action`), how much (`amount`), and the balance on both sides of the move (`balance_before`, `balance_after`). The `created_at` timestamp fills itself in from `DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`, so you do not pass it.

Two details matter. First, the INSERT runs for every successful action, not just some, so the log is complete: any balance the table holds can be reconstructed by replaying the rows. Second, it sits below both rejection `return`s on purpose. A rejected withdraw never moved money, so it never writes an audit row. The log records transactions, not attempts. If you also wanted to record refused attempts for fraud review, you would add a separate INSERT before each `return`, with its own action like `withdraw_rejected`.

### Check yourself: A player runs /withdraw 1000000 while holding 50. What does the audit log show afterward, and why?

Nothing new. The amount is the only thing the player typed; the identity, the balance, and the decision all live on the server. Because the command is registered in a server_script, `RegisterCommand` hands the handler the player's real server slot as `source`, which it passes into the request. The handler derives the `citizenid` from that trusted `src`, reads the true balance of 50 from its own table, sees the requested 1,000,000 is larger, prints a rejection, and `return`s before either the UPDATE or the audit INSERT runs. No money moved, so no row is logged. The player can type any amount, but they cannot touch the balance the server reads, cannot forge the slot the server assigns, and cannot reach the INSERT. The only thing they control is the request, never the record.

### When you reach for this pattern

A server-owned balance plus an append-only audit log is the shape every economy a server admin will ever have to answer for. Three real cases:

- **Money disputes.** A player claims a shop charged them twice. With an audit log you read the two rows, see the two `balance_before -> balance_after` steps, and settle it in seconds instead of guessing. Without it, you have only the current number and no history.
- **Catching exploits.** A balance that jumps from 200 to nine million between two rows is a hole in some other resource. The audit log is where that jump becomes visible, because every legitimate change leaves a small, explainable step.
- **Reversals and refunds.** Because the log records `balance_before` for every move, you can undo a bad transaction by writing the inverse, and that reversal is itself a logged row. The trail stays honest even when you correct it.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `source is nil or attempt to concatenate a nil value (source)` -> You ran deposit or withdraw from the server console instead of in-game. The console has no player, so RegisterCommand passes source as 0 or nil. Join the server and run the commands from your chat box (press T), so the command callback carries a real player source.
- `The balance changes but qu_audit_log stays empty` -> The audit INSERT is above a return, or you only wired it into one branch. It must run after the UPDATE for both deposit and withdraw, below every rejection return, so every successful move writes a row.
- `Unknown column 'created_at' or it inserts a wrong time` -> You added created_at to the INSERT column list. Leave it out. The DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP on the column fills it in with server time; passing it by hand defeats the point.
- `A player deposits a negative number and gains money` -> Your validation is missing or runs after the math. Check amount > 0 and amount == math.floor(amount) at the top of the handler, before you compute balanceAfter, so withdraw -500 and deposit 10.5 are refused at the boundary.
- `balance_after in the log does not match the stored balance` -> You computed the logged value separately from the value you UPDATE. Compute balanceAfter once, write it in the single UPDATE, and log that same variable, so the row can never disagree with the table.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Register a command in a server_script and read the trusted player source the server hands you in the command callback, then pass it into your handler explicitly.
- Accept only an intent (action plus a bounded, positive, whole amount) from the client, and validate it at the boundary before any query runs.
- Read the real balance, decide the new balance on the server, and apply it in a single safe UPDATE that the client cannot influence.
- Write one append-only audit row for every successful transaction, capturing citizenid, source, action, amount, balance_before, balance_after, and an automatic timestamp.
- Read the audit trail back to settle disputes, spot exploits, and reverse a bad transaction with an honest logged inverse.

## Try it yourself

Right now a rejected withdraw vanishes without a trace, which is exactly the attempt a server admin most wants to see. Add a second audit action that records refused withdrawals, so the log captures attempts as well as transactions. Replace the withdraw rejection branch with a logged version:

```lua
if amount > balanceBefore then
    MySQL.insert.await(
        'INSERT INTO qu_audit_log (citizenid, source, action, amount, balance_before, balance_after) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)',
        { citizenid, src, 'withdraw_rejected', amount, balanceBefore, balanceBefore }
    )
    print('[qu_audit_bank] REJECTED withdraw of ' .. amount .. ', balance is only ' .. balanceBefore)
    return
end
```

Restart `qu_audit_bank`, join the server, run `/deposit 100` then `/withdraw 250`, and then run `auditlog` in the console. Confirm a `withdraw_rejected` row now appears with `balance_before` equal to `balance_after` (no money moved), alongside the real deposit. Notice the rejected row logs the same `src` the server trusts, so a player spamming oversized withdrawals leaves a visible pattern you can act on. You just turned the audit log from a record of money moved into a record of intent, which is what real fraud review needs.

## References
- Community Ox oxmysql documentation: https://coxdocs.dev/oxmysql
- FiveM RegisterCommand and the server-side source argument: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/RegisterCommand/
- FiveM triggering events (TriggerEvent is the same-runtime call; TriggerServerEvent is client-only): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/working-with-events/triggering-events/
- MariaDB CURRENT_TIMESTAMP and TIMESTAMP: https://mariadb.com/kb/en/timestamp/
- Qbox qbx_core server exports (GetPlayer, PlayerData.citizenid): https://docs.qbox.re/resources/qbx_core/exports/server
- QBCore PlayerData (citizenid): https://docs.qbcore.org/qbcore-documentation/qb-core/player-data
- ESX server xPlayer (identifier) and GetPlayerFromId: https://docs.esx-framework.org/en/esx_core/es_extended/server/xplayer

title: Bonus · Convars: server.cfg config from your scripts · Quasar Academy
ai:
A **convar** (configuration variable) is a key/value pair you set in `server.cfg` or at runtime, then read from Lua. Convars are how you externalize the things that *change per server*: a debug flag, a database name, a starting balance, an owner ID. You tune them without editing Lua and without rebuilding the resource. The catch that trips up everyone is replication: the prefix you use to declare a convar decides whether a client can ever read it. Get that wrong and your client silently reads the default forever. This lesson makes the difference visible.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: The three convar prefixes (set, setr, sets), exactly who can read each, the four read functions, and why a client can only see a setr value.
- You'll ship: A debug toggle and a configurable starting balance for the banking script: both flippable from server.cfg with no code redeploy.
- Time: ~15 minutes
- Why now: You finished the banking lesson with a hard-coded starting balance. Convars let you tune that per server.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds server.cfg convars read from both server and client Lua, as a working FiveM resource named `qu_convars_server_cfg`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart so you understand why a client can read one convar but not the other.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_convars_server_cfg
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_convars_server_cfg/
 fxmanifest.lua
 server.lua
 client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Declare the convars in server.cfg
_Goal: The server has two config values it can read._

Open `server.cfg` and add these two lines **above** the line that ensures this resource. You will add the `ensure` line in Step 6.

`server.cfg` is NOT inside the `resources` folder. It lives in your server's base data directory, the same folder you launch FXServer from, one level above `resources`. (If you set the server up with txAdmin, you can also open it from the txAdmin **Settings** page.)

```text
set qu_convars_cash 750
setr qu_convars_debug true
```

The two prefixes are not interchangeable. `set` is server-only. `setr` is replicated, which means it is also sent to every client. You will see in a moment why the client half of this lesson reads the `setr` one and not the `set` one.

### Step 5 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('cashcfg', function()
 local cash = GetConvarInt('qu_convars_cash', 500)
 print('[qu_convars_server_cfg] server reads cash ' .. cash)
end, false)
```

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('debugcfg', function()
 local debug = GetConvar('qu_convars_debug', 'false')
 print('[qu_convars_server_cfg] client reads debug ' .. debug)
end, false)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Add this line to `server.cfg`, below the two convar lines from Step 4:

 ```text
ensure qu_convars_server_cfg
```

 Save, then restart the whole server so FXServer re-reads `server.cfg`. txAdmin is the web control panel that manages your server; open it in a browser at `http://<your-server-ip>:40120` (use `http://localhost:40120` if you are on the machine running the server) and log in. In the left sidebar open the txAdmin dashboard and click **Restart** (the server restart action, not a single-resource restart). A resource restart alone does NOT re-execute changed `server.cfg` lines, so the new convar values would not load.

 Run the first test in the txAdmin Live Console:

 ```text
cashcfg
```

 Join the server. While you are in-game, press the **F8** key to open the FiveM developer console (a text overlay that drops down over the game). This is where client-side `print` output appears and where you type client commands. With it open, run the second test there:

 ```text
debugcfg
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_convars_server_cfg] server reads cash 750
F8: [qu_convars_server_cfg] client reads debug true
```

## How it works

It worked, so now make it make sense. Two convars, two read functions, two consoles. The reason the lesson is split that way, and not dumped into one file, is the single most important fact about convars: the prefix you declared each one with decided whether the client could ever see it.

### The three prefixes, and who can read each

When you write a convar in `server.cfg` you choose one of three commands. They look almost identical and they behave completely differently.

- `set name value` declares a **server-only** convar. The server can read it. Clients cannot. If a client tries, it gets the fallback default you passed, not the real value. This is the right choice for anything a player should never see: database strings, API keys, owner IDs.
- `setr name value` declares a **replicated** convar. The `r` is for replicated. The server reads it, and the value is also pushed to every connected client, so client Lua can read the real value too. Use this only for config that is safe for players to know and that the client genuinely needs, like a debug flag that turns on extra on-screen logging.
- `sets name value` declares a **server-info convar** exposed in the server's public info, such as `sets tags "roleplay, economy"`. Treat every `sets` value as public and never place a secret in it. Use `set`/`setr` for application configuration so the intended visibility is obvious.

That is the whole rule. `set` stays on the server, `setr` rides along to clients, `sets` is for the browser.
