In this lesson `qu_convars_cash` is a `set` because the starting balance is a server decision a player has no business reading, and `qu_convars_debug` is a `setr` because the client console needs to know whether debug logging is on.

### Check yourself: If you change qu_convars_debug from setr to set and restart, what does the F8 line print, and why?

It prints `client reads debug false`. With plain `set`, the convar is server-only and never replicates to clients, so on the client side `GetConvar('qu_convars_debug', 'false')` cannot find a value and returns the fallback you supplied, the string `'false'`. The server console would still read the real value, but the client is permanently blind to a non-replicated convar. This is the number one convar bug: a value that "works on the server" but silently reads the default on every client because it was declared with `set` instead of `setr`.

### The four read functions

You read a convar with one of four natives. They differ only in the type they return, and every one of them takes the same two arguments: the convar name, and a fallback default to use when the convar is missing or unreadable.

- `GetConvar(name, default)` returns a **string**. Everything is a string at heart in `server.cfg`, so this is the most general reader.
- `GetConvarInt(name, default)` returns an **integer**. It parses the stored text into a whole number for you, which is why the server side of this lesson uses it on `qu_convars_cash`: a balance is a number, not text.
- `GetConvarFloat(name, default)` returns a **floating-point number**, for decimals like a price multiplier.
- `GetConvarBool(name, default)` returns a **boolean** `true` or `false`.

The default argument is not optional decoration. It is your safety net. If the convar line is missing from `server.cfg`, misspelled, or (the case you met) declared with the wrong prefix so the client cannot see it, the read returns the default instead of crashing. That is exactly why the F8 line prints `false` when the convar does not reach the client: `'false'` is the default you handed `GetConvar`.

### The restricted flag on the command

Both commands here end with `false`:

```lua
RegisterCommand('cashcfg', function()
 -- ...
end, false)
```

That last argument is the **restricted flag**. With `false`, anyone can run the command, including the server console and any connected player. With `true`, the command requires an ace permission, so only the server console (which holds all aces) and explicitly authorized admins can run it. The original version of this lesson used `true` on the server command, which is why a beginner typing it from anywhere but the console got silence. For a teaching command you want to fire freely while learning, `false` is correct. When you later wrap a real admin action like granting cash, you would switch to `true` and grant the ace, so players cannot run it on themselves.

### Runtime versus server.cfg

One more model to lock in. Editing the `server.cfg` **file** does not execute the changed line on a running server. Perform a full server restart, or deliberately re-run the relevant cfg with `exec`, before testing the new value. Restarting only the resource does not re-read `server.cfg`. The Lua `GetConvar*` calls read the current convar value each time they execute. You can also set a convar live from the server console:

```text
setr qu_convars_debug false
```

A live `set`/`setr` takes effect immediately, no restart needed, and a `setr` change is pushed to connected clients right away. The cfg line is the value the convar starts at on boot.

### When you reach for this pattern

Convars are how you stop hard-coding the things that differ between your test server and a live server. Three real cases:

- **A debug switch.** Wrap your noisy `print` calls in `if GetConvarBool('myresource_debug', false) then`. Flip `setr myresource_debug true` while you investigate, flip it off in production, and you never ship a build to change logging.
- **Per-server tuning.** Starting balance, max stack size, payout rates. One resource, many servers, each with its own numbers in its own `server.cfg`.
- **Secrets that must stay server-side.** A Tebex secret, a Discord webhook, a database connection string. Always a plain `set`, never `setr`. A `setr` convar is copied to every connected client, where the value lives in client memory and can be read or extracted by any player, so declaring a secret with `setr` is a real credential leak, not a cosmetic one. Keep secrets as `set` so they never leave the server.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Server reads cash 500 instead of 750` -> The convar is missing or misspelled, or you edited server.cfg without re-executing it. Fix the line, then fully restart the server (or deliberately exec the cfg) before testing again.
- `F8 client reads debug false (not true)` -> The convar was declared with set instead of setr, or the edited cfg was never re-executed. Use setr, then fully restart the server or exec the cfg.
- `debugcfg prints nothing in F8` -> Client convars are read on the client, so you must join the server and run debugcfg in the F8 console, not in txAdmin. txAdmin shows server output only.
- `cashcfg prints nothing` -> Run cashcfg in the txAdmin Live Console. If you had left the restricted flag as true, only the console and authorized aces could run it; this lesson uses false so it runs anywhere.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Name the three convar prefixes and say who can read each: set is server-only, setr replicates to clients, sets is the server-browser info string.
- Explain why a client reading a plain set convar silently gets the default, and that setr is required for any value client Lua must see.
- Pick the right read function by return type: GetConvar (string), GetConvarInt (int), GetConvarFloat (float), GetConvarBool (bool), each with a fallback default.
- Declare config in server.cfg and read it from Lua so a value changes per server with no code redeploy.
- Explain that editing a cfg file does not execute it; restart the server or exec the cfg, while a live set in the server console applies immediately.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| ConVar change doesn't take effect after restart | The resource sets the value in code after startup, overwriting server.cfg | Resources can call `SetConvar()` at runtime. server.cfg sets the initial value, but code wins if it runs after |
| `set` vs `setr` vs `sets` confusion | Each does something different | `set` = server-only value, `setr` = replicated to all clients, `sets` = publicly exposed (server browser and info.json). Never put secrets in `sets` |
| License key in a public GitHub repo | You committed server.cfg with the key in it | Rotate the key immediately at portal.cfx.re. Add server.cfg to .gitignore |
| ConVar value won't change no matter what you do | It's a restricted engine ConVar (like `sv_licenseKey`) | Engine-protected ConVars can only be set in server.cfg at startup. They cannot be changed at runtime |

## Try it yourself

Add a third convar and prove the replication rule with your own hands. Put a server-only secret next to the existing lines in `server.cfg`:

```text
set qu_convars_secret supersecret
```

Now try to read it from **both** sides. Add to `server.lua`:

```lua
RegisterCommand('secretcfg_server', function()
print('[qu_convars_server_cfg] server secret ' .. GetConvar('qu_convars_secret', 'hidden'))
end, false)
```

And add the matching client command to `client.lua`:

```lua
RegisterCommand('secretcfg_client', function()
print('[qu_convars_server_cfg] client secret ' .. GetConvar('qu_convars_secret', 'hidden'))
end, false)
```

Fully restart the server so it re-reads `server.cfg`. Run `secretcfg_server` in txAdmin and `secretcfg_client` in F8. The server prints `server secret supersecret`. The client prints `client secret hidden`, because a plain `set` convar never crosses to the client and the read falls back to your default. You have demonstrated, in your own console, why every secret stays a `set` and never a `setr`.

## References
- FiveM convars reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/convars/
- FiveM server.cfg / setting up a server: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-server/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/

title: Lesson 18 · ox_target interactions · Quasar Academy
ai:
Most FiveM servers have things players can interact with: NPCs, doors, trunks, registers, ATMs. Before `ox_target`, building those interactions meant lots of repeated distance checks and prompt code. With `ox_target`, most of that becomes one small options table. `ox_target` is an Overextended community resource, the successor to qtarget and bt-target, not an official Cfx.re resource. This lesson teaches its one core pattern slowly, then uses it three different ways.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: addModel for peds and props -> addGlobalPlayer for other players -> addBoxZone for world regions -> canInteract -> the shared option schema
- Time: ~25 minutes
- Prereqs: Lesson 16 (ox_lib) and Lesson 17 (Qbox). ox_target installed after ox_lib, with OneSync enabled.
- Outcome: A merchant ped, an ATM zone, and a server-validated $100 transfer request on other players, all using the same target system.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- I have ox_lib and ox_target installed, with ox_lib starting before ox_target.
- My server uses OneSync, so the server can verify both players are nearby.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds three interactions in one working FiveM resource named `qu_ox_target_interactions`: a box zone, a merchant ped, and a give cash option on every other player. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes the options table apart field by field so you know why it works.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_ox_target_interactions
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout. This lesson has a client file (the interactions live where the player is) and a server file (giving cash is a server decision):

```text
resources/qu_ox_target_interactions/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load and what to wait for._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'ox_lib',
    'ox_target',
    '/onesync'
}
```

The canonical manifest key is the plural `dependencies { ... }` with a table. The singular `dependency 'ox_target'` is tolerated by FiveM, but the plural form is the form you should write and the form you will see in real resources. We list both `ox_lib` and `ox_target` because `ox_target` itself depends on `ox_lib` being running first. Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored, since Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the client code
_Goal: Three interactions register the moment the resource starts._

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
-- 1. A box zone over the ATM spot at Legion Square.
exports.ox_target:addBoxZone({
    coords = vec3(215.76, -810.12, 30.73),
    size = vec3(2.0, 2.0, 2.0),
    rotation = 0.0,
    debug = true,
    options = {
        {
            name = 'qu_atm_use',
            icon = 'fa-solid fa-money-bill',
            label = 'Use ATM',
            distance = 2.0,
            canInteract = function(entity, distance)
                return distance <= 2.0
            end,
            onSelect = function()
                print('[qu_ox_target_interactions] ATM used')
            end
        }
    }
})

-- 2. A merchant ped: any ped using this model gets a Talk option.
exports.ox_target:addModel('a_m_y_business_03', {
    {
        name = 'qu_merchant_talk',
        icon = 'fa-solid fa-comment',
        label = 'Talk to merchant',
        distance = 2.5,
        onSelect = function()
            print('[qu_ox_target_interactions] merchant talked to')
        end
    }
})

-- 3. A give cash option on every OTHER player ped.
exports.ox_target:addGlobalPlayer({
    {
        name = 'qu_give_cash',
        icon = 'fa-solid fa-hand-holding-dollar',
        label = 'Give $100',
        distance = 2.0,
        onSelect = function(data)
            local serverId = GetPlayerServerId(NetworkGetPlayerIndexFromPed(data.entity))
            TriggerServerEvent('qu_ox_target_interactions:giveCash', serverId)
        end
    }
})
```

### Step 5 - Write the server code
_Goal: The server fixes the amount, validates the target and distance, and rate-limits the request._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local TRANSFER_AMOUNT = 100
local MAX_DISTANCE = 3.0
local lastRequestAt = {}

RegisterNetEvent('qu_ox_target_interactions:giveCash', function(targetId)
    local fromId = source
    targetId = tonumber(targetId)

    if not targetId or targetId == fromId or not GetPlayerName(targetId) then
        print(('[qu_ox_target_interactions] rejected invalid target from %s'):format(fromId))
        return
    end

    local now = os.time()
    if now - (lastRequestAt[fromId] or 0) < 1 then
        print(('[qu_ox_target_interactions] rate-limited %s'):format(fromId))
        return
    end
    lastRequestAt[fromId] = now

    local fromPed = GetPlayerPed(fromId)
    local targetPed = GetPlayerPed(targetId)
    if fromPed == 0 or targetPed == 0 then return end

    local distance = #(GetEntityCoords(fromPed) - GetEntityCoords(targetPed))
    if distance > MAX_DISTANCE then
        print(('[qu_ox_target_interactions] rejected distant target from %s'):format(fromId))
        return
    end

    -- Proof only. A real transfer must also read the sender's balance on the
    -- server and use the installed framework's server-side money functions.
    print(('[qu_ox_target_interactions] validated %s -> %s for $%s'):format(
        fromId, targetId, TRANSFER_AMOUNT
    ))
end)

AddEventHandler('playerDropped', function()
    lastRequestAt[source] = nil
end)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct consoles._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line. It must come after the lines that ensure `ox_lib` and `ox_target`:

```text
ensure qu_ox_target_interactions
```

Save, then run:

```text
restart qu_ox_target_interactions
```

Join the server. You will see a translucent debug box floating at the ATM spot. Walk into it, aim at it, and select Use ATM. Then aim at any other player nearby and select Give $100.

Expected output:

```
F8: [qu_ox_target_interactions] ATM used
txAdmin: [qu_ox_target_interactions] validated 1 -> 2 for $100
```

The ATM line prints in F8 because `onSelect` runs on the client. The give cash line prints in the server console (and txAdmin's Live Console mirrors it) because that work was sent to the server. FiveM also forwards server prints back to the triggering player's F8, so you may see the give cash line there too. The numbers `1` and `2` are server ids, so they will match whoever is actually connected.

## How it works

You selected an option and the right line appeared in the right console. Now take the code apart so the next time you wire an interaction you are deciding, not copying. The whole system rests on one idea: every `add*` function takes the same shape of option, and the option describes what the player sees and what happens when they pick it.

### The shared option schema

This is the table you will write hundreds of times. Every entry inside `options` supports the same fields, no matter which `add*` function you handed it to:

- `name` is a unique id string for this option. It must be unique across your whole server, which is why we prefix ours with the resource name (`qu_atm_use`, not `use`). If two resources register the same `name`, the second one silently overwrites the first and you lose an interaction with no error.
- `icon` is a Font Awesome class string, like `fa-solid fa-money-bill`. ox_target ships Font Awesome, so you pick from their free icon set. A wrong or misspelled class shows a blank square instead of an icon, which is the number one reason a new option looks broken.
- `label` is the text the player reads in the eye menu, like `Use ATM`. This is the prompt, the human-facing half of the option.
- `distance` is the maximum range in metres at which the option can be selected. ox_target uses it as a first cheap filter before it ever calls your `canInteract`.
- `canInteract(entity, distance)` is an optional function that returns a boolean. ox_target calls it to decide whether to show the option at all. Return `true` to show it, `false` to hide it. `entity` is the thing being aimed at and `distance` is how far the player is from it. This is where gating lives: only show Open till if the player is a cashier, only show Lockpick if the door is locked.
- `onSelect` is the function that runs when the player actually clicks the option. For a model or a player option it receives a `data` table whose `data.entity` is the entity that was targeted. This is the payload half of the option.

### Check yourself: The same options table works for a box zone, a model, and every other player. What is the one field that behaves differently depending on which add* function you used?

`onSelect` (and `canInteract`). For `addBoxZone` there is no specific entity, so `data.entity` is not meaningful and you usually ignore the argument. For `addModel` and `addGlobalPlayer` the player is aiming at a real entity, so ox_target hands you a `data` table and `data.entity` is the ped or prop they targeted. That entity is how you know which player to give cash to in option three. The fields are the same shape everywhere, but what the entity means depends on what you attached the option to.

### addBoxZone: an interaction tied to a place

```lua
exports.ox_target:addBoxZone({
    coords = vec3(215.76, -810.12, 30.73),
    size = vec3(2.0, 2.0, 2.0),
    rotation = 0.0,
    debug = true,
    options = { ... }
})
```

A box zone is an invisible 3D box sitting in the world. When the player aims inside it, the options appear. The box is defined by three things:

- `coords` is the centre point of the box, a `vec3` of world coordinates `(x, y, z)`. `vec3` is a built-in FiveM Lua global (the CfxLua runtime ships it); `vector3` is the same value, and the two are interchangeable. `vec3` is the shorter form Overextended resources tend to use.
- `size` is a `vec3` of the box dimensions in metres along each axis: width, depth, height. `vec3(2.0, 2.0, 2.0)` is a two metre cube. A flat counter would be wide and shallow, like `vec3(2.0, 0.6, 1.2)`.
- `rotation` spins the box around its vertical axis in degrees. `0.0` leaves it aligned to the world. You set this when the thing you are wrapping, a counter or a desk, sits at an angle.

The field that makes placement possible is `debug = true`. With debug on, ox_target draws the box as a translucent shape you can see in game, so you can stand at the spot, read whether the box actually covers the ATM, and nudge the coords. Once it lines up, you set `debug = false` (or delete the line) for production, and the box becomes invisible while still working. Shipping with debug on is a common mistake that leaves coloured boxes all over a live server.

### addModel: an interaction tied to a kind of thing

```lua
exports.ox_target:addModel('a_m_y_business_03', {
    { name = 'qu_merchant_talk', ... }
})
```

`addModel` attaches options to a model rather than a place. Every entity that uses model `a_m_y_business_03`, anywhere on the map, gets the Talk to merchant option. This is how you make a merchant ped: spawn that ped model somewhere, and because it shares the model, it inherits the interaction automatically. Note the argument order is different from `addBoxZone`: the model name comes first, then the options table comes second as its own argument, not wrapped inside a single options key. You can pass one model string or a table of model names to cover several at once.

### addGlobalPlayer: an interaction tied to every other player

```lua
exports.ox_target:addGlobalPlayer({
    {
        name = 'qu_give_cash',
        onSelect = function(data)
            local serverId = GetPlayerServerId(NetworkGetPlayerIndexFromPed(data.entity))
            TriggerServerEvent('qu_ox_target_interactions:giveCash', serverId)
        end
    }
})
```

`addGlobalPlayer` attaches options to the ped of every other player, with no entity or model to name. This is the line that delivers the give cash outcome. The interesting work is in `onSelect`, and it is where the lesson becomes correct rather than convenient.

When you aim at a player, `data.entity` is their ped, which is a local game handle that means nothing to the server. The server thinks in server ids, not ped handles. (One caveat: this is only true inside a client `onSelect` like ours. If you instead point an option at a `serverEvent`, ox_target replaces `data.entity` with the entity's network id, so the ped-to-index conversion below would no longer apply.) So you translate: `NetworkGetPlayerIndexFromPed` turns the ped into a local player index, and `GetPlayerServerId` turns that index into the server id the server understands. Giving someone cash is a server decision. The client therefore sends only the selected target id. It does not choose the amount, because a modified client could send any number it wanted.

On the server, `RegisterNetEvent` receives that untrusted request. The `source` global is the player who sent it. The handler rejects a missing, disconnected, or self target, limits each sender to one request per second, and uses server-side coordinates to reject players more than three metres apart. The amount is the server constant `TRANSFER_AMOUNT`, never client input. This lesson deliberately stops at a validated print: it does not pretend money moved. A real implementation must next read the sender's balance on the server and call the installed framework's server-side debit and credit functions as one controlled operation.

### Check yourself: Why does the give cash logic run on the server instead of finishing inside the client onSelect?

Because money is authoritative state, and the client cannot be trusted with it. A client `onSelect` runs on the player's machine, which can be modified. The client may identify who was selected, but the server owns the amount, checks the target and distance, rate-limits the request, verifies the sender's balance, and applies the transfer. Moving code into a server event is not sufficient by itself; the checks inside that handler are what make the boundary useful.

### addEntity: an interaction tied to one specific spawned entity

`addBoxZone` ties an option to a *place*, and `addModel` ties it to a *kind of thing*. `addEntity` is the third axis: it ties an option to **one exact entity you already have a handle to**, and no other. Use it when you spawned a single ped, vehicle, or prop in code and want the interaction to live only on that one object, not on every other entity that happens to share its model.

```lua
-- You spawned this prop yourself and kept its handle in `myAtm`.
local netId = NetworkGetNetworkIdFromEntity(myAtm)
exports.ox_target:addEntity(netId, {
    {
        name = 'qu_single_atm',
        icon = 'fa-solid fa-credit-card',
        label = 'Use this ATM',
        distance = 2.0,
        onSelect = function(data)
            print('[qu_ox_target_interactions] specific ATM used')
        end
    }
})
```

The first argument is **not** the local entity handle. It is the network id, produced by `NetworkGetNetworkIdFromEntity(entity)`. The reason is that a raw entity handle is a number that is only valid on the machine that created it and only for as long as that entity exists, while the network id is the shared identifier every client and the server agree on. Passing the network id is what lets ox_target track the right object even as it streams in and out around the player. If you pass the bare handle some older guides show, the option will attach to the wrong entity or to nothing. The entity must already exist when you call `addEntity`; if you spawn it a frame later, register the option in the same code path right after the spawn returns a valid handle.

### addGlobalOption: an option with no target at all

There is a fourth registrar that is easy to confuse with the `addGlobalPlayer` you built in step four. `addGlobalPlayer` puts an option on *every other player's ped*. `addGlobalOption` puts an option that is **always available no matter what the player is aiming at**, with no entity behind it at all.

```lua
exports.ox_target:addGlobalOption({
    {
        name = 'qu_request_help',
        icon = 'fa-solid fa-hands-helping',
        label = 'Request help',
        onSelect = function()
            print('[qu_ox_target_interactions] help requested')
        end
    }
})
```

Because there is no entity, `data.entity` is not meaningful here, exactly like `addBoxZone`. Reach for `addGlobalOption` only for actions that genuinely make sense everywhere, like opening a personal menu or calling EMS, because every global option you add appears on *every* target the player ever aims at and clutters the eye menu fast. A common mistake is using `addGlobalOption` when you meant `addGlobalPlayer`: the first shows your option on walls, vehicles, and the sky; the second shows it only when aiming at another player.

### A round box is a sphere zone

The ATM box in step four is a `addBoxZone`, but ox_target also ships `addSphereZone` for round areas, which is simpler when you do not care about rotation or exact edges. It takes a `radius` instead of a `size` and `rotation`:

```lua
exports.ox_target:addSphereZone({
    coords = vec3(215.76, -810.12, 30.73),
    radius = 1.5,
    debug = true,
    options = { --[[ same option shape as the box ]] }
})
```

Everything else, the `options` table and the shared field schema, is identical to the box. Pick a box when you are wrapping something rectangular like a counter and want it aligned, and a sphere when a simple radius around a point is enough.

### Check yourself: You spawn two identical clerk peds with model a_m_y_business_03, but only the one behind the counter should offer 'Open till'. addModel would put the option on both. Which registrar do you use, and what do you pass it?

`addEntity`, passing the network id of just the counter clerk: `exports.ox_target:addEntity(NetworkGetNetworkIdFromEntity(counterPed), { ... })`. `addModel` attaches by model, so it cannot tell two peds of the same model apart, both would get the option. `addEntity` attaches to one exact entity, identified by its network id rather than its local handle, so only the ped you name gets it. You would still use `addModel` for an option that *should* appear on every clerk of that model, like 'Talk'.

### Cleaning up: every add\* has a remove\*, and you owe the server one on stop

The options you register do not vanish when your resource stops or restarts. ox_target keeps them until something removes them. That matters because each `add*` has a matching `remove*`, and forgetting to call it on shutdown is the quiet cause of duplicate options after a restart.

```lua
-- Each registrar has a mirror:
exports.ox_target:removeGlobalOption('qu_request_help')
exports.ox_target:removeModel('a_m_y_business_03', 'qu_merchant_talk')
exports.ox_target:removeZone(zoneId)   -- zoneId is what addBoxZone/addSphereZone returned
exports.ox_target:removeEntity(netId, 'qu_single_atm')
```

The field that makes zone removal possible is the return value: `addBoxZone` and `addSphereZone` hand back a zone id. Capture it (`local atmZone = exports.ox_target:addBoxZone({ ... })`) if you ever intend to remove that zone, because there is no other handle to it afterwards.

The clean way to undo everything at once is the `onResourceStop` event. FiveM fires it for every resource that stops; you check whether the resource that stopped is yours, and if so you tear your options down:

```lua
AddEventHandler('onResourceStop', function(resource)
    if resource ~= GetCurrentResourceName() then return end
    exports.ox_target:removeModel('a_m_y_business_03', 'qu_merchant_talk')
    exports.ox_target:removeGlobalPlayer('qu_give_cash')
    -- remove any zones by the ids you captured, and any global options
end)
```

Without this, here is what you see: you edit `client.lua`, run `restart qu_ox_target_interactions`, and now every ped of that model shows **two** Talk to merchant options, one from the old registration that was never cleaned up and one from the new load. The old debug box may also still float at the ATM. Adding an `onResourceStop` teardown removes the stale registrations the instant the resource stops, so the fresh load starts clean.

> **Restart, not just save**
> Saving `client.lua` does nothing on its own; the running resource keeps its old code and its old registrations. You restart in the server console with `restart qu_ox_target_interactions`. If you registered an `onResourceStop` teardown, that restart cleans up the old options before the new ones load. If you did not, the restart leaves the old options behind and you get the duplicate eye menu described above.

### disableTargeting: hide the whole eye menu during cutscenes

Sometimes you want to switch the entire targeting system off for a moment, not remove individual options, for instance while a cutscene plays or a fullscreen NUI menu is open and aiming would be distracting. `disableTargeting` does that:

```lua
exports.ox_target:disableTargeting(true)   -- player can no longer open the eye menu
-- ... cutscene or menu runs ...
exports.ox_target:disableTargeting(false)  -- targeting works again
```

The single boolean argument is the state: `true` turns the eye menu off, `false` turns it back on. The trap is forgetting the `false` call: if your cutscene errors out before re-enabling, the player is left unable to interact with anything and it looks like ox_target is broken. Always re-enable in the same code path that disabled it, ideally with the re-enable guaranteed to run even on an early return.

### When you reach for this pattern

ox_target is the default answer any time a player should walk up to something and act on it. Three real cases:

- **A fixed station.** Registers, ATMs, clothing stores, garage spawn points. These live at known coordinates, so `addBoxZone` is the tool, and `debug = true` is how you place the box exactly over the counter.
- **A class of object or NPC.** Every shop clerk, every dumpster, every harvestable plant. Attach the option once with `addModel` and every entity of that model inherits it, however many you spawn.
- **Player to player actions.** Give cash, show id, cuff, revive, search. These hang off `addGlobalPlayer`, and they almost always end in a `TriggerServerEvent`, because the interesting outcome is a change to shared state the server must own.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `No debug box appears at the ATM and aiming shows nothing` -> Either debug is false or the coords are wrong. Set debug = true and restart. If the box still does not show, your coords are not where you are standing. Stand at the spot and use a coords command to read your real position, then paste it into vec3.
- `The option shows a blank square where the icon should be` -> The icon Font Awesome class is wrong. Use the full free class string like fa-solid fa-money-bill, not just money-bill. Check the spelling against the Font Awesome free icon list.
- `The option never appears even though the box is visible` -> canInteract is returning false. Add a print inside it to see the distance value, or remove canInteract entirely to confirm the option itself is registered. A canInteract that always returns false silently hides the option with no error.
- `Could not load resource qu_ox_target_interactions, or ox_target is nil` -> ox_lib or ox_target is not started before this resource. Put ensure ox_lib and ensure ox_target above ensure qu_ox_target_interactions in server.cfg, because ox_target depends on ox_lib.
- `Give $100 prints nothing in txAdmin` -> The server event name in TriggerServerEvent must exactly match the name in RegisterNetEvent. Confirm both read qu_ox_target_interactions:giveCash, and that server.lua is listed as server_script in the manifest.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Write the shared ox_target option schema from memory: name, icon, label, distance, canInteract, and onSelect, and say what each field does.
- Place an interaction at a fixed spot with addBoxZone, using debug = true to line the box up over the target.
- Attach an interaction to a kind of entity with addModel so every ped or prop of that model inherits it.
- Attach an interaction to every other player with addGlobalPlayer, and turn data.entity into a server id.
- Explain why a give cash action must cross the network with TriggerServerEvent and finish on the server, not in the client onSelect.
- List ox_lib then ox_target as dependencies so ox_target loads with what it needs.

## Try it yourself

Make the merchant interaction conditional and two-way. Right now any player can Talk to the merchant. Change it so the Talk option only appears when the player is on foot, and add a second option, Rob register, that only appears when the player is crouched. Use `canInteract` to gate each one:

```lua
exports.ox_target:addModel('a_m_y_business_03', {
    {
        name = 'qu_merchant_talk',
        icon = 'fa-solid fa-comment',
        label = 'Talk to merchant',
        distance = 2.5,
        canInteract = function()
            return not IsPedInAnyVehicle(PlayerPedId(), false)
        end,
        onSelect = function()
            print('[qu_ox_target_interactions] merchant talked to')
        end
    },
    {
        name = 'qu_merchant_rob',
        icon = 'fa-solid fa-mask',
        label = 'Rob register',
        distance = 2.5,
        canInteract = function()
            return IsPedDuckingOrCrouching(PlayerPedId())
        end,
        onSelect = function()
            TriggerServerEvent('qu_ox_target_interactions:rob')
        end
    }
})
```

Add a matching `RegisterNetEvent('qu_ox_target_interactions:rob', ...)` on the server that prints the robber's source. Restart, then confirm Talk shows while standing and Rob register shows only while crouched. You just used `canInteract` to drive which options a single entity exposes based on player state, the exact mechanism real jobs use to show the right actions to the right people.

## References
- ox_target documentation (Overextended): https://overextended.dev/ox_target
- Cfx.re: Secure your events: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/developers/server-security/
- Cfx.re: Network and local IDs: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/networking/ids/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/

title: Lesson 24 · Spawning entities safely · Quasar Academy
ai:
Everything you see in the game world -- every car, every pedestrian, every traffic cone -- is an **entity**. In this lesson you learn how to create one from code, track it in a table, and delete it cleanly so it never lingers in the world. This is the foundation every prop, NPC, and vehicle spawner is built on.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: A client resource that loads a model, spawns a prop in front of you, tracks it in a registry table, auto-deletes it after 30 seconds, and clears everything on command and on restart.
- Time: ~25 minutes
- You'll learn: What an entity is, the model load lifecycle, the CreateObject argument list, the registry-and-cleanup pattern, and how the same shape extends to peds and vehicles.
- Prereqs: Comfortable with tables, the client/server split, and registering commands.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds model load, `CreateObject`, a registry table, and clean deletion as a working client resource named `qu_spawning_entities_safely`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line so you can spawn anything, not just this one box.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_spawning_entities_safely
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_spawning_entities_safely/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which file to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is the only Lua runtime now, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local objects = {}

local function loadModel(model)
    if not IsModelInCdimage(model) or not IsModelValid(model) then
        return false
    end

    RequestModel(model)
    local deadline = GetGameTimer() + 5000
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do
        if GetGameTimer() > deadline then return false end
        Wait(0)
    end
    return true
end

RegisterCommand('spawnbox', function()
    local model = joaat('prop_boxpile_07d')
    if not loadModel(model) then
        print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] model is invalid or timed out')
        return
    end

    local coords = GetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId())
    local object = CreateObject(model, coords.x + 2.0, coords.y, coords.z, false, false, false)
    objects[object] = true
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
    print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] spawned object ' .. object)

    SetTimeout(30000, function()
        if DoesEntityExist(object) then
            DeleteEntity(object)
            objects[object] = nil
            print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] timeout deleted object ' .. object)
        end
    end)
end, false)

RegisterCommand('clearboxes', function()
    local count = 0
    for object in pairs(objects) do
        if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
        objects[object] = nil
        count = count + 1
    end
    print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] registry cleared, removed ' .. count)
end, false)

AddEventHandler('onResourceStop', function(name)
    if name ~= GetCurrentResourceName() then return end
    for object in pairs(objects) do
        if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
    end
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the F8 client console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_spawning_entities_safely
```

    Save, then run in the txAdmin Live Console:

    ```text
restart qu_spawning_entities_safely
```

    Now join the server, open the F8 console, and run this test. Spawn one box, spawn a second, then clear them. Watch a box appear two metres in front of you each time:

    ```text
spawnbox
spawnbox
clearboxes
```

    Then spawn one more box and **do not** clear it. Wait 30 seconds and watch it delete itself.

Expected output:

```
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] spawned object 73
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] spawned object 122
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] registry cleared, removed 2
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] spawned object 145
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] timeout deleted object 145
```

    The handle numbers (73, 122, 145) will differ on your machine. They are runtime handles the game hands out, not fixed IDs. What matters is that every spawn prints a handle, `clearboxes` reports how many it removed, and an uncleared box prints its own `timeout deleted` line about half a minute later.

## How it works

You spawned a box, cleared it, and watched a stray one delete itself. Now take the code apart so the next time you need a prop, an NPC, or a parked car you are deciding, not copying. The whole file is built from three ideas stacked on top of each other: load a model, create an entity from it, and never lose track of what you created.

### Loading the model before you spawn

```lua
local model = joaat('prop_boxpile_07d')
if not loadModel(model) then return end
```

A model in GTA is an asset on disk: a mesh, textures, and usually collision data. You cannot place one until the client has loaded it. The helper first rejects a name that is not a valid CD image model, then requests it, yields while it loads, and stops after five seconds instead of trapping the client in an endless loop.

`joaat('prop_boxpile_07d')` turns the model **name** into a **hash**. The game does not look entities up by readable string names at runtime; it looks them up by a number called a joaat hash (the name comes from Jenkins one-at-a-time, the hashing algorithm Rockstar uses). `joaat` is the FiveM helper that computes that number for you from the string. You will also see `GetHashKey('prop_boxpile_07d')` in older code. It does the exact same thing and returns the exact same number. `joaat` is the newer, shorter name; prefer it, but treat the two as interchangeable.

`RequestModel(model)` asks the engine to stream that model in. It does not block and it does not finish instantly. It just files the request and returns.

`RequestModel` is a request, not a guarantee. `HasModelLoaded` flips to `true` only when streaming finishes. `Wait(0)` yields each pass so the engine can continue loading. The deadline handles the other failure mode: an invalid or unavailable model must not leave a command waiting forever. A tight loop with no `Wait` freezes the client; an unbounded yielding loop does not freeze it, but it can leave that command stuck indefinitely.

### Creating the object

```lua
local coords = GetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId())
local object = CreateObject(model, coords.x + 2.0, coords.y, coords.z, false, false, false)
```

`PlayerPedId()` is your character's entity handle, and `GetEntityCoords` returns its position as a vector with `.x`, `.y`, and `.z` fields. You read your own position so you can spawn the box near yourself instead of at some fixed corner of the map.

The `coords.x + 2.0` is deliberate. If you spawned at exactly `coords.x, coords.y, coords.z` the box would appear inside your character, which looks broken and can shove you. Adding 2.0 to the x coordinate offsets the spawn two metres to the side so it lands in front of you where you can see it. Coordinates in GTA are in metres.

`CreateObject` is the native that actually places the prop and returns its **entity handle**, the number you saw printed. Read its arguments in order:

- `model` is the loaded hash from step one.
- `coords.x + 2.0, coords.y, coords.z` is where to place it.
- the first `false` is **isNetwork**. `false` means this prop is local to your client only; other players will not see it. Pass `true` to register it with the network so everyone sees the same object.
- the second `false` is **netMissionEntity** (also called registerAsNetworked or the script-host flag). It only matters when the object is networked; it controls whether the object is treated as a mission entity that survives ownership changes. For a local prop it stays `false`.
- the last `false` is **doorFlag**, a niche flag for door objects. Leave it `false` for ordinary props.

The original draft passed `true, true` for the first two flags, which networks the prop to every player. For a single-player teaching proof that is overkill and makes the entity behave differently than the lesson claims, so this version keeps it local with `false, false`. The line in **When you reach for this pattern** below tells you exactly when to flip them.

### Freeing the model

```lua
SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
```

Once the object exists in the world, the engine no longer needs your reserved copy of the model in memory. `SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded` tells it you are done with that asset so it can be unloaded when nothing else is using it. This does not delete the object you just spawned; the object holds its own reference. Skipping this line will not break a single spawn, but a spawner that runs hundreds of times and never frees its models slowly leaks memory. Call it every time, right after the spawn succeeds.

### The registry and why it exists

```lua
objects[object] = true
```

This one line is what the lesson title is really about. `objects` is a plain Lua table declared at the top of the file. Every time you spawn, you record the new handle as a key. The value `true` is just a marker; you only care that the key exists. Now `objects` is a live list of everything this resource has created and has not yet deleted.

Why bother? Because a handle is the only way to delete an entity later, and the moment `spawnbox` returns, that local `object` variable is gone. Without a registry you would have spawned a box you can never reference again, which means you can never clean it up. Tracking handles in a table is the difference between a spawner and a litter machine.

```lua
for object in pairs(objects) do
    if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
    objects[object] = nil
    count = count + 1
end
```

`clearboxes` walks the registry and deletes each tracked entity. Two details carry the safety:

- `DoesEntityExist(object)` guards every `DeleteEntity`. An entity can already be gone for reasons outside your control: the timeout fired, another resource removed it, the area streamed out. Calling `DeleteEntity` on a handle that no longer points at anything is asking for trouble. The guard means you only delete what is genuinely still there.
- `objects[object] = nil` removes the key from the table after handling it. If you deleted the entity but left the handle in the registry, the next clear would try to delete a dead handle, and your registry would grow forever with ghosts.

### Auto-delete and restart cleanup

```lua
SetTimeout(30000, function()
    if DoesEntityExist(object) then
        DeleteEntity(object)
        objects[object] = nil
        print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] timeout deleted object ' .. object)
    end
end)
```

`SetTimeout(30000, fn)` is a FiveM helper that runs `fn` once after 30000 milliseconds. It is the resource's safety net: even if you never type `clearboxes`, every box removes itself after 30 seconds. Notice the same `DoesEntityExist` guard, because by the time the timeout fires you may have already cleared that box by hand; the guard stops a double delete. This timeout branch is half the point of the lesson, which is why the expected output above shows its `timeout deleted object` line.

```lua
AddEventHandler('onResourceStop', function(name)
    if name ~= GetCurrentResourceName() then return end
    for object in pairs(objects) do
        if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
    end
end)
```

`onResourceStop` fires for every resource that stops, so the first line checks the stopping resource is this one with `GetCurrentResourceName()` and bails out otherwise. When it is your resource, the handler sweeps the registry and deletes whatever is still alive. This is the line that makes the lesson "safe": without it, every time you restart the resource during development, the boxes you already spawned would be orphaned in the world with no script left tracking them, and only a full server restart would clear them. Cleaning up on stop means a restart is always a clean slate.

### Check yourself: Why does the code track handles in the objects table instead of just deleting the prop right after CreateObject?

Because spawning and deleting happen at different moments, and the handle is the only reference you have to the entity. The `spawnbox` handler finishes the instant the box appears, and its local `object` variable disappears with it. The box, however, stays in the world. `clearboxes`, the 30-second timeout, and `onResourceStop` all run later and need a way to find every box that is still out there. Storing each handle as a key in `objects` keeps a live list across those separate moments, so any of them can loop the table, guard with `DoesEntityExist`, and delete cleanly. Delete-immediately would defeat the purpose; the whole point is a prop that exists for a while and is still tracked.

### Entity handle versus network id

The number you printed is an **entity handle**: a runtime reference that is only valid on the client that created it, only while the entity exists. It is not a stable id and it is not shared. If you spawn the box as networked (the first `CreateObject` flag set to `true`), the entity also gets a **network id** that every client agrees on, which you read with `NetworkGetNetworkIdFromEntity(object)`. Other clients then turn that network id back into their own local handle. For this lesson the box is local, so there is no network id and no other player sees it. Keep the distinction straight: handles are local and per-client, network ids are the shared identity that crosses the wire.

### The same shape for peds and vehicles

Props are the gentlest entity to start with, but `CreateObject` is one of three siblings. Peds and vehicles load their models the exact same way and only differ in the create call:

```lua
-- A pedestrian: CreatePed(pedType, model, x, y, z, heading, isNetwork, scriptHostPed)
local model = joaat('a_m_y_business_03')
RequestModel(model)
while not HasModelLoaded(model) do Wait(0) end
local ped = CreatePed(4, model, coords.x + 2.0, coords.y, coords.z, 0.0, false, false)
SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)

-- A vehicle: CreateVehicle(model, x, y, z, heading, isNetwork, netMissionEntity)
local model = joaat('adder')
RequestModel(model)
while not HasModelLoaded(model) do Wait(0) end
local veh = CreateVehicle(model, coords.x + 4.0, coords.y, coords.z, 90.0, false, false)
SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
```

`CreatePed` takes a `pedType` as its first argument; `4` is the conventional civilian value (it maps to `PED_TYPE_CIVMALE`). In practice FiveM ignores this argument and assigns the ped type from the model anyway, so any value works and `4` is just the customary choice. `CreateVehicle` takes a `heading` in degrees so the car faces a direction. Everything else is identical: same load loop, same `SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded`, same idea of tracking the returned handle in a registry and deleting it the same way. Learn the prop and you have learned all three.

### Driving the spawn from a server command

So far you typed `spawnbox` and the client spawned the prop itself. Real ped systems work differently: a player runs a command, the **server** decides whether they are allowed and where, then tells the client to do the spawn. The native `CreatePed` only exists on the client (it touches the local game world), so the server cannot call it directly. The server's job is to validate, then hand off.

Add a tiny server file to the same resource. First update `fxmanifest.lua` so the resource has a server side too:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

Then create `server.lua`:

```lua
local Framework = GetConvar('qu:framework', 'standalone')
local ESX, QBCore

if Framework == 'esx' then
    ESX = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()
elseif Framework == 'qbcore' then
    QBCore = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()
end

local function getCharName(src)
    if Framework == 'esx' and ESX then
        local xPlayer = ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)
        return xPlayer and xPlayer.getName() or nil
    elseif Framework == 'qbcore' and QBCore then
        local player = QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)
        return player and player.PlayerData.charinfo.firstname or nil
    end
    return 'Player' .. src
end

RegisterCommand('spawnnpc', function(source, args)
    if source == 0 then
        print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] run /spawnnpc in-game, not from the server console')
        return
    end

    local model = args[1]
    if not model then
        print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] usage: /spawnnpc [pedModel]')
        return
    end

    local name = getCharName(source)
    print(('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] %s requested ped %s'):format(name, model))
    TriggerClientEvent('qu:spawnNpc', source, model)
end, true)
```

Now add the matching handler at the bottom of `client.lua`, so the client actually spawns when the server says to:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu:spawnNpc', function(modelName)
    local model = joaat(modelName)
    if not loadModel(model) then
        print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] ped model invalid or timed out: ' .. modelName)
        return
    end

    local coords = GetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId())
    local ped = CreatePed(4, model, coords.x + 2.0, coords.y, coords.z, 0.0, false, false)
    objects[ped] = true
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
    print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] spawned ped ' .. ped)
end)
```

Restart the resource from the **txAdmin Live Console**:

```text
restart qu_spawning_entities_safely
```

Then join the server and run this in the **F8 client console** (not the server console, because it is a player command):

```text
spawnnpc a_m_y_business_03
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] PlayerName requested ped a_m_y_business_03   (server console)
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] spawned ped 218   (F8 client console)
```

Walk the path. `source` is the server id of the player who ran the command; `args[1]` is the word they typed after it. `source == 0` means the command came from the server console, where there is no player and no ped to spawn near, so you reject it. The `RegisterCommand` third argument is `true` here, marking the command **restricted**: state-changing commands that spawn things into the world should require an ACE permission so any player cannot flood the map (the server console always bypasses the check). `getCharName` resolves the framework once at startup from a convar instead of guessing, so the same file works on ESX or QBCore. The validated request then crosses the wire with `TriggerClientEvent('qu:spawnNpc', source, model)`, and the client's `RegisterNetEvent` runs the same load-then-create-then-register sequence you already know, dropping the new handle into the same `objects` registry so `clearboxes` and `onResourceStop` clean it up for free.

> **Use the modern framework accessors, not the old ones**
> Older guides (including the Feb 2025 source this lesson is built from) initialise ESX with `ESX = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()` and never validate the source. On es_extended 1.13.5 the modern accessor is `ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)` for the player object, and `xPlayer.getName()` / `xPlayer.getAccount('money').money` for character data. On QBCore it is `exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()` then `QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)`, with the character's first name at `PlayerData.charinfo.firstname` (note `PlayerData.name` is the account name, a different field). Resolve the framework from a convar once at boot, not per-command.

### Persisting spawns to the database

The `objects` registry lives in memory. The moment the resource restarts, `onResourceStop` clears every ped and the registry is empty again, which is exactly what you want for *live* entities. But a real ped system usually wants a **record** that survives restarts: which player placed which ped, where, so you can respawn the scene next boot. That record belongs in the database, not in a Lua table. This is the SQL half the topic is named for.

Use oxmysql, the maintained MySQL layer for FiveM. First create the table once, in your database tool (the **HeidiSQL query tab**, not any in-game console):

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_spawned_peds (
    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    owner_license VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
    model VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
    pos_x DOUBLE NOT NULL,
    pos_y DOUBLE NOT NULL,
    pos_z DOUBLE NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

Then wire oxmysql into the manifest and record each spawn from the server. Update `fxmanifest.lua`:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'oxmysql'
}
```

The server already knows who ran the command. Read the player's most stable identifier and insert a row inside the `spawnnpc` handler, right after you validate the model:

```lua
local license = GetPlayerIdentifierByType(source, 'license')
if not license then
    print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] no license identifier for source ' .. source)
    return
end

MySQL.insert.await(
    'INSERT INTO qu_spawned_peds (owner_license, model, pos_x, pos_y, pos_z) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)',
    { license, model, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0 }
)
print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] recorded ped for ' .. license)
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_spawning_entities_safely] recorded ped for license:1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d   (server console)
```

Every line carries a reason. `GetPlayerIdentifierByType(source, 'license')` asks the server for one specific identifier rather than walking the whole list; `license` is account-changeable but always present, so it is a reasonable owner key for a teaching record (for bans Cfx recommends `fivem:` instead). `MySQL.insert.await` runs the INSERT and pauses this command handler until the database answers, which is safe because command handlers run in a coroutine. The `?` marks are **placeholders**: oxmysql substitutes the values from the second table safely, so a model name like `a_m_y'; DROP TABLE` is treated as data, never as SQL. **Never** build the query by gluing strings together with `..`; that is the SQL-injection hole the source guide leaves wide open. The position values are stubbed to `0.0` here because the server does not know the client's coordinates yet; in the exercise you send them back from the client so the row stores where the ped actually landed.

> **Memory registry and database row do different jobs**
> Keep the two straight. The `objects` table tracks **live handles** so you can delete entities this session and clean up on restart; it is meant to be wiped. The `qu_spawned_peds` row is a **durable record** keyed by a stable identifier so the scene can be rebuilt after a restart. A ped system needs both: the registry for clean teardown now, the database for persistence later.

### When you reach for this pattern

Registry-tracked spawning is not a beginner toy you leave behind. It is the backbone of real resources:

- **Placeable props.** A furniture system, a sign placer, a crime-scene markers tool. Players spawn objects, the resource tracks them, and a clear or a restart never litters the map.
- **Job and event vehicles.** A mechanic that spawns a tow truck, a heist that spawns getaway cars. You hold the handles so you can despawn them when the job ends. For these you set the first `CreateObject`/`CreateVehicle` flag to `true` so every player sees the same entity, and you usually spawn from the server for shared ownership.
- **NPC encounters.** A shopkeeper, a quest giver, a wave of enemies. Spawn with `CreatePed`, register the handles, and tear them down when the scene is over so peds never pile up.

The discipline is always the same: load, create, register, guard, delete on stop. Once that loop is muscle memory you can spawn anything in GTA without leaking it into the world.

### Persisting an entity choice: a server command + SQL

Everything above spawns an entity *into the world* and tracks it *in memory*. But a registry table only lives as long as the resource runs. The moment the resource restarts, or the player reconnects, that knowledge is gone. The other half of "spawning safely" is the part that survives a restart: store the *decision* (which model a player should be) in the database, then re-apply it whenever they come back. The classic first build for this is a **ped system** -- an admin gives a player a saved character model, and that model sticks across reconnects.

This flips the lesson in three ways, and each one is worth naming before you read the code:

- **It runs on the server, not the client.** The command that grants a ped, and the database write, both belong on the server, because the server is the only place that can be trusted with "who is allowed to change whose appearance" and is the only place that talks to the database. The client never touches SQL.
- **The command takes arguments.** `RegisterCommand` hands your callback `source` and `args`. `source` is the server ID of the player who typed it (`0` means the server console). `args` is a list of the words after the command, so `/addped 3 cop s_m_y_cop_01` arrives as `args[1] = "3"`, `args[2] = "cop"`, `args[3] = "s_m_y_cop_01"`. Every one of those is an *untrusted string typed by a person*, so you validate before you trust.
- **The model change happens back on the client.** Only the client can call `SetPlayerModel` on its own character. So the flow is: client types command -> server validates and writes SQL -> server triggers a client event -> client loads the model and swaps its own ped. The model-load loop you already learned is reused verbatim on the client side.

#### Build the server side: `server.lua`

First, the database needs a table to hold each saved ped. Run this once in your SQL tool (HeidiSQL, the txAdmin database tab, or the `mysql` CLI) against your server's database:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_player_peds (
    id          INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    identifier  VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL,
    ped_name    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    ped_model   VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    UNIQUE KEY uniq_player_ped (identifier, ped_name)
);
```

The `UNIQUE KEY` means a given player cannot have two saved peds under the same name; re-adding the same name updates it instead of duplicating. Now the server resource. This assumes you already wired oxmysql with `server_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua'` and a `dependencies { 'oxmysql' }` block in your `fxmanifest.lua` (the same wiring you used in the oxmysql lesson).

```lua
local function getLicense(src)
    return GetPlayerIdentifierByType(src, 'license')
end

RegisterCommand('addped', function(source, args)
    -- source 0 is the server console; this command needs a real player to target
    if source == 0 then
        print('[qu_player_peds] run /addped in-game, not the server console')
        return
    end

    local targetId = tonumber(args[1])
    local pedName = args[2]
    local pedModel = args[3]

    if not targetId or not pedName or not pedModel then
        print('[qu_player_peds] usage: /addped [serverId] [name] [model]')
        return
    end

    local identifier = getLicense(targetId)
    if not identifier then
        print('[qu_player_peds] no license for server id ' .. targetId .. ' (are they connected?)')
        return
    end

    MySQL.insert.await(
        'INSERT INTO qu_player_peds (identifier, ped_name, ped_model) VALUES (?, ?, ?) '
        .. 'ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE ped_model = VALUES(ped_model)',
        { identifier, pedName, pedModel }
    )
    print('[qu_player_peds] saved ped "' .. pedName .. '" (' .. pedModel .. ') for ' .. identifier)
end, true) -- restricted = true: state-changing admin command, gate it with ACE

RegisterCommand('useped', function(source, args)
    local pedName = args[1]
    if not pedName then
        print('[qu_player_peds] usage: /useped [name]')
        return
    end

    local identifier = getLicense(source)
    if not identifier then return end

    local row = MySQL.single.await(
        'SELECT ped_model FROM qu_player_peds WHERE identifier = ? AND ped_name = ?',
        { identifier, pedName }
    )

    if not row then
        print('[qu_player_peds] no saved ped named "' .. pedName .. '" for you')
        return
    end

    TriggerClientEvent('qu_player_peds:applyModel', source, row.ped_model)
end, false) -- a player changing their own saved appearance is allowed for everyone
```

#### Build the client side: `client.lua`

The client only ever receives a *model name string the server already approved*, then runs the exact model-load loop from the top of this lesson before swapping its own ped:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_player_peds:applyModel', function(pedModel)
    local model = joaat(pedModel)
    if not IsModelInCdimage(model) or not IsModelValid(model) then
        print('[qu_player_peds] invalid model: ' .. tostring(pedModel))
        return
    end

    RequestModel(model)
    local deadline = GetGameTimer() + 5000
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do
        if GetGameTimer() > deadline then
            print('[qu_player_peds] model timed out: ' .. pedModel)
            return
        end
        Wait(0)
    end

    SetPlayerModel(PlayerId(), model)
    SetPedDefaultComponentVariation(PlayerPedId())
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
    print('[qu_player_peds] now playing as ' .. pedModel)
end)
```

List both files in the manifest (`server_script` for `server.lua` plus the oxmysql import, `client_script` for `client.lua`), `ensure` the resource, then in the **server console** grant yourself ACE so the restricted `/addped` is allowed -- `add_principal identifier.license:<your-license> group.admin` and `add_ace group.admin command.addped allow`. Join the server, open **F8**, run `/addped <your-server-id> cop s_m_y_cop_01`, then `/useped cop`, and watch your character become a cop. Reconnect and run `/useped cop` again -- it still works, because the choice lives in the database, not in memory.

#### How this half works -- and the why behind every guard

- **Why the identifier, not the server ID.** A server ID like `3` is recycled the instant a player leaves; the next person to join might be `3`. You key the row on `GetPlayerIdentifierByType(src, 'license')` -- the platform-independent Rockstar license hash -- so the saved ped follows the *person* across sessions, not the slot. (`getplayeridentifiers` is **not** a console command; the natives are `GetPlayerIdentifier`, `GetPlayerIdentifierByType`, and `GetNumPlayerIdentifiers`.) For permanent records Cfx.re now recommends the `fivem:` identifier since `license` can change when someone switches Rockstar accounts, but `license` remains the most common anchor in framework code.
- **Why `?` placeholders, never string concatenation.** Every value in the SQL goes through a `?` and the values array. `args[2]` is text a player typed; if you spliced it straight into the query string with `..`, a crafted name could rewrite your statement. That is SQL injection. The `?` placeholder makes oxmysql send the value as *data*, never as part of the command, so it cannot break out. This is non-negotiable on any query that touches user input.
- **Why `restricted = true` on `/addped` but `false` on `/useped`.** `RegisterCommand`'s third argument is the restricted flag. `/addped` changes another player's saved state, so it is an admin action and must be gated behind ACE (`true`) -- only principals you explicitly allow can run it, and the server console always bypasses the gate. `/useped` only lets a player re-apply *their own* already-approved ped, so it is safe to leave open (`false`). The rule of thumb: any command that changes state someone else can see, or that an admin should control, is `true`.
- **Why the server writes and the client applies.** The server is the authority: it decides who may grant a ped and it owns the database connection. The client cannot be trusted to tell the server "I'm allowed to be a cop now," so the client never writes SQL and never picks the model -- it only receives a model name the server already looked up and approved, then renders it. This is the server-authority rule that every money, item, and appearance system lives by.
- **Why the model-load loop reappears on the client.** Changing your ped is still "spawn an entity from a model": you `joaat` the name, `RequestModel`, wait in a bounded `Wait(0)` loop until `HasModelLoaded`, then `SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded` once you are done. `SetPlayerModel` swaps your character to that model, and `SetPedDefaultComponentVariation` gives the fresh ped its default clothing so it does not spawn as an untextured dummy. Same lifecycle you learned at the top -- only the create call (`SetPlayerModel` instead of `CreateObject`) differs.

> **Server-authority is the whole point**
> The unsafe version of a ped system lets the *client* tell the server which model to apply, or splices a typed ped name straight into the SQL with `..`. Both hand control to the player. Always do the lookup and the permission check on the server, pass only an approved value to the client, and feed every user-supplied value through a `?` placeholder. If a command changes state that other players see, register it with `restricted = true` and gate it with ACE.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Resource qu_spawning_entities_safely does not exist` -> The folder is not inside resources, or the ensure line spells the name differently. Match the folder name exactly.
- `Could not load resource qu_spawning_entities_safely` -> Open fxmanifest.lua and fix the first quote, brace, or file name error the console reports. The manifest must list client.lua.
- `The box never appears but no error prints` -> You skipped the load loop, or removed the Wait(0) inside it. CreateObject ran before the model finished streaming. Keep the while not HasModelLoaded(model) do Wait(0) end line exactly as written.
- `spawnbox prints nothing in F8` -> This is a client_script, so its print lands in the F8 client console, not txAdmin. Join the server, press F8, and run the command there.
- `/addped types fine but nothing is saved and no error shows` -> You registered /addped with restricted = true (correct) but have not granted yourself ACE. In the server console run add_principal identifier.license:<your-license> group.admin and add_ace group.admin command.addped allow, or test from the server console which always bypasses ACE.
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'MySQL')` -> The @oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua line is missing from fxmanifest.lua or oxmysql is not started before this resource. Add the server_script import and a dependencies { 'oxmysql' } block.
- `/useped does nothing and prints land in the server console, not F8` -> server.lua runs as a server_script, so its print goes to the txAdmin Live Console / server console, not F8. The client-side qu_player_peds:applyModel print is the one that lands in F8. Check both consoles.
- `no license for server id X` -> GetPlayerIdentifierByType(src, 'license') returned nil because that server ID is not a connected player. Use the player's current server ID from the txAdmin Players list, and remember IDs are recycled when players leave.
- `Boxes stay in the world after you restart the resource` -> The onResourceStop handler is missing or the GetCurrentResourceName check is wrong. Without it, a restart orphans every box you already spawned.
- `attempt to perform arithmetic on a nil value` -> The concatenation print ran against a handle that is nil. Make sure object comes from CreateObject and that you print it on the same line you spawn it.
- `spawnnpc says you do not have permission` -> The command is registered restricted (third arg true), so a normal player needs the ACE permission, or run it from the server console which bypasses the check. Grant it with an add_ace line in server.cfg for testing.
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'MySQL')` -> The server_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua' line is missing from fxmanifest.lua, or oxmysql is not started. Add that line plus the dependencies { 'oxmysql' } block so oxmysql loads first.
- `spawnnpc spawns nothing and the F8 console is silent` -> The server received the command but the client never got the event. Check the event name matches exactly on both sides (qu:spawnNpc) and that the client RegisterNetEvent handler is present in client.lua.
- `no license identifier for source` -> You called GetPlayerIdentifierByType from a context where source is 0 (the server console) or the player has disconnected. The command guards source == 0 already; make sure you are running it in-game as a connected player.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Run the model load lifecycle: joaat the name, RequestModel, then wait in a Wait(0) loop until HasModelLoaded is true.
- Call CreateObject and read all of its arguments, including what the isNetwork, netMissionEntity, and door flags do.
- Explain why SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded is called after the spawn and what leaks if you skip it.
- Track spawned handles in a registry table and explain why a handle you do not store is a handle you can never delete.
- Guard every DeleteEntity with DoesEntityExist, and clean up on onResourceStop so a restart never orphans entities.
- Translate the same pattern to CreatePed and CreateVehicle.
- Persist an entity choice in SQL with a server-side command that validates source and args, keys rows on the player's license identifier, and uses ? placeholders.
- Apply a model on the client only after the server approves it, using TriggerClientEvent, the same model-load loop, SetPlayerModel, and SetPedDefaultComponentVariation -- and explain why state-changing commands are registered restricted = true.

## Try it yourself

Extend the spawner so it can place a parked car as well as a box, and prove your cleanup tracks both. Add a `spawncar` command that loads the `adder` model, calls `CreateVehicle` four metres in front of you, and records the returned handle in the **same** `objects` registry:

```lua
RegisterCommand('spawncar', function()
    local model = joaat('adder')
    RequestModel(model)
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do Wait(0) end

    local coords = GetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId())
    local veh = CreateVehicle(model, coords.x + 4.0, coords.y, coords.z, 90.0, false, false)
    objects[veh] = true
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
    print('[qu_spawning_entities_safely] spawned vehicle ' .. veh)
end, false)
```

Restart the resource, spawn one box and one car, then run `clearboxes`. Because both handles live in the same registry, the single clear loop deletes both and reports `removed 2`. That is the payoff of the pattern: one registry, one cleanup path, no matter how many entity types you add.

Extend the ped system with a read-back command so a player can see every ped saved to their account. Add a `/mypeds` command to `server.lua` that looks up the caller's license, selects **all** their rows (note: `MySQL.query.await` returns a list of rows, unlike `MySQL.single.await` which returns one), and triggers a client event that prints each name and model in F8:

```lua
RegisterCommand('mypeds', function(source)
    local identifier = GetPlayerIdentifierByType(source, 'license')
    if not identifier then return end

    local rows = MySQL.query.await(
        'SELECT ped_name, ped_model FROM qu_player_peds WHERE identifier = ?',
        { identifier }
    )
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_player_peds:listPeds', source, rows or {})
end, false)
```

Then add the matching `RegisterNetEvent('qu_player_peds:listPeds', function(rows) ... end)` on the client that loops `rows` and prints `ped_name | ped_model` for each. Save two peds with `/addped`, run `/mypeds`, and confirm both appear in F8. You just proved you understand why a list query returns a table of tables (you index `row.ped_name` inside a loop) while a single-row lookup hands you one row directly -- and that this read path, like every other, runs the lookup on the server and only ships approved data to the client.

Right now the database row stores `0.0, 0.0, 0.0` for position because the server never learns where the ped actually spawned. Fix that by sending the real coordinates back. Modify the client `qu:spawnNpc` handler so that after `CreatePed` succeeds it fires a server event with the spawn position:

```lua
TriggerServerEvent('qu:pedPlaced', modelName, coords.x + 2.0, coords.y, coords.z)
```

Then on the server, register `qu:pedPlaced` and move the `MySQL.insert.await` call into it, using the real `x, y, z` it receives instead of the `0.0` stubs. Restart the resource, run `spawnnpc a_m_y_business_03` in F8, and confirm the new row in HeidiSQL holds your actual coordinates, not zeros.

One trap to reason about: the server event arguments come from the client, so they are **not trusted**. A malicious client could send absurd coordinates. For this teaching record that is acceptable, but note in a comment that a production system would clamp or validate the position server-side before storing it. That single habit -- treat every value the client sends as a claim, not a fact -- is the line between a spawner and an exploit.

## References
- FiveM CreateObject native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x509D5878EB39E842
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/
- FiveM SetPlayerModel native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x00A1CADD00108836
- FiveM GetPlayerIdentifierByType native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0xA61C8FCAF90C0782
- oxmysql documentation: https://overextended.dev/oxmysql

title: Lesson 25 · Cleanup discipline · Quasar Academy
ai:
Spawning is easy. Cleaning up is the part that separates scripts that work from scripts that destroy servers. If you spawn entities and never delete them, the world fills with ghost objects, entity counts climb, and the server lags or crashes. This lesson teaches the three moments cleanup has to happen, and why one of them cannot be done from the client at all.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: Cleanup handlers for resource stop, player drop, and timed auto-delete.
- Time: ~25 minutes
- You'll learn: Signs of entity leaks, cleanup on resource stop, cleanup on player drop, timeout auto-delete, and why the drop path has to live on the server.
- Prereqs: Lesson 24 (spawning entities, the registry pattern) and OneSync enabled.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- My server uses OneSync, so a client net id can be resolved and checked on the server.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a networked box spawner with three cleanup paths as a working FiveM resource named `qu_cleanup_discipline`. It has a `client.lua` and a `server.lua`, because the player-drop path can only run on the server. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line.

## Build it
