### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_cleanup_discipline
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout. Note the new `server.lua` compared to Lesson 24:

```text
resources/qu_cleanup_discipline/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    '/onesync'
}
```

The `dependencies { '/onesync' }` line tells the server to refuse to start this resource unless OneSync (state awareness) is on. The drop-cleanup path later in this lesson resolves networked entities on the server, which only works with state awareness enabled, so this guard fails loudly at start instead of silently misbehaving later.

There is no `lua54 'yes'` line. As of June 2025 that directive is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is the only Lua runtime now, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the client code
_Goal: The client spawns a networked box and reports it to the server._

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local objects = {}

RegisterCommand('spawnbox', function()
    local model = joaat('prop_boxpile_07d')
    if not IsModelInCdimage(model) or not IsModelValid(model) then return end

    RequestModel(model)
    local deadline = GetGameTimer() + 5000
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do
        if GetGameTimer() > deadline then
            print('[qu_cleanup_discipline] model load timed out')
            return
        end
        Wait(0)
    end

    local coords = GetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId())
    local object = CreateObject(model, coords.x + 2.0, coords.y, coords.z, true, true, false)
    objects[object] = true
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)

    local netId = NetworkGetNetworkIdFromEntity(object)
    TriggerServerEvent('qu_cleanup:registerBox', netId)
    print('[qu_cleanup_discipline] spawned object ' .. object .. ' netId ' .. netId)

    SetTimeout(30000, function()
        if DoesEntityExist(object) then
            DeleteEntity(object)
            objects[object] = nil
            print('[qu_cleanup_discipline] timeout deleted object ' .. object)
        end
    end)
end, false)

RegisterCommand('clearboxes', function()
    for object in pairs(objects) do
        if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
        objects[object] = nil
    end
    TriggerServerEvent('qu_cleanup:clearMine')
    print('[qu_cleanup_discipline] registry cleared')
end, false)

AddEventHandler('onResourceStop', function(name)
    if name == GetCurrentResourceName() then
        for object in pairs(objects) do
            if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
        end
    end
end)
```

### Step 5 - Write the server code
_Goal: The server tracks each player's boxes and deletes them on drop._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local BOX_MODEL = joaat('prop_boxpile_07d')
local MAX_BOXES_PER_PLAYER = 10
local boxesByPlayer = {}

local function countBoxes(owned)
    local count = 0
    for _ in pairs(owned or {}) do count = count + 1 end
    return count
end

local function deleteTracked(src, reason)
    local owned = boxesByPlayer[src]
    if not owned then return 0 end

    local deleted = 0
    for netId in pairs(owned) do
        local entity = NetworkGetEntityFromNetworkId(netId)
        if entity ~= 0 and DoesEntityExist(entity) then
            DeleteEntity(entity)
            deleted = deleted + 1
        end
    end

    boxesByPlayer[src] = nil
    print(('[qu_cleanup_discipline] %s player %s, deleted %s boxes'):format(reason, src, deleted))
    return deleted
end

RegisterNetEvent('qu_cleanup:registerBox', function(netId)
    local src = source
    netId = tonumber(netId)
    if not netId then return end

    local entity = NetworkGetEntityFromNetworkId(netId)
    if entity == 0 or not DoesEntityExist(entity) then return end
    if NetworkGetEntityOwner(entity) ~= src then return end
    if GetEntityModel(entity) ~= BOX_MODEL then return end

    boxesByPlayer[src] = boxesByPlayer[src] or {}
    if countBoxes(boxesByPlayer[src]) >= MAX_BOXES_PER_PLAYER then
        DeleteEntity(entity)
        return
    end

    boxesByPlayer[src][netId] = true
    print('[qu_cleanup_discipline] tracking netId ' .. netId .. ' for player ' .. src)

    SetTimeout(30000, function()
        local owned = boxesByPlayer[src]
        if not owned or not owned[netId] then return end

        local trackedEntity = NetworkGetEntityFromNetworkId(netId)
        if trackedEntity ~= 0 and DoesEntityExist(trackedEntity) then
            DeleteEntity(trackedEntity)
        end
        owned[netId] = nil
        if next(owned) == nil then boxesByPlayer[src] = nil end
    end)
end)

RegisterNetEvent('qu_cleanup:clearMine', function()
    deleteTracked(source, 'cleared')
end)

AddEventHandler('playerDropped', function()
    local src = source
    deleteTracked(src, 'dropped')
end)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_cleanup_discipline
```

    Save, then run:

    ```text
restart qu_cleanup_discipline
```

    Run this test fast, before the 30 second timeout fires:

    ```text
/spawnbox then /clearboxes
```

    The spawn print and the tracking print share one handle and net id pair, so your real numbers will differ from the sample below. The point is the shape: a spawn line, a server tracking line, and a cleared line, with no timeout line because you cleared first. The server may report `deleted 0 boxes` even though cleanup worked: the client deletes its box locally first and then fires the event, so the server can find nothing left to delete by the time it resolves the net id. A zero count here means the client already cleaned up, not that cleanup failed.

Expected output:

```
F8: [qu_cleanup_discipline] spawned object 524292 netId 8
Console: [qu_cleanup_discipline] tracking netId 8 for player 1
F8: [qu_cleanup_discipline] registry cleared
Console: [qu_cleanup_discipline] cleared player 1, deleted 0 boxes
```

## How it works

You spawned a box and cleared it, and a third path is waiting on a timer. Now take the code apart so cleanup becomes a habit you reach for without thinking. The whole lesson is one idea: every entity you create is a debt, and you pay it back in three places. Miss any one of them and the debt piles up as ghost objects.

### Why a leak is dangerous, and how to see it

An entity leak is not abstract. Every networked box you leave alive consumes an entity slot and may need to be synchronized to players for as long as it exists. Repeating `/spawnbox` without cleanup steadily adds work and memory. On a busy server, enough leaked entities contribute to rising frame time, streaming problems, and pool exhaustion.

You can prove the lesson without relying on an undocumented console command. The server prints once when it accepts a box and again when it clears or drops a player's tracked set. Spawn several boxes, run `/clearboxes`, and confirm the objects disappear and the server reports the cleanup. For production profiling, use the documented client resource monitor and server profiler, then correlate any growth with your resource's own registry and logs.

### The registry is the only memory you have

```lua
local objects = {}
-- ...
objects[object] = true
```

You cannot delete what you cannot name. `CreateObject` hands you back one number, the entity handle, and that number is the only way to refer to the box later. The registry is a table whose keys are those handles. Storing `objects[object] = true` means the value does not matter, only the key, so the table behaves like a set of handles. When it is time to clean up you walk the table with `pairs` and you have every box you ever made in one place. Without this table, the moment the spawn function returns and the handle goes out of scope, the box is unreachable. It still exists in the world. You just lost the receipt.

### DoesEntityExist guards every delete

```lua
if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
```

A handle in your registry is a claim that a box existed at spawn time, not a promise it still exists now. Another resource or the timeout may already have deleted it. `DoesEntityExist` asks whether the handle still points at a real entity before you act on it. A stale handle makes cleanup fail or produce misleading results. The guard is cheap and makes the operation explicit: verify immediately before deleting.

### Path one: cleanup on resource stop

```lua
AddEventHandler('onResourceStop', function(name)
    if name == GetCurrentResourceName() then
        for object in pairs(objects) do
            if DoesEntityExist(object) then DeleteEntity(object) end
        end
    end
end)
```

`onResourceStop` fires for every resource that stops anywhere on the server, not just yours. That is why the `name` argument and the `GetCurrentResourceName()` comparison are not optional. Without that filter, your handler would run its cleanup loop every time any unrelated resource on the box restarts, which is constant on a live server. The guard narrows it to the one moment you care about: your own resource shutting down. When you `restart qu_cleanup_discipline`, the stop half fires first, this handler runs, and every box you spawned is deleted before the new copy loads. That is what stops ghost objects surviving a restart, the single most common cleanup failure in the wild.

### Path two: timed auto-delete

```lua
SetTimeout(30000, function()
    if DoesEntityExist(object) then
        DeleteEntity(object)
        objects[object] = nil
        print('[qu_cleanup_discipline] timeout deleted object ' .. object)
    end
end)
```

`SetTimeout` schedules a function to run once after a delay in milliseconds, so `30000` is thirty seconds. The client deletes quickly when it is still alive. The server schedules its own matching timeout after it validates the entity, so cleanup does not depend on the client staying connected or behaving honestly. Both paths check existence first, making a second cleanup harmless when the first one already succeeded.

### Path three: cleanup on player drop, and why it lives on the server

This is the path the client physically cannot do, and the reason this lesson has a `server.lua` at all. Think it through. The cleanup handlers above all live in `client.lua`. They run inside the player's own game. If that player crashes to desktop, loses internet, or force-quits, their game stops executing instantly. No `onResourceStop`, no timeout callback, nothing client-side runs, because the thing that would run it is dead. The boxes they spawned are networked, so they stay alive on the server with no owner to clean them. That is a guaranteed leak that grows every time someone rage-quits.

The server is the one party still alive when a client disappears, so the drop cleanup has to live there. The client reports each box upward as it spawns:

```lua
local netId = NetworkGetNetworkIdFromEntity(object)
TriggerServerEvent('qu_cleanup:registerBox', netId)
```

`NetworkGetNetworkIdFromEntity` converts the local entity handle into a network id that refers to the same entity across machines during that entity's lifetime. Network ids can be reused after an entity is removed, which is another reason to resolve and validate immediately. A raw entity handle is local to one runtime, so you send the net id, not the handle. `TriggerServerEvent` crosses from client to server, and inside the matching handler `source` is the player who sent it:

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_cleanup:registerBox', function(netId)
    local src = source
    netId = tonumber(netId)
    if not netId then return end

    local entity = NetworkGetEntityFromNetworkId(netId)
    if entity == 0 or not DoesEntityExist(entity) then return end
    if NetworkGetEntityOwner(entity) ~= src then return end
    if GetEntityModel(entity) ~= BOX_MODEL then return end

    boxesByPlayer[src] = boxesByPlayer[src] or {}
    boxesByPlayer[src][netId] = true
end)
```

The server must not store a net id merely because a client sent it. A modified client could submit another player's entity id and trick a naive cleanup handler into deleting it later. This handler resolves the id, confirms the entity exists, checks that the sender currently owns it, checks the expected box model, and caps each player at ten tracked boxes. Cfx.re's event-security guidance is the rule behind those checks: client event data is an untrusted request.

`boxesByPlayer` is a registry of registries: one inner table of validated net ids per player server id. `boxesByPlayer[src] = boxesByPlayer[src] or {}` creates the player's bucket on the first accepted box. When `playerDropped` fires, the server has a checked set to delete:

```lua
for netId in pairs(owned) do
    local entity = NetworkGetEntityFromNetworkId(netId)
    if entity ~= 0 and DoesEntityExist(entity) then
        DeleteEntity(entity)
    end
end
```

`NetworkGetEntityFromNetworkId` is the reverse of what the client did: it turns the net id back into a server-side entity handle. This resolution only works because OneSync (state awareness) is on, which is why the manifest depends on `/onesync`. It returns `0` when no entity matches, for example because the box was already cleaned by the timeout, so you check `entity ~= 0` before the existence guard. Delete each one, drop the player's whole bucket, and the server returns to baseline. A crashed player no longer leaks.

### Check yourself: Why can the player-drop cleanup not just live in client.lua next to the other two handlers?

Because client handlers only run while that player's game is running. A crash or hard disconnect stops its code immediately. The server remains alive, so it validates each reported net id while the player is connected, stores only entities matching the expected owner and model, and deletes that set when `playerDropped` fires. Its timeout is also the fallback when the client never runs local cleanup.

### When you reach for this pattern

Any time a resource spawns world entities, all three paths apply, and you wire them once at the start rather than bolting them on after the first leak report:

- **Resource stop** is non-negotiable for anything you develop iteratively. You restart the resource dozens of times a day; without it every restart leaves a fresh pile of ghosts.
- **A timeout or interaction end** fits anything temporary: a dropped item that should despawn, a job prop that vanishes when the job ends, an effect entity tied to an animation.
- **Player drop** is the one beginners forget and the one that hurts most on a busy server, because disconnects are constant. If your entities are networked and tied to a player, the server must clean them on drop, full stop.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Boxes survive after restart qu_cleanup_discipline` -> The onResourceStop handler is missing the GetCurrentResourceName() filter or the loop never runs. Confirm the handler is in client.lua and that it walks the objects table.
- `No tracking netId line in the server console` -> TriggerServerEvent did not reach a RegisterNetEvent. Check both event names match exactly across client.lua and server.lua, and that server_script 'server.lua' is in fxmanifest.lua.
- `Boxes from a crashed player stay in the world` -> The server never accepted them or the playerDropped handler did not run. Confirm OneSync is enabled and look for the tracking line; a missing line means the server could not resolve the entity, owner, or expected model.
- `attempt to call a nil value (NetworkGetEntityFromNetworkId)` -> That native is server-side here. Make sure the playerDropped block is in server.lua, not client.lua, since the two runtimes expose different natives.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain why an unfreed entity is a debt, and read the rising entity count that proves a leak.
- Use a registry table of handles as the only reliable way to find and delete what you spawned.
- Guard every DeleteEntity with DoesEntityExist so stale handles fail safely and cleanup stays predictable.
- Wire the three cleanup paths: onResourceStop filtered to your own resource, a SetTimeout auto-delete, and a server-side playerDropped handler.
- Say why the player-drop path must live on the server, why clients report net ids, and why the server validates owner and model before tracking.

## Try it yourself

Prove the resource-stop path deletes everything, not just the last box. Spawn three boxes back to back, then stop the resource and confirm all three vanish.

```lua
RegisterCommand('spawn3', function()
    for i = 1, 3 do ExecuteCommand('spawnbox') end
    print('[qu_cleanup_discipline] spawned 3 boxes')
end, false)
```

Restart `qu_cleanup_discipline`, run `spawn3`, and watch three spawn lines appear with three different handles. Now run `stop qu_cleanup_discipline` and look at the world: every box is gone in one shot, because `onResourceStop` walks the whole registry, not a single handle. As a stretch, add a `print` inside the stop loop that counts how many boxes it deleted, the same way the server counts on player drop, and confirm it reports 3.

## References
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- playerDropped server event: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/events/server-events/#playerdropped
- Cfx.re: Secure your events: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/developers/server-security/
- Cfx.re: Network and local IDs: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/networking/ids/
- FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/

title: Lesson 26 · Vehicles: spawn, modify, clean up · Quasar Academy
ai:
Vehicles are the most common entity you will work with in FiveM. Garage scripts, dealerships, police cruisers, test commands -- they all start with spawning a car and putting the player in it. This lesson covers the full cycle: stream the model in, spawn the car, customize it, put the player inside, and delete it cleanly when the timer runs out.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: A /testvehicle command that spawns a car, sets a custom plate and color, warps the player into the driver seat, and auto-deletes the car after 60 seconds.
- Time: ~30 minutes
- You'll learn: The model request-and-poll pattern, joaat hashes, CreateVehicle and its network ownership flags, TaskWarpPedIntoVehicle seat indexes, the vehicle color table, and SetTimeout closure cleanup.
- Prereqs: Lesson 24 (spawning entities) and Lesson 25 (cleanup discipline).

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can join my server and read the F8 client console.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds CreateVehicle, plate, color, warp, and auto-delete as a working client-side FiveM resource named `qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once the car spawns, the next section takes the code apart line by line so you know why every argument is there.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
```

Spawning a car and warping the player both happen on the client, so this resource has a `client_script` and no `server_script`. Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `client.lua` and paste this. The lines are spread out on purpose so the next section can walk through them one at a time:

```lua
local testVehicle

local function loadModel(model)
    if not IsModelInCdimage(model) or not IsModelAVehicle(model) then
        return false
    end

    RequestModel(model)
    local deadline = GetGameTimer() + 5000
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do
        if GetGameTimer() > deadline then return false end
        Wait(0)
    end
    return true
end

RegisterCommand('testvehicle', function()
    if testVehicle and DoesEntityExist(testVehicle) then
        DeleteEntity(testVehicle)
    end

    local model = joaat('sultan')
    if not loadModel(model) then
        print('[qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store] invalid vehicle model or load timeout')
        return
    end

    local ped = PlayerPedId()
    local c = GetEntityCoords(ped)
    testVehicle = CreateVehicle(model, c.x + 3.0, c.y, c.z, GetEntityHeading(ped), true, false)

    SetVehicleNumberPlateText(testVehicle, 'QUCAR')
    SetVehicleColours(testVehicle, 12, 12)
    TaskWarpPedIntoVehicle(ped, testVehicle, -1)

    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
    print('[qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store] spawned sultan')

    local spawned = testVehicle
    SetTimeout(60000, function()
        if spawned and DoesEntityExist(spawned) then
            DeleteEntity(spawned)
            print('[qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store] auto-deleted')
        end
    end)
end, false)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the F8 client console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store
```

    Save, then run this in the server console:

    ```text
restart qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store
```

    Join the server, open the F8 console, and run:

    ```text
/testvehicle
```

    A Sultan appears beside you, you are dropped into the driver seat, the plate reads QUCAR, and the body is matte black. Sixty seconds later the car vanishes.

Expected output:

```
F8: [qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store] spawned sultan
F8 after 60 seconds: [qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store] auto-deleted
```

## How it works

The car spawned. Now take the code apart so the next time you write a garage or a dealership you are deciding, not copying. The whole command is one story in five beats: clear the old car, load the model, spawn it, dress it up, and arm a timer to delete it. We will follow that order.

### Why a model must be loaded before you spawn it

```lua
local model = joaat('sultan')
if not loadModel(model) then return end
```

A vehicle in GTA is built from a model: a bundle of meshes, textures, and physics data that the game keeps on disk, not in memory. `CreateVehicle` cannot build a car from a model that is not in memory yet, so you have to ask the engine to stream it in first. That is exactly what these three pieces do.

`RequestModel(model)` tells the engine to start loading the Sultan. It does not wait. The load happens in the background over the next few frames, so the line after `RequestModel` runs long before the model is actually ready. If you called `CreateVehicle` immediately you would get a null entity, because the data simply is not there yet.

The helper also checks `IsModelInCdimage` and `IsModelAVehicle` before requesting, then gives streaming five seconds. `Wait(0)` yields each pass so the game keeps running. The timeout matters when a model is unavailable: without it, a yielding loop can wait forever even though it does not freeze the whole client.

### What joaat actually returns

```lua
local model = joaat('sultan')
```

`joaat` is the Jenkins one-at-a-time hash function, and it turns the text `'sultan'` into a number. Internally the game does not identify models by their string name; it identifies them by a 32-bit hash of that name. `CreateVehicle` and `RequestModel` both expect that hash, not the word. `joaat('sultan')` and `GetHashKey('sultan')` compute the exact same number, so they are interchangeable. `joaat` is just the shorter, native Lua-side spelling. Either way, the rule is the same: hand the spawn natives the hash, never the raw string.

### CreateVehicle and the two flags that confuse everyone

```lua
testVehicle = CreateVehicle(model, c.x + 3.0, c.y, c.z, GetEntityHeading(ped), true, false)
```

This is the line that builds the car. The first five arguments are the obvious ones: the model hash, then x, y, z, then the heading. We read the player's position with `GetEntityCoords(ped)` and add `3.0` to x so the car spawns a few metres to the side instead of inside the player. `GetEntityHeading(ped)` points the car the same way the player is facing.

The last two booleans are where most beginners are silently wrong, and they are the network ownership basics this lesson is really about.

- The first boolean, `true`, is `isNetwork`. When it is `true`, the entity is registered with FiveM's networking, gets a network ID, and is replicated to other players. The car you spawn is a real shared object everyone on the server can see. Set this to `false` and the car would exist only on your own machine; no one else would see it. For a car a player sits in, you want `true`.
- The second boolean, `false`, is `netMissionEntity` (sometimes called `bScriptHostObject`). Leave it `false` for ordinary spawns. You set it `true` only for entities a single script wants to keep authority over across clients, which is an advanced mission-script concern, not something you need for a garage car.

The client that calls `CreateVehicle` becomes the initial network owner of the car. Ownership is the right to control the entity's physics and state, and it can migrate to another player as they drive near it. The takeaway: `isNetwork = true` is what makes a spawned vehicle a proper networked entity instead of a ghost only you can see.

### Check yourself: A teammate spawns a car with isNetwork set to false and asks why nobody else can see it. What do you tell them?

The `false` first boolean means the car was never registered with FiveM's networking, so it exists only on the machine that spawned it. Other players have no network ID for it and the server never replicates it, so to them the spot is empty. The fix is to spawn with `isNetwork = true` (the first boolean), which gives the car a network ID and makes it a shared entity. The second boolean, `netMissionEntity`, stays `false` for an ordinary car and is unrelated to whether others can see it.

### Dressing the car: plate and color

```lua
SetVehicleNumberPlateText(testVehicle, 'QUCAR')
SetVehicleColours(testVehicle, 12, 12)
```

`SetVehicleNumberPlateText` takes the vehicle handle and a string, and stamps that string onto the plate. Plates are capped at eight characters, so `QUCAR` is fine.

`SetVehicleColours(vehicle, primary, secondary)` is the line with the magic numbers. Those two numbers are not RGB values. They are indexes into GTA's built-in paint table, where each number maps to a specific named color. `12` is matte black, which is why the body comes out a flat black. The full table lives in the FiveM and GTA modding references under vehicle colors; common anchors are `0` (metallic black), `12` (matte black), `27` (metallic red), `64` (metallic blue). Passing the same index for primary and secondary, as we do here, paints the whole car one solid color. The reason to use an index instead of raw RGB is that these are the factory paints the game already ships, so they render correctly under the game's lighting without you defining a custom color.

### Putting the player in the seat instantly

```lua
TaskWarpPedIntoVehicle(ped, testVehicle, -1)
```

`TaskWarpPedIntoVehicle` teleports a ped straight into a seat with no walking animation. The third argument is the seat index, and the seat numbering is the part to memorise: `-1` is the driver seat, `0` is the front passenger, `1` and `2` are the rear seats, and so on. So `-1` here drops the player behind the wheel the instant the car exists.

The word "warp" is the contrast to learn. `TaskWarpPedIntoVehicle` is instant and unconditional, which is exactly what you want for a test command or an admin spawn. The alternative, `TaskEnterVehicle`, makes the ped physically walk over, open the door, and climb in, which looks natural but takes seconds and can fail if the path is blocked. For a spawn-and-go command, warp wins.

### Releasing the model

```lua
SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
```

Back when you called `RequestModel`, you told the engine to pin the Sultan model in memory. Once the car is built, the spawned entity holds its own reference to that data, so the pin you placed is no longer needed. `SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded` lifts your hold and lets the streamer free the model when nothing else is using it. Skipping this will not crash anything, but pile up enough un-released models across a session and you leak memory. Release what you requested: that is the discipline.

### The timer, the closure, and the existence guard

```lua
local spawned = testVehicle
SetTimeout(60000, function()
    if spawned and DoesEntityExist(spawned) then
        DeleteEntity(spawned)
        print('[qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store] auto-deleted')
    end
end)
```

`SetTimeout(60000, fn)` schedules the function `fn` to run once, 60000 milliseconds (60 seconds) later. The function you hand it is a closure: it captures the variables in scope when it was created and remembers them until it fires. Here it captures `spawned`, which we set to the current `testVehicle` on the line above.

That extra `local spawned = testVehicle` is deliberate, and it is the named takeaway of this lesson. `testVehicle` is the shared variable at the top of the file, and it changes every time the command runs. If the timer closed over `testVehicle` directly, then running `/testvehicle` a second time would overwrite `testVehicle` with the new car, and the first car's 60-second timer would fire and delete the new car by mistake. By snapshotting the handle into a fresh `spawned` for each run, every timer deletes exactly the car it was created for.

`DoesEntityExist(spawned)` is the guard. By the time the timer fires, the car might already be gone: the player could have left and rejoined, another script could have cleaned it up, or the entity could have been streamed out. Calling `DeleteEntity` on a handle that no longer points to a live entity is exactly the kind of careless call that throws errors in the F8 console. The guard says "only delete this if it is still really here," which is the cleanup discipline from Lesson 25 applied to a real entity.

### When you reach for this whole pattern

Stream a model, spawn a networked entity, customize it, seat the player, and arm a guarded cleanup: that five-beat shape is the skeleton under almost every vehicle feature in FiveM.

- **A garage or dealership.** Spawning the car the player selected, painting it to their saved color, and dropping them into the driver seat is this exact code with the model and colors read from a database instead of hard coded.
- **A test or admin command.** Spawn any vehicle on demand to check handling or a livery, then let the timer clean it up so you do not litter the map.
- **Temporary mission vehicles.** A getaway car or a delivery truck that should disappear when the job ends uses the same snapshot-and-guard cleanup so the right entity gets removed at the right time.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `No car appears and F8 shows nothing` -> This is a client_script, so /testvehicle must be typed in the F8 console while you are in-game, not in the server console. Confirm you joined the server, then check the resource started with restart qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store.
- `The game freezes for a moment then a car appears far away` -> You likely removed the Wait(0) inside the model-loading loop or changed it to a positive number. Keep it Wait(0) so the loop yields a frame each pass and the model can stream in.
- `The car spawns but no one else can see it` -> The first CreateVehicle boolean (isNetwork) is false. Set it to true so the car is registered with networking and replicated to other players.
- `attempt to call a nil value (global 'joaat')` -> joaat is spelled in all lowercase. If it still fails, swap it for GetHashKey('sultan'), which computes the identical hash.
- `Running /testvehicle twice deletes the wrong car after a minute` -> You let the timer close over testVehicle directly. Add local spawned = testVehicle before SetTimeout and delete spawned inside the closure so each timer owns its own car.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Stream a model in with RequestModel and a Wait(0) poll loop, and explain why CreateVehicle would fail without it.
- Turn a model name into the hash CreateVehicle needs with joaat, and know GetHashKey gives the same number.
- Read CreateVehicle's isNetwork and netMissionEntity flags and say what network ownership means for who can see the car.
- Customize a vehicle with a plate string and an index from the GTA color table, and warp the player into the driver seat with seat -1.
- Release a requested model with SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded, and snapshot a handle so a SetTimeout closure deletes the exact entity it was armed for, guarded by DoesEntityExist.

## Try it yourself

Make the command accept a model name and a color so it is a real spawn tool instead of a fixed demo. FiveM passes the typed words into the handler as the `args` table:

```lua
RegisterCommand('testvehicle', function(source, args)
    local name = args[1] or 'sultan'
    local color = tonumber(args[2]) or 12
    local model = joaat(name)
    if not IsModelInCdimage(model) or not IsModelAVehicle(model) then
        print('[qu_vehicles_spawn_modify_store] not a valid vehicle: ' .. name)
        return
    end
    RequestModel(model)
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do
        Wait(0)
    end
    -- spawn, then: SetVehicleColours(testVehicle, color, color)
end, false)
```

Run `/testvehicle adder 27` and confirm a red Adder spawns. Then run `/testvehicle notacar` and confirm the `IsModelInCdimage` and `IsModelAVehicle` guard prints the rejection line instead of trying to spawn a broken entity. You just turned a hard-coded demo into a command that validates user input before touching the spawn natives, which is the difference between a toy and a tool.

## References
- CreateVehicle native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0xAF35D0D2583051B0
- TaskWarpPedIntoVehicle native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x9A7D091411C5F684
- FiveM vehicle color reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/game-references/vehicle-references/vehicle-colors/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: Lesson 27 · Handling basics and safe edits · Quasar Academy
ai:
Every vehicle in FiveM has a hidden file that controls how it drives: how fast it accelerates, how hard it brakes, how much grip the tires have. That file is `handling.meta`, and it is **XML, not Lua**. In this lesson you build a tiny streaming resource called `qu_handling`, change one safe value, test it, and prove you can revert it with one click in GitHub Desktop.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: A safely edited handling.meta for one vehicle, tested in-game, with a Git backup you can revert.
- Time: ~25 minutes
- You'll learn: What handling.meta does, where it lives, the safe-edit workflow, the four fields beginners touch, and how to revert when things go wrong.
- Prereqs: Lesson 9b (Git & GitHub Desktop), basic resource structure (Module 01).

### Before you start
- I have a vehicle that already spawns in-game, or I can spawn one with a /car command.
- I have GitHub Desktop set up and I can make a commit (Lesson 9b).
- I am comfortable editing an XML file in VS Code without breaking tag balance.
- I can run server commands in the txAdmin Live Console and restart one resource.

> **Backup first, edit second**
> Handling is physics data the game reads at resource start. One bad number turns a car into a rocket or a brick. Before you touch any value, make a Git commit (or copy the whole folder) so a broken car is always one revert away. Do one step, read the proof, then move to the next.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder and the stream folder
_Goal: You have an empty qu_handling resource with a stream/ folder ready for the handling file._

Handling files live inside a `stream/` folder. The `stream/` folder is a special FiveM folder: anything inside it replaces the matching base-game file without modifying the game itself. Using your operating system's file manager (Windows File Explorer or the VS Code Explorer panel), open your server's `resources` folder -- the same folder you set up in Module 01 (commonly `...\txData\<your-server>\resources\` on a txAdmin install). Create new folders and empty files there to match this layout:

```text
resources/qu_handling/
  fxmanifest.lua
  stream/
    handling.meta
  client.lua
```

Leave the files empty for now. You fill them in the next steps.

### Step 2 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows this resource ships a handling override and a client script._

A file in `stream/` is loaded automatically for models, but a data file like `handling.meta` needs an explicit `data_file` line so FiveM knows what kind of data it is. Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this exactly:

Note one thing that trips people up: streamed model files (`.yft`/`.ytd`) auto-mount from `stream/` with no manifest entry, but a metadata file registered with `data_file` must ALSO be declared in a `files {}` block, or the mounter cannot locate the file and the override silently fails. That is why the manifest below lists `stream/handling.meta` twice, once in `files {}` and once in `data_file`. (The conventional home for such metas is a `data/` folder, but `stream/` works fine as long as the `files` entry points at it.)

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

author 'You'
description 'Vehicle handling overrides'
version '1.0.0'

-- Tells FiveM to load our handling override from stream/
files {
    'stream/handling.meta'
}

data_file 'HANDLING_FILE' 'stream/handling.meta'

client_script 'client.lua'
```

Do not add a `lua54 'yes'` line. Lua 5.4 is the only runtime on modern FXServer, so that line is deprecated and ignored.

### Step 3 - Copy one complete, working handling entry
_Goal: stream/handling.meta contains a complete entry from the vehicle you will test._

Do not hand-author a five-field handling block. A real `CHandlingData` item contains many related fields and may include sub-handling data; a partial sample is not a safe runnable file.

Start with an add-on vehicle that already works on your staging server. In that vehicle's `vehicles.meta`, find its `<modelName>`, then read the `<handlingId>` in the same item. In the package's `handling.meta`, find the complete `
` whose `<handlingName>` exactly matches that id. Copy the **whole item**, including every field and `SubHandlingData`, into the standard `CHandlingDataMgr` wrapper in `stream/handling.meta`.

The model name and handling id are often similar, but they are not required to match. Follow the actual `vehicles.meta` link instead of guessing. Also keep the original vehicle resource's load order documented: two resources defining the same handling id can override each other, so this staging override must be ensured after the vehicle resource and removed when the exercise is done.

Fields you will commonly inspect include:

- `fInitialDriveForce`, which contributes to acceleration rather than representing horsepower directly.
- `fInitialDriveMaxFlatVel`, which contributes to theoretical drive speed but does not guarantee the measured top speed by itself.
- `fBrakeForce`, which contributes to braking strength.
- `fTractionCurveMax`, one part of the tire-grip model that interacts with other traction fields.
- `fMass`, which affects physics mass but should not be tuned in isolation.

There is no universal “normal range” that fits every class. Use the working vehicle's values as the baseline and change one field by 5 to 10 percent.

### Step 4 - Commit the untouched baseline to Git
_Goal: GitHub Desktop holds a clean snapshot you can revert to before any edit._

This is the step that makes every later edit safe. Open the resource folder in GitHub Desktop (Lesson 9b), stage `qu_handling`, and commit it with a clear message:

```text
chore: handling baseline before brake edit
```

Now prove the revert works before you trust it. Add a temporary comment line near the top of `handling.meta`, save, and confirm GitHub Desktop shows exactly that one file changed. Then right-click the change and choose **Discard changes**. Reopen the file and confirm the comment is gone.

Expected output:

```
GitHub Desktop history shows: chore: handling baseline before brake edit
After Discard changes, handling.meta has no temporary comment.
```

If you are not using Git yet, copy the whole folder and name the copy `qu_handling_BACKUP` before touching any XML.

### Step 5 - Add a /testhandling command
_Goal: One command spawns a test car in front of you so you can feel each edit fast._

Typing spawn menus every time is slow. Paste this helper into `client.lua`. It spawns a vehicle right in front of you and puts you in the driver seat.

Security note: the `false` at the end of `RegisterCommand` means this command is NOT permission-restricted, so on a live server ANY connected player could run `/testhandling` and spawn networked vehicles everyone sees. This is a staging/dev tool only. Keep `qu_handling` off your public server, or change the `false` to `true` and grant yourself the `command.testhandling` ACE permission, and remove the resource entirely when the exercise is done.

```lua
local testVehicle

RegisterCommand('testhandling', function(_, args)
    local model = args[1]
    if not model then
        print('[qu_handling] usage: /testhandling your_model_name')
        return
    end

    local hash = joaat(model)
    if not IsModelInCdimage(hash) or not IsModelAVehicle(hash) then
        print('[qu_handling] invalid vehicle model: ' .. model)
        return
    end

    RequestModel(hash)
    local deadline = GetGameTimer() + 5000
    while not HasModelLoaded(hash) do
        if GetGameTimer() > deadline then
            print('[qu_handling] model load timed out: ' .. model)
            return
        end
        Wait(0)
    end

    if testVehicle and DoesEntityExist(testVehicle) then
        DeleteEntity(testVehicle)
    end

    local ped = PlayerPedId()
    local coords = GetEntityCoords(ped)
    local heading = GetEntityHeading(ped)

    testVehicle = CreateVehicle(hash, coords.x, coords.y, coords.z, heading, true, false)
    SetPedIntoVehicle(ped, testVehicle, -1) -- -1 = driver seat
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(hash) -- let the game unload the model later

    print('[qu_handling] spawned ' .. model .. ' for handling test')
end, false)

AddEventHandler('onResourceStop', function(resourceName)
    if resourceName == GetCurrentResourceName() and testVehicle and DoesEntityExist(testVehicle) then
        DeleteEntity(testVehicle)
    end
end)
```

### Step 6 - Start the resource and spawn the baseline car
_Goal: The car spawns with the unedited handling so you have something to compare against._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

```text
ensure qu_handling
```

Save, then in the txAdmin Live Console run:

```text
restart qu_handling
```

Join the server, then run the test command in-game chat. Replace `your_model_name` with the real spawn name of the vehicle whose handling entry you copied in Step 3 (for example, `adder` for the stock Adder). The word `your_model_name` is a placeholder; typing it literally will print `invalid vehicle model: your_model_name` because no such model exists:

```text
/testhandling adder
```

You should drop into the vehicle you named (an Adder, if you used `adder`). Drive it, then brake. Remember how long it takes to stop, because the next step changes exactly that.

Expected output:

```
Console line (model name is whatever you typed, e.g. adder):
[qu_handling] spawned adder for handling test
```

### Step 7 - Change ONE value, restart, and feel the difference
_Goal: The car brakes noticeably harder, and you know the brake field caused it._

Open `stream/handling.meta` and change only the brake force. Leave every other number alone:

```text
<!-- Example: a 10 percent increase from an original 0.800000 -->
<fBrakeForce value="0.880000" />
```

The game reads handling only at resource start, so the edit does nothing until you restart. In the txAdmin Live Console run:

```text
restart qu_handling
```

Calculate the new number from the value in your own complete entry; do not assume its original value is `0.800000`. Spawn a fresh car with `/testhandling your_model_name`, drive the same route, then brake at the same marker. If the evidence is better, commit the exact model and percentage. Otherwise revert. Changing one value at a time is the key habit: if you change four numbers and the car feels wrong, you cannot identify the cause.

## How it works

You built it and the brake edit landed. Now take the two dense parts apart, the `/testhandling` command and the XML, so the next time you touch a vehicle you are deciding, not copying.

### Why a restart is the only way the edit takes

The lesson repeats one rule twice: change a number, nothing happens until you restart the resource. Here is the mechanism behind that rule. The game does not re-read `handling.meta` while it runs. It loads every vehicle's physics data once, when the resource that ships the file starts. After that the numbers live in memory and the engine never looks at the file again. So editing the XML on disk changes nothing the running game can see. The `restart qu_handling` step is what forces the engine to drop the old physics data and load your new values. That is also why a typo in the XML can fail silently until the next restart: the bad file is only parsed at start time.

### The model-loading wait loop

The first three lines of the command body are a pattern you will write in almost every FiveM script that spawns something:

```lua
if not IsModelInCdimage(hash) or not IsModelAVehicle(hash) then return end
RequestModel(hash)
local deadline = GetGameTimer() + 5000
while not HasModelLoaded(hash) do
    if GetGameTimer() > deadline then return end
    Wait(0)
end
```

Models stream on demand. The first check rejects an unknown name or a model that is not a vehicle. `RequestModel` starts loading, `Wait(0)` yields so the game can continue, and the five-second deadline prevents an unavailable asset from leaving the command stuck forever.

### Spawning the vehicle and seating the driver

```lua
testVehicle = CreateVehicle(hash, coords.x, coords.y, coords.z, heading, true, false)
SetPedIntoVehicle(ped, testVehicle, -1)
SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(hash)
```

`CreateVehicle` takes the model hash, then the x, y, z where the car appears, then the heading it faces. The last two arguments are the ones beginners skip past. The `true` is `isNetworked`: the vehicle is created as a networked entity so other players on the server can see it, not just you. The `false` is `netMissionEntity`: leave it false for a normal spawn. `SetPedIntoVehicle(ped, vehicle, -1)` drops you into the car, and the `-1` is the seat index: `-1` always means the driver seat, while `0` is the front passenger and higher numbers are the rear seats. `SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(hash)` tells the engine you are done holding the model in memory so it can be unloaded later, which keeps the request from leaking.

### How the XML maps to a real car

The handling file is XML, and the link starts in `vehicles.meta`. Read the vehicle's real `<handlingId>`, then find the identical `<handlingName>` value in the complete handling entry:

```text
<handlingId>EXAMPLECAR</handlingId>
<handlingName>EXAMPLECAR</handlingName>
```

`
` contains a `
` collection with complete items. The model name does not directly select an item. Its `vehicles.meta` record supplies the handling id, and the matching `handlingName` selects the physics data. Multiple vehicles can intentionally share one handling id, so search for every use before assuming a change affects only one car.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Could not load resource qu_handling` -> Open fxmanifest.lua and check the data_file line and quotes; the path must be 'stream/handling.meta' exactly.
- `The car drives exactly the same after editing` -> You forgot restart qu_handling, or you edited the wrong <handlingName> block, or the data_file line is missing from the manifest.
- `The vehicle becomes unstable or unrealistic` -> Revert the single edited field, restart, and prove the baseline is restored. Do not compensate by changing several other fields.
- `More than one vehicle changed` -> Search vehicles.meta for every model using this handlingId. Shared handling ids intentionally apply one entry to multiple vehicles.
- `attempt to call a nil value at /testhandling` -> Type /testhandling in in-game chat (client side), not the server console, and confirm client.lua is listed in the manifest.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Build a staging override from one complete working CHandlingData entry and load it with HANDLING_FILE.
- Follow modelName to handlingId in vehicles.meta, then handlingName in handling.meta.
- Follow the safe workflow: commit a baseline, change one value, restart qu_handling, test, then commit or revert.
- Match a broken-car symptom (flying, stuck, sliding) to the field that caused it, and undo it with a GitHub Desktop revert.

## Try it yourself

Revert to the baseline and restart `qu_handling`. Read your vehicle's current `fTractionCurveMax`, reduce it by 5 percent, restart, spawn a fresh copy with `/testhandling your_model_name`, and repeat one fixed corner. Write down the difference, then revert and prove the baseline returned. Do not use an arbitrary value copied from another vehicle.

## References
- FiveM data files reference (HANDLING_FILE): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/game-references/data-files/
- Handling.meta field descriptions (community wiki): https://gtamods.com/wiki/Handling.meta
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/

title: Bonus · NUI from scratch: ui_page, SendNUIMessage, RegisterNUICallback · Quasar Academy

toc.label: A different NUI entry point: the loading screen

ai:
**NUI** is FiveM's name for the in-game browser layer, a real Chromium tab the client renders on top of GTA. Every menu, hud, scoreboard, and pause UI you have ever seen in a server is NUI. So far you have used `ox_lib` and other framework UIs that wrap NUI for you. (`ox_lib` is a community library from the Overextended team, not a Cfx.re built-in, but it is everywhere.) This bonus shows the layer underneath: how to declare a page in `fxmanifest.lua`, how to push state from Lua with `SendNUIMessage`, how to receive clicks with `RegisterNUICallback`, and how to capture the cursor with `SetNuiFocus`. Once you understand these four pieces, you can read any NUI resource and build your own without a framework. ### What this lesson covers - You'll learn: The GetParentResourceName callback URL, the Lua to JS message and callback APIs, focus capture, and how to attach Chrome devtools to a live NUI page. - You'll ship: A minimal NUI menu: a command opens a Chromium overlay with one button, the button sends data back to Lua, and a Lua-triggered chat message confirms the round-trip. - Time: ~25 minutes - Why now: You finished the vehicle handling lesson. Knowing how NUI works under the hood lets you debug any UI bug for the rest of the course. ### Before you start - I can find my server resources folder. - I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource. - I can join the server and read F8 client output. > **Build the proof, then understand it** > This lesson builds `ui_page`, the `files` list, `SendNUIMessage`, and `RegisterNUICallback` as a working FiveM resource named `qu_nui_from_scratch`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes it apart line by line so you know why it works. NUI is a client feature, so the proof appears in F8, not the server console. ## Build it ### Step 1 - Make the resource folder _Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._ Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder: ```text resources/qu_nui_from_scratch ``` ### Step 2 - Create the files _Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._ Create this exact file layout: ```text resources/qu_nui_from_scratch/ fxmanifest.lua client.lua web/index.html web/app.js web/style.css ``` ### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua _Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._ Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this: ```lua fx_version 'cerulean' game 'gta5' client_script 'client.lua' ui_page 'web/index.html' files { 'web/index.html', 'web/app.js', 'web/style.css' } ``` Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out. ### Step 4 - Write the lesson code _Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._ Open `client.lua` and paste this: ```lua RegisterCommand('openui', function() SetNuiFocus(true, true) SendNUIMessage({ action = 'open', title = 'NUI from scratch' }) print('[qu_nui_from_scratch] opened, focus captured') end, false) RegisterNUICallback('buttonClicked', function(data, cb) SetNuiFocus(false, false) local label = data.label or 'unknown' print('[qu_nui_from_scratch] callback received, label = ' .. label) TriggerEvent('chat:addMessage', { color = { 0, 200, 120 }, args = { 'NUI', 'Round-trip complete. JS sent: ' .. label } }) cb({ ok = true }) end) ``` Open `web/index.html` and paste this: ```html

menu

``` Open `web/app.js` and paste this: ```js const menu = document.getElementById('menu'); const title = document.getElementById('title'); const button = document.getElementById('action'); // Lua to JS: reveal the menu only when Lua pushes the open message. window.addEventListener('message', (event) => { if (event.data.action === 'open') { title.textContent = event.data.title; menu.classList.remove('hidden'); } }); // JS to Lua: send a real payload back, then hide on the callback's reply. button.addEventListener('click', () => { fetch(`https://${GetParentResourceName()}/buttonClicked`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({ label: 'Send to Lua and close' }) }).then(() => { menu.classList.add('hidden'); }); }); ``` Open `web/style.css` and paste this: ```css body { margin: 0; display: grid; place-items: center; min-height: 100vh; font-family: sans-serif; } #menu { background: #14161a; color: #fff; padding: 28px; border-radius: 10px; text-align: center; } .hidden { display: none; } ``` ### Step 5 - Start and test it _Goal: The expected proof appears in F8 and in chat._ Open `server.cfg` and add this line: ```text ensure qu_nui_from_scratch ``` Save, then run this in your server console (the FXServer window, or the txAdmin **Live Console** if you run txAdmin): ```text restart qu_nui_from_scratch ``` Now join the server. Run this test from the in-game chat box (press T to open it, type the command, press Enter): ```text /openui, then click "Send to Lua and close" ``` Expected output: ``` [qu_nui_from_scratch] opened, focus captured [qu_nui_from_scratch] callback received, label = Send to Lua and close ``` And in the chat box you see one line: `NUI: Round-trip complete. JS sent: Send to Lua and close`. That chat line is the proof the data made it from the browser back into Lua, not just an empty request. ## How it works You typed a command, a panel appeared, you clicked once, and a chat line came back carrying text the button sent. Four moving parts made that happen, and the order they fire in is the whole mental model. Take them in the order the data flows: declare the page, capture focus, push state Lua to JS, then send a payload JS to Lua. ### The manifest: ui_page and files ```lua ui_page 'web/index.html' files { 'web/index.html', 'web/app.js', 'web/style.css' } ``` `ui_page` does one job: it names the single HTML file FiveM mounts as this resource's NUI layer. That page renders fullscreen on top of the game, transparent where you do not draw anything. There is exactly one `ui_page` per resource. `files` is a separate, mandatory list. NUI runs inside a sandboxed browser, and that browser may only load assets you explicitly whitelist here. The HTML page itself is loaded because it is the `ui_page`, but every asset the page then asks for, the stylesheet, the script, an image, a font, must also appear in `files`. Leave `app.js` out of the `files` block and the browser's request for it returns a 404, the script never runs, and your button does nothing, with no Lua error to explain why. That silent 404 is the single most common reason a NUI page looks dead. When a NUI feature half-works, check `files` first. Note the dependency-style detail while you are here. If this resource needed another resource loaded first, the canonical key is the plural block: ```lua dependencies { 'some_resource' } ``` The singular `dependency 'some_resource'` is tolerated but the plural block is the form to learn. We do not need it for this lesson, since NUI is built into the client runtime. ### SetNuiFocus: who is the keyboard and mouse talking to ```lua SetNuiFocus(true, true) ``` By default the game owns your mouse and keyboard, and the NUI layer is click-through: input passes straight to GTA. `SetNuiFocus` is how you hand input to the browser instead. It takes two booleans, and they are independent. - The first boolean is **keyboard/input focus** (`hasFocus`). `true` routes key presses into the page and captures input (it also blocks WASD movement) — what you want for a text input or search box. - The second boolean is **mouse-cursor focus** (`hasCursor`). `true` shows the cursor and lets the browser receive clicks. Without it, your button is visible but unclickable because every click still goes to the game underneath. The rule that saves you hours: whatever you capture, you must release. When the menu closes you call `SetNuiFocus(false, false)`. Forget it and the player is stuck staring at a cursor, unable to move, walk, or open the chat, with the game apparently frozen. It is not frozen. Focus never went back to the game. In this lesson the release happens on the first line of the callback, the moment the click comes in, so focus returns before anything else runs. ### SendNUIMessage: pushing state from Lua to JS ```lua SendNUIMessage({ action = 'open', title = 'NUI from scratch' }) ``` ```js window.addEventListener('message', (event) => { if (event.data.action === 'open') { title.textContent = event.data.title; menu.classList.remove('hidden'); } }); ``` `SendNUIMessage` is the one-way channel from Lua into the page. You hand it a Lua table; FiveM serializes that table to JSON and dispatches it to the browser as a DOM `message` event. On the JS side you listen with `window.addEventListener('message', ...)` and read the payload off `event.data`. The keys you set in Lua are the keys you read in JS: `action` and `title` here. This is the piece the old version of this lesson was missing, and it is why the page now starts hidden. The menu carries the `hidden` class until a message with `action` equal to `open` arrives, and only then does it reveal itself and stamp in the title Lua sent. So the UI is not just sitting there waiting; Lua is driving it. That is the real pattern: you almost never want a NUI page that shows itself. You want Lua to decide when, and to ship the data the page should display in the same message. An `action` field is the convention for telling the page which of its many states to switch into. `SendNUIMessage` is fire-and-forget. It does not return a value and the page cannot reply through it. For a reply you need the other direction. ### RegisterNUICallback: sending a payload from JS back to Lua ```js fetch(`https://${GetParentResourceName()}/buttonClicked`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({ label: 'Send to Lua and close' }) }); ``` ```lua RegisterNUICallback('buttonClicked', function(data, cb) SetNuiFocus(false, false) local label = data.label or 'unknown' -- ... cb({ ok = true }) end) ``` The page talks back to Lua by making an HTTP request. The current documented form is `https://${GetParentResourceName()}/`. `GetParentResourceName()` is provided inside NUI and returns the resource that owns the page, so the code keeps working if the folder is renamed. The endpoint after the slash must exactly match the string passed to `RegisterNUICallback`. Do not hardcode `cfx-nui-` for callbacks: that prefix is the resource-file URL scope, while the official callback API uses the parent resource name directly. On the Lua side, `RegisterNUICallback('buttonClicked', function(data, cb) ... end)` is the listener for that endpoint. The handler is handed two arguments: - `data` is the request body, already parsed from JSON into a Lua table. The `{ label: '...' }` object the `fetch` sent arrives as `data.label`. That is how you read the value the button carried. Defaulting with `data.label or 'unknown'` guards against a malformed body. Security note: a NUI callback body is client input, so treat it as untrusted. `data.label or 'unknown'` only guards against a *missing* value, not a malicious one. The moment a callback does anything that matters (gives an item, moves money, fires a server event), validate every field server-side and never trust a value just because your own page sent it. - `cb` is the response function. The browser's `fetch` is a promise waiting on a reply, and `cb({ ok = true })` is that reply. Until you call `cb`, the `fetch` promise never resolves, so the `.then` that hides the menu never runs. You must call `cb` exactly once in every callback, even if you only send `cb({})`. A callback that forgets `cb` leaves the page hanging on every click. The chat line is the visible proof the data crossed: `TriggerEvent('chat:addMessage', ...)` fires the built-in `chat` resource's message event on the client and prints the `label` the JS sent. If that text shows up in chat, the round-trip is real. An empty request body that the callback ignored, which is what the old lesson shipped, could never produce it. ### Closing from inside the page: a dedicated release callback So far focus is only released inside `buttonClicked`, the action button. But what happens to a player who opens the menu and then changes their mind without clicking it? Right now: nothing. The cursor stays captured, movement stays blocked, and they are stuck. A real menu always gives the page its own way to back out, and that close action needs to tell Lua to release focus. That means a second callback whose only job is to hand input back to the game. Add a close button to `web/index.html`, inside the `#menu` div, right after the existing button: ```html ``` Then wire it in `web/app.js`. The page owns the close decision, so it hides itself immediately and fires a `closeUI` callback to release focus: ```js const closeButton = document.getElementById('close'); function closeMenu() { menu.classList.add('hidden'); fetch(`https://${GetParentResourceName()}/closeUI`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({}) }); } closeButton.addEventListener('click', closeMenu); // Escape should also back out, the same way every game menu does. window.addEventListener('keyup', (event) => { if (event.key === 'Escape') closeMenu(); }); ``` Now register the matching callback in `client.lua`: ```lua RegisterNUICallback('closeUI', function(data, cb) SetNuiFocus(false, false) cb({ ok = true }) end) ``` Notice this callback does only two things: it releases focus and it calls `cb`. There is no money moving, no item granted, nothing to validate, so the body is empty and that is fine. The point is that focus release now has its own path that does not depend on the player clicking the main action. > **A close callback that forgets the two essentials** > A common version of this you will see copied around omits both the `SetNuiFocus(false, false)` and the `cb` call, firing the fetch as pure fire-and-forget. The result is the exact freeze this lesson warned about: the page hides, but focus never returns to the game and the player cannot move. The close path is the one place you can least afford to skip the release, because it is the player's escape hatch. Always release focus and always call `cb`, even when the callback body is otherwise empty. One JS detail worth naming: `getElementById` only finds an element that already exists in the DOM. Because ` ``` Open `web/style.css` and paste this: ```css body { margin: 0; font-family: sans-serif; } .backdrop { display: grid; place-items: center; min-height: 100vh; background: #0b0d10; color: #fff; } .loadbar { width: 60%; height: 18px; background: rgba(140,140,140,.4); border-radius: 9px; overflow: hidden; } #loading-bar { width: 0%; height: 100%; background: #fff; transition: width .2s; } ``` Open `web/app.js` and paste this: ```js // Handover data is seeded by the server BEFORE the page renders. window.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => { const data = window.nuiHandoverData || {}; // serverAddress is injected automatically by FiveM; name comes from our server script. if (data.name) { document.getElementById('welcome').innerText = `Welcome, ${data.name}`; } }); let total = 0; // FiveM's boot sequence pushes these events as it streams the world in. const handlers = { startInitFunctionOrder(d) { total = d.count; }, initFunctionInvoking(d) { document.getElementById('loading-bar').style.width = ((d.idx / total) * 100) + '%'; }, startDataFileEntries(d) { total = d.count; }, performMapLoadFunction() { // No per-step index here; just nudge the bar toward done. const bar = document.getElementById('loading-bar'); bar.style.width = Math.min(parseFloat(bar.style.width || 0) + 2, 100) + '%'; } }; window.addEventListener('message', (e) => { (handlers[e.data.eventName] || function () {})(e.data); }); ``` ### Step 4 - Seed the player's name from the server _Goal: The loading screen greets each player by name._ Loading-screen handover data comes from a **server script**, because the player has not finished connecting yet, there is no client to send it. Add a `server.lua` (and a `server_script 'server.lua'` line to your `fxmanifest.lua`): ```lua AddEventHandler('playerConnecting', function(name, _, deferrals) local src = source deferrals.handover({ name = GetPlayerName(src) }) end) ``` This runs in the **server console's** runtime, not F8. `deferrals.handover` ships a Lua table to the connecting player's loading screen, where it arrives as `window.nuiHandoverData`. FiveM automatically adds `serverAddress` to that object, so even with no script you would still see `window.nuiHandoverData.serverAddress`. ### Step 5 - Enable and connect _Goal: You see the custom screen with your name while joining._ Add `ensure qu_loadingscreen` to `server.cfg`, then in the **server console** (FXServer window or txAdmin **Live Console**) run: ```text ensure qu_loadingscreen ``` Fully disconnect, then reconnect with `connect 127.0.0.1:30120` from the FiveM client. The screen renders before your character spawns. Expected output: ``` A dark page reading "Welcome, [your name]" with a white bar that fills as the world streams in, then vanishes the moment you spawn. ``` ### How the loading-screen channel differs The page listens with the same `window.addEventListener('message', ...)` you used for `SendNUIMessage`, but who fires the messages is the whole difference. - **You do not send these messages. FiveM does.** During connection the engine streams the world and fires progress events as it goes: `startInitFunctionOrder` and `initFunctionInvoking` (init functions), `startDataFileEntries` and `performMapLoadFunction` (data files and map load). Each arrives as a DOM `message` with an `eventName` field, exactly like a `SendNUIMessage` payload, except the boot sequence is the sender. You read `e.data.eventName` and update your bar. The full list lives in the FiveM loading-screen docs; you do not have to handle every event, only the ones whose `count`/`idx` numbers you want to turn into a progress bar. - **Data arrives once, up front, via `window.nuiHandoverData`, not `SendNUIMessage`.** Because the page renders before any client code runs, there is no Lua client to push it state. Instead the server seeds it during `playerConnecting` with `deferrals.handover`, and the page reads `window.nuiHandoverData` on `DOMContentLoaded`. Treat anything in there as display-only and escape it: use `innerText`, never `innerHTML`, so a player whose name contains markup cannot inject it into your page. - **There is no callback and no focus to manage.** A loading screen does not call back into Lua and you never call `SetNuiFocus`, the player is not in the world yet. `loadscreen_cursor 'yes'` is the only input switch, and only if the page has something to click. > **By default FiveM shuts the loading screen for you** > As soon as the player spawns, FiveM tears the loading screen down automatically, so a plain `loadscreen` needs no shutdown code. You only take over the lifetime if you add `loadscreen_manual_shutdown 'yes'` to the manifest, which keeps the screen up until your **client** code calls `ShutdownLoadingScreen()` (and `ShutdownLoadingScreenNui()` to also remove the NUI layer). People use manual shutdown to hold the screen through a character-selection step. The trap: enable manual shutdown, forget to call the shutdown native, and every player is stuck on the loading screen forever, fully connected but never seeing the game. ### Check yourself: Your loading screen shows the progress bar fine, but `window.nuiHandoverData.name` is always undefined even though serverAddress is present. Where is the bug? The `name` field is missing because nothing on the **server** put it there. `serverAddress` is injected by FiveM automatically, which is why it survives; `name` only exists if a server script calls `deferrals.handover({ name = ... })` inside `playerConnecting`. Check that you have a `server_script` line in the manifest and that the handover runs server-side, not in a client file, `playerConnecting` and `deferrals` exist only on the server. ## If something went wrong ## Common errors and fixes - `The menu never appears after /openui` -> The page starts hidden on purpose and only reveals on the open message. Open the NUI devtools at http://localhost:13172, check the Console for a JS error, and confirm app.js is listed in the files block so the browser can load it. - `Mouse and movement are frozen after closing` -> Focus was captured but never released. Make sure SetNuiFocus(false, false) runs in the callback. The game is not frozen, input is still being routed to the browser. - `Clicking the button does nothing, no callback prints` -> The fetch URL or endpoint is wrong. Use https://${GetParentResourceName()}/buttonClicked in browser-side JS, and make sure the RegisterNUICallback name is buttonClicked. Confirm the request in the devtools Network tab. - `The button works but the menu stays open` -> The Lua callback never called cb. Every RegisterNUICallback must call cb exactly once, even cb({}), or the fetch promise never resolves and the .then that hides the menu never runs. ## What you can do now ## Recap - Declare a NUI page with ui_page and whitelist every asset it loads in the files block, and explain why a missing file 404s silently. - Capture and release input with SetNuiFocus, knowing the first boolean is the keyboard/input focus and the second is the mouse cursor. - Push state from Lua to JS with SendNUIMessage and read it off event.data in a window message listener. - Send a payload from JS back to Lua with https://${GetParentResourceName()}/ and read it as the data argument of RegisterNUICallback. - Call cb exactly once to resolve the fetch, and prove the round-trip with a chat line carrying the data the page sent. - Open the NUI devtools at http://localhost:13172 to inspect the Console and Network tabs of a live page. ## Try it yourself Turn the one button into a two-button menu that tells Lua which one was pressed. Add a second button to `index.html` and send the button's own label in the `fetch` body: ```js document.querySelectorAll('button').forEach((btn) => { btn.addEventListener('click', () => { fetch(`https://${GetParentResourceName()}/buttonClicked`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({ label: btn.textContent }) }).then(() => menu.classList.add('hidden')); }); }); ``` The Lua callback already reads `data.label`, so it needs no change. Restart `qu_nui_from_scratch`, open the menu, and click each button. Confirm the chat line names the exact button you pressed. You just used one callback to handle many UI actions by letting the page say which one fired, which is how real menus route every option through a single endpoint instead of one callback per button. ## References - FiveM NUI development guide: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/nui-development/full-screen-nui/ - FiveM NUI callbacks: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/nui-development/nui-callbacks/ - FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/ - FiveM native reference: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/

title: Lesson 28 · Debugging like a developer · Quasar Academy
ai:
Something will break. That is not a maybe, it is a certainty. The difference between a beginner and a developer is not that the developer writes perfect code. The difference is that the developer knows how to *read the error*, find the line, and fix it calmly. This lesson teaches you that skill.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll ship: Structured logging in your scripts, a pcall wrapper for risky code, and the ability to read any FiveM error message.
- Time: ~30 minutes
- You'll learn: Logging with tags, F8 vs server console, pcall for error recovery, stack traces, data-collection checklist, performance basics, and common error messages decoded.
- Prereqs: Functions (Lesson 06), client vs server (Lesson 10).

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds tagged logging, a `pcall` wrapper, and a stack trace as a working FiveM resource named `qu_debugging_like_a_developer`. It is a client-side resource on purpose, so the output lands in F8, the in-game console. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it works, the next section takes the code apart line by line so you can read any error you ever hit.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_debugging_like_a_developer
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_debugging_like_a_developer/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
```

Older tutorials add a `lua54 'yes'` line here. As of June 2025 that setting is deprecated and ignored: Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime, so you leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local function log(message)
    print('[qu_debugging_like_a_developer] ' .. message)
end

local function risky()
    error('planned failure')
end

RegisterCommand('debugfail', function()
    local ok, result = pcall(risky)

    if ok then
        log('risky() succeeded, returned: ' .. tostring(result))
    else
        log('risky() failed, error was: ' .. tostring(result))
        log(debug.traceback('stack at point of failure', 2))
    end
end, false)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the correct console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

```text
ensure qu_debugging_like_a_developer
```

Save. Because this folder is brand new, the running server does not know it exists yet, so run these two lines in the txAdmin Live Console, one at a time:

```text
refresh
ensure qu_debugging_like_a_developer
```

`refresh` rescans the `resources` folder so the server discovers your new folder. `ensure` then starts it (and on later edits, `ensure` restarts it). Use `restart qu_debugging_like_a_developer` only after the resource has already been started once in this session.

Now join the server, press F8 to open the in-game console, and run the test there:

```text
/debugfail
```

Because this is a `client_script`, the print lands in F8, not in txAdmin. You will see two lines:

Expected output:

```
[qu_debugging_like_a_developer] risky() failed, error was: @qu_debugging_like_a_developer/client.lua:6: planned failure
[qu_debugging_like_a_developer] stack at point of failure
stack traceback:
  [the exact frames here depend on your FiveM build; copy what F8 prints]
```

The exact line number after `client.lua:` will match wherever the `error(...)` call sits in your file. In the code above that is line 6.

## How it works

You typed `/debugfail` and instead of your server crashing, you got two calm, tagged lines telling you exactly what broke and where. That is the whole lesson in one sentence: a developer does not avoid errors, a developer catches them, reads them, and points at the failing line. Now take the code apart so you can do that with any error, not just this one.

### The tagged log helper

```lua
local function log(message)
    print('[qu_debugging_like_a_developer] ' .. message)
end
```

This is a tiny wrapper around `print`. It does one thing: it glues a tag, `[qu_debugging_like_a_developer]`, onto the front of every message before printing it. The `..` operator joins two strings end to end, so `'[tag] ' .. message` becomes one line.

Why bother instead of calling `print` directly. On a real server, twenty resources are all printing into the same console at the same time. Raw `print('it worked')` lines are noise. You cannot tell which script said what. A tag turns the firehose into something you can filter. In F8 or txAdmin you can search for your tag and see only your own lines. Defining `log` once also means that if you later want to add a timestamp, route to a file, or silence logging in production, you change one function instead of fifty scattered prints. That is the difference between scattering `print` and having a logging habit.
