Add the base line, run npm run build again, then restart qu_nui_react. - `Couldn't load resource qu_nui_react: ui/dist/index.html not found` -> You never ran the build, so dist/ does not exist. cd into resources/qu_nui_react/ui and run npm install then npm run build before ensuring the resource. - `Code changes do not show up in game after restart` -> You edited ui/src but did not rebuild. The game serves ui/dist. Run npm run build in the ui/ folder, then restart qu_nui_react. - `Mouse and movement are frozen after closing` -> Focus was captured but never released. Confirm SetNuiFocus(false, false) runs in the close callback and that the React Close button reaches https://${GetParentResourceName()}/close. - `The button POSTs but the panel stays open` -> The Lua close callback never called cb. Every RegisterNUICallback must call cb exactly once, even cb({}), or the fetch promise never resolves and the .then that sets open to false never runs. ## What you can do now ## Recap - Lay out a NUI resource as a ui/ source project plus a generated ui/dist build artifact, and explain why the game only ever loads dist. - Configure Vite for NUI with base set to a relative path so hashed assets resolve under the cfx-nui scope. - Point ui_page and the files block at ui/dist, using assets/*.js and *.css globs so the whitelist survives every rebuild. - Drive React state from a typed SendNUIMessage, switching on a type field and cleaning up the message listener in useEffect. - POST from React back to Lua with https://${GetParentResourceName()}/ and resolve the fetch with cb in the callback. - Run the build-then-refresh dev loop without debugging a change that was never compiled, and use npm run dev for a faster inner loop. ## Try it yourself Make the round-trip carry data both ways. Add a Deposit button next to Close that sends an amount to Lua, and have Lua reply with the new balance so the React panel updates without reopening. On the React side, add a second endpoint and use the value `cb` returns: ```jsx function deposit() { nuiPost('deposit', { amount: 500 }) .then((res) => res.json()) .then((data) => setBalance(data.balance)); } ``` On the Lua side, register the matching callback and reply with the updated number through `cb`: ```lua local balance = 4200 RegisterNUICallback('deposit', function(data, cb) balance = balance + (data.amount or 0) print('[qu_nui_react] deposit, new balance ' .. balance) cb({ balance = balance }) end) ``` Rebuild the ui with `npm run build`, restart `qu_nui_react`, open `/phone`, and click Deposit. Confirm the balance on screen jumps to `$4700` without closing the panel, and that F8 prints the new balance. You just used the `cb` reply as a real return value, which is how a NUI app reads a server-authoritative result back into its own state instead of guessing. Keep the boundary rule in mind: a production version would route that deposit through a server event so the server, not the client, owns the real balance. ## References - FiveM NUI development guide: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/nui-development/full-screen-nui/ - FiveM NUI callbacks: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/nui-development/nui-callbacks/ - FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/ - Vite build guide: https://vite.dev/guide/build.html - Vite 8 release: https://vite.dev/blog/announcing-vite8 - React versions: https://react.dev/versions - React useEffect reference: https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffect

title: P14 · Shipping · Quasar Academy
ai:
A restaurant does not open for guests without prepped stations, a backup line, and a rollback plan if a dish fails. Same here: convars pinned, ACE locked, the database backed up, monitoring on, and a known-good build to revert to. You will build one small resource named `qu_release` that prints a **boot header** on start, then rehearse a backup and a one-command rollback before you ever touch production.

### What this lesson covers
- You will learn: The pre-flight, the hot-standby, the rollback path.
- Time: ~30 min
- Prereqs: P8 anti-cheat patterns, P9 persistence, and secret handling from convars/server.cfg.
- Pass condition: Staging restore drill, rollback command, and boot header proof are captured before production launch.
- Project: release-v1

### Before you start
- I have a free license key from the Cfx.re Portal (portal.cfx.re) in my server.cfg.
- I have a staging server running the same FXServer artifact version as production.
- I can open txAdmin in my browser and read its Live Console.
- I can export my database to a .sql file (HeidiSQL, mysqldump, or a host snapshot button).

> **Shipping is a checklist, not a feature**
> Treat go-live as a readiness checklist. Each item flips green or it does not ship: pin the build, lock permissions, back up the database, watch for trouble, and keep one command that rolls you back to the last known-good build. Do one step, read the proof, then move to the next step.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the release resource
_Goal: One folder holds your boot-header proof._

txAdmin ships bundled inside FXServer, so you already have an admin panel at `http://localhost:40120`; you do not install it separately. (On a brand-new server the first visit shows txAdmin's one-time setup wizard and a master PIN; once setup is done, that same URL is where you reach the Live Console used below.) Inside your server's `resources` folder, create one folder for this lesson:

```text
resources/qu_release/
fxmanifest.lua
server.lua
VERSION
```

Put your release tag in `VERSION` (no quotes, no extension content beyond the tag):

```text
v1.0.0
```

### Step 2 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FXServer knows which file to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this. Note there is **no** `lua54 'yes'` line. Lua 5.4 is the only runtime now, so that line is deprecated and ignored. Leave it out.

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'

files {
'VERSION'
}
```

### Step 3 - Print a boot header
_Goal: Every start logs the release tag in the first lines._

During an incident you should never have to ask "what version is running?". Open `server.lua` and paste this. It reads `VERSION` from the resource and prints a short header when the resource starts.

```lua
AddEventHandler('onResourceStart', function(resource)
if resource ~= GetCurrentResourceName() then return end

local version = LoadResourceFile(GetCurrentResourceName(), 'VERSION') or 'dev'
version = version:gsub('%s+', '')

print('[qu_release] release : ' .. version)
print('[qu_release] game build: ' .. GetConvar('sv_enforceGameBuild', 'unset'))
print('[qu_release] boot ok')
end)
```

### Step 4 - Pin the build and lock permissions
_Goal: Clients cannot drift; admins cannot kill the server by accident._

Open `server.cfg`. Choose the GTA content build intentionally, then load and lock the resource. The `sv_enforceGameBuild` value comes from Cfx.re's supported **game-build list**, not the FXServer artifacts page. Keep the official recipe's value unless your assets require another supported build.

```text
# license: free from the Cfx.re Portal (portal.cfx.re). Required even locally.
sv_licenseKey "cfxk_yourkeyhere"

# GTA content build, not an FXServer artifact number; verify the live supported list
sv_enforceGameBuild 3751

set onesync on
sv_maxclients 48

# lock commands: allow admins, but deny quit so a misclick cannot stop the server
add_ace group.admin command allow
add_ace group.admin command.quit deny
add_principal identifier.fivem:0000000 group.admin

# keep secrets out of git; rotate them after exposure and on your security schedule
exec server-secrets.cfg

ensure qu_release
```

### Step 5 - Restart on staging and read the header
_Goal: The boot header appears with no errors below it._

Open the txAdmin Live Console in your browser at `http://localhost:40120`. Restart only this resource:

```text
restart qu_release
```

Read the first lines. You should see the header, and crucially nothing red underneath it.

Expected output:

```
[qu_release] release : v1.0.0
[qu_release] game build: 3751
[qu_release] boot ok
```

### Step 6 - Back up the database before you deploy
_Goal: A restorable snapshot exists on disk._

A backup you have never made is hope. Take a snapshot before any production deploy. On a hosted panel this is the database backup button. On a machine with `mysqldump` you can run:

```bash
mariadb-dump -u BACKUP_USER -p --single-transaction --quick --routines fivem > backup-before-release-v1.sql
```

Swap `BACKUP_USER` and `fivem` for a MariaDB account with the required backup privileges and your database name. The `-p` flag prompts for the password instead of exposing it in shell history or the process list. `--single-transaction` gives a consistent snapshot for transactional InnoDB tables; check the command's exit status and test a restore. Keep a copy off the game host.

Expected output:

```
backup-before-release-v1.sql written, size greater than 0 bytes
```

### Step 7 - Rehearse the rollback path
_Goal: One pair of commands returns you to the last known-good build._

Before you ever need it, prove the rollback works on staging. Keep the previous version's folder on disk as `qu_release_v0`. To roll back, stop the new one and start the old one from the txAdmin Live Console:

```text
stop qu_release
ensure qu_release_v0
```

The old boot header should print, telling you the previous version is live again. This is muscle memory you want before launch night, not during it.

Expected output:

```
[qu_release_v0] release : v0.9.0
[qu_release_v0] boot ok
```

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Server refuses to start: invalid license key.` -> Get a free key from the Cfx.re Portal at portal.cfx.re and paste it into sv_licenseKey. It is required even for a local test server.
- `txAdmin will not open.` -> It is bundled in FXServer and runs on http://localhost:40120 on first boot. Use the Recommended (green) artifact channel and check that port is not blocked.
- `Could not load resource qu_release.` -> Open fxmanifest.lua and remove any lua54 'yes' line. Confirm fx_version 'cerulean' and game 'gta5' are present and the quotes match.
- `Boot header reads release : dev.` -> The VERSION file is missing or not in the files block. Add files { 'VERSION' } to fxmanifest.lua and put the tag in VERSION.
- `Players report a game-build mismatch after an update.` -> Check sv_enforceGameBuild against Cfx.re's supported game-build list and your asset requirements. Change it only after staging, then perform a full restart.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Build qu_release with fxmanifest.lua, server.lua, and a VERSION file, with no deprecated lua54 line.
- Print a boot header so the running release and enforced GTA game build show in the first console lines.
- Pin sv_enforceGameBuild to a current build and lock admin commands with ACE.
- Take a database backup and rehearse a stop / ensure rollback to the last known-good build.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Server shows in browser list but nobody can join | Port 30120 is blocked by firewall or router | Forward port 30120 (TCP + UDP) on your router. Allow it in Windows Firewall |
| Players join but disconnect after a few seconds | Missing resources or server.cfg error | Check the Live Console for red error lines. One failing resource can drop all connections |
| "Direct Connect" link doesn't work for friends | They're using the wrong IP or port | Give them `fivem://connect/YOUR_PUBLIC_IP:30120`. Find your public IP at whatismyip.com |
| Server runs fine locally but lags with players | Your upload speed or CPU can't handle the load | Check your internet upload speed. FiveM needs at least 5 Mbps upload per 10 players |
| Cfx.re join code doesn't work | The server isn't registered or the code expired | Register at portal.cfx.re. Join codes need the server to be online and public |

## Try it yourself

Bump `VERSION` to `v1.0.1`, run `restart qu_release` in the txAdmin Live Console, and confirm the boot header prints the new tag with no errors below it. Then run the rollback pair (`stop qu_release`, `ensure qu_release_v0`) once and write down the exact header you saw, so you trust the path before launch night.

## References
- Cfx.re Portal (free license keys): https://portal.cfx.re/
- FiveM server setup with txAdmin: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-server-txadmin/
- Cfx.re supported GTA game-build list: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/#sv_enforcegamebuild-build
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: Step 00 · FiveM in 2026: orientation · Quasar Academy

toc.label: 1. Plan first or regret it later
2. What you need before you start

ai:
Do not start by downloading 30 scripts. Start by deciding what you are building and what each part depends on. This lesson gives a complete beginner the map first, then sends you to the detailed installation lessons in the right order.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A one-page server plan and dependency map you can use before buying hosting or installing scripts.
- Time: 20-30 minutes. No installs yet.
- You need: A browser and a blank file named server-plan.md.
- Pass condition: Your plan names the server idea, hosting route, framework shortlist, required systems, dependencies, install order, and first test.
- Verified: June 2026 against current Cfx.re, txAdmin, ESX, QBCore, Qbox, and Community Ox sources.

### Before you start
- I want to own or operate a FiveM server, even if I have never configured one before.
- I can create and edit one plain-text or Markdown file.
- I will check official documentation before trusting an old video, copied config, or Discord message.

> **Your first deliverable is a plan, not a server**
> By the end, `server-plan.md` will tell you what to install, why you need it, and what must load first. That small file prevents incompatible framework purchases, missing dependencies, and random `server.cfg` ordering later.

## 1. Plan first or regret it later

The fastest way to waste a weekend is to install resources before deciding what the server is. Write the promise, hosting route, framework shortlist, must-have systems, and dependency order first. The plan can change later. Its job is to stop random installs today.

### The map in plain English

A roleplay server is a stack of parts. Each part has one job:

| Part | What it does | Typical 2026 choice |
| --- | --- | --- |
| FiveM server platform | Runs FXServer, networking, resources, and player connections. | Cfx.re FXServer with txAdmin |
| Database | Keeps money, characters, vehicles, jobs, and inventories after a restart. | MariaDB |
| Database connector | Lets server-side resources query the database. | oxmysql |
| Framework | Gives resources a shared model for players, jobs, accounts, and callbacks. | ESX Legacy, QBCore, or Qbox |
| Libraries and systems | Add reusable menus, targeting, inventory, voice, and other shared features. | ox_lib, ox_target, inventory choice, pma-voice |
| Gameplay resources | Add the actual jobs, housing, garages, phones, maps, and other features. | Compatible resources you choose |

The order matters because upper layers depend on the layers below them. A garage resource cannot save a vehicle if its database tables or connector do not exist. A QBCore job resource cannot run on an ESX server unless the author explicitly supports both.

### Build your server plan

Create a blank file called `server-plan.md`, then complete each step below. The examples are starting points, not decisions made for you.

### Step 1 - Write the server promise
_Goal: a stranger can understand the server in one sentence._

Write who the server is for, the style of roleplay, and what players should spend most of their time doing.

```md
# My FiveM server plan

## Server promise
A beginner-friendly serious-roleplay city for 32 players, built around police,
EMS, mechanic work, player-owned businesses, and a controlled economy.
```

Avoid vague promises such as "the best city" or "everything included." A useful promise limits the first version so you can actually test it.

### Step 2 - Choose local or hosted
_Goal: you know where the first working server will run._

Pick one route for the first setup:

| Choose | Use it when | First proof |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Local Windows PC | You are learning alone and want cheap, safe testing. | FiveM connects to `127.0.0.1:30120`. |
| Hosted server or VPS | Staff or remote testers need access now. | A second PC connects using the hosted address. |

Add the decision and reason to your plan:

```md
## Hosting route
Route: Local Windows PC
Why: I am learning alone and do not need public access yet.
Move to hosting when: two remote testers are ready.
```

Do not mix a Windows-local tutorial with Linux VPS paths. The concepts are the same, but folders, permissions, firewall setup, and service management differ.

### Step 3 - Map the platform and data layers
_Goal: you can name the program behind each basic server job._

Record these roles in your own words:

- **FXServer artifact:** the Cfx.re server program. Pin the build you test successfully so you can roll back after an update.
- **txAdmin:** the browser control panel included with FXServer. It starts, stops, monitors, and configures the server.
- **Cfx.re Portal:** the current place to create and manage the server license key. Use `https://portal.cfx.re`, not old Keymaster instructions.
- **MariaDB:** the database engine that stores persistent server data.
- **HeidiSQL:** a desktop database client used to inspect MariaDB and import SQL files.
- **oxmysql:** a FiveM resource that lets server-side scripts query MariaDB.

Add this section:

```md
## Foundation
Platform: FXServer with txAdmin
License management: Cfx.re Portal
Database: MariaDB
Database viewer: HeidiSQL
Database connector: oxmysql
Artifact rollback record: artifacts.md
```

### Step 4 - Shortlist a framework
_Goal: you compare compatibility instead of choosing from hype._

A framework gives resources shared concepts such as players, jobs, money, and callbacks. Use one primary framework.

- **ESX Legacy:** established ecosystem and broad third-party script availability.
- **QBCore:** established QB ecosystem with its own conventions and resources.
- **Qbox:** a QBCore-derived framework that is built around the ox ecosystem and provides an official txAdmin recipe.

There is no universal winner. Check the official recipe, documentation, maintenance activity, and every must-have resource you plan to use. Do not buy an ESX-only package before choosing Qbox, or a QB-only package before choosing ESX.

```md
## Framework shortlist
Candidate 1:
Candidate 2:
Must-have resources checked for compatibility:
Final choice: Not chosen until compatibility checks are complete
```

### Step 5 - Separate must-have systems from later ideas
_Goal: the first playable version has a controlled scope._

Make two lists. The first list contains only what is required for a player to join and complete your server's basic roleplay loop. Everything else waits.

```md
## Must have for the first playable test
- Framework and character system
- Database connector
- Inventory
- Voice
- One legal job and one emergency job
- One vehicle shop and garage path

## Add after the first test passes
- Housing
- Custom phone
- Extra jobs
- Paid maps and vehicle packs
```

This is scope control, not a permanent restriction. A small server that starts cleanly is useful. A huge resource folder with 200 errors is not.

### Step 6 - Build the dependency map
_Goal: each resource has an owner, source, and prerequisites before installation._

For each must-have system, record the exact resource, official source, supported framework, database needs, and dependencies.

```md
## Dependency map
| System | Resource | Framework support | Needs | SQL included | Official source checked |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Database connector | oxmysql | Framework independent | MariaDB | No | Yes |
| Shared UI/library | ox_lib | Check each resource | None | No | Yes |
| Inventory | To decide | To verify | Framework, ox_lib, oxmysql | To verify | No |
| Voice | pma-voice | Standalone | OneSync/audio config | No | No |
```

Never assume a dependency from a folder name. Read the current README, documentation, release notes, and `fxmanifest.lua` for the exact version you will install.

### Step 7 - Turn dependencies into an install order
_Goal: you have a safe sequence and a test after every layer._

Install the foundation before gameplay resources. The exact names vary, but the reasoning stays stable:

```text
FXServer + txAdmin
  -> database and connection string
  -> database connector
  -> framework recipe and framework dependencies
  -> shared libraries
  -> inventory, target, voice, and other core systems
  -> one gameplay resource
  -> test, back up, then add the next resource
```

Do not copy that diagram directly into `server.cfg`. Official recipes often manage multiple resources and category folders for you. The later `server.cfg` lesson teaches the real `ensure` order for the stack you install.

```md
## Install and test order
1. Start the base recipe and confirm txAdmin has no repeating errors.
2. Confirm the database connection before installing database-backed scripts.
3. Join with one clean test account.
4. Install one resource.
5. Restart that resource and check txAdmin plus F8.
6. Back up the working state before adding another resource.
```

### Step 8 - Define the first proof
_Goal: you know exactly when planning is finished and setup can begin._

Finish your file with a pass condition:

```md
## First proof
[ ] Hosting route chosen
[ ] Framework shortlist checked against must-have resources
[ ] Foundation tools named
[ ] Dependencies recorded from official sources
[ ] Install order written
[ ] First test defined: one player connects and creates a character
[ ] Rollback rule: back up before every new resource or update
```

Expected output:

```
`server-plan.md` explains what you are building, where it will run, which framework candidates fit, what the required systems depend on, and what you will test first.
```

## 2. What you need before you start

You need a legitimate GTA V installation and FiveM client, a Cfx.re account, a local Windows PC or hosted machine, an editor, archive tool, and a safe place for credentials. Database-backed frameworks also need MariaDB plus a database client such as HeidiSQL.

Create the server license key through [portal.cfx.re](https://portal.cfx.re/). Keymaster instructions are obsolete. Treat the license key, database password, Tebex secrets, and webhooks as credentials. Do not place them in screenshots or public repositories.

## 3. Installing server artifacts

An artifact is the FXServer program, not a GTA game build and not your resource folder. Download it only from the official Windows or Linux artifact page used by the current Cfx.re setup documentation.

- For a new test server, select a current supported build.
- For an existing server, record the working build before changing it.
- Keep server binaries separate from `server-data`, resources, configuration, and database backups.
- Stop the server, replace the binaries, start it, run `status`, connect once, and roll back if the proof fails.

Do not blindly teach "always install latest." Current is useful only when you can test and return to the last known-good build.

## 4. Setting up with txAdmin

txAdmin ships with FXServer. It is the browser panel for profiles, recipes, console output, resources, players, scheduled restarts, and server settings.

The safe first run is: launch FXServer, open the printed txAdmin URL, link the correct Cfx.re account, create a profile, choose an official recipe or blank template, verify the data path, and let the recipe finish before editing files. Run `status` in the Live Console and save the recipe name and artifact build in your notes.

## 5. How it all fits together

The request path is easier to debug when every layer has one name:

```text
FiveM client
  -> FXServer and txAdmin
  -> framework resources
  -> oxmysql
  -> MariaDB
```

Gameplay resources sit on the framework and shared libraries. MariaDB stores persistent records. HeidiSQL only lets an operator inspect that database; it is not part of the running FiveM request path.

## 6. server.cfg walkthrough

`server.cfg` is the startup plan. It declares endpoints, server identity, convars, the license key, permissions, and resource order. Use the config generated by the current recipe as the starting point.

```cfg
endpoint_add_tcp "0.0.0.0:30120"
endpoint_add_udp "0.0.0.0:30120"
sv_hostname "My RP Server"
sv_maxclients 32
sv_licenseKey "YOUR_PRIVATE_KEY"

ensure oxmysql
# framework recipe resources next
# shared libraries and systems next
# gameplay resources last
```

That is a teaching skeleton, not a complete production file. Do not copy old `set onesync on` lines or Steam-based admin examples without checking the current command and permission references. The dedicated `server.cfg` lessons provide the complete version.

## 7. Keeping files organized

Keep disposable server binaries apart from data you own. Inside `resources`, bracket folders such as `[framework]`, `[standalone]`, `[voice]`, `[maps]`, and `[vehicles]` organize related resources. They are category folders, not resources themselves.

Do not rename a downloaded resource until you know its exports, dependencies, or documentation do not expect the original name. Keep one backup outside the live server and record every manual edit.

## 8. Tools you will use

| Tool | Job |
| --- | --- |
| VS Code | Edit configs, Lua, JavaScript, and documentation. |
| 7-Zip or WinRAR | Extract downloaded archives without hiding extensions. |
| WinSCP or another SFTP client | Move files to a hosted Linux server. |
| MariaDB | Store persistent server data. |
| HeidiSQL | Inspect schemas, import SQL, and run controlled queries. |
| Git and GitHub Desktop | Record changes and restore known-good files. |
| txAdmin | Operate FXServer and read server console output. |

## 9. MariaDB, HeidiSQL, and oxmysql

MariaDB is the database engine. HeidiSQL is an operator interface. oxmysql is the server resource that executes parameterized queries from scripts. A framework may use tables named `users`, `players`, or something else; never assume one framework's schema applies to another.

Keep the connection string server-side, verify the database host accepts connections from the server, and never expose credentials with a replicated convar.

## 10. Running SQL safely

Back up before importing vendor SQL. Select the correct database, open the file, read it, and stop if it contains destructive statements you do not understand. `CREATE TABLE` is expected during setup. `DROP TABLE`, broad `DELETE`, and schema-changing `ALTER TABLE` statements require a migration and rollback plan.

After running the file, refresh the schema and confirm the exact tables or columns the resource documentation names. Do not ignore every "table already exists" message; compare the expected and installed schema first.

## 11. ESX vs QBCore vs Qbox

- **ESX Legacy** has an established ecosystem and many third-party resources.
- **QBCore** has its own established conventions, documentation, and package ecosystem.
- **Qbox** is QBCore-derived, uses the ox ecosystem heavily, and provides an official recipe and migration guidance.

Choose based on maintained recipes, the compatibility of your must-have resources, staff knowledge, and migration cost. Do not install two primary frameworks together unless you are executing a documented migration or bridge plan.

## 12. Adding resources

Read the README first. Confirm framework, dependencies, database changes, config path, and required artifact or game build. Back up, install dependencies, upload one resource, import its reviewed SQL, add it after dependencies, then run `refresh` and `ensure RESOURCE_NAME`.

Check the server console, F8, and the visible feature. Do not install the next resource until this one passes.

## 13. What is fxmanifest.lua?

Every resource uses `fxmanifest.lua` to declare its metadata, scripts, files, and dependencies.

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script 'config.lua'
client_script 'client/main.lua'
server_script 'server/main.lua'

dependencies {
    'ox_lib'
}
```

FiveM uses Lua 5.4 by default in 2026. Do not add the retired `lua54 'yes'` directive. A dependency declaration tells FiveM what must be started, but your `server.cfg` should still make the intended order readable.

## 14. Vectors and placing things

`vector3(x, y, z)` stores a position. `vector4(x, y, z, heading)` stores a position plus facing direction. Whether a config needs three or four values depends on the resource. Use a heading when the spawned ped, player, or vehicle must face a direction; do not treat vector4 as universally required for every spawn.

## 15. Script config patterns

A good config exposes safe choices without forcing buyers to edit core logic. Expect tables for jobs, item names, prices, coordinates, framework adapters, webhooks, and feature toggles. Change one group at a time, preserve data types and commas, then restart only the affected resource and test the changed path.

## 16. Tracking changes with Git

Track server configuration and editable resources in Git, but exclude secrets, caches, logs, database dumps, and escrowed or licensed files you are not permitted to publish. A private repository does not make a leaked credential safe. If a secret is committed, rotate it.

Commit before each risky install and after each verified result. That turns "what changed?" into a diff instead of a guessing session.

## 17. Performance

Use `resmon` and the profiler before calling a resource slow. Avoid per-frame work when the player is far away, do not run database queries inside hot loops, and add timeouts to model or network waits. Measure idle and active behavior with the same test conditions, then change one cause at a time.

## 18. Security

The client requests; the server decides. Validate permissions, ownership, item counts, money, distance, state, and rate limits on the server before granting a valuable result. Never place database credentials, license keys, Tebex secrets, or webhook URLs in client or shared files.

ACE uses principals and objects. A valid pattern grants an object to a group, then attaches an identifier to that group. Do not copy the external guide's reversed Steam identifier example.

## 19. Debugging

Read the first real error, not the last symptom. The server console reports server scripts and startup failures. F8 reports client scripts. Check the named resource, file, line, missing dependency, wrong runtime side, database connection, and config syntax in that order.

Use `ensure` for the first start, `restart` after a running resource changes, and `refresh` when a new resource folder was added while the server was already running.

## 20. Common mistakes and next steps

## Common errors and fixes
- `I cannot choose a framework yet` -> Leave two candidates in the shortlist. Check your must-have resources against both official ecosystems before deciding.
- `I already bought scripts` -> Record each package in the dependency map. Do not install it until its supported framework, required libraries, SQL, and documented load order are known.
- `I want local testing and hosted staff access immediately` -> Pick one first proof. Build locally if you are learning alone; choose hosted if remote access is required now. Move only after the first route works.
- `A guide sends me to Keymaster` -> Use portal.cfx.re for current server management and license keys.
- `My plan contains dozens of launch-day systems` -> Move everything that is not required for the first playable loop into the later list.

Your plan is the map. Track A implements it one controlled layer at a time: client, tools, artifacts and txAdmin, database, `server.cfg`, console proof, then one resource at a time.

> **Corrections made from the source tutorial**
> The current license-key home is the Cfx.re Portal. Artifact updates require a recorded rollback. Qbox belongs in the framework comparison. Steam identifiers are not a general admin recipe. Database tables vary by framework. Lua 5.4 no longer needs a manifest flag.

### What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain the platform, database, connector, framework, libraries, and gameplay-resource layers.
- Choose local or hosted setup based on who needs access now.
- Compare ESX Legacy, QBCore, and Qbox using compatibility and official sources.
- Map dependencies before writing resource order into server.cfg.
- Limit the first playable version and test one resource at a time.
- Use portal.cfx.re and keep an artifact rollback record.

### Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Overwhelmed by all the options (ESX, QBCore, Qbox, ox, etc.) | This lesson is a MAP, not a test. You don't need to choose now | Read the "ESX vs QBCore vs Qbox" section. Pick one that matches your goals. You can switch later |
| Can't install anything because "I don't have a server yet" | You're supposed to plan first, THEN install | Write your server-plan.md. The next lessons walk you through each install step by step |
| "I already know some of this" — tempted to skip ahead | Skipping the planning step is why most servers fail in week one | At minimum, read "How it all fits together" and "ESX vs QBCore vs Qbox" — those two sections prevent the most common mistakes |

## Try it yourself

Give `server-plan.md` to someone who knows nothing about your idea. Ask them these four questions: What kind of server is this? Where will it run first? Which framework choices are still being considered? What must be installed before the first gameplay resource? If the file answers all four without you explaining it, your plan is ready and you can continue to the FiveM client lesson.

## References
- Cfx.re · Setting up a server: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-server/
- Cfx.re Portal · license keys: https://portal.cfx.re/
- txAdmin · official repository: https://github.com/citizenfx/txAdmin
- ESX Legacy · official documentation: https://docs.esx-framework.org/
- QBCore · official documentation: https://docs.qbcore.org/
- Qbox · official documentation: https://docs.qbox.re/
- Community Ox · official documentation: https://coxdocs.dev/

title: Module 02 · Set up VS Code for FiveM · Quasar Academy
ai:
Writing Lua in Notepad is how beginners burn hours on typos. The right editor catches the mistake *as you type* instead of in the server console after a restart. This lesson sets up VS Code the way working FiveM developers run it in 2026: the Lua language server plus the Cfx native definitions, so `RegisterCommand` autocompletes and a missing `end` lights up red.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A FiveM-ready editor: VS Code with the Lua language server and the Cfx Lua native definitions, so you get autocomplete and red squiggles before you ever restart the server.
- Time: ~15 minutes.
- You need: A Windows PC and a resources folder to open. No coding yet, just tooling.
- You'll learn: Install VS Code -> add the Lua extension -> add Cfx native definitions -> open your server as a workspace -> prove autocomplete works

### Before you start
- I have a resources folder (a local FiveM server) to open as a workspace.
- I can install Windows programs.
- I finished the orientation, so framework and library names are familiar.

> **Why two extensions, not one**
> The **Lua** extension (the language server) understands Lua syntax: it autocompletes your variables and flags missing `end` or `then`. It does *not* know FiveM exists. The **Cfx Lua** extension adds definitions for FiveM's thousands of natives, so `GetEntityCoords` autocompletes too. You want both.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Install VS Code
_Goal: VS Code opens and you can open a folder as a workspace._

Download and run the Windows User Installer, accept the defaults, and tick the **Open with Code** context-menu options on the Additional Tasks screen.

Expected output:

```
Right-clicking a folder in File Explorer shows "Open with Code".
```

### Step 2 - Install the Lua language server
_Goal: The Lua extension by sumneko is installed and enabled._

Open the Extensions panel (the four-squares icon in the left bar, or `Ctrl+Shift+X`). Search for **Lua** and install the one published by **sumneko** (the identifier is `sumneko.lua`, marketplace name "Lua"). This is the language server: autocomplete, go-to-definition, and live error checking.

Do not confuse it with older "Lua" extensions that only do syntax coloring. You want the one with millions of installs by sumneko.

Expected output:

```
The Extensions panel shows "Lua" by sumneko with a blue "Disable" / "Uninstall" state, meaning it is installed.
```

### Step 3 - Add the Cfx native definitions
_Goal: FiveM natives autocomplete, not just plain Lua._

Still in Extensions, search for **Cfx Lua** and install **cfxlua-vscode** published by **overextended** (identifier `overextended.cfxlua-vscode`). It plugs FiveM's native function definitions into the Lua language server.

Configs for this extension change over time, so treat its marketplace README as the source of truth for the exact `.luarc.json` snippet. The reliable parts below do not change.

### Step 4 - Open your server as a workspace and add .luarc.json
_Goal: The editor treats your resources as one Lua 5.4 project with FiveM globals known._

In VS Code choose **File -> Open Folder** and pick your `server-data` (or `resources`) folder. Opening the *folder*, not a single file, is what lets the language server see your whole project.

At the root of that folder, create a file named `.luarc.json` and paste this safe baseline. It sets the Lua version FiveM uses and stops false "undefined global" warnings for the most common FiveM globals:

```json
{
  "runtime.version": "Lua 5.4",
  "diagnostics.globals": [
    "exports",
    "RegisterCommand",
    "RegisterNetEvent",
    "AddEventHandler",
    "TriggerEvent",
    "TriggerServerEvent",
    "TriggerClientEvent",
    "Citizen",
    "vector3",
    "vector4",
    "GetResourceState",
    "GetPlayerName"
  ]
}
```

With the Cfx Lua extension installed, the full native list autocompletes on top of this. The `.luarc.json` is just the floor.

### Step 5 - Prove autocomplete works
_Goal: Typing a native shows a suggestion popup and a missing end shows a red squiggle._

Open any `client.lua` or `server.lua` (or make a scratch `test.lua`). Start typing:

```lua
RegisterComm
```

A suggestion popup should offer `RegisterCommand`. Press Tab to accept it. Then delete an `end` from a function and watch the red squiggle appear under the line.

Expected output:

```
Typing "RegisterComm" pops up a "RegisterCommand" suggestion, and removing an "end" underlines the code in red.
```

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `No autocomplete at all` -> You opened a single file, not a folder. Use File -> Open Folder on your resources/server-data folder, then reopen the .lua file.
- `Natives like GetEntityCoords are underlined as undefined` -> The Cfx Lua extension is not active. Confirm overextended.cfxlua-vscode is installed and reload the window (Ctrl+Shift+P -> Developer: Reload Window).
- `Everything is flagged red, even valid code` -> Two competing Lua extensions are installed. Keep sumneko.lua, uninstall any other syntax-only Lua extension, then reload.
- `Wrong Lua version warnings` -> Check .luarc.json has "runtime.version": "Lua 5.4". Lua 5.4 is the only FiveM runtime as of 2025, so no fxmanifest flag is needed.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Install VS Code with the Open with Code shortcut.
- Add the sumneko Lua language server for autocomplete and live error checking.
- Add the overextended Cfx Lua extension so FiveM natives autocomplete.
- Open your whole server folder as a workspace and add a .luarc.json baseline.
- Confirm the setup by watching RegisterCommand autocomplete and a missing end go red.

## Try it yourself

Open a real lesson resource (like `qu_first_line_of_lua`), delete a closing `end`, and watch the editor flag it before you ever restart the server. Put the `end` back. That red squiggle is the feedback loop you just bought yourself.

## References
- VS Code download: https://code.visualstudio.com/
- Lua (sumneko) on the marketplace: https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=sumneko.lua
- Cfx Lua (overextended) on the marketplace: https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=overextended.cfxlua-vscode
- cfxlua-vscode on GitHub: https://github.com/overextended/cfxlua-vscode

title: Module 02 · From localhost to VPS with Git · Quasar Academy
ai:
Editing files straight on a live server is how beginners take their server down at 2am with no way back. The professional loop is different: you code on your PC, you commit when it works, you push, and the VPS pulls a known-good version. Git is the time machine that lets you roll back the moment a deploy breaks. This lesson wires that loop end to end for one resource.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A safe deploy loop: develop on your PC, commit with GitHub Desktop, push to a private repo, then pull onto your VPS and restart, with no secrets ever leaving your machine.
- Time: ~30 minutes.
- You need: GitHub Desktop set up (the version-control lesson), a GitHub account, and a VPS you can reach over SSH.
- You'll learn: A private repo -> .gitignore for secrets -> clone on the VPS -> the edit/commit/push/pull/restart loop -> roll back a bad deploy

### Before you start
- I can commit and push with GitHub Desktop (the version-control lesson).
- I have a VPS and can open an SSH session or its web console.
- My resource runs locally and I can restart it in txAdmin.

> **Secrets never go in Git**
> Your license key, database password, and any Discord webhook are secrets. If you commit one, treat it as leaked the instant it reaches GitHub, even a private repo. You will use a `.gitignore` and server convars so secrets stay on each machine and never enter the repo. There is a recovery step at the bottom if you slip.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Add a .gitignore before the first commit
_Goal: Secret files are invisible to Git from the start._

Open your resource folder in VS Code and create a file named `.gitignore` at its root. List anything that holds a secret or is machine-specific:

```text
# secrets and machine-specific files, never committed
*.env
license-key.txt
db-creds.txt
config.secret.lua
node_modules/
```

Keep real config out of code. Instead of writing a key into a committed file, set it as a convar in `server.cfg` (which lives on the server and is never in the repo) and read it at runtime. The convars lesson goes deep on this, but here is the shape so the secrets claim is not hand-waved:

```lua
-- server.cfg (on the VPS, NOT in the repo):
--   set my_license_key "your-real-key-here"

-- server.lua (in the repo, no secret in sight):
local key = GetConvar('my_license_key', 'unset')
print('license key loaded:', key ~= 'unset')
```

The code ships the variable name, not the value. Each machine supplies its own value through its own `server.cfg`.

Expected output:

```
In GitHub Desktop, the Changes list does NOT show license-key.txt, .env, or db-creds.txt.
```

### Step 2 - Publish a PRIVATE repository
_Goal: Your code is on GitHub, private, with the secret files excluded._

In GitHub Desktop, commit your work, then choose **Publish repository**. Crucial: untick **Keep this code private** only if you truly want the world to read it. For a server, leave it **private**.

Expected output:

```
The repo appears on github.com with a "Private" badge, and no secret files are listed in it.
```

### Step 3 - Install Git on the VPS and clone into resources
_Goal: A first copy of the resource exists on the VPS, pulled from GitHub._

Open an SSH session to your VPS (on Windows, type `ssh user@your-vps-ip` in PowerShell or Windows Terminal; or use the browser-based console in your VPS provider's control panel). Install Git if it is missing (`sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y git` on Ubuntu/Debian). Because the repo is private, GitHub will not accept your account password on clone. You need a credential the VPS can use. The fastest one to create is a fine-grained personal access token:

1. On github.com, go to **Settings -> Developer settings -> Personal access tokens -> Fine-grained tokens -> Generate new token**.
2. Scope it to **Only select repositories** and pick this one repo. Under **Repository permissions**, set **Contents** to **Read-only**. That token can pull this repo and nothing else.
3. Set a short expiry (30 to 90 days) and copy the token once. GitHub shows it a single time.

Now clone into the server's resources folder. When Git asks for a username, enter your GitHub username; when it asks for a password, paste the token (not your real password):

```bash
cd /home/fivem/server-data/resources
git clone https://github.com/your-username/your-resource.git qu_deploy_demo
```

The last word, `qu_deploy_demo`, is the folder name Git creates on the VPS (it overrides the repo's own name). Every command later in this lesson uses that exact name, so if you pick a different folder name, swap it in everywhere `qu_deploy_demo` appears.

A read-only token is the safer choice for a deploy box: even if the VPS is compromised, the attacker can only read one repo, never push or touch your account. An SSH deploy key (a keypair added to the repo's **Deploy keys**) achieves the same scoping and avoids token expiry if you prefer keys. Then `ensure qu_deploy_demo` in `server.cfg` and restart the server once so it loads.

Expected output:

```
Cloning into 'qu_deploy_demo'...
remote: Enumerating objects ... done.
Resolving deltas: 100% ... done.
```

### Step 4 - Run the update loop
_Goal: A change made on your PC reaches the VPS with two commands and a restart._

This is the loop you repeat forever. On your PC: make the change, test locally, then commit and push in GitHub Desktop. On the VPS: pull and restart.

On the VPS, inside the resource folder:

```bash
cd /home/fivem/server-data/resources/qu_deploy_demo
git pull
```

Then in the txAdmin Live Console:

```bash
restart qu_deploy_demo
```

Expected output:

```
Example only — your hashes and filenames will differ:
Updating a1b2c3d..e4f5g6h
Fast-forward
 client.lua | 2 +-
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
```

### Step 5 - Roll back a bad deploy
_Goal: You can return the VPS to the last working commit in seconds._

A deploy breaks the server. Stay calm: Git remembers every version. On the VPS, see recent commits and reset to the last good one:

```bash
git log --oneline -n 5
git reset --hard <good_commit_hash>
```

`git log --oneline -n 5` lists your last five commits, newest first; each line starts with a short hash like `a1b2c3d` followed by its message. Replace `<good_commit_hash>` with the short hash from the line of the last commit you know worked (read the messages to find it). Do not type the angle brackets.

Then `restart qu_deploy_demo`. The resource is back to that exact known-good state. This is why you push working code in small commits: every commit is a save point you can return to.

Expected output:

```
HEAD is now at a1b2c3d your last working commit message
```

## How it works

The loop works because every machine plays one fixed role, and Git moves a known-good snapshot between them in one direction only. Four ideas make that safe.

### Why the pull was a fast-forward

When you ran `git pull`, the output said `Updating a1b2c3d..e4f5g6h` and `Fast-forward`. That range is two commit hashes: where the VPS was (`a1b2c3d`) and where GitHub now is (`e4f5g6h`). A fast-forward means the VPS had not made any commits of its own, so Git did not have to merge anything. It just slid the VPS's pointer forward to match GitHub and updated the files. No merge, no conflict, no decision to make. That is exactly what you want on a deploy box, and it only stays this clean if the VPS never commits.

### What the one-directional flow buys you

Your PC is the source of truth. The VPS is a read-only consumer that only ever pulls. The instant someone edits a file directly on the VPS, the VPS has a change GitHub does not know about, and the next `git pull` stops with `local changes would be overwritten`. Git is refusing to silently throw that edit away. The fix is never to edit on the VPS in the first place: make the change on your PC, push, and let the VPS pull it. One direction in, no surprises.

### What `git reset --hard` actually does

`git reset --hard <hash>` does two things at once. It moves `HEAD` (your current position) back to that commit, and it overwrites the working files on disk to match, discarding anything uncommitted. That is what makes it perfect for a rollback (snap the whole resource back to a known-good commit in one move) and dangerous everywhere else: a softer command like `git revert` records a new commit that undoes a change while keeping history and your other edits intact. Reach for `reset --hard` only when you genuinely want to erase whatever is currently on the VPS.

### Why a leaked secret stays leaked

A `.gitignore` pattern is matched against file paths: `*.env` blocks any file ending in `.env`, and `node_modules/` blocks that whole folder. But ignoring only works before the first commit of a file. If you already committed `license-key.txt`, deleting it in a later commit removes it from the latest version, not from history. Anyone can still check out the old commit and read it. That is why a committed secret is leaked the moment it reaches GitHub: the only real fix is to rotate the secret, then add the file to `.gitignore` so it never lands again.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `I committed license-key.txt / a password / a webhook` -> Treat it as leaked NOW. Revoke and regenerate the key on portal.cfx.re, change the DB password, and rotate the webhook. Then add the file to .gitignore. Deleting it in a later commit does not un-leak it.
- `git: command not found on the VPS` -> Install Git: sudo apt install -y git (Debian/Ubuntu). Confirm with git --version.
- `fatal: not a git repository` -> You are in the wrong folder. cd into the cloned resource folder (the one that contains a hidden .git folder) before git pull.
- `Authentication failed on clone/pull` -> Private repos reject account passwords. Use a GitHub personal access token or an SSH deploy key for the VPS.
- `git pull says 'local changes would be overwritten'` -> Someone edited files directly on the VPS. That breaks the loop. Note that git reset --hard origin/<your-branch> (your default branch is usually main, but some repos use master; run git branch to see it) will permanently discard those VPS edits to match GitHub, so first copy out anything you want to keep. If the edit matters, paste it into your PC copy, commit and push, then pull on the VPS instead. After that, only ever edit on your PC.
- `Pulled but nothing changed in game` -> You forgot the restart. git pull only updates files; restart qu_deploy_demo loads them.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Exclude secrets with .gitignore before the first commit, and read them at runtime with GetConvar.
- Publish a private GitHub repo from GitHub Desktop.
- Install Git on a VPS and clone a private repo into resources with a token or deploy key.
- Run the edit/commit/push on your PC, then pull and restart on the VPS.
- Roll back a broken deploy to the last good commit with git reset --hard.

## Try it yourself

Change one printed string in your resource on your PC, commit and push, then `git pull` and `restart` on the VPS. Confirm the new string appears in the server console. Then run `git log --oneline` on the VPS and find the commit you just deployed.

## References
- GitHub Desktop: https://desktop.github.com/
- GitHub docs · About README and private repos: https://docs.github.com/en/repositories
- Git docs · git reset: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-reset
- Cfx.re docs · Server commands and convars: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/

title: A3 · Server security baseline · Quasar Academy

toc.label: Prove it on your own server

ai:
Most beginner servers are not hacked by geniuses. They leak a license key on Discord, give every staffer god-mode, run a random script with a backdoor, or post their database webhook in a public channel. This is the baseline that closes those doors. Treat it as a checklist you run before you ever go public, and again after any big change.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll do: Lock down the things that get new servers wiped or robbed: admin permissions, secrets, resource trust, and webhook hygiene.
- Time: ~25 minutes.
- You need: A running server with txAdmin and a server.cfg you can edit.
- You'll learn: ACE permissions -> secret handling -> txAdmin roles -> resource trust -> a baseline you re-run before every launch

### Before you start
- I can edit server.cfg and restart the server.
- I have txAdmin admin access.
- I know where my license key and database password live.

> **The one rule under all of this**
> The server is the only thing you trust. Never let the client decide money, items, jobs, or admin power. Every check that matters happens server-side. Most exploits are a client lying to a server that believed it.

## The baseline

### Step 1 - Give permissions by role, not by person
_Goal: Admin power flows through ACE groups, not hardcoded names._

Use ACE permissions and groups so you can add or remove a staffer in one line. Grant the narrowest power that does the job; only owners get the parent `command` object that covers all server commands.

```text
add_ace group.admin command allow
add_principal identifier.fivem:00000 group.admin
```

In txAdmin, give moderators a limited role, not full admin. Review the admin list monthly and remove anyone who left.

### Step 2 - Keep secrets out of the repo and out of chat
_Goal: No key, password, or webhook is ever pasted where others can read it._

License key, database password, and Discord webhooks are secrets. Read them at runtime with convars in `server.cfg` (which is not in your public repo) and never paste them in Discord, screenshots, or videos. If one leaks, rotate it the same hour.

### Step 3 - Trust only resources you can read or vouch for
_Goal: No unknown script runs with full server access._

Every resource you `ensure` runs with full server power. Install from the official author only. Skim server-side files for suspicious `PerformHttpRequest` calls to unknown URLs, base64 blobs, or `load()`/`assert(load(...))` running fetched code. If you cannot read it and cannot trust the source, do not run it.

### Step 4 - Lock down webhooks and logging
_Goal: Your Discord logs cannot be spammed or read by outsiders._

Store webhook URLs in convars, send them only from the server side, and never expose them to clients. Anyone with the URL can post to that channel, so treat it like a password.

Expected output:

```
Your server.cfg has no plaintext secrets. ACE permissions use groups instead of individual names. Every installed resource passes the trust check. You can run this checklist in 10 minutes before any launch.
```

## How the ACE example works

Step 1 hands you two lines and tells you to trust them. That is exactly how servers end up with a permission system the owner cannot reason about. Take the two lines apart so you can write your own instead of pasting someone else's.

```text
add_ace group.admin command allow
add_principal identifier.fivem:00000 group.admin
```

The permission system has three pieces: principals, objects, and a verdict.

- A **principal** is a who. It can be a single person, identified by `identifier.fivem:<id>` or `identifier.discord:<id>`, or it can be a group like `group.admin`. Groups are themselves principals, which is the trick that makes the whole system scale.
- An **object** is a what. `command` means every command. `command.kick` means only the kick command. The dotted name is a tree, so `command` is the parent of every `command.something` under it.
- The verdict is `allow` or `deny`.

Read the lines back in plain English. `add_ace group.admin command allow` says the `group.admin` principal is allowed to use the `command` object, which is every command. `add_principal identifier.fivem:00000 group.admin` says the player whose full FiveM identifier is `00000` (a placeholder for the real value, which is matched exactly as a whole string, not by its ending) is a member of `group.admin`. The second argument of `add_principal` is the parent, so a child principal inherits everything its parent group is allowed.

That inheritance is the point. You grant a power to a group once, then you add or remove people by adding or removing one `add_principal` line. The staffer maps to a group, the group maps to a set of commands, and you never edit a command grant to onboard a new moderator.

`identifier.fivem:` and `identifier.discord:` are two possible identifiers for a player. A Discord identifier is an account ID, not a Discord-role check, and it may be unavailable when Discord identity is not present. Copy the full identifier from txAdmin and prefer a stable identifier that is present for your players. A typo silently grants nothing.

### Scope a moderator, do not clone an admin

A moderator does not need `command` (all commands). Grant only the objects the role actually uses, and explicitly deny the dangerous parents:

```text
add_ace group.mod command.kick allow
add_ace group.mod command.mute allow
add_ace group.mod command.quit deny
add_principal identifier.discord:000000000000000000 group.mod
```

`allow` and `deny` are not symmetric in importance. An object with no rule is already denied by default, so you do not have to deny everything. You add an explicit `deny` where a child could otherwise inherit `allow` from a parent. `command.quit` shuts the whole server down, so even on `group.admin` it is worth an explicit `add_ace group.admin command.quit deny` unless the owner alone holds it. The broader the parent-object grant, the more carefully you fence off destructive child commands.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Every staffer can run any command` -> Replace blanket admin with scoped ACE groups. Give moderators a limited txAdmin role.
- `License key or DB password seen in Discord` -> Rotate it now (new key at portal.cfx.re, new DB password), then move it to a convar in server.cfg.
- `A free script you installed phones home` -> Stop the resource, read its server files for unknown HTTP calls or load() of fetched code, and remove it if you cannot verify it.
- `Players give themselves money or items` -> Move the decision server-side. The client may request; only the server may grant, after a check.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| ACE permissions don't apply after a server restart | You didn't use `add_principal` to assign the group to a player | ACE groups are empty until you add principals. Use `add_principal identifier.fivem:XXXXX group.admin` in server.cfg |
| Resource stops another resource without permission | ACE doesn't cover `stop` commands | Add `add_ace resource.trusted_resource command.stop allow` for resources that need to control others |
| Secret leaked in a screenshot or video | You showed the console or config file with the key visible | Rotate the key immediately at portal.cfx.re. Never screenshot your server.cfg |
| "I don't know which resources to trust" | You installed resources without reading their code | Open the resource's server-side files. Look for `PerformHttpRequest` to unknown URLs or obfuscated code |

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Grant admin power through ACE groups and roles, not hardcoded names.
- Keep the license key, DB password, and webhooks in convars and never in chat.
- Vet every resource before you ensure it, because it runs with full access.
- Re-run this baseline before launch and after any major change.

## Prove it on your own server

Turn the ACE model into something you have watched work, not read. Create a `group.mod` that can kick but cannot stop the server, and confirm both halves.

Add these lines to `server.cfg` (use your own Discord ID, and leave the placeholder until you do):

```text
add_ace group.mod command.kick allow
add_ace group.mod command.quit deny
add_principal identifier.discord:YOUR_DISCORD_ID group.mod
```

Restart the server, then open the server console and type the commands there. If you run txAdmin, that is the **Live Console** tab in the web panel (http://your-server-ip:40120); otherwise it is the black FXServer console window the server prints its logs into. Test both objects without kicking anyone or stopping the server:

```text
test_ace identifier.discord:YOUR_DISCORD_ID command.kick
```

Then the denied one:

```text
test_ace identifier.discord:YOUR_DISCORD_ID command.quit
```

`test_ace` prints a line like `TEST ACL [<principal> -> <object>] ... -> ALLOW` (or `-> DENY`) — the result is the uppercase `ALLOW`/`DENY` at the end of the line, not a `true`/`false`. So the first command should end in `-> ALLOW` and the second in `-> DENY`. (`test_ace` only reports the policy; it does not kick anyone or stop the server.) If a result is the opposite of what you expect, inspect inherited principals and any broader `command` grant the principal also picks up.

## References
- Cfx.re docs · Server commands and ACE permissions: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- Cfx.re docs · txAdmin: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/resources/txAdmin/

title: A3 · DDoS, proxy, and networking basics · Quasar Academy
ai:
Owners ask the same three questions: why can nobody join, why does it lag under load, and why am I getting attacked. This lesson gives you the mental model and a tiny resource that prints your live networking config so the numbers stop being abstract. You will not become a network engineer, but you will stop guessing and know what to check and what to ask your host.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: Why your server is unreachable or laggy, what a proxy/filter actually does, and which ports matter.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: A server you can reach (local or VPS) and access to its firewall settings.
- You'll learn: Ports -> why connections fail -> what DDoS protection does -> when to use a proxy/filter -> what hosting handles for you

### Before you start
- I have a server running (local or on a VPS).
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and open my firewall or VPS network settings.

> **Two ports, two jobs**
> A standard FiveM config binds both TCP and UDP on the same game port (default `30120`), and both protocols must be allowed by the host firewall. Do not reduce the whole connection flow to "TCP is the handshake, UDP is gameplay": FXServer exposes multiple HTTP/TCP and UDP services, and the public-list ingress behavior has changed over time. Separately, txAdmin uses its own web port, `40120` by default, and that port is configurable.

## Build it

We are going to make the networking config concrete. This is a server-only resource that reads your live network settings and prints them to the console, so the ports, the OneSync mode, and the player cap stop being words in a guide and become numbers you can see. Follow the names exactly. Once it prints, the next section takes it apart and explains what each number controls.

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_networking_basics
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_networking_basics/
 fxmanifest.lua
 server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Set the endpoints in server.cfg
_Goal: The server binds TCP and UDP on the same port._

Open `server.cfg`. Near the top, where your other networking lines live, make sure both endpoints are declared on the same port:

```cfg
endpoint_add_tcp "0.0.0.0:30120"
endpoint_add_udp "0.0.0.0:30120"
set qu_game_port 30120
```

`0.0.0.0` means "listen on every network interface on this machine", and `30120` is the port players connect to. The extra `qu_game_port` line is a lesson-owned convar so the probe below can report the number; keep it equal to the endpoint port. Cfx.re's default config binds both protocols, so allow both through the firewall.

### Step 4 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which file to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

No `lua54` line. As of June 2025 that directive is deprecated and ignored, because Lua 5.4 is now the only Lua runtime.

### Step 5 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now a runnable probe of your real config._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
RegisterCommand('netinfo', function()
local port = GetConvarInt('qu_game_port', 0)
local maxClients = GetConvarInt('sv_maxClients', 48)
local oneSync = GetConvar('onesync', 'off')

print('[qu_networking_basics] game port (TCP+UDP): ' .. port)
print('[qu_networking_basics] txAdmin panel port: check txAdmin host settings (default 40120)')
print('[qu_networking_basics] sv_maxClients: ' .. maxClients)
print('[qu_networking_basics] onesync: ' .. oneSync)
end, true)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: Your real networking numbers appear in the server console._

Add this line to `server.cfg`:

```text
ensure qu_networking_basics
```

Save, then run:

```text
restart qu_networking_basics
```

Run this test from the txAdmin Live Console:

```text
netinfo
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_networking_basics] game port (TCP+UDP): 30120
[qu_networking_basics] txAdmin panel port: check txAdmin host settings (default 40120)
[qu_networking_basics] sv_maxClients: 48
[qu_networking_basics] onesync: on
```

Your `sv_maxClients` and `onesync` values will be whatever your own `server.cfg` sets. The point is that they are now readable, not memorized.

## How it works

You printed the four numbers that decide whether players can reach you, how many fit, and how much bandwidth each one costs. Take the code apart so each line maps to a real decision instead of a magic value.

### Reading your live config

```lua
local port = GetConvarInt('qu_game_port', 0)
local maxClients = GetConvarInt('sv_maxClients', 48)
local oneSync = GetConvar('onesync', 'off')
```

`GetConvar` and `GetConvarInt` are the server-side way to read a configuration variable that was set in `server.cfg`. The first argument is the convar name. The second argument is a fallback default: if the convar was never set, you get that value instead of `nil`. `GetConvarInt` returns a number; `GetConvar` returns a string, which is why `onesync` (whose values are the words `off`, `on`, and `legacy`) is read as a string while the port and player cap are read as integers.

The mental model: a convar is a setting outside your code that a script can read. The bound port still comes from `endpoint_add_udp` and `endpoint_add_tcp`; `qu_game_port` is only a lesson-owned label that you keep in sync. It does not introspect the socket and is not proof that a firewall rule works. The real proof is the endpoint lines, the host firewall/provider rules, and an external connection test.

### Why the port needs both TCP and UDP

This is the single most common reason a fresh server is unreachable. The connection happens in two phases on the same port number.

TCP and UDP are separate protocols, so firewalls treat `30120/TCP` and `30120/UDP` as separate rules even though the number matches. Cfx.re's default config binds both. If one is blocked, connection, file-service, discovery, or live-session behavior can fail in ways that vary by path and proxy. Open both on the same game port, then verify from outside the host network.

### Game port 30120 versus txAdmin port 40120

These get confused constantly. `30120` is the conventional **game port** in the default config. `40120` is txAdmin's **default** web-panel port, but hosts can change it. The panel is for administrators, not players: restrict it with the provider firewall, VPN, or trusted source IPs where your hosting setup supports that.

### Check yourself: A player says your server shows up in the list and starts connecting, but freezes at the loading screen and times out. The server console shows no errors. What is the most likely cause?

First confirm that both `30120/TCP` and `30120/UDP` are allowed at the OS firewall, provider firewall, and any router/NAT, then test externally. A listing entry alone does not prove either inbound path is healthy. If both are open, continue with server console errors, proxy configuration, resource downloads, and client-specific issues.

### Telling a server problem from a network problem

When players complain about lag, there are two completely different homes for the cause, and they need opposite fixes.

**Server-side lag** is your scripts being slow. You read this with **resmon**. Heads up: `resmon` and `netgraph` are *developer* commands. On a normal FiveM client the F8 console answers `Access denied for command resmon`. Enable developer mode first by launching FiveM with the extra argument `+set moo 31337` (add it to your FiveM shortcut, then reopen F8) -- only then will `resmon` open. As a no-dev-mode alternative, watch the server thread time in txAdmin instead. Each resource shows a per-frame millisecond cost. If one resource is over budget, the whole server hitches for everyone at once, and no amount of network tuning helps. Fix the script.

**Network-side lag** is the connection between players and your machine: high ping, packet loss, jitter. You read this in-game with the **netgraph**. These are CLIENT commands: join the server, press F8 to open the FiveM client console (not the server or txAdmin console), and type `netgraph true` (`netgraph 1` also works) to watch the ping and packet-loss numbers. For an FPS/ping/packet-loss overlay in the screen corner, type `cl_drawperf true` in the same F8 console. If the server thread time is healthy in txAdmin but the netgraph shows high ping or dropped packets, the problem is the network, not your code, and that is when distance, bandwidth, and attacks come into play.

The diagnostic order is always the same: check resmon and the server thread first, because that is the cause you can fix yourself. Only when the server side is healthy do you look at the network.

### How OneSync and the player cap drive bandwidth

```lua
local maxClients = GetConvarInt('sv_maxClients', 48)
local oneSync = GetConvar('onesync', 'off')
```

These two are not settings; they are your bandwidth budget. `sv_maxClients` is how many players the server allows at once. **OneSync** is the modern state-synchronization mode that lets a server go past the old 32-slot ceiling and have the server, not the clients, own the authoritative game state.

The link to bandwidth is direct: every additional player is more entity and event traffic the server must replicate to everyone else. OneSync `on` is required once you cross the old limits (32-plus slots need OneSync, and above 64 you must run `onesync on`), and a higher `sv_maxClients` with OneSync on is a heavier, steadier outbound load on your connection. This matters because a server that is comfortable at 24 players can saturate its uplink at 100, and the symptom looks exactly like network lag even though nobody is attacking you. Knowing your cap and your sync mode tells you how much bandwidth you are signing up for.

Routing buckets, instancing, and other advanced OneSync features all require OneSync to be enabled in the first place, so this single setting is the gate to most modern multiplayer behavior.

### What DDoS protection and a proxy actually do

A DDoS attack floods your connection with junk traffic so that real players' packets cannot get through. The defense is not on your server at all; it is upstream, in the network.

A **scrubbing center** or **filtered IP** is a machine that sits in front of your server and absorbs the flood. Concretely, at the packet level: all traffic for your advertised address arrives at the scrubbing center first, which drops the malicious packets and forwards only the clean UDP gameplay traffic on to your real machine, the **origin**. Players connect to the protected front; the attack hits the front and dies there.

A **proxy** adds the second half: it keeps your origin IP private. If attackers know the raw IP of your actual machine, they can route the flood straight at it and bypass the protection entirely. A proxy means the only address the public ever sees is the protected front, and your origin stays hidden behind it. The combination is "filtered IP plus hidden origin", and it is why a leaked origin IP undoes everything.

This is almost entirely a **hosting feature**. A beginner does not hand-build a scrubbing center. The practical move is to choose a host whose plan includes filtered or anti-DDoS networking, and to never let your raw origin IP leak. Note that the FiveM convar `sv_endpointPrivacy` hides player IP addresses from the server's public reports; it does not hide your server's own origin IP, so do not mistake it for DDoS protection.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Server boots, appears in the list, then players freeze on the loading screen` -> Verify BOTH TCP and UDP rules for the configured game port from outside the network. If both paths work, inspect console errors, proxy/file-server settings, and client-specific failures instead of assuming one protocol.
- `Nobody can find or reach the server at all` -> The endpoints are wrong or the port is fully blocked. Confirm endpoint_add_tcp and endpoint_add_udp are both set to 0.0.0.0:30120 and that the port is open to the public internet, not your LAN.
- `Everyone lags or hitches at the same instant` -> Server-side, not network. Check resmon and the txAdmin server thread time. A resource over its per-frame budget freezes the whole server. Fix the script before touching the network.
- `Fine at 20 players, unplayable at 80` -> You are saturating your uplink. High sv_maxClients with onesync on is a heavy outbound bandwidth load. Confirm your host's bandwidth or lower the cap.
- `Constant targeted attacks even after enabling protection` -> Your raw origin IP has leaked, so the flood bypasses the filter. Rotate to a fresh protected IP through a host that hides the origin behind a proxy.
- `Admin panel reachable by strangers` -> You exposed 40120 to the public. That is the txAdmin port, not the game port. Restrict 40120 to your own IP or access it locally; only 30120 should be public.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Declare both endpoint_add_tcp and endpoint_add_udp on the same game port and verify both firewall rules externally.
- Tell the configured game port apart from txAdmin's separately configured admin web port.
- Diagnose lag in order: resmon and server thread time for server-side, the netgraph (netgraph 1) for ping and packet loss on the network side.
- Read sv_maxClients and onesync at runtime and explain why more players plus OneSync means more outbound bandwidth.
- Describe what a scrubbing center and a proxy do at the packet level: absorb the flood, forward clean UDP, hide the origin IP.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| "Connection refused" when joining by IP | The game port (30120) isn't open or isn't forwarded | Forward port 30120 TCP+UDP on your router. Allow it in Windows Firewall and any VPS firewall |
| Server works on localhost but not from the internet | You're connecting to `localhost` but friends need your PUBLIC IP | Use your public IP (find at whatismyip.com) or a Cfx.re join code |
| Everyone lags or times out under load | Your internet upload speed is the bottleneck | Check speedtest.net. If upload is under 10 Mbps, upgrade your plan or use a VPS |
| txAdmin page loads but server browser shows "Offline" | The game port isn't open even though the web port works | txAdmin uses port 40120 (web). The game uses port 30120 (game). Both need to be open |
| "endpoint_add_tcp" not working | Wrong format or placed after resources start | Put endpoint lines at the very top of server.cfg, before any `ensure` lines |

## Try it yourself

Prove your own port is actually reachable, end to end. First, confirm the server is listening: restart `qu_networking_basics`, run `netinfo`, and read back your real `30120`, `sv_maxClients`, and `onesync` values from the console. Then test the UDP path specifically, which is the one beginners forget.

Test from outside your network by connecting a FiveM client to the public address while watching the server console. Generic web port-check sites often test TCP only and cannot reliably prove a stateful UDP game service. Confirm the OS firewall, provider firewall, and router/NAT each allow both protocols on your configured game port, then record which layer was blocking the connection.

Write down two lines: which ports your server reports listening on, and the result of the external UDP check (open or closed, and what you changed to fix it).

## References
- Cfx.re docs · Server commands (endpoint_add_tcp / udp): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- Cfx.re docs · Setting up a server: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-server/
- Cfx.re docs · OneSync: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/onesync/
- Cfx.re docs · sv_maxClients & onesync slot thresholds (Server commands): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- Cfx.re docs · Client console commands (netgraph / resmon / cl_drawperf): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/client-manual/console-commands/

title: A3 · Artifact update SOP · Quasar Academy
ai:
FiveM artifacts are not forever. Cfx.re supports them on a clock, and an outdated artifact eventually makes your server unjoinable from the browser. But blindly grabbing the newest build the night before a launch is how servers break. This SOP makes updating boring: pin what works, back it up, update, test, and have a one-step rollback.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A safe, repeatable routine for updating your FXServer artifact: pin the current build, update, test, and roll back if it breaks.
- Time: ~15 minutes to set up; minutes per update after.
- You need: A running server and the artifact-pin note from the setup track.
- You'll learn: Why artifacts expire -> the support windows -> a backup-first update routine -> how to roll back fast

### Before you start
- I have a running server with a known artifact build number.
- I recorded my current build in an artifact-pin note.
- I can stop and start the server safely.

> **The support clock**
> Cfx.re supports artifacts on a clock, and the exact windows change over time, so do not memorize numbers. As a rough mental model, Recommended builds stay supported for a while after the next release, Latest builds drop off support sooner, and any artifact that gets old enough becomes unsupported and unjoinable from the server browser.
