Before you plan an update, read the current windows from the support article linked at the bottom of this lesson. The point stands either way: update on your schedule, not in a panic, and do not let an artifact rot until the browser kicks your players.

## The update routine

### Step 1 - Record the build you run today
_Goal: You know exactly what to roll back to._

Keep a tiny note next to the server:

```text
Current build: <number>
Channel: Recommended
Known-good: yes
Updated: <date>
Rollback build: <previous number>
```

### Step 2 - Back up the artifact binaries before you touch anything
_Goal: The exact build you run today is saved off to one side._

Know the two halves of your server folder. The artifact binaries are the FXServer files you downloaded and extracted: `FXServer.exe`, `run.cmd` (or `run.sh` on Linux), the `citizen` folder, and the rest of the runtime. Your server-data is everything you own: the `resources` folder, `server.cfg`, your database, your `.env`. An update only swaps the binaries. It must never touch server-data.

Copy the current binaries to a dated backup, for example `fxserver-2026-06-12`. That copy is your undo button if the new build misbehaves. Leave `resources`, `server.cfg`, and your database exactly where they are.

### Step 3 - Swap in the Recommended build
_Goal: The server runs a current, stable build with your config untouched._

Download the latest **Recommended** artifact. Stop FXServer, extract the new archive into a fresh artifact directory, and point your existing txAdmin/server-data setup at that directory. Replacing the whole artifact directory avoids a mixed build containing stale files. Never extract over `resources`, `server.cfg`, `txData`, or the database. Then start the server. Use Latest only when a specific verified fix requires it and you have tested it on staging.

### Step 4 - Smoke-test, then mark known-good or roll back
_Goal: You either confirm the build or revert in one step._

Run the same checklist every time so a regression cannot hide:

- **Console clean.** Watch the console as resources load. No new red errors, no resource that failed to start.
- **Connect.** Join your own server from the FiveM client. If it is running on the same PC, open the F8 console in FiveM and type `connect 127.0.0.1:30120` (30120 is the default FXServer game port). You should reach character select or spawn, not a load loop.
- **Spawn.** Confirm your ped actually spawns in the world.
- **Framework health check.** Use one documented, non-destructive action that you already verified on the previous build, such as opening character selection or reading player/job data. Do not assume `/car`, `/setjob`, or `/giveitem` exists or is safe on every framework configuration.
- **resmon (needs developer mode).** `resmon` is a developer-only client command, so it only works if you launched the FiveM client in developer mode (add the launch argument `+set moo 31337`). On a normal client, `resmon 1` returns `Access denied for command resmon`. With developer mode on, open the F8 console, type `resmon 1`, and confirm no resource is suddenly burning CPU it did not burn before (`resmon 0` closes it). A build change can shift timing. If you cannot run developer mode, watch txAdmin's performance panel instead.

If every line passes, update the note to the new build and mark it known-good. If any line fails, do not debug live: stop the server, restore the dated artifact backup from Step 2, start it again, and you are back on the build you trusted this morning.

Expected output:

```
The startup banner reads `FXServer-master v1.0.0.XXXX windows` (or `linux`), where `XXXX` is your artifact build number; there are no new resource load errors, your join succeeds, and (if you have developer mode) resmon shows normal timings.
```

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Players kicked: artifact outdated / End of Support` -> Your build is past the support window. Update to a current Recommended artifact following this SOP.
- `New build breaks a resource` -> Restore the dated artifact binaries you saved in Step 2 and start the server. Your resources and config were never touched, so you are back on the known-good build. Then trial the update on a separate test server before retrying.
- `No idea which build was working` -> Keep the artifact-pin note updated every time. Without it you cannot roll back cleanly.
- `Game build mismatch after update` -> Artifact builds and GTA content builds are separate. Check sv_enforceGameBuild against Cfx.re's supported game-build list and your asset requirements, then perform a full restart.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Server won't start after updating artifacts | New artifact requires newer VC++ Redistributable or breaks an old resource | Restore from your backup artifact. Check the release notes at runtime.fivem.net for breaking changes |
| Backup artifact is missing or corrupted | You didn't save the old server.7z before updating | From now on, always save the current artifact before replacing it. Name it `server-backup-YYYYMMDD.7z` |
| "Which build should I update to?" | You're looking at the "Latest" channel instead of "Recommended" | Always use the Recommended (green) channel. Latest can be unstable. Check the Cfx.re changelog first |
| Resources stopped working after update | New artifact changed a default or deprecated a feature | Check the release notes. Common causes: new default ConVars, removed natives, or stricter OneSync scoping |

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain why artifacts expire, and check the current support windows in the Cfx.re support article instead of trusting memorized numbers.
- Pin the current build in a note before every update.
- Tell the artifact binaries apart from your server-data, and back up only the binaries before swapping.
- Update on Recommended, run the connect / spawn / framework command / resmon / clean-console smoke test, and roll back to the dated artifact backup if any line fails.

## References
- Cfx.re support · Outdated / End of Support artifacts: https://support.cfx.re/hc/en-us/articles/12242287993500-Server-artifact-version-is-outdated-or-End-of-Support-End-of-Life-error-messages-when-trying-to-join-a-server
- FiveM Windows server artifacts: https://runtime.fivem.net/artifacts/fivem/build_server_windows/master/
- Cfx.re supported GTA game builds: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/#sv_enforcegamebuild-build

title: A3 · Choosing an anti-cheat · Quasar Academy

toc.label: The layers that actually stop cheaters

ai:
New owners think anti-cheat is a product you buy that makes cheating stop. It is not. The strongest protection is a server that never trusts the client. Anti-cheat is a layer on top of that, not a replacement for it. This is the decide companion to the anti-cheat build lesson: that one writes the server guards, this one helps you evaluate what to buy on top of them so you spend money on the right thing.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: What anti-cheat can and cannot do, how to score options without getting sold snake oil, the layers that actually stop cheaters, and the red flags when buying.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: An understanding of client vs server authority (the trust lesson).

### Before you start
- I understand the server must stay in charge (the trust and authority lesson).
- I can read a resource's claims critically.
- I have a budget mindset, not a panic mindset.

> **No anti-cheat fixes a trusting server**
> If your scripts let the client set money, items, or admin power, no anti-cheat saves you. Fix server authority first. Anti-cheat catches things authority cannot, like injected menus and resource tampering, but it is the second line, never the first.

Expected output:

```
You can look at any anti-cheat product and answer: what it protects against, what it costs, whether it needs a framework, and whether it covers the attacks your server actually faces. You have a shortlist of 2-3 options matched to your budget and framework.
```

## How to evaluate an option

## The layers that actually stop cheaters

The order here is deliberate. Each layer catches a class of attack the one above it cannot, and a product anti-cheat only earns its keep once the first four are in place.

- **Server authority.** The client may request, only the server grants, after a check. This is the floor. A client that asks for money gets recomputed by the server, never trusted.
- **Rate limits.** Cap how often an action can happen so spam and automation are blocked. A legitimate player robs a store every few minutes; a bot tries every frame.
- **Server-side invariants.** Reject impossible state transitions, invalid event arguments, out-of-range actions, and requests that do not follow the expected server-owned sequence.
- **Logging and review.** Catch patterns after the fact and ban with evidence, not vibes. In practice this means recording suspicious requests server-side (to your server console, the txAdmin Live Console, or a logging resource) so a ban can point to a logged event rather than a hunch.
- **Anti-cheat product.** A layer for injected menus and known cheat signatures, sitting on top of all of the above. Never under it.

## How it works

The reason this lesson exists is that the words above stay abstract until you know what each layer is actually fighting. Two threats get blurred together constantly, and the difference decides which layer addresses them.

### Injected menus versus resource tampering

An **injected menu** (often called an external menu or mod menu) is a separate program the cheater runs alongside the game. It hooks into the game client's memory and calls game functions directly, or fires fake network events at your server. Nothing on your server gave it permission. It exists entirely inside the player's machine, which the cheater controls completely.

**Resource tampering** is different. Here the cheater modifies a resource your server actually sent them, or runs an extra client resource that talks to your scripts. The attack rides on top of legitimate resources rather than bypassing them. An integrity layer is aimed at this case: it looks for client state that does not match what the server shipped. (As the next section notes, ordinary Lua cannot reliably hash every client file, so treat any product's integrity claims as something to verify, not assume.)

Knowing which is which tells you which layer does the work. Server authority and rate limits defend against both, because they live on the server and judge requests no matter where they came from. Integrity checks and a product anti-cheat are aimed mostly at the injected and tampered client.

### Why client-side detection can be faked

This is the mechanism behind never trust the client. The cheater owns the client process. Anything that runs there, including a detection script that is supposed to report cheating, runs inside a sandbox the cheater controls. They can patch it, feed it false data, or stop it from ever sending its report.

So a check that runs on the client and sends a clean here, nothing wrong message to the server is worthless on its own. A cheater makes that message always say clean. The same logic, run on the server against a request the client cannot forge, holds, because the server decides the outcome and the client cannot edit the server. That is the entire reason server authority sits at the bottom of the stack: it is the only place a check cannot be tampered out of existence.

### What the server can actually inspect

Do not promise that ordinary Lua can reliably enumerate a cheater's private injected code or prove every client file hash. Focus first on behavior and state the server can observe and own:

- **Invalid event arguments.** Reject wrong types, impossible quantities, invalid entity ownership, and coordinates outside the allowed interaction area.
- **Rate and sequence violations.** A purchase arriving before the server opened a transaction, or hundreds of requests inside a legitimate cooldown, is server-observable evidence.
- **Impossible state transitions.** A reward request without the server-recorded prerequisite, or movement/value changes outside allowed bounds, should be rejected and logged.

None of these trust a clean/dirty self-report from the client. They validate the request against server-owned state. A commercial anti-cheat may add platform-specific detections, but evaluate those claims with evidence and do not replace these controls.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Bought anti-cheat, still getting robbed` -> The exploit is server trust, not injection. Move money/item/job decisions server-side first, the way the anti-cheat build lesson does.
- `Anti-cheat tanks server performance` -> Measure its resmon cost. If it is heavy, the cure is worse than the disease; find a lighter option.
- `It is an opaque blob with full access` -> You are trusting an unknown resource completely. Prefer options you or your community can audit.
- `Sold on hype in the seller's own channel` -> Ask about bypass history in neutral communities. Recency of patches matters more than marketing.
- `Relying on a client-side detection script alone` -> The cheater controls the client process and can silence or fake its report. Detection that matters runs server-side against requests the client cannot forge.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Anti-cheat blocks legitimate players | The anti-cheat is too aggressive or poorly configured | Check the anti-cheat's config. Lower sensitivity on false-positive triggers (e.g., speed hacks on fast cars) |
| Anti-cheat doesn't catch obvious cheaters | It's not configured for your framework or missing basic protections | Even the best anti-cheat needs server-side validation. Never trust the client to report its own cheats |
| Anti-cheat causes server lag | It's doing too many checks per tick or scanning too many entities | Reduce scan frequency, limit entity range, or disable non-critical checks. Performance over paranoia |
| "I bought an anti-cheat but the cheater still got in" | Anti-cheats are detection tools, not prevention. A good one catches most, none catch all | Layer your defenses: ACE permissions + server-side validation + anti-cheat. No single tool is enough |

## What you can do now

## Recap
- State plainly that server authority beats any anti-cheat product, and explain why a client-side report can be faked.
- Tell an injected menu apart from resource tampering, and name which layer addresses each.
- Score an anti-cheat on detection, maintenance, performance, transparency, and bypass history.
- Name the server-observable checks that matter: event arguments, rates and sequences, entity ownership, and impossible state transitions.
- Avoid opaque, stale, or hype-only options, and treat any product as the layer on top of server authority, never the replacement for it.

## References
- Cfx.re docs · Server security (never trust the client): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/developers/server-security/
- Cfx.re docs · Scripting guides (overview): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/
- Cfx.re docs · OneSync and server authority: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/onesync/

title: A3 · Incident log runbook · Quasar Academy
ai:
When something breaks, the worst response is panic edits on the live server. This runbook turns chaos into a routine: capture the evidence, reproduce it, classify it, fix or escalate, and write it down so the same fire does not start twice. It pairs txAdmin logs with your Discord reports into one workflow.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A repeatable routine for turning a vague 'server broke' report into a logged, reproducible, fixable incident.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: txAdmin access and a Discord channel for reports.
- You'll learn: Capture -> reproduce -> classify -> fix or escalate -> record, so problems stop recurring

### Before you start
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and the txData logs.
- I have a channel where players and staff report issues.
- I deploy changes through version control, not live edits (the Git lesson).

> **Where the evidence lives**
> txAdmin writes per-server logs under `txData/<your-server>/logs` (admin, fxserver, server). The Live Console shows what is happening now; the log files show what happened earlier. Both are your evidence.

## The runbook

### Step 1 - Capture before you change anything
_Goal: You have the error text, time, and who/what triggered it._

Grab the exact console error, the timestamp, the player or resource involved, and what they were doing. A screenshot of the report plus the matching console line is enough. Do not start editing yet.

### Step 2 - Reproduce it
_Goal: You can make the problem happen on demand._

Try to trigger it yourself on a test server or quiet time. A bug you can reproduce is a bug you can fix; a bug you cannot reproduce gets a watch entry, not a guess-fix.

### Step 3 - Classify the severity
_Goal: You know whether to fix now or schedule it._

Sort it: **critical** (server down, money/items exploitable), **major** (a core feature broken), or **minor** (cosmetic, rare). Critical jumps the queue; minor goes on the list.

### Step 4 - Fix or escalate, then deploy safely
_Goal: The fix reaches the server through your normal deploy loop._

If it is yours, fix it on your PC, test, and deploy with the Git loop. If it is a paid resource, open a support ticket with your captured evidence. Never patch directly on the live server.

### Step 5 - Record the incident
_Goal: A future you can find what happened and what fixed it._

Log one line per incident: date, symptom, cause, fix, and the resource or commit involved. (A *commit* is the unique ID Git gives each saved change — see the Git lesson — so 'commit a1b2c3d' lets a future you jump straight to the exact code that fixed it.) Over time this becomes the fastest debugging tool you own.

```text
2026-06-12 | shop dupe on rapid-click | client trusted for grant | added server cooldown + qty check | commit a1b2c3d
```

Expected output:

```
You have a text file template you can open, fill in, and save in under 2 minutes whenever something breaks. Next time your server goes down or a player reports a bug, you log it the same way every time — and you can find any past incident by searching for its date.
```

## How it works

The five steps are easy to nod along to and hard to actually run when a report lands at 2am. So walk one real incident through the whole loop. The point is not the specific bug; it is that the same five moves work on every report you will ever get.

### One report, all five steps

A player posts in your bug channel: "car shop lets me dupe cars". That is all you have. Here is the loop.

**1. Capture.** You do not edit anything yet. Ask for the time, shop, character/server ID, and exact clicks. txAdmin's activity logs cover platform/admin activity such as joins, drops, chat, kills, explosions, and commands; they do **not** automatically record every custom resource event. Search the raw FXServer log and any audit log the shop resource actually implements. If no purchase audit exists, record that evidence gap and reproduce on a test server (a separate, non-public copy of your server, sometimes called staging) with temporary server-side logging — a `print(...)` line you add inside the resource's *server* script so the action shows up in the FXServer console, which you remove again once you have your evidence. Never claim txAdmin captured `vehicle:purchase` unless your resource explicitly logged it.

**2. Reproduce.** On a test server, stand at the same shop and spam the buy button the way they described. The vehicle is granted several times before any balance check catches up. It reproduces every time. A bug you can trigger on demand is a bug you can fix; if it had only happened once and never again, it would get a watch entry instead.

**3. Classify.** Players minting free cars is duplicated value, which is a money/item exploit. That is **critical**. It jumps the queue ahead of the cosmetic bugs on your list, and you stop the bleed first by stopping or restricting that one shop resource while you work, rather than editing it live.

**4. Fix or escalate.** Read the buy flow. When you click buy, the player's game (the client) fires a named network event — here `vehicle:purchase` — at the server, and the server grants the car. Because the client is what fires that event, a cheater can fire it themselves, as fast and as often as they like. The server trusts the client to fire it once and never checks how fast the requests arrive or whether the player can afford each one. The fix lives on the server, not the client: charge and validate on the server before granting, and reject a second request that arrives before the first finishes. If this is your own resource, you make that change on your PC, test it against the same spam, and deploy through the Git loop. If it is a paid resource, you open a support ticket with the evidence packet from step 1 so the author can fix it the same way. Either way, nothing gets patched directly on the live server.

**5. Record.** One line in the log, the same shape as the example above:

```text
2026-06-13 | luxury autos dupe on rapid buy | server trusted client to fire purchase once | charge + validate on server, reject rapid repeats | commit 9f2c7e1
```

Now the abstract steps are concrete, and the log line is not a note. It names the real cause.

### Why this class of bug keeps coming back

> **Security rule**
> The client (the player's game) is attacker-controlled: anything it sends can be faked, replayed, or spammed. Any value the server grants in response to a client-fired event MUST be validated and rate-limited on the server before it is granted. Never let the client decide the amount, the price, or how many times an action runs.

Look at the cause field in both log lines. The first one says "client trusted for grant." The second says "server trusted client to fire purchase once." That is the same bug wearing a different shop. Any time the server hands out value because the client asked nicely, without checking on the server side, a player who can fire that request faster or more often than you expected gets free value. Shops, ATMs, crafting, item transfers all share the shape.

This is why the record step matters more than it looks. When the next dupe report lands, you search your log for "dupe", see two prior entries that both trace back to a trusting server, and you already know where to look before you reproduce anything. The runbook does not close one incident. Over time it teaches you the patterns in your own server, and the server-authority pattern is the one you will meet most.

### Reading txData logs by timestamp

Step 1 leans on finding the right lines in a file, so make that concrete. txAdmin keeps rotating logs in the `txData` folder, which sits next to your server's install folder (on Windows, typically `<your FXServer folder>/../txData`; on a host like ZAP it is exposed in the file manager). Inside it, each server profile has a `logs` folder — `txData/<your-server-profile>/logs` — holding dated, rotated text files. Open them in a plain-text editor such as VS Code or Notepad (NOT in the game). The three files you care about are:

- `fxserver` is the raw server console output, the same stream you see live, including resource `print` lines and Lua errors.
- `server` is txAdmin's activity log for events txAdmin records, such as joins, drops, chat, deaths/kills, explosions, and commands. Custom gameplay events appear only if a resource explicitly logs them.
- `admin` is who did what in the txAdmin panel: kicks, bans, restarts, console commands.

To work an incident, take the report time and search the activity and FXServer logs that actually cover the symptom. Find the minute, then read outward. For a custom economy action, use the resource's audit table/log if it has one; txAdmin cannot manufacture application-level evidence. The Live Console is for now, while rotating files preserve earlier output according to your retention settings.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Edited the live server and made it worse` -> Stop. Roll back via Git, reproduce on a test setup, then deploy the fix through the normal loop.
- `Report says 'it broke', nothing else` -> Ask for time, what they did, and any error text. No reproduction means a watch entry, not a blind change.
- `Same bug keeps coming back` -> You fixed the symptom, not the cause. Use the incident log to find the real trigger, which for value exploits is usually a server that trusts the client.
- `Lost the error after a restart` -> Pull it from txData logs by timestamp. The Live Console clears; the log files do not.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Capture the error, time, and trigger before changing anything.
- Reproduce before you fix; watch what you cannot reproduce.
- Classify severity and fix or escalate accordingly.
- Deploy fixes through Git, never on the live server, and log every incident.
- Read txData logs by timestamp to recover an error the Live Console already cleared.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| "I forgot to log what happened and now I can't remember" | The incident happened hours ago and details are lost | Log it NOW. Even 2 sentences are better than nothing. Time, what broke, what you changed |
| Incident log is scattered across Discord DMs, chat, and memory | No single source of truth for server problems | Keep ONE file (`incidents.md` or a Google Doc). Every incident goes there. Searchable by date |
| "I don't know what caused it so I can't log it" | You don't need the root cause to log the symptoms | Log what you SAW: "Server CPU at 100%, players timing out." The cause can be filled in later |
| Log says "Fixed it" but doesn't say HOW | The fix is obvious to you now, but won't be in 3 months when it happens again | Write the exact command or config change. "Reduced sv_maxclients from 64 to 32" not "Adjusted settings" |

## Try it yourself

Run the runbook on a report cold, the way you will at 2am. The report is one line: "got robbed at the ATM but my balance went up." You have nothing else.

Write out, in order:

1. The three questions you ask the reporter to capture, and which txData log file you open first to find the matching event by timestamp.
2. How you would reproduce it on a test server.
3. The severity, with one sentence saying why.
4. Where the fix most likely lives, client or server, and the one sentence of cause you would write.
5. The single log line you would record, in the `date | symptom | cause | fix | commit` shape used above.

If your cause sentence in step 4 ends up being some version of "the server trusted the client," you have spotted the pattern this lesson is really about. Compare your log line to the two worked examples and confirm all five fields are filled, not the symptom.

## References
- Cfx.re docs · txAdmin: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/resources/txAdmin/
- Cfx.re docs · Server console commands: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- txAdmin docs · Log files: https://github.com/tabarra/txAdmin/blob/master/docs/logs.md

title: A3 · Voice setup and troubleshooting · Quasar Academy
ai:
Voice is the feature players notice instantly and the one that quietly breaks launches. The fix is rarely mysterious: a missing dependency, a mic permission, or a config that fights the framework. This is the get-it-working-and-keep-it-working guide.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll do: Get proximity voice working and fix the handful of issues that block it on launch day.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: A running framework server and a voice resource (pma-voice is the common modern choice).
- You'll learn: How FiveM voice works -> install and config -> the common blockers -> a fast troubleshooting order

### Before you start
- I have a framework server running (ESX Legacy, QBCore, or Qbox).
- I can install a resource and edit server.cfg.
- I have a working microphone in the FiveM client.

> **How FiveM voice works**
> FiveM includes an internal Mumble voice system. The community resource `pma-voice`, maintained in the `AvarianKnight/pma-voice` repository, configures proximity, radio, and call channels on top of it. A normal setup does not require a separate TeamSpeak or Mumble server, though pma-voice also supports an explicitly configured external Mumble address. Network loss can hurt voice, but so can the wrong input device, conflicting voice resources, or incorrect convars, so diagnose in order rather than assuming one cause.

## Get it working

### Step 1 - Install pma-voice by itself
_Goal: One voice resource starts with no errors._

Download the release from the official `AvarianKnight/pma-voice` repository and place it in `resources` with the folder name `pma-voice`. It uses FiveM's internal Mumble support; **do not install or run `mumble-voip` beside it**. The pma-voice README says it is incompatible with other voice systems, including vMenu voice unless that feature is disabled.

```cfg
ensure pma-voice
```

Restart and watch the console. If another voice resource is running, stop it and remove its `ensure` line before troubleshooting pma-voice.

### Step 2 - Set proximity and mode in config
_Goal: Talk, whisper, and shout ranges behave as intended._

pma-voice's supported configuration is through convars in `server.cfg`. Start with the defaults and override only values you intentionally want to change:

```cfg
# 1 = Whisper, 2 = Normal, 3 = Shouting
setr voice_defaultVoiceMode 2
setr voice_useNativeAudio false
setr voice_enableRadioAnim false
```

These lines do not set three distance values. `voice_defaultVoiceMode` chooses the initial mode. `voice_useNativeAudio` changes the audio path and is required for submixes. `voice_enableRadioAnim` only controls the radio animation. The project's current ranges live in its shared code; changing them is a maintained fork, not a normal convar tweak. Follow the README for the release you installed.

### Step 3 - Confirm mic permission and device
_Goal: Your voice is captured and transmitted._

In FiveM settings, set the correct input device and push-to-talk key, and allow microphone access at the OS level. A muted OS mic looks exactly like a broken server.

### Step 4 - Test with a second player
_Goal: Two players hear each other by proximity._

Voice is hard to test alone. Bring a second client (or a friend), stand close, and confirm proximity falloff as you walk apart.

Expected output:

```
Players hear each other in-game with clear audio. The voice server runs on its own port. You know which ConVars control range, channels, and audio quality — and which log files to check when voice breaks.
```

## How it works

Two facts explain almost every voice problem you will hit, and both are worth holding in your head before you start tuning anything.

**Voice uses FiveM's Mumble integration.** In the default setup, pma-voice uses the internal voice server. It can also be pointed at an external address and port with documented convars, so it is inaccurate to assume every installation uses one identical network path. If audio cuts out, check the client device and push-to-talk, confirm only one voice resource controls the natives, inspect pma-voice's debug output, and then check latency, packet loss, and server health.

**Proximity has three modes.** The default mode is selected by `voice_defaultVoiceMode`, and players cycle modes with the configured key (F11 by default for new players). First confirm the player's current Whisper/Normal/Shouting mode. Do not tell operators to edit a nonexistent `config.lua`; use the README's convars, and treat changes to the resource's shared code as a fork you must maintain.

## Troubleshooting order

## Common errors and fixes
- `Nobody can hear anyone` -> Confirm pma-voice started, disable every other voice system, then check OS microphone permission and the FiveM input/output devices.
- `I hear others but they cannot hear me` -> Your mic input or push-to-talk is wrong. Set the correct device and key in FiveM settings and unmute the OS mic.
- `My range feels wrong, people are too quiet or too loud` -> Cycle Whisper/Normal/Shouting and verify voice_defaultVoiceMode. If you customized pma-voice code, compare it with the official release before changing more settings.
- `Voice cuts out or is robotic` -> Check the device, conflicting voice scripts, pma-voice debug output, latency/packet loss, and server health. Do not assume it is only network or only config.
- `Radio or phone channels do not work` -> Those are framework integration. Confirm the framework bridge (ESX Legacy, QBCore, or Qbox) and the radio/phone channel settings in the voice resource config.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Voice chat doesn't work at all | pma-voice or mumble not installed, or wrong ConVars | Check `ensure pma-voice` is in server.cfg. Verify `voice_useNativeAudio` and `voice_useSendingRangeOnly` are set |
| Players can hear each other but it's choppy | Network issues or voice server overloaded | Check the voice server (mumble) is on a separate port. Lower `voice_maxPlayersPerChannel` |
| Voice works for some players but not others | Individual player settings or mute | Ask them to check FiveM settings → Voice. "Voice Chat" must be ON and "Output Device" set correctly |
| "Mumble connection failed" in console | The voice server isn't running or port is blocked | Check `voice_externalAddress` and `voice_externalPort` match. Open the voice port in firewall |
| Echo or feedback when talking | Microphone picking up speaker output | Lower mic sensitivity in FiveM settings. Use headphones instead of speakers |

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Install pma-voice as the only voice resource; it does not depend on a separate mumble-voip resource.
- Use the documented convars for mode and audio behavior, and verify the installed release's README before changing defaults.
- Confirm OS mic permission and the FiveM input device before blaming the server.
- Diagnose cutting voice across device, resource conflict, pma-voice config/debug output, network, and server health.
- Troubleshoot in order: resource started, then mic, then voice mode, then network, then channel config.

## References
- Cfx.re docs · Voice (mumble) natives: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/#voicechat
- pma-voice (Overextended): https://github.com/AvarianKnight/pma-voice
- Cfx.re docs · Setting up a server: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-server/

title: A3 · Clothing, EUP, and asset policy · Quasar Academy
ai:
Clothing and EUP (police, medical, and other uniform variations) are among the most requested additions, and among the easiest to do badly. Done wrong they balloon your download size, hurt streaming memory, and can put you on the wrong side of asset licensing. This lesson builds one tiny streamed resource so you can see the mechanism for real, then teaches you how to keep packs fast and clean.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A correctly shaped asset resource, then validate a real licensed pack with the client streaming tools.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: A framework server you can restart, and the streaming display turned on (resmon).
- You'll learn: How the stream/ folder auto-registers assets -> how EUP differs from normal clothing -> how component IDs map to menu slots -> how to measure streaming memory instead of guessing -> the IP and policy lines you do not cross.

### Before you start
- I have a framework server running.
- I can add a resource folder and restart it.
- I understand resources are folders the server loads.

> **The IP line you do not cross**
> Do not stream ripped real-world brands, leaked paid packs, or assets you have no license to use. It is the fastest way to a takedown, a marketplace ban, or worse. Use assets you created, bought with a server license, or that are explicitly free for FiveM use. Keep a written note of where each pack came from. If you cannot point to a license, do not ship it.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds the structure of an asset resource named `qu_stream_proof`. An empty or renamed placeholder is **not** a valid GTA asset, so it cannot prove streaming. The structural check proves only that the resource starts; the final exercise uses a real asset you are licensed to run and validates it in the client.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this layout. The `stream/` folder name is not optional. FiveM scans a folder with exactly that name and auto-registers whatever asset files it finds inside.

```text
resources/qu_stream_proof/
 fxmanifest.lua
 server.lua
 stream/
```

Leave `stream/` empty for the structural check. When you validate a real pack later, its valid `.ydd`, `.ytd`, `.ymt`, `.ytyp`, or other documented files go here. Renaming an empty text file to `.ytyp` does not create a valid archetype and can produce a misleading or broken test.

### Step 2 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows the resource and registers the streamed type._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

No `lua54 'yes'` line is needed. When a real prop pack includes a `.ytyp`, copy the pack author's manifest entry, commonly `data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST' 'stream/name.ytyp'`. Do not add a `data_file` entry for a file that does not exist or invent one for a clothing pack that does not ship it.

### Step 3 - Write the proof line
_Goal: The resource reports the moment it registers its stream._

Open `server.lua` and paste this. It listens for the resource starting and prints one line confirming the `stream/` folder went live:

```lua
AddEventHandler('onResourceStart', function(resourceName)
 if resourceName ~= GetCurrentResourceName() then return end

 print('[qu_stream_proof] resource started; validate real assets in the client')
end)
```

This print proves only that the server resource loaded. Server-side `onResourceStart` cannot prove that a client downloaded or rendered an asset. We guard it with the `resourceName` check so it reacts only to this resource.

### Step 4 - Start it
_Goal: The proof line appears in the server console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

```text
ensure qu_stream_proof
```

Save, then run this in the txAdmin Live Console:

```text
restart qu_stream_proof
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_stream_proof] resource started; validate real assets in the client
```

### Step 5 - Measure it in resmon
_Goal: Streaming memory becomes a number, not a vibe._

In your own FiveM client connected to the server, open the F8 console and run:

```text
strmem 1
```

Use the streaming diagnostics only after adding a **valid, licensed asset**. An empty folder has nothing meaningful to measure. `strmem` is a developer command, blocked on a normal client with `Access denied / disabled in production mode`. Relaunch FiveM with launch arg `set moo 31337` or a non-production update channel (same for `resmon`/`strdbg`/`strlist`), then reconnect and run `strmem 1`. Use the official client-console reference linked below and confirm the asset visually in game as well as in diagnostics.

## How it works

You restarted the resource and proved its manifest and server script load. You have **not** proved asset streaming yet because the folder is empty. A real validation needs a valid licensed asset, a clean client download, and an in-game visual or streaming diagnostic. Keep that boundary clear: a server start line is not a client-render proof.

### The stream/ folder is the magic, not a config

Most FiveM features need you to wire something up: register an event, call a native, list a file. Streaming is different. There is one rule: any asset file inside a folder named `stream/` is auto-registered when the resource starts. You do not list each `.ydd` or `.ytd` in the manifest. FiveM walks the folder for you.

```text
stream/
 qu_proof.ytyp
```

When a client connects, the server sends it the manifest of streamed files for every started resource. The client downloads them on first join and caches them. This is why a 500 MB clothing pack is felt as a slow first join: every player pulls every byte before they can play. The pack author does not control that cost. You do, by what you choose to put in `stream/`.

There are exactly two kinds of file in that folder. The meshes and textures (`.ydd` drawables, `.ytd` texture dictionaries) auto-register with no help. The type definitions (`.ytyp`, used by prop packs to declare new object types) are the exception that needs the manifest line you wrote.

### Why the data_file line is required for props

```lua
data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST' 'stream/qu_proof.ytyp'
```

A `.ytyp` is a type archetype: it tells the game a new prop or object class exists, its bounds, its LOD distances. Auto-registration handles the drawables and textures, but the engine will not load a `.ytyp` as an active type request on its own. `data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST'` is the explicit instruction to do so. The first argument names the data file kind the game expects; the second is the path inside your resource. Leave this line out of a prop pack and you get the classic symptom: the resource starts with no error, but the props are invisible or fall through the world because their type was never requested.

Pure clothing packs (drawables and textures only, no new prop types) often do not need this line. The moment a pack includes accessory props with their own `.ytyp`, it does.

### How EUP differs from a normal clothing swap

"EUP" is commonly used for emergency-service uniform packs, but it is not one universal resource layout. A pack may be a replacement or an add-on clothing collection and may include drawable/texture files plus metadata such as `.ymt` files. Install the exact manifest and metadata shipped by the licensed pack; do not assume every pack must be renamed `eup-stream` or requires a `.ytyp`. First prove the resource starts, then use the game's drawable/texture counts or the appearance resource's documented refresh path to prove the new variations exist.

### How a clothing menu maps slots to your drawables

Your players never type file names. They open a menu (illenium-appearance, qb-clothing, or similar) and pick a number. That menu speaks the game's component model, which you have to understand to wire a pack correctly. Every ped has a fixed set of component slots, each an integer:

Within a slot, a **drawable** is which model variation (the jacket shape), and a **texture** is which skin on that drawable (the color or pattern). So a menu selection is three numbers: component ID, drawable index, texture index. When you add a clothing pack, you are appending new drawable indexes onto one of these slots. The menu's config is where you tell it the new indexes exist, which is the concrete version of the old lesson's hand-wavy "wire it to your menu." You are mapping slot 11, drawable 42, texture 0 to a label your players can click.

### Check yourself: A new EUP pack downloads fine (you see it in resmon) but the uniforms never appear in your clothing menu. The files are streaming. What is the most likely cause?

Streaming bytes is only one stage. Confirm the pack's shipped metadata loaded, the pack targets the ped model you are testing, and the appearance resource supports or refreshes add-on collections. Follow the pack and menu's official install instructions rather than assuming every menu needs a hand-written index list.

### Measuring instead of guessing

"Keep textures at sane resolutions" is useless advice because it has no number attached. Here is the number. With `strmem 1` running, the streaming overlay shows each resource's memory in MB and the total against your streaming budget. A healthy clothing pack is tens of MB, not hundreds. Most bloat is `.ytd` texture dictionaries saved at 2048x2048 or 4096x4096 when 1024x1024 would look identical at gameplay distance. Open a fat pack's textures, downscale the oversized ones, repack, and watch the resmon number drop. That is the whole optimization loop: change something, restart, read the overlay, compare. You ship the version with the smaller number that still looks right.

### When you reach for this pattern

The `stream/` folder plus an optional `data_file` line is not for clothing. It is how you add every custom visual asset to FiveM: vehicles, weapons, map props, MLO interiors, and clothing all ride the same mechanism. Once you can build, start, and measure `qu_stream_proof`, you can reason about any of them, because they are all the same folder doing the same job. The only variation is which file types are inside and whether any of them need a `data_file` line to register their type.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `strmem or resmon says Access denied` -> Those are developer commands, disabled on a normal production client. Relaunch FiveM with launch arg set moo 31337 or a non-production update channel, then reconnect and rerun.
- `Resource starts but the proof line never prints` -> The onResourceStart handler is checking the wrong name, or server.lua is not listed as a server_script in the manifest. Confirm the guard uses GetCurrentResourceName() and that the manifest has server_script 'server.lua'.
- `Props from the pack are invisible or fall through the map` -> The .ytyp type was never requested. Add data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST' 'stream/yourfile.ytyp' to the manifest, one line per .ytyp the pack ships.
- `Pack downloads but no uniforms appear in the menu` -> Confirm the pack's metadata loaded, it targets the ped model you are testing, and your appearance resource supports or refreshes add-on collections. Follow both projects' install docs.
- `First join takes forever after adding a pack` -> The pack is too big or you stacked several. Read each resource's MB in the strmem overlay, downscale oversized .ytd textures, and cut variants you will never use.
- `Memory warning or crash under player load` -> Streamed asset memory counts against your streaming budget. Trim pack size and texture resolution until the strmem total sits comfortably under the limit.
- `Got a takedown or marketplace warning` -> You streamed unlicensed or ripped assets. Remove them and replace with licensed or free-for-FiveM packs, and document the license for each one.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Build the structure of an asset resource and explain why onResourceStart proves only server startup, not client rendering.
- Explain why the stream/ folder auto-registers assets while a .ytyp needs data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST'.
- Treat EUP as a pack category rather than one universal folder layout, and preserve each pack's shipped manifest and metadata.
- Map a menu selection to a component ID, drawable index, and texture index.
- Read streaming memory in resmon with strmem so pack size is a measured number, not a guess.
- Use only licensed or free-for-FiveM assets and document each one.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Custom clothes don't appear on character | Files not in `files {}` or wrong folder structure | Check every .ytd/.yft is listed in `files {}`. Verify the stream folder path matches |
| Clothes appear but are solid white (no textures) | .ytd texture file missing or not loaded | The texture dictionary (.ytd) must be in `stream/` and listed in `files {}` |
| "Could not load resource" after adding EUP pack | Too many files or broken manifest syntax | Check fxmanifest for missing commas. Large packs may need splitting into multiple resources |
| Clothes override default GTA clothes | The addon is replacing instead of adding | Check vehicles.meta (for clothes: peds.meta) has a unique `<modelName>` that doesn't match a GTA original |
| EUP/clothes pack crashes the server | Missing dependency or wrong game build | Check the pack requires a specific game build. Update `sv_enforceGameBuild` if needed |

## Try it yourself

Turn the structure into a real size test. Find a clothing or prop pack you are licensed to use, preserve its documented folder layout, and copy its valid `stream/` contents into `qu_stream_proof/stream/`. If it ships `.ytyp` files, preserve the author's corresponding manifest entries.

```lua
data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST' 'stream/pack_props_a.ytyp'
data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST' 'stream/pack_props_b.ytyp'
```

Restart the resource, then run `strmem 1` in F8 and write down the MB the resource reports. Now open the pack's largest `.ytd` in OpenIV or CodeWalker, find any texture saved above 1024x1024, downscale it, repack, and restart again. Read the overlay a second time. Confirm the MB dropped and the asset still looks right in game. You ran the optimization loop on real numbers: change, restart, measure, compare. That is the difference between shipping a pack and shipping a pack you can defend.

## References
- Cfx.re assets guide · resource stream folder: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/assets-manual/beginner-series/part-4/
- Cfx.re data-file types: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/game-references/data-files/
- Cfx.re docs · Resource manifest (data_file): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- Cfx.re client console commands · streaming diagnostics: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/client-manual/console-commands/
- Cfx.re Platform License Agreement: https://fivem.net/terms

title: A3 · Discord community ops kit · Quasar Academy
ai:
Your Discord is where the community actually lives, and a messy one bleeds players. This kit is the starting layout that scales: clear channels, a few role tiers, a ticket flow so support is not chaos, and an escalation path so staff know who handles what. Set it up once, then grow it.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A starter Discord structure for a FiveM server: channels, rules, roles, tickets, staff escalation, and announcements.
- Time: ~30 minutes.
- You need: A Discord server you own and admin rights on it.
- You'll learn: Channel layout -> role tiers -> a ticket flow -> staff escalation -> announcement and event rhythm

### Before you start
- I own a Discord server and have admin rights.
- I know my server's role tiers (member, supporter, staff, admin).
- I have a way to issue tickets (a bot or a simple channel flow).

> **Tie it to your gate**
> If your lessons or perks are role-gated (the Discord role gate), the same roles should live here. One role system for access, perks, and community standing keeps it simple and is what your members already expect.

## The starter structure

## Roles and escalation

### Step 1 - Define role tiers
_Goal: Every member has exactly one clear standing._

Keep tiers few and obvious: Member, Supporter/Donator, Staff, Admin, Owner. More tiers means more confusion. Map perks and access to these, not to one-off custom roles.

### Step 2 - Set up a ticket flow
_Goal: A player issue becomes a tracked, assignable item._

Use a ticket bot so opening a ticket creates a private thread or channel only the player and authorized staff can see. Configure the panel to ask what broke, when it happened, the in-game/character name, and any console error. Ask for a raw player identifier only when staff genuinely need it, keep it in the private ticket, and define retention/deletion rules; identifiers and logs are user data, not decoration. Treat ticket transcripts and screenshots the same way: redact identifiers before pasting them anywhere outside the private ticket.

### Step 3 - Write the escalation path
_Goal: Staff know who handles what without asking._

Document it: support staff handle player issues, admins handle bans and server changes, owner handles money and policy. Put it in staff-only so nobody freezes during an incident.

### Step 4 - Establish an announcement rhythm
_Goal: Players trust one channel for real news._

Post launches, updates, and events in announcements only. A predictable rhythm (weekly update, event teasers) keeps players returning.

Expected output:

```
Your Discord server has clear channels, roles that match your FiveM permissions, and automated welcome messages for new members. You have a moderation workflow and a backup of your server template.
```

## How it works

Two pieces of this kit look simple but trip up real servers: the logs webhook and the ticket thread. Both are about treating community ops like infrastructure, not chat.

### Why the logs webhook URL is a secret

To get server events into your private logs channel, open that channel's settings (gear icon, or right-click the channel) -> Integrations -> Webhooks -> New Webhook -> Copy Webhook URL. Discord gives you a URL that looks like this (the same Webhooks screen is where you later Delete or Regenerate it if it leaks). A server-side resource, built in a later lesson, reads the convar below and POSTs to this URL; this lesson only sets up the channel and stores the secret, so the logs channel stays empty until that resource exists. That empty channel is expected, not a mistake. If you want to confirm the webhook itself works before that resource exists, send one optional test message from PowerShell, replacing the URL with your own:

```powershell
curl.exe -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"content":"webhook test"}' "https://discord.com/api/webhooks/123.../AbCd..."
```

A single line saying "webhook test" should appear in your logs channel. Delete that test message afterward.

```text
https://discord.com/api/webhooks/123456789012345678/AbCdEf...your-token...
```

That URL is a credential, not a link. Anyone who has it can POST a message to your logs channel as your server, with no login and no permission check. There is no separate password. Possession of the URL is the permission. If it leaks, someone can spam your staff channel, impersonate your bot, or bury a real incident under noise, and your only fix is to delete the webhook and issue a new URL. You do this in the same place you created it: Channel settings -> Integrations -> Webhooks -> select the webhook -> Delete Webhook, then create a fresh one and update the convar value in server.cfg. The old URL stops working the moment you delete it.

So treat it exactly like any other secret. Do not paste it in a public channel, a screenshot, or a commit. It belongs in your `server.cfg` as a convar, read by your resources, never hard coded into a script you might share:

```cfg
set discord_log_webhook "https://discord.com/api/webhooks/.../..."
# Use `set` (server-side only). Do NOT use `setr` (it replicates the value to every
# connected client) or `sets` (it publishes the value on the public server list and the
# open http://<server_ip>:<server_port>/info.json file). Either one leaks the webhook.
```

This is the same rule code review enforces on every other secret: keys and tokens live in config the server reads, not in the source. A webhook URL is no different.

### How a ticket maps to the incident runbook

A ticket bot that opens a private thread is doing more than starting a chat. The thread is a per-incident container: one issue, one place, only the player and staff inside. That maps directly onto the incident runbook, where each incident is its own tracked item with a clear owner.

The fields you make the panel collect (what broke, when, in-game name or license ID, the error text) are the inputs the runbook needs first: who and where to reproduce it, what the symptom was, and the raw error to search the logs for. Capturing them up front in the thread means staff are not chasing the player for basics before they can even start. The thread title becomes the incident, the fields become the report, and your escalation path decides who picks it up. That is why the ticket flow and the runbook share the same shape: the ticket is where an incident is born.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Support is buried in general chat` -> Move it to a dedicated ticket flow. Keep community chat and support separate.
- `Too many roles, nobody knows who does what` -> Collapse to a few tiers and document the escalation path in staff-only.
- `Log webhook posted publicly` -> The webhook URL is a secret. Keep it in a private channel and in server convars only.
- `Players miss updates` -> One read-only announcements channel with a steady rhythm; do not scatter news.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Welcome messages not sending to new members | Bot doesn't have permission or the welcome channel doesn't exist | Give the bot "Send Messages" and "Read Message History" in the welcome channel. Check the channel ID is correct |
| @everyone or @here being abused by members | Permission to mention large groups was given to regular roles | Remove "Mention @everyone" from member roles. Only admins should have it |
| Discord channels are a mess — no one knows where to post | Too many channels or no clear organization | Keep it simple: #announcements, #general, #support, #staff. Add more only when a channel gets too noisy |
| Members leaving because Discord is "dead" | No regular activity or engagement | Post updates about the server (events, changes, new content). A weekly #server-update post keeps people checking back |

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Stand up a clear channel layout with read-only rules and announcements.
- Use a few role tiers that map to access and perks.
- Run support through a ticket flow that captures evidence.
- Document staff escalation and keep a steady announcement rhythm.

## References
- Discord · Enabling your Community server: https://support.discord.com/hc/en-us/articles/360047132851-Enabling-Your-Community-Server
- Discord · Intro to webhooks: https://support.discord.com/hc/en-us/articles/228383668-Intro-to-Webhooks
- Ticket Tool · Ticket bot docs: https://tickettool.xyz/
- Cfx.re docs · txAdmin Discord integration: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/resources/txAdmin/

title: A3 · Database backups and migrations · Quasar Academy
ai:
Your players' money, vehicles, and characters live in one database. One bad migration, one crash, one fat-fingered DELETE, and it is gone unless you have a backup you have actually tested. CRUD lessons teach you to write data; this one teaches you to never lose it.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A backup routine and a safe migration habit so a bad change or a crash never costs you player data.
- Time: ~25 minutes.
- You need: MariaDB running, HeidiSQL connected, and a database your server uses.
- You'll learn: Why backups matter -> how to dump and automate it on a schedule -> safe schema migrations -> a restore test you actually run

### Before you start
- MariaDB is running and HeidiSQL connects to it.
- I know which database my server uses (often 'fivem').
- I can stop and start the server safely.

> **A backup you never restored is a guess**
> The only real backup is one you have restored at least once. Untested backups fail at the worst possible moment. Run the restore test in this lesson before you need it for real.

## The routine

### Step 1 - Take a full dump on a schedule
_Goal: You have a recent, complete copy of the database._

Export the whole database to a `.sql` file on a schedule (daily for an active server). In HeidiSQL: right-click the database, Export database as SQL, to a dated file. From the command line, the dump tool does the same.

Run the command below in a terminal **on the machine where MariaDB is installed** (a Linux shell, or on Windows the PowerShell/Command Prompt window where the MariaDB `bin` folder is on your PATH — that is where `mariadb-dump` lives):

```bash
mariadb-dump -u root -p fivem > fivem-2026-06-12.sql
```

What each part means: `-u root` is the database username (use your DB user, not necessarily `root`); `-p` makes it prompt you for that user's password (type it when asked — nothing shows as you type, that is normal); `fivem` is the database name to back up (use yours); and `> fivem-2026-06-12.sql` writes the dump into a file with today's date in the current folder.

On a pure MariaDB box the dump tool is `mariadb-dump`. The old `mysqldump` name still works because MariaDB ships it as a compatibility symlink, but if `mysqldump` returns `command not found`, use `mariadb-dump` instead. They take the same flags.

To make daily real instead of aspirational, run that command on a schedule. On Linux, a cron line (added with `crontab -e`) that dumps every night at 4am:

```bash
0 4 * * * /usr/bin/mariadb-dump --defaults-extra-file=/root/.mariadb-backup.cnf --single-transaction --routines fivem > /backups/fivem-$(date +\%F).sql
```

Reading it left to right: `0 4 * * *` is the cron schedule (minute 0 of hour 4, every day); `--defaults-extra-file=...` points at a credentials file so no password is typed on the line; `--single-transaction` takes a consistent snapshot of InnoDB tables without blocking your players' writes; `--routines` includes stored procedures/functions; `fivem` is the database; and `> /backups/fivem-$(date +\%F).sql` writes to a dated file. The backslash in `\%F` is required because cron treats a bare `%` as a newline — escaping it lets `date +%F` insert today's date (YYYY-MM-DD).

Put the backup user's credentials in `/root/.mariadb-backup.cnf` so the password never appears in the command. That file is plain text in MariaDB option-file format, for example:

```ini
[mariadb-dump]
user=backup_user
password=your-backup-user-password
```

Then lock it down so only its owner can read it (`chmod 600 /root/.mariadb-backup.cnf`), and never place a password directly in a cron line or command history. On Windows, use Task Scheduler with a protected option file. txAdmin documents scheduled **restarts and events**, not a general operating-system task runner, so do not rely on it to execute a backup script.

### Step 2 - Store backups off the server box
_Goal: A server failure does not take the backups with it._

Copy dumps somewhere other than the server machine (another disk, a private bucket). A backup that dies with the server is not a backup.

### Step 3 - Migrate schema safely
_Goal: Adding columns/tables never destroys existing data._

Before any schema change: take a fresh dump, run the change on a throwaway copy first, and prefer additive changes (`ADD COLUMN`) over destructive ones. Never run an untested `ALTER`/`DROP` against live data.

First build the copy from your latest dump and run the migration there:

```bash
mariadb -u root -p -e "CREATE DATABASE fivem_migrate_test;"
mariadb -u root -p fivem_migrate_test < fivem-2026-06-12.sql
mariadb -u root -p fivem_migrate_test -e "ALTER TABLE players ADD COLUMN bank INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;"
```

Only once that runs clean on the copy do you touch production. Put the server in maintenance and stop the resources that write to the affected tables so application code cannot race the migration. Then run the change and bring the resources back only after validation:

```sql
ALTER TABLE players ADD COLUMN bank INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
```

### Step 4 - Run a restore test
_Goal: You have proven you can bring data back._

Create a throwaway database and import a dump into it. This is the step everyone skips and everyone regrets skipping.

```bash
mariadb -u root -p -e "CREATE DATABASE fivem_restore_test;"
mariadb -u root -p fivem_restore_test < fivem-2026-06-12.sql
```

Do not eyeball it. Prove the restore by comparing a real row count per table between production and the restore. Open an interactive client session by running `mariadb -u root -p` (it prompts for your password, then shows a `MariaDB [(none)]>` prompt), then type each query and press Enter:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fivem.players;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fivem_restore_test.players;
```

The interactive client prints a boxed table like this (the number 1487 is an example — yours will be your own row count):

Expected output:

```
+----------+
| COUNT(*) |
+----------+
| 1487 |
+----------+
+----------+
| COUNT(*) |
+----------+
| 1487 |
+----------+
```

Note: if you instead run these with the `-e "..."` flag from the shell (as in the exercise below), the client prints plain tab-separated output (a `COUNT(*)` header line, then the number) rather than the boxed table — the value is the same, only the formatting differs.

The two counts match. If they differ, the dump is incomplete or the import errored, and that backup is not safe to rely on.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Lost data after a crash` -> Restore the latest dump. If you have none, this is the lesson; start daily backups now.
- `A migration wiped a column` -> Restore from the pre-migration dump you took in step 3. Always dump before ALTER/DROP.
- `Backups are on the same machine that died` -> Move dumps off-box (another disk or a private bucket) going forward.
- `Backup file imports with errors` -> Run the restore test regularly. A backup proven only when needed is too late.
- `ERROR 1045 (28000): Access denied for user` -> Wrong username or password. Re-check the -u value and retype the password at the -p prompt; for cron, check the credentials in your .cnf file.
- `ERROR 1049 (42000): Unknown database` -> You tried to import into a database that does not exist yet. Run the CREATE DATABASE step first, then import.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Dump the full database on a schedule with mariadb-dump (or HeidiSQL), automated via cron or Task Scheduler.
- Store backups off the server machine.
- Run every migration on a throwaway copy first, stop writes before a live ALTER, and prefer additive changes.
- Run a restore test and compare row counts so a backup is proven, not assumed.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Backup file is 0 bytes or corrupted | mysqldump failed silently | Run the dump command manually and check for errors. Common causes: wrong password, disk full, or MySQL not running |
| Restore fails with "table already exists" | The backup includes CREATE TABLE statements that conflict with existing tables | Drop the existing tables first, or use `--add-drop-table` in your mysqldump command |
| Backup takes too long and times out | The database is too large for a single dump | Back up individual tables: `mysqldump database_name table_name > backup.sql` |
| Can't find the backup when you need it | You didn't name it clearly or saved it somewhere random | Always name backups with the date: `backup-2026-06-19.sql`. Keep them in ONE folder: `C: ackups\` |

## Try it yourself

Run one full backup-and-restore-test cycle on your own database, end to end, and report the numbers.

1. Take a dated dump of your live database with `mariadb-dump`.
2. Create `fivem_restore_test`, import the dump into it.
3. Pick one table that actually has rows on your server, for example `players` or `users`, and run `SELECT COUNT(*)` against both the live database and the restore.

```bash
mariadb-dump -u root -p fivem > fivem-test.sql
mariadb -u root -p -e "CREATE DATABASE fivem_restore_test;"
mariadb -u root -p fivem_restore_test < fivem-test.sql
mariadb -u root -p -e "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fivem.players;"
mariadb -u root -p -e "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fivem_restore_test.players;"
```

The two counts must match exactly. Write down both numbers. If they match, you have a backup you have actually restored, which is the only kind that counts. When you are done, drop the test database with `DROP DATABASE fivem_restore_test;` so it does not sit around getting stale.

## References
- oxmysql documentation: https://overextended.dev/oxmysql
- MariaDB · mariadb-dump: https://mariadb.com/kb/en/mariadb-dump/
- HeidiSQL · export and import: https://www.heidisql.com/

title: B8 · Paid script support workflow · Quasar Academy
ai:
Selling a script means signing up for support, and unmanaged support eats your time and your reviews. A workflow fixes that: a fixed intake template, a triage rule, and a written support boundary so buyers know what they get. This is the difference between a side income and a second unpaid job.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A repeatable support process for a script you sell: issue intake, reproduction templates, versioning, and a clear support boundary.
- Time: ~25 minutes.
- You need: A script you ship (or plan to), and a place buyers can reach you.
- You'll learn: Intake template -> clean-server reproduction -> triage -> fix, version, and release -> the support scope and response time you commit to

### Before you start
- I have a script I sell or am preparing to sell.
- I use Git/GitHub for versioning (the version-control lessons).
- I have a support channel (Discord, ticket system, or store messaging).

> **Scope is kindness**
> A clear support boundary protects both sides. State what is covered (bugs in your script, install help) and what is not (custom edits, conflicts with other paid scripts, framework-wide debugging). Buyers respect a clear line far more than a vague promise you cannot keep.

## The workflow

### Step 1 - Use a fixed intake template
_Goal: Every report arrives with the info you need to act._

Require buyers to fill the same fields. No template, no triage.

```text
Script version:
Framework + version (ESX Legacy / Qbox / QBCore):
FXServer artifact/build (in the FXServer console window, or the txAdmin Live Console, type `version` and copy the line it prints):
GTA game build (a separate content version, set by `sv_enforceGameBuild` in server.cfg, e.g. 3258; leave blank if not set):
What you did:
What you expected:
What happened (exact console error):
```

### Step 2 - Reproduce before you promise a fix
_Goal: You know whether it is your bug or their setup._

Reproduce on a clean test server with only your script plus its declared dependencies. If another resource is required to reproduce, classify it as an integration conflict. It is only out of scope if your published compatibility/support terms say so; a conflict with an integration you claim to support is still your responsibility.

### Step 3 - Triage by cause
_Goal: Each ticket gets the right response, fast._

Sort tickets: **your bug** (fix and release), **install/config** (point to docs), **conflict** (out of scope, suggest direction), **feature request** (backlog, not support). Most tickets are config, not bugs.

### Step 4 - Fix, version, and release
_Goal: The fix ships with a version bump and a note._

Fix on a branch, bump the version (semver), write a one-line changelog, and release. Reply to the ticket with the version that contains the fix so the buyer knows exactly what to update to.

> **Commit to a response time, not a fix time**
> A support boundary is incomplete without a clock. Publish a first-response time you can actually hit, for example "first reply within 24 hours on weekdays." You are promising to read and classify the ticket, not to ship a fix on a deadline. That single line ends most "are you even there" follow-ups and keeps your reviews calm.

## How it works

The four buckets only matter when you can route a real ticket through them without thinking twice. Here is one vague report taken end to end, the way you will handle dozens of them.

### The ticket arrives

A buyer messages your Discord:

```text
your shop script is broken, garage doesnt open. fix asap please
```

This is unactionable. There is no version, no framework, no error. You do not start debugging. You reply with the intake template from step 1 and ask them to fill every field. Hold the line here: the template is what makes the next four steps fast, so a report without it is not yet a ticket.

### The buyer fills the template

```text
Script version: 2.1.0
Framework + version: ESX Legacy 1.10.6
FXServer version output: FXServer-master v1.0.0.13495 win32 (illustrative artifact number; the last segment is the artifact build, in the 13000-plus range, not a GTA build)
sv_enforceGameBuild: 3258
What you did: walked into the garage zone and pressed E
What you expected: the garage menu opens
What happened (exact console error):
  [script:qs_garage] SCRIPT ERROR: @qs_garage/server/main.lua:42:
  attempt to index a nil value (field 'Functions')
```

Now you can act. That error names your resource, your file, and your line. It is almost certainly your code, but you do not promise a fix yet. You reproduce first.

### Reproduce on a clean server

Spin up a clean test server: the framework (ESX Legacy 1.10.6), your dependencies (ox_lib, oxmysql), and `qs_garage` 2.1.0. Nothing else. Add the line `sv_enforceGameBuild 3258` to your server.cfg before the server starts to match. It is a startup-only setting and cannot be changed in the live console. Walk into the zone and press E.

If the error reproduces, it is your bug. Line 42 calls `ESX.Functions.GetPlayerFromId(...)` — but ESX has no `Functions` table (that is QBCore syntax: `QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer`). On ESX the function lives directly on the ESX object as `ESX.GetPlayerFromId(...)`, so `ESX.Functions` is nil and indexing it throws. If it does not reproduce, the cause lives in something the buyer has that you do not, and you go hunting for a conflict instead.

### Classify and respond

It reproduced on a clean server, so this is bucket one: **your bug**. Compare that with the other three outcomes you could have landed on:

- **Install/config**: it only broke because their `sv_enforceGameBuild` did not match what the script targets. You point them at the install doc, not a code fix.
- **Conflict**: it only reproduced once a second paid inventory script was loaded. Out of scope. You name the likely culprit and suggest a load order or asking that script's author.
- **Feature request**: "can the garage also store boats." Backlog, not support. You acknowledge and move on.

Here it is your bug, so you fix line 42 on a branch, then reply with a real version number, not a vague "fixed soon":

```text
Confirmed on a clean server, this is on us. Fixed in 2.1.1 (out now):
the garage code used QBCore-style `ESX.Functions.*` syntax; on ESX
those functions live directly on `ESX` (e.g. `ESX.GetPlayerFromId`),
so the call hit a nil `ESX.Functions`. Update qs_garage to 2.1.1 and
restart the resource. Thanks for the clean report, it made this a
five-minute fix.
```

### Why the version number carries the meaning

The reply lands because `2.1.1` is not a random label. Semantic versioning gives each part a job, and that is what makes your one-line changelog readable at a glance:

- **Patch** (2.1.0 to 2.1.**1**): a backward-compatible bug fix. The buyer updates and restarts. Nothing else changes. This is the answer to most support tickets.
- **Minor** (2.1.0 to 2.**2**.0): new feature, still backward compatible. Existing configs keep working, so the buyer can update safely and adopt the feature when ready.
- **Major** (2.1.0 to **3**.0.0): a breaking change. Configs or database schema change, so the buyer must read migration notes before updating, never mid-event.

Because the buyer knows `2.1.1` is a patch, they know the update is safe and routine. Your changelog line says exactly that, and that is why "update to 2.1.1" is a complete support answer:

```text
## 2.1.1 - 2026-06-16
### Fixed
- Garage menu failed to open on ESX Legacy 1.10.6 (called nil ESX.Functions instead of ESX.GetPlayerFromId on line 42).
```

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Tickets are vague and unfixable` -> Enforce the intake template. No version, framework, and exact console error means it is not a ticket yet, so reply with the template instead of guessing.
- `You promised a fix, then it was a conflict` -> Reproduce on a clean server with only your script and its dependencies before you commit. If it needs a third paid script to break, it is out of scope.
- `Drowning in 'it does not work'` -> Triage by cause. Config and conflicts are not bugs; point to docs or mark out of scope. Feature requests go to the backlog, not the support queue.
- `Buyers expect free custom edits` -> Publish the support scope up front. Customization is paid work, not support.
- `Nobody knows which version fixed it` -> Bump semver and changelog every release; reply with the fixed version number so 'update to 2.1.1' is the whole answer.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Reply with the intake template instead of debugging a vague report, so every ticket arrives actionable.
- Reproduce on a clean server with only your script and its dependencies before committing to a fix.
- Route a ticket through the four buckets: your bug, install/config, conflict, or feature request.
- Map a fix to the right semver part (patch, minor, major) and reply with the version number that contains it.
- Publish a support scope and a first-response time you can actually hold.

## References
- Tebex · developer docs: https://docs.tebex.io/developers
- Semantic Versioning 2.0.0: https://semver.org/
- Keep a Changelog: https://keepachangelog.com/
- GitHub · issue & PR templates: https://docs.github.com/en/communities/using-templates-to-encourage-useful-issues-and-pull-requests/configuring-issue-templates-for-your-repository
- Cfx.re · server commands: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/server-commands/
- ESX · GetPlayerFromId: https://documentation.esx-framework.org/legacy/Server/Functions/getplayerfromid/
- QBCore · server function reference: https://docs.qbcore.org/qbcore-documentation/qb-core/server-function-reference

title: B8 · Release notes and semantic versioning · Quasar Academy
ai:
Buyers update with confidence when versions mean something. Semantic versioning (semver) and a short changelog turn "new update lol" into "this is a safe patch" or "this one needs a config change." It is five minutes per release and it kills a whole category of support tickets.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A versioned release with a grouped changelog and a GitHub tag a buyer can roll back to.
- Time: ~15 minutes.
- You need: A script under Git and a place to publish changelogs.
- You'll learn: What MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH means -> how each maps to a real FiveM change -> how to write a changelog -> how to tag a release

### Before you start
- My script is in a Git repository.
- I can create a GitHub release or tag.
- I know the difference between a bug fix and a breaking change.

> **MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH**
> Versions are three numbers. **PATCH** (1.2.3 -> 1.2.4): a bug fix, safe to take. **MINOR** (1.2.x -> 1.3.0): new feature, backwards compatible. **MAJOR** (1.x -> 2.0.0): a breaking change that needs config or migration work. The number tells the buyer how nervous to be.

## Version and document a release

### Step 1 - Decide the bump
_Goal: The version number matches the kind of change._

For a FiveM resource, the bump is not a feeling, it maps to concrete things a buyer's server depends on:

- **PATCH** (1.2.3 -> 1.2.4): you fixed a bug and nothing the buyer touches changes. A fixed dupe exploit, a corrected blip colour, a typo in a notification. Drop in the new files, restart, done.
- **MINOR** (1.2.x -> 1.3.0): you added something optional and the old setup still works untouched. A new optional `Config` block with a sensible default, a brand new export alongside the existing ones, a new command. A buyer who ignores the new feature notices nothing.
- **MAJOR** (1.x -> 2.0.0): you broke something the buyer has wired into.
