A renamed or removed `Config` key, a changed export signature (different arguments or return), a required SQL migration, a dependency swap (for example moving from a deprecated MySQL wrapper to oxmysql). Their current config or database no longer works as-is.

The test for MAJOR: would a buyer who drops in your new files and restarts get errors or silent breakage with their existing config and database? If yes, it is a MAJOR bump and it needs a migration note.

### Step 2 - Write a short changelog
_Goal: Buyers can read what changed in ten seconds._

Group your lines under headings. The Keep a Changelog standard categories are Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, and Security. Breaking changes do not get their own standard category, so call them out loudly at the top, a bold **Breaking** line above the rest, because that is the one thing a buyer must read before updating. One line each.

```text
## 2.0.0
Breaking: config key `cooldown` renamed to `cooldownMs` — update your config.
Breaking: requires the new SQL migration in /sql/2.0.0.sql before first start.
Added: support for Qbox alongside ESX Legacy and QBCore.
Fixed: dupe when spamming the use key.
```

### Step 3 - Tag the release
_Goal: The version is a real, downloadable point in history._

In your project folder, in a terminal (Windows PowerShell or a Linux/macOS shell), tag the current commit and push the tag with: `git tag -a v2.0.0 -m "v2.0.0"` and then `git push origin v2.0.0`. Here `v2.0.0` is the version with a leading `v` (the common Git tag convention) and `origin` is the default name Git gives your GitHub remote. Then on github.com open your repository, click **Releases** (right sidebar), then **Draft a new release**, pick the `v2.0.0` tag you just pushed under **Choose a tag**, paste your changelog into the description, and click **Publish release**. What you should see: a release page at `github.com/<you>/<repo>/releases/tag/v2.0.0` with your notes and an auto-generated **Source code (zip)** download. The tag pins this exact commit, so a buyer can download `v2.0.0` as a zip, or drop back to `v1.9.4` if your update breaks their live server. That rollback is the safety net: when a server owner takes your update mid-session and it errors, they need to revert to a known-good version in seconds, not dig through commit history while players sit in a broken city.

### Step 4 - Announce with the migration note
_Goal: Nobody is surprised by a breaking change._

When you ship a MAJOR, tell buyers exactly what they must change: which config keys moved, which SQL file to run, which dependency to install first. A breaking update with no migration note is how you earn refunds and bad reviews. Pin the migration note above the download so nobody misses it.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Buyers afraid to update` -> Use semver and a changelog. A PATCH should feel safe because the number says so.
- `A renamed config key shipped as a patch` -> Renaming or removing a Config key breaks every existing buyer's config. That is a MAJOR bump with a migration note, never a quiet PATCH.
- `An export's arguments changed and dependent resources broke` -> A changed export signature breaks any resource that calls it. Bump MAJOR and document the new signature so buyers can update their callers.
- `A required SQL migration left out of the notes` -> If first start needs a new table or column, the migration file and the run order are the most important line in the changelog. Lead with it.
- `No way to roll back after a bad update` -> Tag every release on GitHub so a server owner can revert to an exact version mid-incident instead of waiting on you.
- `The tag shows locally but is not on GitHub` -> git tag only creates the tag in your local repo. Push it explicitly with git push origin v2.0.0 (a plain git push does not send tags). If the release page cannot find the tag, it was never pushed.
- `Tagged the wrong commit, or want to redo a tag` -> Delete it locally and remotely, then re-tag the right commit: git tag -d v2.0.0 then git push origin :refs/tags/v2.0.0, then tag again. Never silently reuse a published version number for different code, bump instead.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Map a FiveM change to a PATCH (fix), MINOR (optional addition), or MAJOR (config, export, or SQL break).
- Write a grouped changelog that leads with breaking changes and names the SQL migration.
- Tag each release on GitHub so versions are downloadable and a buyer can revert mid-incident.
- Ship breaking changes with a clear migration note pinned above the download.

## Try it yourself

Classify each of these real resource changes as PATCH, MINOR, or MAJOR before you read the answers:

1. You fixed a money dupe that triggered when a player spam-clicked the use key.
2. You added an optional `Config.UseTarget` flag that defaults to `false`, so existing servers behave exactly as before.
3. You renamed the export `getCash` to `getMoney` to match your other resources.
4. You added a new column to the `player_garages` table and the resource errors on start without it.

Answers: (1) PATCH, a bug fix with nothing for the buyer to change. (2) MINOR, a new optional feature that is backwards compatible. (3) MAJOR, a changed export signature breaks every resource that called `getCash`. (4) MAJOR, a required SQL migration; ship the `.sql` file and lead the changelog with the run-it-first instruction. Now do the same for the last change you actually made to one of your scripts, write its one-line changelog entry, and decide the bump.

## References
- Semantic Versioning 2.0.0: https://semver.org/
- Keep a Changelog: https://keepachangelog.com/
- GitHub · creating releases: https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/releasing-projects-on-github

title: B8 · Production code review checklist · Quasar Academy
ai:
Code review is how teams keep quality up without heroics. This is the FiveM-specific checklist: the handful of things that, if you check them every time, catch most of what blows up a server. Use it on your own code before shipping and on teammates' pull requests before merging.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A checklist to review a FiveM resource before it ships or merges, so the same mistakes stop reaching production.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: A resource to review (yours or a teammate's) and the core concepts from Track B.
- You'll learn: Security checks -> performance checks -> correctness checks -> the questions to ask on every review

### Before you start
- I understand client vs server authority.
- I know what resmon and the tick budget are.
- I can read a fxmanifest and a resource's file split.

> **Review server trust first**
> The first question on every FiveM review is the same: does the client decide anything that should be the server's call? Money, items, jobs, admin, prices. If yes, that is the bug, no matter how clean the rest looks.

## The checklist

## How it works

The checklist tells you what to look for. This section walks the three checks that catch the most damage, so you are reviewing with intent instead of pattern matching. Take one suspect line, ask the right question, and you can usually decide approve or block in seconds.

### Server authority, in practice

Authority means one question: if this value decides money, items, a job, admin rights, or a price, did the server compute it, or did it trust whatever the client sent? Here is the bug you are hunting:

```lua
-- client.lua  (what an exploited client can send)
TriggerServerEvent('bank:deposit', 999999)

-- server.lua  (the version you must block)
RegisterNetEvent('bank:deposit', function(amount)
    local src = source
    addMoney(src, amount)   -- trusts the client's number
end)
```

The client decided the amount, so a player editing the trigger can deposit nine hundred thousand. The fix is to make the server the source of truth and recompute from server-held state, never from the payload (the `getCash`/`removeCash`/`addBank` helpers below are illustrative placeholders for whatever your framework provides; the pattern, not the exact names, is what matters):

```lua
-- server.lua  (server authority)
RegisterNetEvent('bank:deposit', function(amount)
    local src = source
    amount = tonumber(amount)
    if not amount or amount <= 0 then return end   -- validate the input
    local cash = getCash(src)                       -- server-held truth
    if cash < amount then return end                -- server decides if it is allowed
    removeCash(src, amount)
    addBank(src, amount)
end)
```

Note that `source` is only trustworthy because the server fills it in, not the client -- that is what 'validate source' on the checklist means: use the server-set `source`, never an id the client passed inside the payload, and confirm that player is actually allowed to perform the action before you act on it. On a FiveM review, this is the first pass you make: trace every value-bearing net event back to whoever decided the number. If the answer is the client, that is the bug, no matter how clean the rest reads.

### Parameterized queries, in practice

The SQL row on the checklist exists to stop one specific attack. Concatenating a player-supplied value straight into a query string lets that value break out of the data and become SQL. Compare the two forms:

```lua
-- Vulnerable: `name` is a player-supplied value glued straight into the query string
MySQL.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = "' .. name .. '"')

-- Safe: oxmysql sends the value as a parameter, never as code
MySQL.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?', { name })
```

In the first line, a name of `" OR "1"="1` turns the query into one that matches every row, and a more hostile value can drop tables. That is SQL injection. The `?` placeholder form hands the value to MariaDB separately from the query text, so it is always treated as data. The review rule is simple: every value that came from a player must travel as a `?` parameter, never inside the query string.

### resmon and the tick budget, in practice

The performance section assumes two terms from earlier in the track, so here they are in one line each. resmon is the in-game resource monitor you open from the FiveM client console (press F8 in-game to open that console) by typing the `resmon 1` command (type `resmon 0` to close it again); it shows each resource's CPU time per tick in milliseconds. One catch: `resmon` is a developer command. On a normal client it returns `Access denied for command resmon`. To use it you must run the client in developer mode -- launch FiveM with the `+set moo 31337` argument, or use a Beta/Latest update channel -- then reopen the F8 console and run `resmon 1` again. The tick budget is the time a single client frame allows: at 60 frames per second a frame is about 16 milliseconds, shared across every running resource, so any one resource sitting at a fraction of a millisecond at idle is healthy and anything climbing toward a full millisecond while nothing is happening needs a longer `Wait` or distance gating. With those two numbers in hand, the resmon row becomes a concrete check rather than a vibe: read the millisecond figure at idle, then under load, and confirm both stay small.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Clean code that still gets exploited` -> You reviewed style, not trust. Check server authority on every value-bearing action first.
- `Approved a resource that tanks resmon` -> Add the performance section to every review: threads, budget, cleanup, distance gating.
- `Merged code with hardcoded secrets` -> Block it. Secrets come from convars; the review fails if a key is in the source.
- `No README, buyers cannot install` -> Docs are part of the review. No install/dependency/config doc, no merge.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Review server authority and event/SQL safety before anything else.
- Check threads, resmon budget, cleanup, and distance gating for performance.
- Require config, error handling, docs, and a version bump.
- Run the same checklist on your own code and on every pull request.

## References
- Cfx.re docs · scripting manual: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/
- oxmysql · parameterized queries: https://overextended.dev/oxmysql

title: B8 · Webhooks and privacy · Quasar Academy
ai:
Webhooks are how your server tells Discord what happened: a sale, a ban, an error. Done carelessly they leak the webhook URL, dump player data into a channel, or spam Discord until it rate-limits you. This lesson keeps logging useful and safe.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: How to log to Discord webhooks without leaking secrets, spamming channels, or exposing player data.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: The HTTP/webhooks lesson and a server you can configure.
- The arc: Webhooks are secrets, send server-side only, decide what NOT to log, then rate-limit and redact.

### Before you start
- I can send a PerformHttpRequest from the server (the HTTP/webhooks lesson).
- I read secrets from convars (the convars lesson).
- I have a private Discord channel for logs.

> **A webhook URL is a password**
> Anyone with the webhook URL can post to that channel. Never put it in client code, never commit it, never paste it in public. Store it in a convar and read it server-side only. If it leaks, delete the webhook in Discord and make a new one.

## Log safely

### Step 1 - Send only from the server
_Goal: The webhook URL never reaches a client._

All webhook calls happen in server scripts, with the URL read from a convar. A client never sees the URL and never triggers the send directly without a server check. `PerformHttpRequest` is a server native, so this code can only live in a `server_script`.

Read the URL from a convar, then post a Discord embed. Discord expects JSON, so set the `Content-Type` header and encode the body with `json.encode`.

```lua
local hook = GetConvar('logs_webhook', '')

local function sendLog(title, description)
    if hook == '' then return end -- no webhook configured, do nothing
    local body = json.encode({
        embeds = {
            {
                title = title,
                description = description,
                color = 3447003, -- a blue stripe down the embed
            }
        }
    })
    PerformHttpRequest(hook, function(status)
        if status ~= 200 and status ~= 204 then
            print(('[logs] webhook returned %s'):format(status))
        end
    end, 'POST', body, { ['Content-Type'] = 'application/json' })
end
```

### Step 2 - Decide what NOT to log
_Goal: No private or sensitive data goes to a channel._

Log events, not secrets. Never send license keys, passwords, full IPs, tokens, or anything that identifies a real person beyond what you actually need.

Do not use FiveM's `GetHashKey` as privacy protection. It is a fast 32-bit game hash, not a cryptographic hash, so it can collide and does not provide a meaningful irreversible pseudonym. Keep raw identifiers in your access-controlled database when they are genuinely required. Post an internal audit-row id or a short-lived server source to Discord, then let authorized staff look up the private record.

```lua
-- The private database row contains the durable identifier and evidence.
-- Discord receives only the internal case id and current server slot.
local caseId = 1842
local src = source
sendLog('Ban issued', ('Case #%d, current source %d'):format(caseId, src))
```

### Step 3 - Rate-limit your sends
_Goal: Discord does not throttle or block your webhook._

Discord rate-limits webhooks. Batch or throttle high-frequency events instead of firing one request per tick. A log that spams itself stops logging when Discord cuts it off.

Push lines onto a queue and flush them on a timer. One request every few seconds carries many events instead of one request per event.

```lua
local queue = {}

local function logEvent(line)
    queue[#queue + 1] = line
end

CreateThread(function()
    while true do
        Wait(3000) -- flush at most once every 3 seconds
        if #queue > 0 then
            local batch = table.concat(queue, '\n')
            queue = {}
            sendLog('Activity', batch)
        end
    end
end)
```

### Step 4 - Separate channels by severity
_Goal: Important logs are not buried in noise._

Send errors and security events to a private staff channel, routine activity to another. Mixing a critical ban alert into a firehose of chat logs means nobody sees it.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Webhook URL leaked` -> Delete the webhook in Discord, create a new one, and move the URL into a server convar read server-side only.
- `Player personal data dumped to a channel` -> Log events, not identities. Redact or hash IPs/tokens; never send secrets.
- `Discord throttled or blocked the webhook` -> You are sending too fast. Batch or throttle high-frequency logs.
- `Critical alerts lost in noise` -> Split channels by severity; errors and security to a quiet staff channel.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Treat webhook URLs as secrets: convar-stored, server-side, never committed.
- Log events, not secrets or personal data; redact identifiers.
- Throttle or batch high-frequency logs to avoid Discord rate limits.
- Separate channels by severity so critical alerts are seen.

## References
- Cfx.re docs · PerformHttpRequest: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/PerformHttpRequest/
- Discord · webhook resources: https://discord.com/developers/docs/resources/webhook

title: B8 · Resource licensing and escrow · Quasar Academy

toc.label: What to understand before you transact
How to recognize escrow in the wild

ai:
Before you sell or buy a script, you need to understand how FiveM protects code. Some scripts ship open and editable; others ship escrowed, where the core is protected and only a config is exposed. Getting this wrong means buyers cannot make the edits they expected, or sellers ship code they did not mean to give away.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: What escrow and licenses actually mean for FiveM scripts: what buyers can edit, what they cannot, how to recognize an escrowed resource on disk, and how to choose for what you sell or buy.
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: An interest in selling or buying scripts the right way.

### Before you start
- I understand a resource is a folder of files the server loads.
- I know the difference between core logic and a config file.
- I am selling or buying through a legitimate channel (Tebex).

> **Escrow in one sentence**
> FiveM's asset escrow lets a seller protect the core code of a resource while still exposing a config (and sometimes open files) for the buyer to edit. The buyer runs it, configures it, but cannot read or copy the protected core.

## What to understand before you transact

### Step 1 - Know open vs escrowed
_Goal: You can tell what a buyer will and will not be able to edit._

**Open** resources ship as readable Lua: fully editable, fully copyable. **Escrowed** resources protect the core; the buyer edits the exposed config and any files the author left open, but not the protected logic. Neither is "better"; they fit different products.

### Step 2 - As a buyer, check editing rights before you buy
_Goal: No surprise that you cannot make the change you needed._

If you bought a script specifically to modify its core behavior, an escrowed version will block you. Read the listing: what is configurable, what is open, what is locked. Ask before buying, not after.

### Step 3 - As a seller, decide what to protect
_Goal: You expose a clean config and protect what is yours._

Expose a well-documented config so buyers rarely need the core. Protect the logic that is your actual product. Leaving everything open invites resale of your work; locking everything frustrates legitimate customization.

### Step 4 - Respect licenses both ways
_Goal: You only ship and run what you are licensed to._

Do not resell, re-leak, or strip another author's escrow. On the buy side, a license is per the terms you agreed to (often per-server). Reselling someone else's work is the fast path to a marketplace ban.

## How to recognize escrow in the wild

You will not always be told a script is escrowed. You have to read the signs, both in the files you receive and in the listing before you buy.

### What an escrowed resource looks like on disk

When you unzip an escrowed resource, most of it is unreadable. The core Lua files are encrypted, and opening one in a text editor shows a protected blob, not code you can follow or edit. What stays readable is whatever the author deliberately left out of protection: usually a `config.lua` (or a `locales` folder) so you can change settings without touching the core.

The author controls that line with one manifest directive. In `fxmanifest.lua` the seller lists the files to keep open with `escrow_ignore`:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script 'config.lua'
client_script 'client/main.lua'
server_script 'server/main.lua'

-- Files the seller leaves OPEN and editable. Everything else is encrypted.
escrow_ignore {
    'config.lua',
    'locales/*.lua'
}

dependencies {
    'ox_lib'
}
```

When that resource is packaged for sale, FiveM's Asset Escrow system encrypts the protectable files *except* the ones named in `escrow_ignore`. You may also notice a small `.fxap` file inside an escrowed resource: that is the escrow manifest file the system writes in, and the resource needs it (plus a valid grant) to load. At runtime the protected core executes normally on the server, so the script works as intended, but the buyer never sees that code on disk in readable form. If you open `config.lua` and it reads like plain settings, while `server/main.lua` is an unreadable blob, that is escrow doing exactly its job.

> **What escrow does not do**
> Escrow protects the source from being read and copied; it does not give the seller remote control of your server. But it is not purely offline: an escrowed resource only runs when your server's Cfx.re license key holds a valid grant for that asset, and the runtime checks that entitlement when the resource loads. If the key has no grant you get a "You lack the required entitlement" error and the resource will not start. So it is code protection plus an ownership check, not a remote kill switch the seller can flip at will.

> **Escrow protects the seller, not you**
> Escrow hides the core code from YOU as much as from anyone else. Because you cannot read an escrowed server file, you cannot verify what it does — a malicious author could hide a backdoor or an external HTTP call inside the protected core. Escrow is not a safety guarantee for the buyer. Only buy escrowed scripts from reputable authors through a legitimate marketplace (Tebex), and watch your server console and outbound connections after installing anything new.

### How to read a listing before you buy

A good Tebex listing tells you where the author drew the escrow line before you pay. Look for the section that splits the resource three ways:

- **Configurable** means exposed in the open `config.lua`: prices, coordinates, labels, item names. You can change these freely.
- **Open** means a full file left out of escrow that you can read and edit, sometimes a client UI file or a hook the author wants you to extend.
- **Locked** means encrypted core: the logic that is the actual product. You cannot read it, edit it, or copy it.

Match that split against the change you actually need. If the one thing you came to modify sits in the locked column, an escrowed copy will not let you do it, no matter how good the config is. When a listing does not spell this out, ask the seller which files are open before you buy.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Bought a script, cannot edit the part I needed` -> It is escrowed and that logic is protected. Check the configurable / open / locked split in the listing before buying next time.
- `Opened a file and it is an unreadable blob` -> That file is escrowed (encrypted). Edit the open config.lua instead, or whatever the author named in escrow_ignore. The blob is not corrupt.
- `Shipped open code, now it is being resold` -> Escrow the core logic and name only your config in escrow_ignore. Open everything only if you intend it to be freely modified.
- `Config is too thin, buyers keep asking for core edits` -> Expand the config and docs so common changes do not require touching protected code. Add the field to escrow_ignore, not the whole core.
- `Marketplace warning for redistribution` -> You shipped or resold work you were not licensed to. Use only your own or properly licensed code.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain open vs escrowed resources and what each lets a buyer edit.
- Recognize an escrowed resource on disk: encrypted core files plus an open config, controlled by escrow_ignore in the manifest.
- Read a listing's configurable / open / locked split and match it against the change you need.
- As a seller, expose a clean config and protect your core logic.
- Respect licenses: never resell, re-leak, or strip another author's escrow.

## References
- Cfx.re docs · Asset escrow: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/asset-escrow/
- Tebex · developer docs: https://docs.tebex.io/developers

title: B8 · Build and grow a trustworthy Tebex store · Quasar Academy
ai:
A buyer is not purchasing Lua files. They are purchasing a result for their server and confidence that the result will install, work, and receive support. Build that confidence with proof, clear compatibility, safe delivery, and a store you have tested before asking anyone to pay.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A tested store foundation, one complete package page, a simple offer map, and a 30-day publishing plan.
- Time: 45-60 minutes for the lesson, plus store review and content production time.
- You need: A finished FiveM resource you own, a Tebex Creator account, support contact details, and product media.
- Pass condition: A private test purchase delivers correctly, the listing states compatibility and support clearly, and every growth feature you enable has a measured purpose.
- Verified: June 2026 against current Tebex Creator and Cfx.re monetization documentation.

### Before you start
- I own or have permission to sell every file, image, brand asset, and dependency in the package.
- The resource has passed the production code-review and release checklist in this module.
- I understand that live Cfx.re and Tebex rules override examples in this lesson.
- I have a private test server and a separate test customer identity.

> **Tebex is the required commerce route for Cfx.re servers**
> Cfx.re identifies Tebex as its authorized monetization partner and prohibits other payment providers for Cfx.re server goods under the Platform License Agreement. Read the live Cfx.re agreement, Tebex Creator Agreement, Acceptable Use Policy, and store-review requirements before listing anything. This lesson is operational guidance, not legal advice.

## 1. The core diagnosis

Strong code does not automatically create a strong store. A product can work perfectly and still fail because buyers cannot find it, understand it, trust its compatibility, or see evidence that support exists. Diagnose the store in five areas: trust, traffic, packaging, offer structure, and presentation.

Do not guess which one is weak. Review package-page questions, support tickets, delivery failures, refund reasons, and the statistics available in the Tebex Control Panel.

## 2. Trust is the foundation

Before a buyer pays, the page must answer five questions without a Discord ticket:

1. What does this product improve for my server or players?
2. Does it support my framework, inventory, target system, database, and artifact requirements?
3. What exactly will Tebex deliver?
4. How do I install, update, and get support?
5. What happens if it does not work or I need a refund?

Trust comes from evidence: a working demo, current documentation, precise compatibility, real support terms, visible release history, and honest buyer feedback. Do not manufacture testimonials, exchange products for guaranteed positive reviews, or claim usage numbers you cannot prove.

## 3. You are not selling code

A server owner buys a player or operator outcome: a cleaner dealership, easier staff workflow, stronger roleplay loop, less configuration work, or reliable support. Lead with that result. Put technical details directly below it so an informed buyer can verify compatibility.

Compare these two openings:

```text
Feature-first: Advanced vehicle shop with oxmysql, exports, categories, and callbacks.

Outcome-first: Give players a clear vehicle-buying experience that installs without
replacing your existing garage. Tested on the listed ESX and Qbox versions.
```

The outcome earns attention. The compatibility statement earns trust. You need both.

## 4. Tebex store optimization

### Step 1 - Create and prepare the Tebex project
_Goal: the project is ready for review before products are promoted._

1. Create the Creator account through `creator.tebex.io` and verify the email address.
2. Complete the identity, business, wallet, and project details requested by the current onboarding flow.
3. Connect the FiveM game-server integration from the Tebex Control Panel.
4. Add a support page, contact route, privacy information, and clear package terms.
5. Submit the project for Tebex review and resolve every requested change.

Store review and identity checks can change. Follow the current dashboard checklist instead of copying screenshots from an older tutorial.

```md
# Tebex launch record
Creator email verified: yes / no
Identity verification: pending / complete
Wallet configured: yes / no
FiveM integration connected: yes / no
Support page URL:
Policies reviewed on:
Store review status:
```

### Step 2 - Define the customer result
_Goal: the package leads with a concrete server outcome instead of a feature dump._

Write one sentence using this shape:

```text
Help [server owner] create [player or operator result] without [specific setup pain].
```

Example:

```text
Help ESX and Qbox server owners give players a clear vehicle-shopping flow
without replacing their current garage or editing protected files.
```

Then write the proof that supports the promise: supported frameworks, measured idle usage, installation time from a clean test, configuration surface, and a short real-server demonstration.

### Step 3 - Create one complete package
_Goal: a buyer can judge, install, and support the product from the listing._

In **Packages**, create a category and one package. Tebex currently supports one-time, recurring, or combined pricing plus multiple delivery types. Choose the delivery method that actually matches the product.

Use this listing order:

```md
# Product name and result
One sentence describing the server or player outcome.

## See it working
Short video and readable screenshots from a real test server.

## What is included
Exact resources, editable files, protected files, configs, and documentation.

## Compatibility
Framework versions, inventory, target, database, artifact or game-build needs,
OneSync assumptions, and known incompatibilities.

## Installation
Dependencies first, SQL or migration steps, ensure order, configuration, and proof.

## Performance and security
Measured test conditions, server-side validation boundaries, and stored data.

## Support and updates
Support channel, required evidence, response target, update access, and changelog.

## Terms
Delivery, refund route, license scope, and links to the current policies.
```

Tebex package pages support images and YouTube video. Keep the short showcase focused on the result. Put installation and troubleshooting in a separate practical walkthrough so buyers can evaluate both the experience and the setup.

### Step 4 - Test delivery without a real purchase
_Goal: the package reaches the correct account and every delivery action is recorded._

Use Tebex's documented test path before making the store public:

1. Keep the store private.
2. Enable **Test Mode** under the current checkout settings.
3. Complete the purchase with the test customer.
4. Confirm the transaction, webhook behavior, FiveM asset or file delivery, Discord action, and customer email as applicable.
5. Test refund or subscription-expiry behavior only through the supported path for that delivery type.
6. Disable Test Mode before launch.

Tebex also supports manual payments, but its documentation notes that manual payments do not test the same webhook and statistics path. Use Test Mode when you need to prove the checkout flow. FiveM assets have special revocation limits for test and manual payments, so follow the current FiveM package-testing instructions exactly.

Expected output:

```
The test customer receives the intended deliverable, no unrelated item or role is granted, the support link works, and the package can be installed from the published instructions.
```

Tebex currently documents regional pricing and supported currencies, coupons and sales, gift cards, abandoned-basket recovery, purchase follow-ups, upselling, offers, and store statistics. Availability can depend on the current plan, project type, region, and dashboard configuration.

Use a measurement record for every feature:

```md
## Growth experiment
Feature:
Buyer problem it addresses:
Packages included:
Start date:
End or review date:
Baseline metric:
Success metric:
Result:
Keep / change / disable:
```

- **Regional pricing and currencies:** use the current country-based controls. Check settlement and conversion behavior before changing prices.
- **Abandoned-basket recovery:** use a clear reminder, sensible frequency limits, and an optional discount only when margin allows it.
- **Purchase follow-ups:** send installation, support, changelog, or genuinely related product information. Do not turn support email into spam.
- **Coupons and sales:** set a real reason, start, and end. Do not leave every package permanently discounted.

Do not copy conversion percentages from another store. Your statistics page is the source for your own visits, payments, and average transaction value.

## 5. The value ladder

Do not start with a wall of unrelated packages. Group products around buyer jobs and keep the choice small:

| Offer level | Purpose | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Free proof | Demonstrates documentation, code quality, and support process. | A useful standalone utility with the same install quality as paid work. |
| Focused package | Solves one clear server problem. | Vehicle shop, dispatch add-on, or staff tool. |
| Outcome bundle | Combines products that solve one larger job. | A police operations pack with explicitly listed compatibility. |
| Recurring package | Funds real ongoing value that continues after purchase. | Maintained library, continuing releases, or defined support access. |

Each level must be useful on its own and make the next choice understandable. A free product should demonstrate the same documentation and support quality as paid work, not act as broken bait.

## 6. Bundles: sell solutions

Bundle products that solve one larger job and have been tested together. A police operations bundle might contain dispatch, evidence, and custody tools, but only if their framework, inventory, target, and database requirements can coexist.

Publish the included products, shared dependencies, install order, duplicate-purchase handling, and bundle discount. Do not call a random collection a solution.

## 7. Upsells and downsells

An upsell offers a compatible next step, such as a tested bundle containing the current package. A lower-cost alternative can help a buyer who does not need the full product. Both must be relevant, clearly different, and honest about what is omitted.

Never hide a required dependency as an upsell. If a package cannot function without another paid product, say so before checkout.

## 8. Membership that works

Do not create a subscription only to make billing recur. State what new value continues each billing period, how cancellation works, what access ends, and how existing licenses behave. Ongoing releases, maintained libraries, defined premium support, and genuine early-access programs can support recurring value. A static download with no continuing service usually cannot.

Avoid fake urgency, permanent countdowns, and "best value" labels with no defensible comparison.

## 9. Organic traffic channels

Publish where actual buyers already look: your support Discord, YouTube, the Cfx.re community when its rules allow the post, and the channels you can maintain consistently. Answer useful questions, share release notes, and link to real documentation. Do not paste the same sales message into unrelated communities.

Choose two channels you can maintain for 90 days. Consistency and searchable proof matter more than opening six abandoned profiles.

## 10. Video strategy

Create two kinds of product media:

1. **Short showcase:** 30 to 90 seconds showing the feature in a believable server situation.
2. **Practical walkthrough:** installation, compatibility, configuration, and the expected result on a clean server.

The short showcase earns attention. The practical walkthrough answers compatibility and setup questions. Put both on the package page when the current template supports them.

## 11. Storytelling in everything

Show the product inside a believable server moment. Establish the player's goal, show the friction, demonstrate the product, and finish with the result. A vehicle-shop showcase should show a player arriving, browsing, purchasing, receiving the vehicle, and driving away. A floating menu on an empty test map proves much less.

Keep the story truthful. Do not imply multiplayer behavior, performance, or compatibility that the test did not prove.

## 12. Brand and commercial packaging

Use consistent thumbnails, readable screenshots, one naming system, and the same compatibility format across every listing. Keep the store landing page focused on products and useful categories. Make price, included items, support, and next action easy to find.

The package should feel complete before purchase: listing, demonstration, documentation, changelog, support route, and policy links. Code is one part of the product.

## 13. Language and voiceover workarounds

Language should not block clear product education. Record naturally in the language you speak best, then add reviewed English captions, a translated script, or a properly disclosed dubbed track. Verify technical terms and product claims after translation.

Do not publish an unreviewed generated voiceover that misstates compatibility, prices, policies, or installation steps. Clear captions and on-screen labels are better than inaccurate narration.

## 14. Your 30-day action plan

```md
# 30-day Tebex plan

## Week 1: foundation
[ ] Complete project, identity, wallet, FiveM integration, policy, and review steps
[ ] Create one category and one complete package
[ ] Test checkout and delivery privately

## Week 2: proof
[ ] Publish one short showcase
[ ] Publish one installation walkthrough
[ ] Add compatibility, FAQ, changelog, and support evidence to the package

## Week 3: offer structure
[ ] Add one genuinely related bundle or upsell
[ ] Review regional pricing and supported currencies without changing them blindly
[ ] Configure one measured follow-up or recovery experiment

## Week 4: learn
[ ] Review store statistics and support questions
[ ] Fix the biggest source of buyer confusion
[ ] Publish the result and next release note
```

### If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `The store review is blocked` -> Open the current Tebex review feedback and resolve identity, ownership, policy, support, or product-evidence requests. Do not create another project to bypass review.
- `The test delivered nothing` -> Check the selected delivery type, FiveM integration, package event, target account, and transaction log. Test again while the store remains private.
- `Buyers ask whether their framework works` -> Move compatibility to the top half of the package page and name tested versions plus known unsupported combinations.
- `A bundle contains incompatible products` -> Disable it. Test the complete combination on one clean server and publish one shared dependency and ensure-order document before relisting.
- `A growth tool is enabled but no one knows whether it helped` -> Record a baseline, review date, and one metric. Disable experiments that add buyer friction without a measured benefit.
- `A buyer offers a positive review for a free product` -> Ask for honest feedback without requiring a positive public statement or hiding the relationship. Never fabricate social proof.

### What you can do now

## Recap
- Prepare a Tebex Creator project and FiveM integration through the current onboarding flow.
- Write a benefit-led package page with exact compatibility, installation, support, and terms.
- Test checkout and delivery privately before launch.
- Build focused packages, compatible bundles, and subscriptions with ongoing value.
- Use supported engagement tools as measured experiments rather than copied promises.
- Publish product proof and practical documentation where buyers can find it.

### Try it yourself

Choose one finished resource and complete its package page using the template in Step 3. Give the draft to a server owner who did not build it. Without messaging you, they must be able to identify the result, compatibility, dependencies, delivery, installation order, support route, and refund-policy link. Fix every question they still have, then complete a private Test Mode purchase. After that proof passes, continue to the Tebex compliance and refunds lesson before launch.

> **Next lesson**
> Continue to [Tebex compliance and refunds](/lessons/tebex-compliance-refunds) before publishing the store. Growth controls never replace current platform rules, truthful terms, delivery records, or a support process.

## References
- Cfx.re · Setting up a Tebex Store: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-tebex-store/
- Cfx.re · Platform License Agreement: https://fivem.net/terms
- Tebex · Project launch checklist: https://docs.tebex.io/creators/initial-setup-guide/tebex-project-launch-checklist-from-account-to-activation
- Tebex · Create a package: https://docs.tebex.io/creators/tebex-control-panel/how-to-create-packages/how-to-create-a-package
- Tebex · Test a package: https://docs.tebex.io/creators/tebex-control-panel/how-to-create-packages/how-to-test-a-package
- Tebex · Regional pricing: https://docs.tebex.io/creators/tebex-control-panel/settings/checkout/regional-pricing
- Tebex · Store best practices: https://docs.tebex.io/creators/growth-and-success/tebex-store-best-practices
- Tebex · FiveM package best practices: https://docs.tebex.io/creators/growth-and-success/fivem-best-practices-for-packages
- Tebex · Acceptable Use Policy: https://www.tebex.io/terms-creator-agreement/acceptable-use-policy

title: B8 · Tebex compliance and refunds · Quasar Academy
ai:
Setting up a store is the easy part. Staying in business means handling refunds, chargebacks, and the rules of the platform and the wider FiveM ecosystem. This lesson is the sober follow-up to store setup: the policies and habits that keep your store open and your reputation intact.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: Where to verify current monetization rules -> refund vs chargeback -> useful dispute records -> policies to publish
- Time: ~20 minutes.
- You need: A Tebex store draft and tested package from the previous store setup and growth lesson.

> **Complete the store foundation first**
> If you have not created and privately tested a package yet, complete [Build and grow a trustworthy Tebex store](/lessons/tebex-store-growth) first. This lesson assumes delivery, compatibility, and support information already exist.

### Before you start
- I have a Tebex store draft and one package that passed a private delivery test.
- I sell digital goods (scripts) or server perks.
- I understand I am running a small business, not just a Discord.

> **Sell what you are allowed to sell**
> Monetization rules can change. Read the current Cfx.re Platform License Agreement, Rockstar/Cfx monetization policy, Tebex Acceptable Use Policy, and any publisher-specific restrictions before listing a package. Do not resell assets you do not own, and use the payment route the current Cfx agreement requires. Take payment ONLY through Tebex -- never collect card numbers yourself or accept side payments (PayPal, crypto, direct transfer) for server goods; doing so breaks the Platform License Agreement, voids Tebex's fraud protection, and exposes you to chargebacks and account loss. This lesson is operational guidance, not legal advice or a substitute for the live policies.

## Run a compliant store

### Step 1 - Publish a clear refund policy
_Goal: Buyers and you both know the terms up front._

State your refund window and conditions before purchase: digital goods are often non-refundable once delivered, and you decide and publish the policy. One caveat: a blanket "no refunds" line is not automatically enforceable everywhere. Some regions grant consumers a cooling-off period, and a few keep limited rights even on digital goods unless the buyer explicitly waived them at checkout. Write the policy to respect that rather than pretend it does not exist. A written policy you can point to still defuses most disputes.

### Step 2 - Understand chargebacks
_Goal: You know the difference between a refund and a chargeback._

A **refund** is money you voluntarily return to the buyer through Tebex. A **chargeback** is different: the buyer skips you entirely and asks their card provider (their bank or card network -- the "issuer") to forcibly reverse the charge, which can cost you a fee on top of the lost sale. A payment dispute raised this way is decided by that bank or network, not by you or Tebex. Use the exact response deadline shown in your Tebex creator panel (the management dashboard at creator.tebex.io, where you manage the store you set up in the Tebex setup lesson) -- do not rely on a generic number of days, because the dispute window is set by the card network, not by Tebex or by this course. Submit truthful order, delivery, terms, and support records. The issuing bank or payment network decides the outcome, and evidence never guarantees a win.

### Step 3 - Keep records
_Goal: You can prove delivery and terms in a dispute._

Keep order records, delivery confirmation, and your published terms. In a chargeback, evidence that the buyer accepted the terms and received the goods is what protects you.

### Step 4 - Stay inside platform and ecosystem rules
_Goal: Your store and server are not at risk from what you sell._

The live Cfx.re agreement, publisher restrictions, and Tebex AUP set the hard limits. Do not infer compliance from labels such as cosmetic, supporter, or pay-to-win. Compare the exact deliverable against the current policy pages before sale, keep a dated record of that review, and ask Tebex/Cfx support when the wording is unclear.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Endless refund arguments` -> Publish a clear refund policy before purchase and point to it. Vague terms invite disputes.
- `Chargeback fees piling up` -> Respond with your published terms and delivery evidence; reduce chargebacks by being clear up front and supporting buyers fast.
- `Store flagged for prohibited items` -> Review Tebex terms and Cfx.re monetization rules. Drop pay-to-win and prohibited perks.
- `No proof in a dispute` -> Keep order, delivery, and terms records for every sale.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Publish a refund policy buyers see before purchase.
- Distinguish refunds from chargebacks and respond to disputes with evidence.
- Keep order, delivery, and terms records.
- Sell only what Tebex and Cfx.re policies allow; avoid pay-to-win.

## Try it yourself

Draft your store's refund policy in five lines, before you ever take a sale. Write one line for each: the refund window (for example, "no refunds once a package is delivered, except as required by law"), what counts as delivery, how a buyer requests help instead of charging back, the response time you commit to, and the one sentence the buyer ticks to accept the terms at checkout. Keep it plain. Then read it against the three boundaries in Step 4 and confirm nothing you sell crosses a Cfx.re or Tebex line. This five-line draft is the core of the evidence you submit through Tebex when a chargeback lands -- Tebex forwards it to the payment processor and the buyer's bank, so you do not contact the bank yourself.

## References
- Cfx.re · monetization and Tebex policy: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-tebex-store/
- Cfx.re · Platform License Agreement: https://fivem.net/terms
- Tebex · Acceptable Use Policy: https://www.tebex.io/terms-creator-agreement/acceptable-use-policy
- Cfx.re · Setting up a Tebex Store (current monetization rules): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/server-manual/setting-up-a-tebex-store/

title: B9 · Multi-character and identity systems · Quasar Academy
ai:
Identity is the spine of a roleplay server: who is this player, which character are they on, and what belongs to that character. Every other script, money, inventory, jobs, keys off the character identifier, so if the identity layer is wrong, everything downstream is wrong. This lesson builds a tiny identity store you can run, then takes it apart so you understand the model instead of trusting a framework blindly.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A server resource named qu_identity that maps one license to two characters and proves money belongs to the character, not the account.
- Time: ~25 minutes.
- You need: A framework server, MariaDB wired to oxmysql, and the database lessons.
- You'll learn: FiveM identifiers and why license: is the anchor -> the two-table account/character model -> how a framework loads a character on selection -> why keying money on the wrong column shares it across characters.

### Before you start
- I have a framework running (ESX Legacy, QBCore, or Qbox).
- I have MariaDB wired to oxmysql and can run SQL against it.
- I understand oxmysql insert, single, and the ? placeholder.
- I know money, inventory, and jobs are stored per character.

> **The identifier that everything hangs on**
> A player connects with a `license:` identifier (a Rockstar Online Services hash) that names the account. It is the conventional account anchor, though it can change if the player switches Rockstar accounts. A multi-character system maps that one license to several characters, each with its own generated `citizenid`. Every other resource must store data against the `citizenid`, not the license, or two characters on the same account will share money, items, and jobs.

## Build it

This is a server-only resource. There is no client file and no F8 output: identity lives entirely on the server, against the database, which is the whole point. You will run one command in the server console and read the proof there.

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_identity
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_identity/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua and the tables
_Goal: FiveM loads oxmysql first, and the database has the account/character shape._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'

dependencies {
    'oxmysql'
}
```

Then run this SQL against your MariaDB before starting the resource. These two tables are the entire identity model in miniature:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_accounts (
  license VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (license)
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_characters (
  citizenid  VARCHAR(16) NOT NULL,
  license    VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
  first_name VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL,
  money      INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (citizenid),
  UNIQUE KEY uniq_license_name (license, first_name),
  FOREIGN KEY (license) REFERENCES qu_accounts (license)
);
```

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The license-to-character mapping and the per-character money rule are runnable._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local function makeCitizenId()
    return ('QU%07d'):format(math.random(0, 9999999))
end

local function ensureCharacter(license, firstName)
    local existing = MySQL.scalar.await(
        'SELECT citizenid FROM qu_characters WHERE license = ? AND first_name = ?',
        { license, firstName }
    )
    if existing then return existing end

    local citizenid = makeCitizenId()
    MySQL.insert.await('INSERT INTO qu_characters (citizenid, license, first_name) VALUES (?, ?, ?)',
        { citizenid, license, firstName })
    return citizenid
end

RegisterCommand('identitytest', function()
    local license = 'license:qu000000000000000000000000000000demo'

    MySQL.insert.await('INSERT IGNORE INTO qu_accounts (license) VALUES (?)', { license })

    local charA = ensureCharacter(license, 'Avery')
    local charB = ensureCharacter(license, 'Blake')

    MySQL.update.await('UPDATE qu_characters SET money = ? WHERE citizenid = ?', { 5000, charA })

    local moneyA = MySQL.scalar.await('SELECT money FROM qu_characters WHERE citizenid = ?', { charA })
    local moneyB = MySQL.scalar.await('SELECT money FROM qu_characters WHERE citizenid = ?', { charB })

    print(('[qu_identity] one license: %s'):format(license))
    print(('[qu_identity] character A %s money %d'):format(charA, moneyA))
    print(('[qu_identity] character B %s money %d'):format(charB, moneyB))
end, true)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: Two characters share one license, but only one has the money._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_identity
```

    Save, then run this in the server console (txAdmin Live Console):

    ```text
restart qu_identity
```

    Now run the test command in the same console:

    ```text
identitytest
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_identity] one license: license:qu000000000000000000000000000000demo
[qu_identity] character A QU0431785 money 5000
[qu_identity] character B QU2980014 money 0
```

    The two `QU` ids are random, so yours will differ. What must match is the shape: both characters hang off the same license line, character A holds 5000, and character B holds 0. You just stored money against a character, and the other character on the same account never saw it.

> **Security: this command is restricted, and that is on purpose**
> The `true` at the end of `RegisterCommand('identitytest', ..., true)` marks the command as **restricted**: only a player or console with the matching ACE permission can run it. Any command that creates accounts, moves money, or touches identity must be permission-gated this way — never leave such a command runnable by every player. In production you would also derive the license from the connected player with `GetPlayerIdentifierByType(src, 'license')` and validate every value server-side, never from a hardcoded or client-sent string. The fixed demo license here exists only so the console (`source` 0) can run the example.

## How it works

You ran one command and watched two characters on a single account hold different balances. That is the entire job of an identity system, and it lives in three places: the identifier you anchor on, the two tables that map one account to many characters, and the column you key downstream data against. We will go through each, then connect it back to what your real framework does on character selection.

### The identifier you anchor on

A connecting player does not arrive with one id. FiveM hands you a list of identifiers, and you can read them on the server with `GetPlayerIdentifiers(src)` or pull a single type with `GetPlayerIdentifierByType(src, 'license')`. A typical list looks like this:

```text
license:qu1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b...
license2:1a2b3c...
steam:110000112345678
discord:281234567890123456
fivem:1234567
ip:203.0.113.42
```

Each one names the player from a different angle. `steam:` only exists if they launched through Steam. `discord:` only exists if they linked Discord. `ip:` changes the moment they move house. By convention frameworks anchor the account on `license:`, the Rockstar Online Services hash, because it is present for normal players and tied to their Rockstar account rather than a launcher or network. Be aware of its real limits: the `license:` value CHANGES if the player switches to a different Rockstar/Social Club account, and FiveM's docs name no identifier as always-present or impossible to spoof. For high-stakes use such as bans, Cfx.re recommends the `fivem:` (Cfx.re user) identifier instead. In production you would write `local license = GetPlayerIdentifierByType(src, 'license')`; the build above uses a fixed demo license so the command runs deterministically from the console, where `source` is `0` and there is no real player to read identifiers from.

### Check yourself: Why anchor the account on license: instead of steam: or ip:?

Because `license:` is normally present and is tied to the player's Rockstar account rather than a launcher or a network connection. `steam:` is missing for anyone who did not launch through Steam, `discord:` is missing unless they linked it, and `ip:` changes whenever the player's network changes, which would orphan their account and characters. `license:` is the conventional anchor because it has fewer of those failure modes for everyday players — but it is not bulletproof: the value is derived from the Rockstar account, so it CHANGES if the player switches Rockstar/Social Club accounts, and FiveM does not treat any identifier as unspoofable. For bans specifically, Cfx.re points to the `fivem:` identifier rather than `license:`.

### The two-table model

The schema is the lesson in miniature. There are two tables and the relationship between them is the whole idea.

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_accounts (
  license VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (license)
);
```

`qu_accounts` is one row per human. Its primary key is the `license`, so the database guarantees a license appears at most once. This is the account layer: it knows the person exists, nothing more.

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS qu_characters (
  citizenid  VARCHAR(16) NOT NULL,
  license    VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
  first_name VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL,
  money      INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (citizenid),
  UNIQUE KEY uniq_license_name (license, first_name),
  FOREIGN KEY (license) REFERENCES qu_accounts (license)
);
```

`qu_characters` is one row per character, and a single license can own many rows here. Read the keys in order:

- `citizenid` is the **primary key**: a generated id, unique per character, that everything downstream will reference. It is deliberately separate from the license, because one license owns several citizenids.
- `license` is a plain column plus a **foreign key** back to `qu_accounts`. The foreign key means you cannot create a character for an account that does not exist, which keeps orphan characters out of the table.
- `UNIQUE KEY uniq_license_name (license, first_name)` stops the same account creating two characters with the same name, and it is what makes the build idempotent: re-running the test cannot duplicate Avery. Without a unique key, an upsert has nothing to collide on, so every run inserts again.

The `citizenid` is generated, never the license. That separation is the point: the account is one thing, a character is another, and the link is the `license` column on the character row.

### Generating and reusing the character id

```lua
local function makeCitizenId()
    return ('QU%07d'):format(math.random(0, 9999999))
end
```

A character id has to be unique and stable. Real frameworks generate a longer alphanumeric id; this is a teaching-sized version that pads a random number to seven digits, like `QU0431785`. The exact format does not matter. What matters is that it is generated once and then stored, so the character keeps the same id forever. (This demo does not seed `math.random`, so collisions are possible in theory; a real generator would seed it and check for an existing id before inserting, which is why the `(license, first_name)` unique key acts as the backstop here.)

```lua
local function ensureCharacter(license, firstName)
    local existing = MySQL.scalar.await(
        'SELECT citizenid FROM qu_characters WHERE license = ? AND first_name = ?',
        { license, firstName }
    )
    if existing then return existing end

    local citizenid = makeCitizenId()
    MySQL.insert.await('INSERT INTO qu_characters (citizenid, license, first_name) VALUES (?, ?, ?)',
        { citizenid, license, firstName })
    return citizenid
end
```

This is read-then-create. First it asks the database whether this account already has a character with that name, using `MySQL.scalar` to pull back a single value. If a `citizenid` comes back, the character exists and you reuse it. If not, you generate a fresh id and insert the row. That guard is why running `identitytest` twice does not create four characters: the second run finds Avery and Blake already there and returns their existing ids. The `(license, first_name)` unique key is the database-level backstop for the same rule.

### The column that decides everything

```lua
MySQL.update.await('UPDATE qu_characters SET money = ? WHERE citizenid = ?', { 5000, charA })
```

Here is the single most important line in the lesson. The money is set `WHERE citizenid = ?`, keyed to character A's id. Character B has a different `citizenid`, so this update does not touch B's row. When you read both back, A has 5000 and B has 0. That is correct multi-character behavior, and it falls out of one decision: keying money on `citizenid`.

Now picture the bug the audit and every shared-money report come from. Suppose a banking script stored balances against the account instead:

```lua
-- The bug: money keyed on the license (the account), not the character
MySQL.update.await('UPDATE qu_balances SET money = ? WHERE license = ?', { 5000, license })
```

Both of your characters share that one license. A row keyed on `license` is shared by every character on the account, so depositing 5000 as Avery means Blake logs in to find 5000 too. Switch characters, the money follows, because there was only ever one balance row for the whole account. The fix is never some clever sync; it is to key the balance on `citizenid` so each character owns its own row. One wrong column in one query is the difference between a working economy and "all my characters share money" in your bug channel.

### Check yourself: A player reports that money on one character shows up on all their characters. Where do you look first?

The query that writes money. Find the `UPDATE` or `INSERT` that stores the balance and look at its `WHERE` clause and the column it keys on. If it keys on `license`, `identifier`, or any account-level id instead of the character's `citizenid`, that is the bug: one row is shared by every character on the account. Change the key to `citizenid` (and migrate the existing rows so each character gets its own). The same audit applies to inventory and jobs: anything that should be per-character must key on the character id, not the account.

### What your framework does on selection

The build is a hand-cranked version of what a real multichar resource does for you. When a player picks a slot in a character menu, the framework runs the same shape: it has the player's `license`, it loads or creates the matching character row, and then it sets that `citizenid` as the active character for the session so every other resource reads the right one.

- **Qbox** uses `qbx_multicharacter`, generates a `citizenid`, and on selection you read the active character server-side with the core export `exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)`, whose `PlayerData.citizenid` is the active character. Qbox is a community-maintained fork of QBCore built on the ox stack (`ox_lib` and `oxmysql` are hard dependencies).
- **QBCore** uses `qb-multicharacter` with the same `citizenid` concept and `QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)`.
- **ESX Legacy** uses `esx_multicharacter` and keys characters by the `identifier` (the license) plus a character slot, exposed through `ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)`. ESX does not generate a separate citizenid the way QBCore and Qbox do; it leans on the identifier plus slot.

The practical rule when you add any new script: find where it stores per-player data and confirm it keys on the framework's active character id, not the raw license. If it keys on the license, you have just reintroduced the shared-money bug for every player with more than one character.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'MySQL')` -> The @oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua line is missing from fxmanifest.lua, or oxmysql is not started. Add the server_script line and the dependencies block, and ensure oxmysql loads first.
- `Cannot add or update a child row: a foreign key constraint fails` -> You inserted into qu_characters before qu_accounts. The account row must exist first, which is why the build runs INSERT IGNORE INTO qu_accounts before any character insert.
- `Both characters print the same money` -> The update is keyed on license instead of citizenid, so one shared row backs both characters. Key the money update on citizenid so each character owns its own value.
- `Running identitytest twice creates duplicate characters` -> The (license, first_name) unique key is missing, so the upsert has nothing to collide on. Add UNIQUE KEY uniq_license_name and keep the read-before-insert guard.
- `GetPlayerIdentifierByType returns nil` -> You called it for source 0 (the console) or a player who already dropped. Identifiers only exist for a connected player, which is why the demo uses a fixed license string from the console.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- List FiveM's identifiers and explain why license: is the stable anchor for an account.
- Model identity as two tables: one account row per license, many character rows each with a generated citizenid.
- Use a unique key plus a read-before-insert guard so creating the same character twice is a no-op.
- Key per-character data (money, inventory, jobs) on citizenid, never on the license, and spot the shared-money bug when a query keys on the wrong column.
- Name the multichar resource and active-character object for Qbox, QBCore, and ESX Legacy, and audit a new script for which id it stores against.

## Try it yourself

Add a third character and a per-character inventory so you prove the same rule on a second data type. Extend `server.lua`: after the existing characters, create a third with `ensureCharacter(license, 'Casey')`, then give character A a single item keyed on its `citizenid` and read it back for both A and Casey.

```lua
local charC = ensureCharacter(license, 'Casey')

MySQL.insert.await(
    'INSERT INTO qu_inventory (citizenid, item, count) VALUES (?, ?, ?) ' ..
    'ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE count = VALUES(count)',
    { charA, 'water', 3 }
)

local itemsA = MySQL.scalar.await('SELECT count FROM qu_inventory WHERE citizenid = ? AND item = ?', { charA, 'water' })
local itemsC = MySQL.scalar.await('SELECT count FROM qu_inventory WHERE citizenid = ? AND item = ?', { charC, 'water' })
print(('[qu_identity] A water %d, C water %s'):format(itemsA or 0, tostring(itemsC)))
```

First create the table with a `UNIQUE KEY (citizenid, item)` so the upsert has a key to collide on. Restart `qu_identity`, run `identitytest`, and confirm A shows `3` while C shows `nil`. You have now proven the citizenid rule on inventory as well as money, which is exactly the audit you run on every new script before you trust it.

## References
- FiveM identifiers (GetPlayerIdentifiers): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/GetPlayerIdentifiers/
- Qbox documentation: https://docs.qbox.re/
- ESX Legacy documentation: https://docs.esx-framework.org/
- oxmysql documentation (Overextended): https://overextended.dev/oxmysql
- MariaDB foreign keys reference: https://mariadb.com/kb/en/foreign-keys/

title: B9 · Inventory migration: QB, ESX, ox · Quasar Academy
ai:
Replacing your inventory is one of the most disruptive changes you can make, because half your scripts touch items. Moving to ox_inventory is popular for good reason, but it is a migration, not a drop-in. This lesson gives you the plan, the bridge concept, the data-shape difference that actually loses items, and the test that proves you did not.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll learn: Why inventory is load-bearing, what the compatibility bridge covers, how QB/ESX item data differs from ox_inventory, and how to plan the migration so nobody loses items.
- Time: ~25 minutes.
- You need: A framework server and the ox/oxmysql lessons.

### Before you start
- I have a framework with a working inventory.
- I understand ox_inventory depends on oxmysql and ox_lib.
- I have database backups (the backup SOP).

> **Back up before you migrate**
> Inventory data is player property. Take a full database backup before you touch anything. Run this in your server's terminal (Windows PowerShell, or the Linux shell on a VPS) from the folder you want the file saved in — NOT in the FXServer console: `mysqldump -u root -p your_db > backup_pre_ox.sql` (replace `your_db` with your database name; `-p` makes it prompt for the MySQL/MariaDB password). On newer MariaDB the binary may be `mariadb-dump`. A failed inventory migration without a backup means wiped player items and a community revolt.

> **A note on frameworks in 2026**
> When older guides say QB, they usually mean QBCore. QBCore and ESX (specifically ESX Legacy) are the two established, most widely used frameworks — pick the one your scripts and community already run on. Qbox is a newer, ox-native fork of QBCore that keeps QBCore compatibility; it is worth knowing and it is what this migration lesson targets, but it is not a default you must switch to. ox_inventory, oxmysql, and ox_lib are community resources (originally Overextended, now maintained under the CommunityOx org), not official Cfx.re ones, so you install them from their GitHub releases and read their docs (overextended.dev still resolves; the current docs also live at coxdocs.dev), not the FiveM docs.

## Plan the migration

### Step 1 - Map what touches the inventory
_Goal: You know every script that adds, removes, or checks items._

List the resources that call inventory functions: shops, jobs, crafting, drugs, anything that grants or takes items. These are what will break, and what you must update or bridge.

### Step 2 - Understand the compatibility bridge
_Goal: You know whether old scripts can keep working during the move._

ox_inventory ships compatibility shims that re-expose the common framework inventory exports (the QB `AddItem`/`RemoveItem`/`GetItemByName` calls and the ESX `addInventoryItem`/`removeInventoryItem`/`getInventoryItem` calls) so many scripts work with minimal change. The docs are explicit that these are best-effort: anything that read the framework's raw inventory table or used a niche export still needs real edits. Plan for both.

### Step 3 - Migrate the items definitions and the data
_Goal: Existing items map into the ox_inventory format and player data carries over._

Convert your framework `items` (QB `shared/items.lua` or the ESX `items` table) into ox_inventory's `data/items.lua` format, then migrate the existing player inventory rows. For ESX, ox_inventory ships an official migration command: with the server running, type `convertinventory esx` into the FXServer console (the same window where you see server logs, or the txAdmin Live Console), then `convertinventory esxproperty` if you use property storage, and restart the server when it finishes. For QBCore, ox_inventory ships `convertinventory qb`, which migrates both player and vehicle inventories (and maps `QBShared.Items` into ox_inventory's item data); run it the same way and restart the resource when it finishes. Either way, do it on a copy of the database first, against your backup. The next section shows the exact shape difference so you understand what the converter (or your script) is doing.

### Step 4 - Test on a copy before going live
_Goal: Items survive and scripts still grant/take correctly._

On a test server with the backup restored: load a character, confirm their items are intact, then run each item-touching script (buy, sell, job reward) and confirm it works.

Expected output:

```
Log the test character into the test server and open the inventory (default key F2). The items you expect are present with the right counts and the bag is not empty. ox_inventory does not print a per-character "loaded N items" line, so the proof is the in-game inventory itself, not console text.
```

## How it works

The plan above hides one genuinely hard step inside Step 3. The reason inventory migrations wipe items is that QB, ESX, and ox_inventory do not store items the same way. If you copy rows across blind, the new system reads garbage and shows an empty bag. So before you map anything, you need to see the shapes.

### The data-shape difference

Older ESX setups typically stored each character's items in the `users` row (the `inventory` column, a JSON string) as an object keyed by item name, like `{"bread":2,"water":1}`; exact shape varies by ESX version and inventory resource, so always inspect YOUR own `users.inventory` value before transforming. QBCore stored a JSON array of slot objects on the player row, like `[{"name":"bread","amount":2,"slot":1}]`. ox_inventory standardises on a single JSON column (`inventory`) on the character row, holding an array of slot objects with `name`, `count`, `slot`, and an optional `metadata` table:

```json
[
  { "name": "bread", "count": 2, "slot": 1 },
  { "name": "water", "count": 1, "slot": 2, "metadata": {} }
]
```

The migration is therefore a transform, not a copy. The two fields that trip people up: ESX/QB call the quantity `amount`, ox_inventory calls it `count`; and the ESX object form has no slots at all, so you have to assign `slot` numbers as you convert. Metadata (durability, serials, registration) only exists if the old system tracked it, so most items map with an empty or absent `metadata`.

### What an items definition looks like

ox_inventory will not show an item it has never heard of, so every item name in the data above must exist in `data/items.lua`. A single definition looks like this:

```lua
['bread'] = {
    label = 'Bread',
    weight = 100,
    stack = true,
    close = true,
    description = 'Fills you up a little'
},
```

The field that actually matters for a migration is the key (`['bread']`): it must match the `name` you wrote into the inventory JSON exactly, lowercase and identical. `label` is the display name, `weight` drives the new weight-based limit (ox_inventory uses weight, not slot count, as the real cap), and `stack` decides whether two of the same item share a slot. Any item that exists in player data but is missing from `items.lua` silently disappears on load, which is the most common cause of "some items came back, some did not".

### What the bridge actually covers

The compatibility bridge from Step 2 intercepts the framework's inventory exports and reroutes them into ox_inventory. In practice that means the QB `exports.ox_inventory:` shims for `AddItem`, `RemoveItem`, `GetItemByName`, and `GetItemCount`, and the ESX equivalents `addInventoryItem`, `removeInventoryItem`, and `getInventoryItem`. A script that called those exports keeps working. A script that reached past the export and read `Player.PlayerData.items` directly, or queried the old inventory column in SQL, is not covered by best-effort and has to be edited to ask ox_inventory instead. That is the line between "the bridge handled it" and "I have to touch this script".

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Players lost items after migration` -> The data transform mapped amount->count or slot numbers wrong, or item names did not match items.lua keys. Restore the pre-migration backup in your terminal (NOT the FXServer console) with `mysql -u root -p your_db < backup_pre_ox.sql`, re-run the transform on a copy until items survive, then deploy.
- `Some items return, some vanish` -> The vanished items have no matching key in data/items.lua. ox_inventory drops any item name it has not been told about. Add the missing definitions and reload the character.
- `Shops/jobs no longer give items` -> Those scripts used the old inventory exports. Confirm the compatibility bridge is loaded, or update them to ox_inventory's exports, and test each one.
- `ox_inventory will not start` -> Missing oxmysql or ox_lib, or a syntax error in data/items.lua. Check the Overextended dependencies and the items file. These are community resources, so install them from the Overextended GitHub.
- `Bridge works for some scripts, not others` -> Compatibility shims are best-effort and only cover the standard exports. A script that read the raw framework inventory table needs real edits to ox_inventory's API.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- List every script that touches the inventory before migrating.
- Explain how QB array rows and ESX keyed objects differ from ox_inventory's single inventory JSON column.
- Convert item amount to count and assign slot numbers when transforming player data.
- Define every migrated item in data/items.lua so ox_inventory does not drop it on load.
- Use the compatibility bridge for the standard exports, and edit scripts that read the raw inventory table.
- Test that items survive and item-touching scripts still work on a copy before going live.

Before you migrate anything, write the transform by hand on one fake character so the shape sticks. Take this old ESX-style row and convert it into the ox_inventory array:

```json
{ "bread": 2, "water_bottle": 1, "phone": 1 }
```

The answer is an array of slot objects where `amount` becomes `count`, you assign `slot` 1, 2, 3 in order, and every name must already exist as a key in `data/items.lua`:

```json
[
  { "name": "bread", "count": 2, "slot": 1 },
  { "name": "water_bottle", "count": 1, "slot": 2 },
  { "name": "phone", "count": 1, "slot": 3 }
]
```

If you can do this for one character on paper, you can write the SQL or Lua that does it for ten thousand. Two safety rules when you do: (1) never run the transform against the live database first — run it only against a restored copy until items survive; (2) if you build SQL in Lua via oxmysql, always use `?` placeholders with a params table, never string-concatenate item names or values into the query (that both breaks on apostrophes and is an injection risk). If you cannot do the by-hand transform, you are not ready to run it against real data yet — and for ESX, prefer the built-in `convertinventory esx` command over a hand-written script.

## References
- ox_inventory documentation: https://overextended.dev/ox_inventory
- ox_inventory items definitions: https://overextended.dev/ox_inventory/Guides/items
