- **A connection queue.** While a player is deferred you can hold them and stream `deferrals.update` messages like "you are number 4 in queue" until a slot opens, then call `deferrals.done()`. The held state is what makes a queue possible at all.
- **An adaptive card prompt.** `deferrals.presentCard(card, cb)` renders a richer Adaptive Card on the connecting screen with buttons and inputs, for example a rules screen with an Accept button or a code entry. You build the card as a Lua table or JSON, and the callback fires when the player submits it. You still finish with `deferrals.done()` or `deferrals.done('reason')` based on what they chose.

All three are the same shape: defer, communicate, decide. You built the smallest complete version of it.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Players get stuck on the connecting screen forever` -> A code path reached the end without calling deferrals.done(). Make sure every branch, both allow and reject, calls done() exactly once. An error thrown after defer() also leaves the player hanging, so wrap risky lookups carefully.
- `attempt to call a nil value (field 'defer')` -> The deferrals argument was not received, usually because the handler signature is wrong. The order is function(name, setKickReason, deferrals) and you must use AddEventHandler('playerConnecting', ...), not RegisterNetEvent.
- `Everyone is rejected even after you add a real license` -> The license string in the table does not match exactly. Copy the full value from the console log, keep the license: prefix, and watch for trailing spaces. Table keys are compared character for character.
- `license comes back nil so every player is rejected` -> You read source after a Wait without capturing it first, or asked for an identifier the player does not have. Capture local source = source before any Wait, and gate on 'license', which every Cfx account has.
- `The reject reason does not show on the player's screen` -> You passed the reason to setKickReason or called deferrals.done() with no argument. The reason must be the string argument to deferrals.done('Not whitelisted').

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain that playerConnecting fires on the server before the player loads, and that it is the only place a join can be blocked.
- Use deferrals.defer() to hold a connection open and deferrals.update() to show the held player a message.
- Read a connecting player's license server side with GetPlayerIdentifierByType(source, 'license') and trust the value because the check is server-authoritative.
- Allow a join with deferrals.done() and reject one with deferrals.done('reason'), calling done exactly once on every path.
- Gate entry against a hardcoded allowlist and know how that same shape grows into a queue or an adaptive card prompt.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Players stuck on "Connecting" forever | A deferral was called but never completed | Check your deferral handler calls `deferrals.done()` after validation. Without it, the player hangs forever |
| Deferral works but is too slow (players time out) | The check takes too long (database query, external API) | Set a timeout: `deferrals.defer()`, do the check, then `deferrals.done()`. Keep the check under 5 seconds |
| "Connection rejected" for all players | `deferrals.done("reason")` is being called for everyone | Check your validation logic — it might be rejecting valid connections. Add a print to see what's being checked |
| Queue doesn't move — nobody gets in | `deferrals.update("message")` isn't being called | You need periodic updates so the player sees progress. Call it every 2-3 seconds while they wait |

## Try it yourself

Turn the silent reject into a friendly, informative one, and prove the held state is real. Two changes:

First, before you decide, hold the player for three seconds while counting down a visible message, so you can see `deferrals.update` working over time:

```lua
for i = 3, 1, -1 do
deferrals.update(('Verifying access... %d'):format(i))
Wait(1000)
end
```

Second, give the player a generic support message. Do not expose or encourage posting raw identifiers in public channels; staff can correlate the attempt from the server log and timestamp:

```lua
deferrals.done('Not whitelisted. Contact staff with the time of this attempt.')
```

Restart `qu_connection_gate` and connect with a license that is not in the table. Confirm the countdown ticks from 3 to 1, then the generic rejection appears while the server log retains the identifier for authorized staff. You have used defer to hold, update to communicate, and done with a reason to reject. Bonus: move the allowlist into a separate server-only file so adding a license never means touching the handler logic.

## References
- FiveM deferrals and player connection: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/events/list/playerConnecting/
- FiveM GetPlayerIdentifierByType: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0xA61C8FCAFF3E230E
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: B5 · Persistence without a database (KVS) · Quasar Academy
ai:
Most beginners think the only way to make data survive a restart is a database. It is not. FiveM ships a per-resource **key-value store**, the KVS, that writes small values straight to disk for you. No schema, no connection string, no SQL. One caution up front: the KVS stores values as plain, unencrypted data on the server's disk. Never put secrets in it (no passwords, API keys, Tebex/Discord tokens, or webhook URLs) -- those belong in environment-controlled config, not the KVS. In this lesson you build a counter that climbs every time you run a command, then survives a full server restart untouched. Once it works, you will know exactly when the KVS is the right tool and when it is the wrong one.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A server resource that increments a counter on command and reloads the same number after a full server restart, proving the value lives on disk and not just in memory.
- Time: ~18 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM practice setup you can restart, a text editor, and the heal-command or natives-toolbox resource already working so you are comfortable with RegisterCommand on the server.
- You'll learn: The built-in key-value store, the typed get and set natives, deleting and listing keys, and the exact line where KVS stops being enough and you reach for MariaDB plus oxmysql instead.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console output.
- I can register a server command with RegisterCommand.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a persistent counter from one Lua file as a working FiveM resource named `qu_kvs_counter`. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. The whole point is the second test: you stop the entire server, start it again, and the number is still there. Once the proof holds, the next section takes the code apart line by line.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_kvs_counter
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_kvs_counter/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

The KVS natives are available in Cfx scripting contexts, but this lesson deliberately uses them in a `server_script` so the saved counter is controlled by the server and not by a player's local client store. There is no `lua54 'yes'` line; Lua 5.4 is the only Lua runtime.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The counter reads from disk, increments, and writes back._

Open `server.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local KEY = 'visit_count'

-- Read the saved number on startup. An unset integer key reads as 0.
local count = GetResourceKvpInt(KEY)
print(('[qu_kvs_counter] loaded count from KVS: %d'):format(count))

RegisterCommand('countup', function()
    count = count + 1
    SetResourceKvpInt(KEY, count)
    print(('[qu_kvs_counter] count is now %d (saved to disk)'):format(count))
end, true)

RegisterCommand('countshow', function()
    print(('[qu_kvs_counter] current count: %d'):format(GetResourceKvpInt(KEY)))
end, true)

RegisterCommand('countreset', function()
    DeleteResourceKvp(KEY)
    count = 0
    print('[qu_kvs_counter] count deleted from KVS, back to 0')
end, true)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The number climbs, then survives a full restart._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_kvs_counter
```

    Save, then run these two lines in the txAdmin Live Console, in order. `refresh` makes the server notice the new folder you just created; `ensure` then starts it:

    ```text
refresh
ensure qu_kvs_counter
```

    (If the resource was already running from a previous attempt, `restart qu_kvs_counter` reloads it instead. But a brand-new resource needs `refresh` first, or the console replies that it cannot find the resource.)

    Run the counter up three times, then check it:

    ```text
countup
countup
countup
countshow
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_kvs_counter] loaded count from KVS: 0
[qu_kvs_counter] count is now 1 (saved to disk)
[qu_kvs_counter] count is now 2 (saved to disk)
[qu_kvs_counter] count is now 3 (saved to disk)
[qu_kvs_counter] current count: 3
```

    Now the real proof: a cold boot of the whole server, the thing a database is supposed to survive. This is NOT `restart qu_kvs_counter`. Do a real full restart of the FXServer process: in the txAdmin web panel, open the dashboard and click **Restart** (the server-control button at the top), or in a raw console window press **Ctrl+C** to stop FXServer and then launch it again. When the resource loads, watch the startup line:

Expected output:

```
[qu_kvs_counter] loaded count from KVS: 3
```

    The number came back as `3` with no database anywhere. That is the KVS doing its job.

## How it works

You ran it, the count climbed, and it survived a cold restart. Now take the code apart so you understand where the data actually lives and why no `console.log` style guesswork was needed to confirm it. There are three natives doing the real work and one habit that keeps you out of trouble.

### Reading the saved value on startup

```lua
local count = GetResourceKvpInt(KEY)
print(('[qu_kvs_counter] loaded count from KVS: %d'):format(count))
```

`GetResourceKvpInt(key)` is a SERVER native that reads an integer you previously stored under that key. The key is just a string you choose, here `'visit_count'`. The store is **scoped to this one resource**, so `qu_kvs_counter` cannot see, read, or stomp on a key written by `qu_persistence` or any other resource. Each resource gets its own private drawer.

The most important detail is what happens the very first time, before you have ever written the key. `GetResourceKvpInt` returns `0` for a key that has never been set, so your `count` starts at a clean zero with no extra guard. If you used `GetResourceKvpString` instead, an unset key comes back as `nil`, which is why string reads almost always need an `or` fallback. The integer and float variants are friendlier here because they hand you a usable number on a fresh server.

This line runs once, at the top level of the file, the moment the resource starts. That timing is the whole reason persistence works: on every boot, the first thing the resource does is rehydrate its state from disk before any command can touch it.

### Incrementing and writing back

```lua
RegisterCommand('countup', function()
    count = count + 1
    SetResourceKvpInt(KEY, count)
    print(('[qu_kvs_counter] count is now %d (saved to disk)'):format(count))
end, true)
```

`SetResourceKvpInt(key, value)` writes an integer to the store under that key. There is no application-level save button or transaction to commit. Treat the call as a persistence write, not as free in-memory work: do not run it every frame or use KVS for high-frequency economy state.

Notice the two-step rhythm: bump the in-memory `count`, then push it to the KVS. The local variable is your fast working copy for this session; the server-side KVS is the durable copy. The restricted flag is `true`, so the command requires the `command.countup` ACE; the server console can run it. A remote client cannot call this server-side KVS code directly, but any network event you expose around it would still need normal server validation.

FiveM gives you three typed pairs, and you pick by the shape of your value:

- `SetResourceKvpInt` / `GetResourceKvpInt` for whole numbers like this counter.
- `SetResourceKvpFloat` / `GetResourceKvpFloat` for decimals like `1000.50`.
- `SetResourceKvp` / `GetResourceKvpString` for text, and for anything structured you serialize with `json.encode` into a string first.

Match the setter to the getter. Write with `SetResourceKvpInt` and read with `GetResourceKvpInt`. Mixing types, for example writing an int and reading it as a string, gives you garbage or `nil`.

### Deleting a key

```lua
RegisterCommand('countreset', function()
    DeleteResourceKvp(KEY)
    count = 0
    print('[qu_kvs_counter] count deleted from KVS, back to 0')
end, true)
```

`DeleteResourceKvp(key)` removes the key from the store entirely. After this, `GetResourceKvpInt(KEY)` reads `0` again because the key no longer exists. Setting the value to `0` and deleting the key are not the same thing: a deleted key is gone, while a key set to `0` still occupies a slot you can find when you list. We reset the local `count` to `0` in the same handler so the working copy matches the now-empty store.

### Check yourself: After countreset, you restart the whole server. What does the startup line print, and why?

It prints `loaded count from KVS: 0`. `DeleteResourceKvp` removed the `visit_count` key, so on the next boot `GetResourceKvpInt(KEY)` finds nothing under that key and returns its default of `0`. The number did not come back as `3` because there is no longer anything saved to come back. That is the difference between deleting a key and merely zeroing it: deleting wipes the durable copy, so the restart starts from the same blank slate a brand-new resource would.

### Listing every key your resource owns

The three commands above each work on one known key. Sometimes you do not know the keys ahead of time, for example you stored one entry per player under keys like `player_money_42` and `player_money_57` and now you want to walk all of them. That is what the find iterator is for:

```lua
RegisterCommand('countkeys', function()
    local handle = StartFindKvp('')
    local found = 0
    while true do
        local key = FindKvp(handle)
        if not key then break end
        found = found + 1
        print(('[qu_kvs_counter] key %d: %s'):format(found, key))
    end
    EndFindKvp(handle)
    print(('[qu_kvs_counter] total keys: %d'):format(found))
end, true)
```

`StartFindKvp(prefix)` opens a search over every key in this resource's store whose name begins with the prefix string. An empty `''` prefix matches every key. It hands back a **handle**: a number that is a ticket pointing at this specific search. You pass that same number to the next two calls so they know which search you mean. `FindKvp(handle)` returns the next matching key name each time you call it, and returns `nil` once there are no more, which is why the `if not key then break end` line ends the loop. `EndFindKvp(handle)` closes the search and frees it. Always pair a `StartFindKvp` with an `EndFindKvp`, the same way you pair `function` with `end`, or you leak the handle.

The find natives return key **names**, not their values. To read each one you take the returned key and call the matching getter, for example `GetResourceKvpInt(key)`. The iterator finds the drawers; you still open each drawer yourself.

### When you reach for the KVS, and when you do not

The KVS is the right tool when all of these are true:

- The data is **small** and **per-resource**: settings, a counter, a feature toggle, the last value a script saw. No other resource needs to read it.
- There is **no schema and no relationships**. You are storing loose keys, not rows that join to other rows.
- You will **never query it**. You can fetch a key by name or list keys by prefix, and that is the entire query language. There is no "find every player with more than 5000 dollars".

You reach for **MariaDB plus oxmysql** the moment any of those breaks. oxmysql is the Overextended community resource that connects your server to a MariaDB database, and MariaDB is the database engine to prefer in 2026 over plain MySQL. You need it when the data is **relational** (players own vehicles, vehicles belong to garages), when you must **query and filter** (leaderboards, "all characters for this license"), or when the data is **shared across many resources** that each read and write the same tables. A whole-server economy with 64 players hammering wallets is squarely database territory, not KVS territory.

This is the line that separates this lesson from **Persistence: cache plus write-back**. KVS is for small, low-frequency, resource-private state. It still performs persistent storage work, so cache or debounce frequent changes. MariaDB plus oxmysql is for relational, shared, queryable data and supports transactions and indexing. Pick by the data model and durability requirements, not by which API is shorter.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `Writing with SetResourceKvpInt but reading back nil or a wrong number` -> You read with the wrong typed getter. An int written with SetResourceKvpInt must be read with GetResourceKvpInt, a float with GetResourceKvpFloat, a string with GetResourceKvpString. Match the getter to the setter exactly.
- `attempt to perform arithmetic on a nil value (local 'count')` -> You used GetResourceKvpString to read a numeric key. String reads return nil for an unset key, and nil cannot be added to. Use GetResourceKvpInt for whole numbers, which returns 0 for an unset key, or add an 'or 0' fallback.
- `The counter resets to 0 every time you restart the whole server` -> You are reading the key into count but never writing it with a Set call, or you are writing to a different key string than you read from. Confirm the same KEY value is used in both the GetResourceKvpInt and the SetResourceKvpInt calls.
- `countkeys prints nothing or hangs` -> Either the store is genuinely empty (run countup first), or you forgot the 'if not key then break end' guard so the while loop never ends. FindKvp returns nil when there are no more keys, and that nil is your only signal to stop.
- `Other resources cannot read your visit_count key` -> That is by design, not a bug. The KVS is scoped per resource. If two resources must share the same data, the KVS is the wrong tool and you want a database via oxmysql instead.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Store data that survives a full server restart with zero database setup, using the per-resource key-value store.
- Read and write whole numbers with GetResourceKvpInt and SetResourceKvpInt, and pick the float or string pair when the value is a decimal or text.
- Remove a key with DeleteResourceKvp, and explain why a deleted key differs from a key set to 0.
- Walk every key your resource owns with the StartFindKvp, FindKvp, EndFindKvp iterator, always closing the handle.
- State the line where KVS stops being enough and you move to MariaDB plus oxmysql: relational data, real queries, or data shared across resources.

## Try it yourself

Turn the single counter into a per-player visit tracker that survives restarts, then list the leaderboard. The key for each player is built from their identifier, and the find iterator reads them all back. Build a server command that increments the caller's own count and one that prints every stored player count:

```lua
-- Server side. Use a stable identifier for the durable key.
RegisterCommand('myvisits', function(source)
    local license = GetPlayerIdentifierByType(source, 'license')
    if not license then
        print('[qu_kvs_counter] no license identifier for source ' .. source)
        return
    end
    local key = ('visits_%s'):format(license)
    local n = GetResourceKvpInt(key) + 1
    SetResourceKvpInt(key, n)
    print(('[qu_kvs_counter] player %s now has %d visits'):format(source, n))
end, false)

RegisterCommand('allvisits', function()
    local handle = StartFindKvp('visits_')
    while true do
        local key = FindKvp(handle)
        if not key then break end
        print(('[qu_kvs_counter] %s = %d'):format(key, GetResourceKvpInt(key)))
    end
    EndFindKvp(handle)
end, true)
```

The command receives a temporary server source, then converts it to a stable `license:` identifier before building the persistent key. Never use `source` itself as a durable key: sources are connection slots and are reused. The `'visits_'` prefix makes `allvisits` ignore the main `visit_count` key. Because this dump prints player identifiers, keep `allvisits` ACE-restricted and never expose its output to clients. Once you need sorting, filtering, cross-resource access, or many players, move this data to MariaDB.

## References
- FiveM native reference (search SetResourceKvpInt): https://docs.fivem.net/natives/
- FiveM native reference (search GetResourceKvpInt): https://docs.fivem.net/natives/
- FiveM native reference (search StartFindKvp): https://docs.fivem.net/natives/
- FiveM player identifiers: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/functions/GetPlayerIdentifiers/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/
- oxmysql documentation (CommunityOx): https://coxdocs.dev/oxmysql

title: B4 · Built-in resources: chat, spawnmanager, baseevents · Quasar Academy
ai:
Every FiveM server you start, even an empty one, already runs a handful of stock resources from Cfx.re. They handle chat, where players spawn, and the basic life events of a session. You do not install them and you do not write them. You call into them. This lesson shows you the three you reach for most, then proves it by wiring a single resource that talks to all three: a chat suggestion, a styled system message, and a reaction to a real game event.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A resource that registers a chat command hint, posts a styled system message into the chat box, and reacts the moment a player gets into a vehicle.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A FiveM practice server you can join and restart, plus the qu_first_line_of_lua and qu_natives_toolbox mental models from the earlier modules.
- You'll learn: What chat, spawnmanager, baseevents, and sessionmanager already give you for free, and how to call into them instead of rebuilding them

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- I can write a fxmanifest.lua with a client_script and a server_script.

> **These ship with the server, so check before you build**
> Open your server's default `server.cfg` and you will already see lines like `ensure chat`, `ensure spawnmanager`, and `ensure sessionmanager`. Those are stock Cfx.re resources that boot before yours. You never write them; you trigger their events, listen for the ones they fire, and call their exports. This lesson builds one resource named `qu_builtins_tour` that leans on three of them. Follow the names and code exactly, then the next section takes every line apart.

## What you already have running

Before the build, here is the map of what is sitting in your server doing work you never wrote. Each one is a real resource folder inside the FiveM artifacts, and each one exposes events or exports you call by name.

The point of this lesson is a habit, not a single script. When you need a chat message, a spawn point, or a notification that a player got in a car, your first move is to ask whether a stock resource already does it. It almost always does.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_builtins_tour
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

This resource talks to chat from the client, but it reacts to the `baseevents:enteredVehicle` event, which the stock `baseevents` resource fires on the *server*. So the chat suggestion and message live in a `client_script`, and the vehicle handler lives in a `server_script`. Create this exact layout:

```text
resources/qu_builtins_tour/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load and which resources must load first._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

dependencies { 'chat', 'baseevents' }

client_script 'client.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

The `dependencies` block names the stock resources you call into. It tells the server to load `chat` and `baseevents` before `qu_builtins_tour`, so their events exist by the time your code runs. The `client_script` holds the chat suggestion and message; the `server_script` holds the vehicle handler, because `baseevents:enteredVehicle` is a server event. Note there is no `lua54 'yes'` line: that directive was deprecated in June 2025 and is ignored, since Lua 5.4 is now the only runtime.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `client.lua` and paste this. These two calls are genuinely client-side: the chat box lives on each player's own game instance.

```lua
-- 1. Tell the chat box about a command we want to hint at.
TriggerEvent('chat:addSuggestion', '/wave', 'Wave at nearby players', {
    { name = 'style', help = 'Optional: short or long' }
})

-- 2. Post one styled system message into the chat box.
TriggerEvent('chat:addMessage', {
    color = { 0, 200, 120 },
    multiline = true,
    args = { 'Quasar', 'Builtins tour loaded. Get in a vehicle to test baseevents.' }
})
```

Now open `server.lua` and paste this. `baseevents:enteredVehicle` is a *server* event: the stock `baseevents` resource detects the vehicle on the client and fires it across to the server with `TriggerServerEvent`, so the handler must live here and read which player it was about from `source`.

```lua
-- 3. React to the baseevents:enteredVehicle SERVER event.
-- baseevents fires this with TriggerServerEvent, so we register a net event.
RegisterNetEvent('baseevents:enteredVehicle')
AddEventHandler('baseevents:enteredVehicle', function(currentVehicle, currentSeat, vehicleDisplayName, vehicleNetId)
    local src = source

    -- Send the confirmation line back to just that player's chat box.
    TriggerClientEvent('chat:addMessage', src, {
        color = { 0, 200, 120 },
        multiline = true,
        args = { 'Quasar', ('You entered a %s (seat %d).'):format(vehicleDisplayName, currentSeat) }
    })

    print(('[qu_builtins_tour] player %d enteredVehicle: %s seat %d netId %s'):format(src, vehicleDisplayName, currentSeat, tostring(vehicleNetId)))
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the chat box and the server console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line below the stock resources:

    ```text
ensure qu_builtins_tour
```

    Save, then run:

    ```text
restart qu_builtins_tour
```

    Join the server so your ped exists. As you load in, watch the chat box for the system message. Then type `/` and look for the `/wave` hint. Finally, get into any vehicle and watch chat plus the server console (txAdmin Live Console):

    ```text
open the server console, get into a car
```

Expected output:

```
[Quasar] Builtins tour loaded. Get in a vehicle to test baseevents.
[Quasar] You entered a Sultan (seat -1).
[qu_builtins_tour] player 1 enteredVehicle: Sultan seat -1 netId 65538
```

    The vehicle name and netId depend on the car you enter, and seat `-1` is the driver's seat. Both `[Quasar]` lines appear in your in-game chat box: the first from the client message, the second from the server sending `chat:addMessage` back to your player with `TriggerClientEvent`. The `[qu_builtins_tour]` line appears in the server console (not F8) because the `print` now runs server-side, and `player 1` is your server id from `source`.

## How it works

You ran it, the message appeared, the hint showed up, and getting in a car triggered a reaction. None of that chat or vehicle plumbing is yours. You sent events to resources that were already running, and listened for one they fire. Now take each call apart so you know exactly which resource you are talking to, on which side, and why.

### The chat suggestion: a hint, not a command

```lua
TriggerEvent('chat:addSuggestion', '/wave', 'Wave at nearby players', {
    { name = 'style', help = 'Optional: short or long' }
})
```

`TriggerEvent` fires a named event on the same side it is called from, here the client. The event name `chat:addSuggestion` belongs to the stock `chat` resource. The colon is just a naming convention: the part before it tells you which resource owns the event. You are not defining this event, you are sending it to chat, which is listening for it.

The arguments are read left to right. `'/wave'` is the command text that shows up in the autocomplete list when a player types `/`. `'Wave at nearby players'` is the help text shown next to it. The fourth argument is a table of parameter hints, where each entry has a `name` and a `help` string, so the player sees what to type after the command.

One thing this does not do: it does not create the `/wave` command. A suggestion is only the hint in the autocomplete dropdown. If you want `/wave` to actually do something you still register it yourself with `RegisterCommand`, exactly as you learned in the first-line and natives lessons. Pairing a real `RegisterCommand` with a matching `chat:addSuggestion` is the polished pattern: the command works, and players can discover it.

### The system message: color, args, and the name tag

```lua
TriggerEvent('chat:addMessage', {
    color = { 0, 200, 120 },
    multiline = true,
    args = { 'Quasar', 'Builtins tour loaded. Get in a vehicle to test baseevents.' }
})
```

`chat:addMessage` is how every server announcement, notification, and bot line you have ever seen in a FiveM chat box gets there. You hand it one table with three fields.

`color` is an RGB triple as a Lua table: red, green, blue, each from 0 to 255. Here `{ 0, 200, 120 }` is a calm green that tints the name tag. `multiline = true` lets a long message wrap onto more than one line instead of being clipped. `args` is the message itself, split into parts. By convention the first entry is the sender name shown in brackets, and the second is the body text. So `{ 'Quasar', 'Builtins tour loaded...' }` renders as a `[Quasar]` tag followed by the message. You can think of the name tag as a label that says who is talking, which is why server messages usually read `[System]` or the server name.

### The baseevent handler: reacting to the game

```lua
RegisterNetEvent('baseevents:enteredVehicle')
AddEventHandler('baseevents:enteredVehicle', function(currentVehicle, currentSeat, vehicleDisplayName, vehicleNetId)
    local src = source
    -- ...
end)
```

`AddEventHandler` registers a listener for an event that some other code fires. This is different from `TriggerEvent`, which sends an event. Sending versus listening is the whole shape of event-driven code: one resource triggers, another handles. Here the `baseevents` resource is doing the triggering. Its client-side code watches the game and, the moment a player's local ped sits in a vehicle, it fires `baseevents:enteredVehicle` across to the *server* with `TriggerServerEvent`. That is why this handler lives in `server.lua` and pairs `RegisterNetEvent` with `AddEventHandler`: `RegisterNetEvent` allows the event to be received over the network, and `AddEventHandler` runs your code when it arrives.

Read the parameters in order, the four values baseevents sends: `currentVehicle` is the entity handle of the car. `currentSeat` is which seat the player took, where `-1` is the driver and `0` is the front passenger. `vehicleDisplayName` is the readable model name like `Sultan`. `vehicleNetId` is the network id, the identifier other clients and the server use to refer to that same vehicle across the network. On top of those, `source` is set on every net event the server receives: it is the server id of the player the event came from. We copy it into `local src = source` right away, because `source` can change if any other event fires before we use it.

Inside the handler we do two things: send a chat message back to that one player and `print` a line to the server console. Because the handler runs on the server, it cannot just `TriggerEvent('chat:addMessage', ...)` and expect it in someone's chat box. Instead it uses `TriggerClientEvent('chat:addMessage', src, ...)`, which sends the event to the chat resource running on that specific player's client. The chat line is the user-facing proof; the `print` is your developer probe, and it lands in the server console rather than F8. The `('...'):format(...)` method fills the placeholders, `%s` for the model name string and `%d` for the integer seat, exactly the formatting you saw in the natives lesson.

Why use the stock baseevent instead of writing your own vehicle detection? Without it you would have to spin up a thread on every client that runs every frame, calls `IsPedInAnyVehicle`, tracks whether the answer changed since last frame, and reports it to the server yourself. That is a real loop you would have to write, tune, and keep from eating CPU. `baseevents` already does that work and simply tells the server the instant it happens. Calling its event is cheaper, cleaner, and the same data every other resource on the server is reading.

### Check yourself: Why does the vehicle reaction use AddEventHandler, while the chat message uses TriggerEvent?

Because the two calls are on opposite ends of the same event. `TriggerEvent` sends an event out, so you use it to push a message into the `chat` resource that is listening for `chat:addMessage`. `AddEventHandler` listens for an event, so you use it to catch `baseevents:enteredVehicle`, which the stock `baseevents` resource fires when a player gets in a car. The catch: `baseevents:enteredVehicle` is a *server* event, fired with `TriggerServerEvent`, so the listener lives in `server.lua` and you pair `AddEventHandler` with `RegisterNetEvent` so the event can arrive over the network. You are the sender for chat and the listener for baseevents. If you swapped the sender and listener, or put the vehicle handler in a `client_script`, it would never run, because triggering an event you mean to listen for, listening for one you mean to send, or listening on the wrong side, does nothing useful.

### Sides matter: which events live where

This is where most beginners get burned, so read the stock code, not your assumptions. `chat:addMessage` and `chat:addSuggestion` are triggered from the client, because the chat box exists on each player's own game instance, which is why those two calls are in `client.lua`.

But `baseevents:enteredVehicle` and `baseevents:leftVehicle` are *server* events. The stock `baseevents` resource does detect the vehicle on the client, but it does not hand you a client event: its client code fires `TriggerServerEvent('baseevents:enteredVehicle', ...)`, and `server.lua` registers it with `RegisterServerEvent`. So a `client_script` that does `AddEventHandler('baseevents:enteredVehicle', ...)` never fires, no matter what. You listen on the server with `RegisterNetEvent` plus `AddEventHandler`, and `source` tells you which player got in the car.

The death events behave differently again. `baseevents:onPlayerDied` and `baseevents:onPlayerKilled` fire on *both* sides: the stock code triggers each one with a client-local `TriggerEvent` and a `TriggerServerEvent` at the same time. So a `client_script` can listen for them with `AddEventHandler` and read the death data directly (there is no `source` on the client), while a `server_script` listens with `RegisterNetEvent` and reads the player who died from `source`. Pick the side that has the data you need. `sessionmanager` rounds this out: it fires `playerActivated` on the server once a player has fully loaded in, which is the correct moment to set up that player's data, far more reliable than reacting to them merely connecting.

The rule to carry forward: read which side a stock event actually fires on before you write the handler. The client can request things, but only the server grants them, and listening for an event on the wrong side is the most common reason a beginner's handler "never fires."

### When you reach for these

You will touch these stock resources in nearly every script you write:

- **chat:addMessage** for any server notification, command feedback, or bot line. Before you reach for a third-party notification library, remember the chat box is already there and free.
- **chat:addSuggestion** every time you add a `RegisterCommand`, so players can discover it by typing `/`. A command without a suggestion is a command nobody finds.
- **baseevents** when a feature should react to a player getting in a car, leaving it, dying, or being killed. Seatbelt scripts, insurance, and kill feeds all start here.
- **spawnmanager** when you control where players appear. `exports.spawnmanager:addSpawnPoint` registers a location, `setAutoSpawnCallback` lets you decide the spawn point in code, and `forceRespawn` sends the player there on demand.
- **sessionmanager** when setup must wait until a player is genuinely ready. `playerActivated` is that moment.

## Common mistakes

## Common errors and fixes
- `The /wave hint never appears in the autocomplete list` -> chat:addSuggestion only registers the hint, not the command, and it is a client event. Make sure the TriggerEvent call is in a client_script and that 'chat' is listed in your dependencies so it loads first.
- `attempt to index a nil value, or the message never shows` -> The table passed to chat:addMessage is malformed. color must be a three-number table like { 0, 200, 120 }, and args must be a table, usually { 'Name', 'Body text' }. A missing comma or brace breaks the whole table.
- `The enteredVehicle handler never runs` -> baseevents:enteredVehicle (and leftVehicle) are server events: the stock baseevents resource fires them with TriggerServerEvent. A client_script AddEventHandler for them never fires. Put the handler in a server_script, register it with RegisterNetEvent plus AddEventHandler, and read the player from source.
- `onPlayerDied or onPlayerKilled never fires in your client file` -> These actually fire on both the client and the server, since the stock code triggers each with TriggerEvent and TriggerServerEvent. A client handler is fine and gets the death data with no source. If yours is silent, check the event name spelling and that baseevents is in your dependencies, not the side.
- `The colors look wrong or the message is one washed-out shade` -> RGB values run 0 to 255, not 0 to 1. { 0, 200, 120 } is correct; { 0, 0.8, 0.5 } collapses to near black because the numbers are read as raw 0 to 255 values.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Name the four stock resources, chat, spawnmanager, baseevents, and sessionmanager, and say what each one already does for you.
- Post a styled message to the chat box with chat:addMessage, controlling the name tag, color, and body through the args and color fields.
- Register a slash-command hint with chat:addSuggestion, and explain why it is only a hint and still needs a real RegisterCommand.
- Listen for the stock baseevents:enteredVehicle server event with RegisterNetEvent plus AddEventHandler, read the values it hands your handler, and send a line back to that player with TriggerClientEvent, instead of writing your own per-frame detection loop.
- Tell which side each stock event fires on: enteredVehicle and leftVehicle are server events, onPlayerDied and onPlayerKilled fire on both sides, and playerActivated is server-side, so you read source only when handling on the server.

## Try it yourself

Make the resource a real driving companion. First, register an actual `/wave` command so the suggestion you added is not a dead hint. Then add a second handler for `baseevents:leftVehicle` so the chat box reacts when the player gets out, not just in. Remember `leftVehicle` is a server event, the same as `enteredVehicle`, so its handler goes in `server.lua` with the same pattern.

Add the command to `client.lua`, since the command runs on the player typing it and the chat box is client-side:

```lua
-- A real command to back the suggestion you already added.
RegisterCommand('wave', function(source, args)
    local style = args[1] or 'short'
    TriggerEvent('chat:addMessage', {
        color = { 0, 200, 120 },
        multiline = true,
        args = { 'Quasar', ('You wave (%s) at the locals.'):format(style) }
    })
end, false)
```

Add the leftVehicle handler to `server.lua`, next to the enteredVehicle one, and send the line back to that player with `TriggerClientEvent`:

```lua
-- React when the player leaves a vehicle (also a SERVER event).
RegisterNetEvent('baseevents:leftVehicle')
AddEventHandler('baseevents:leftVehicle', function(currentVehicle, currentSeat, vehicleDisplayName, vehicleNetId)
    local src = source
    TriggerClientEvent('chat:addMessage', src, {
        color = { 200, 120, 0 },
        multiline = true,
        args = { 'Quasar', ('You stepped out of the %s.'):format(vehicleDisplayName) }
    })
end)
```

Restart `qu_builtins_tour`, type `/wave long` and confirm the styled line appears, then get into a car and back out and watch the green entered line followed by the orange left line. For a stretch, add a `chat:addSuggestion` for `/wave` so a player typing `/` sees the style argument hint, and notice how the command, the hint, and the events all route through chat without you writing a single line of UI.

## References
- FiveM chat resource and message API: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/resources/chat/
- FiveM spawnmanager resource: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/resources/spawnmanager/
- FiveM baseevents (player and vehicle events): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/events/list/
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: C1 · Reading player input: controls and keys · Quasar Academy
ai:
Almost every interaction in FiveM starts with a key. Open a door, talk to an NPC, loot a body, sprint, holster a weapon. FiveM does not hand you raw keyboard codes for these. It hands you **control IDs**, stable numbers that survive the player remapping their keys. This lesson teaches you to read those controls in a client thread, then proves it with a resource that detects the E key near a coordinate and takes over a second key while a menu is open.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A client resource that watches for the E key near a fixed coordinate and prints when you press it, plus a second key it takes over from the game while a fake menu is open.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: Your client-script mental model from the natives toolbox lesson, a local server you can restart, and the FiveM controls reference open in your browser.
- You'll learn: What a control ID is -> the input group argument -> IsControlJustPressed vs IsControlPressed vs IsControlReleased -> disabling a game key while a menu is open -> RegisterKeyMapping as the rebindable alternative.

### Before you start
- I can find my server resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource.
- I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output.
- I know why a client_script runs on my own game instance, not the server.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a per-frame input thread as a working FiveM resource named `qu_controls_and_input`. It detects the E key near a fixed spot, then disables and re-reads a second key while a fake menu flag is on. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it runs, the next section takes every line apart so you can read any control without copying.

## Control IDs, not keycodes

When you press E, your operating system sees a raw keyboard scancode. FiveM does not. FiveM works one layer above that, in terms of **controls**: named game actions like "context interact", "jump", or "vehicle handbrake". Each control has a stable number, the **control ID**. You ask the game "is control 38 pressed right now" and the game answers, no matter which physical key the player has bound to that action.

This matters because players rebind keys. Someone on an AZERTY keyboard, someone using a controller, someone who moved interact off E on purpose, all of them still trigger control 38 when they hit their bound key. If you read raw keycodes you break for every one of those players. If you read control IDs you work for all of them. This is why every input native in this lesson takes a control ID, never a letter.

Here are the control IDs you will reach for most often, straight from the FiveM controls reference:

Do not memorize this table. The short descriptions above are informal notes on how scripts commonly use each control, not the official names. On the controls reference each ID has an official INPUT_ name (for example 38 is INPUT_PICKUP, 47 is INPUT_DETONATE, 246 is INPUT_MP_TEXT_CHAT_ALL); the default key is what matters here, and many scripts repurpose a control regardless of its original name. Bookmark the controls reference and look the number up when you need it, the same way you look up any other native.

### The input group argument

Every input native starts with a number you will see set to `0` almost everywhere:

```lua
IsControlJustPressed(0, 38)
```

That leading `0` is the **input group** (also called the padIndex). It tells the game which input layer to read. Group `0` is the normal "in the world, controlling your player" layer, and it is what you want roughly all of the time. The other groups exist for special contexts like camera or frontend menus, and you will almost never touch them. Treat `0` as the default and move on. The control ID, the second argument, is the part that actually changes.

### Check yourself: A player remaps interact from E to T. You wrote IsControlJustPressed(0, 38). Does your code still fire for them?

Yes. `38` is the control ID for the interact action, not the letter E. When the player rebinds interact to T, the game maps T to control 38 for that player. Your check reads the control, so it fires on T for them and on E for everyone who left the default. That is the entire reason FiveM gives you control IDs instead of raw keycodes: you write against the action, the player owns the key.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_controls_and_input
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout:

```text
resources/qu_controls_and_input/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM knows which files to load._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

client_script 'client.lua'
```

Reading input means reading your own keyboard on your own game instance, so all the code is a single `client_script`. There is no `server_script` here on purpose: the server never sees a key press, only the results you choose to send it. Keep this in mind for later: because input is read on the player's own machine, a key press is client-controlled and can be faked. When you eventually send a key result to the server with an event, the server must re-validate it (is the player actually near the door, do they own the item) and never trust the client's word alone. Note there is no `lua54 'yes'` line. As of June 2025 the Cfx.re docs mark that setting as deprecated: Lua 5.3 was removed and all Lua scripts now run on Lua 5.4 by default, so you no longer need the line and leave it out.

### Step 4 - Write the lesson code
_Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code._

Open `client.lua` and paste this:

```lua
local targetCoords = vector3(215.0, -810.0, 30.0)
local menuOpen = false

-- Toggle a fake "menu" on and off so we can demonstrate taking over a key.
RegisterCommand('togglemenu', function()
    menuOpen = not menuOpen
    print('[qu_controls_and_input] menuOpen is now ' .. tostring(menuOpen))
end, false)

CreateThread(function()
    while true do
        local sleep = 500
        local ped = PlayerPedId()
        local dist = #(GetEntityCoords(ped) - targetCoords)

        if dist < 3.0 then
            sleep = 0  -- close enough to interact, read input every frame

            -- E (control 38) interact, one fire per press.
            if IsControlJustPressed(0, 38) then
                print('[qu_controls_and_input] E pressed near the target')
            end
        end

        if menuOpen then
            sleep = 0  -- a menu is open, read input every frame

            -- Stop the GAME from using F (control 23) this frame.
            DisableControlAction(0, 23, true)

            -- Now read our own meaning for that same disabled key.
            if IsDisabledControlJustPressed(0, 23) then
                print('[qu_controls_and_input] F captured by the menu, not the game')
            end
        end

        Wait(sleep)
    end
end)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in the F8 console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```text
ensure qu_controls_and_input
```

    Save, then in your server console (the txAdmin Live Console, or the FXServer.exe window if you run it directly) type:

    ```text
restart qu_controls_and_input
```

    Now open FiveM, connect to your server (`connect 127.0.0.1:30120` if it is running on this same machine, where 30120 is the default game port) so your ped exists, then walk to roughly `215.0, -810.0` near Legion Square. Press F8 to open the in-game client console, then run these tests (type `/togglemenu` in the in-game chat or the F8 console, and watch the printed lines appear in that same F8 console):

    ```text
Stand on the spot and tap E.
Then run /togglemenu and tap F.
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_controls_and_input] E pressed near the target
[qu_controls_and_input] menuOpen is now true
[qu_controls_and_input] F captured by the menu, not the game
```

    While `menuOpen` is true, tapping F should print the captured line and should NOT make your ped enter a nearby vehicle, because you disabled the game's use of that control for the frame. Run `/togglemenu` again to turn it off and confirm F goes back to its normal vehicle behavior.

## How it works

You ran it and the lines appeared. Now take the code apart so the next time you read a key you are deciding, not copying. There are three ideas here: how often you read input, which read function to use, and how to take a key away from the game.

### The thread and the distance gate

```lua
CreateThread(function()
    while true do
        local sleep = 500
        local ped = PlayerPedId()
        local dist = #(GetEntityCoords(ped) - targetCoords)

        if dist < 3.0 then
            sleep = 0
            -- ...read input...
        end

        Wait(sleep)
    end
end)
```

Input is not an event you subscribe to. You **poll** it: every frame you ask the game "is this control down right now". That is why the code lives inside a `while true` loop in a thread. `CreateThread` is the FiveM native that starts a background loop that runs alongside the game; `Wait(sleep)` at the bottom yields control back so the game keeps running, then resumes the loop after `sleep` milliseconds.

The `sleep` variable is the most important performance idea in any input thread. `Wait(0)` means "run again next frame", which on a 60 FPS client is sixty times a second. Doing that forever, for every player, just to check a key they are nowhere near, is wasted work that adds up across a full server. So you gate it: the default `sleep = 500` makes the loop nearly idle, waking only twice a second to check distance. Only when `dist < 3.0`, when the player is actually standing near the interaction spot, do you set `sleep = 0` and read input every frame. `#(a - b)` is the vector length operator: it gives you the straight line distance between two vector3 points in one expression. This distance gate is the difference between a script that scales and one that quietly eats frames.

### IsControlJustPressed vs IsControlPressed vs IsControlReleased

This is the choice beginners get wrong, and it changes how your feature feels. All three take the input group and a control ID and return a boolean, but they answer different questions:

- **`IsControlJustPressed(0, 38)`** is true on the single frame the key goes down, then false until it is released and pressed again. This is "one fire per press". Use it for discrete actions: open a door, confirm a menu, toggle something. You used it for E because one tap should mean one interaction.
- **`IsControlPressed(0, 38)`** is true on every frame the key is held down. If you put it in a per-frame loop it fires sixty times a second while the key is held. Use it for continuous actions: holding sprint, holding to charge a throw, a hold-to-loot progress bar that fills while the key stays down.
- **`IsControlReleased(0, 38)`** is true on every frame the key is NOT held — it is the inverse of `IsControlPressed`, not an edge. For the single frame the key comes back up, use **`IsControlJustReleased(0, 38)`**. Use JustReleased to pair with a hold: start charging on pressed, finish and fire on released.

The trap is reaching for `IsControlPressed` when you meant `JustPressed`. If you open a door with `IsControlPressed`, a normal half-second hold of E triggers the open thirty times in a row, and your door slams open and shut. The mental rule: a one-shot action wants `JustPressed`; a held-down action wants `Pressed`; the end of a hold wants `Released`.

### Check yourself: You want a hold-to-search action: the search fills a progress bar while E is held and cancels if the player lets go early. Which read functions do you use?

Two of them, working together. You start the search on `IsControlJustPressed(0, 38)`, the single frame E goes down, so it begins exactly once. While the bar fills you check `IsControlPressed(0, 38)` every frame; the moment it returns false the player has let go, so you cancel. You could also watch `IsControlJustReleased(0, 38)` for that same release edge. What you would never use here is `IsControlJustPressed` for the fill loop, because it is only true for one frame and would never keep the bar going.

### Taking over a key: DisableControlAction and IsDisabledControlJustPressed

```lua
if menuOpen then
    sleep = 0
    DisableControlAction(0, 23, true)
    if IsDisabledControlJustPressed(0, 23) then
        print('[qu_controls_and_input] F captured by the menu, not the game')
    end
end
```

Here is the problem this solves. When your menu is open, you want F to mean "do a menu thing", but F (control 23) is also the game's enter-vehicle key. If you only read the press normally, the player presses F to pick a menu option and their ped also jumps into the nearest car. You need to take the key away from the game first.

`DisableControlAction(0, 23, true)` is the native that does it. The three arguments are the input group, the control ID, and a boolean that, when `true`, disables that control. It only lasts **one frame**, which is why it sits inside the per-frame loop: you re-disable it every frame the menu is open. Stop calling it and the control wakes back up next frame. While it is disabled, the game ignores F, so no more accidental vehicle entry.

But now a plain `IsControlJustPressed(0, 23)` returns false too, because as far as that native is concerned the control is off. That is what `IsDisabledControlJustPressed` is for. It is the exact same edge detector as `IsControlJustPressed`, one fire per press, except it reads the key **even though it is disabled**. So the pattern is always a pair: `DisableControlAction` to silence the game's use of the key, and `IsDisabledControlJustPressed` (or `IsDisabledControlPressed`) to read your own meaning for it. There are disabled-control versions of all three read functions, matching the three above one for one.

### When you reach for this pattern

The disable-then-read-disabled pattern is everywhere in real FiveM resources, because almost every menu or interaction overlaps a key the game already uses:

- **Any in-world menu or context UI.** A radial menu, an inventory, a target eye. They all disable movement, attack, and accept keys so the player drives the menu and not the character behind it.
- **Mini games and skill checks.** A lockpick or a hacking game disables the normal keys and reads the disabled versions, so pressing the game key does only the minigame thing.
- **Vehicle and weapon overrides.** Scripts that change what aim or handbrake does while a feature is active disable the real control for the frame and substitute their own logic.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `Nothing prints when I tap E, even standing on the spot` -> Your ped is not within 3.0 units of 215.0, -810.0, so the sleep never drops to 0 and input is never read. Run a /whereami style command or walk closer; the gate is intentionally tight.
- `The E line prints many times from one press` -> You used IsControlPressed instead of IsControlJustPressed. Pressed is true every frame the key is held; JustPressed is true only on the down edge. Switch to JustPressed for a one-shot action.
- `F still makes my ped enter a vehicle while the menu is open` -> DisableControlAction must be called every frame, inside the loop, because it only lasts one frame. Confirm it sits inside the menuOpen block with sleep set to 0, not run once at startup.
- `F prints nothing while the menu is open` -> You disabled the control but read it with IsControlJustPressed, which returns false for a disabled control. Read it with IsDisabledControlJustPressed instead.
- `The whole script feels like it lags the game` -> Your loop is running Wait(0) every frame regardless of distance. Keep the default sleep = 500 and only set sleep = 0 inside the conditions that need per-frame reads.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain why FiveM gives you control IDs instead of raw keycodes, and why that keeps your script working when players rebind keys.
- Read input in a polling thread and use a distance gate so the loop sleeps until the player is near the interaction.
- Choose between IsControlJustPressed, IsControlPressed, and IsControlJustReleased for one-shot, held, and release-edge actions.
- Take a game key over with DisableControlAction every frame and read your own meaning for it with IsDisabledControlJustPressed.
- Look up any control ID in the FiveM controls reference instead of guessing.

## Try it yourself

Turn the E interaction into a hold-to-confirm. Right now one tap of E prints once. Change it so the player must hold E for a full second near the spot before it fires, and so letting go early cancels and prints a cancel line.

```lua
local holdStart = nil

-- inside the dist < 3.0 block, replace the JustPressed check:
if IsControlPressed(0, 38) then
    if not holdStart then
        holdStart = GetGameTimer()  -- record when the hold began
    elseif GetGameTimer() - holdStart >= 1000 then
        print('[qu_controls_and_input] held E for 1s, confirmed')
        holdStart = nil
    end
else
    if holdStart then
        print('[qu_controls_and_input] released E early, cancelled')
    end
    holdStart = nil
end
```

`GetGameTimer()` returns the milliseconds the game has been running, so subtracting the start time tells you how long E has been held. Test it: tap E quickly and confirm you get the cancel line, then hold E for a full second and confirm you get the confirm line. You just combined `IsControlPressed` for the hold with the release branch for the cancel, which is the exact skeleton behind every hold-to-loot progress bar.

> **The modern rebindable alternative: RegisterKeyMapping**
> Polling a control in a thread is the right tool when the key only matters in a narrow context, like standing near one spot. For a key that should work anywhere and that players should be able to rebind in the GTA settings menu, FiveM gives you `RegisterKeyMapping`. You register a command, a description, a default input source, and a default key, and FiveM wires the binding for you and exposes it in the settings UI:
>
> ```lua
RegisterCommand('+myinteract', function()
    print('[qu_controls_and_input] interact key pressed')
end, false)

RegisterKeyMapping('+myinteract', 'Interact with the world', 'keyboard', 'E')
```
>
> The leading `+` makes it a key-down command, and FiveM pairs it with a matching `-` command on release automatically. The win is that the player can open Settings, find "Interact with the world", and rebind it without you writing a single line of polling. Reach for `RegisterKeyMapping` for global, user-rebindable keys; reach for the polling thread you built above when the key only has meaning in a specific place or state.

## References
- FiveM controls reference (control IDs): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/game-references/controls/
- FiveM IsControlJustPressed: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x580417101DDB492F
- FiveM DisableControlAction: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0xFE99B66D079CF6BC
- Cfx.re console key bindings and RegisterKeyMapping: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/cookbook/2020/01/06/using-the-new-console-key-bindings/
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

title: C3 · DUI: render HTML on 3D surfaces · Quasar Academy

toc.label: DUI vs NUI: pick the right tool

ai:
Full-screen NUI paints a webpage over the whole screen. **DUI** paints a webpage onto a single GTA texture instead, so the page becomes a surface in the world: a TV, a billboard, an advert, a dashboard. The browser is the same one NUI uses. The only thing that changes is where the pixels land. This lesson builds the smallest honest DUI: a live HTML clock that replaces a prop's texture so it ticks in the world, then takes the native chain apart so you can point it at any texture you like. ### What this lesson covers - You'll build: A resource that renders a live HTML clock onto an in-world texture, so a billboard prop shows a webpage you control instead of its baked GTA texture. - Time: ~28 minutes - You need: The NUI lessons done. You should already know SetNuiFocus, SendNUIMessage, RegisterNUICallback, and the difference between callback and resource-file URLs. DUI reuses the web stack, pointed at a texture instead of the screen. - You'll learn: What DUI is and how it differs from full-screen NUI -> the native chain CreateDui -> GetDuiHandle -> CreateRuntimeTxd -> CreateRuntimeTextureFromDuiHandle -> AddReplaceTexture -> how to push updates with SendDuiMessage, swap pages with SetDuiUrl, and clean up with DestroyDui ### Before you start - I finished the full-screen NUI lessons and can wire SendNUIMessage to a window message listener. - I know NUI callbacks use https://${GetParentResourceName()}/ and resource files use the cfx-nui- resource scope. - I can find my server resources folder, edit server.cfg, and restart one resource. - I can read txAdmin Live Console and F8 output. > **Build the proof, then understand it** > This lesson builds a working FiveM resource named `qu_dui_surfaces`. It creates a DUI from a local HTML page, wraps it in a runtime texture, and uses `AddReplaceTexture` to paint that texture over a known prop so a live clock shows in-world. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it runs, the next section takes the native chain apart line by line so you can target any texture you want. ## DUI vs NUI: pick the right tool Both use the same embedded Chromium browser the runtime ships with. The difference is the render target. - **NUI** renders your page to a full-screen layer over the game. You reach for it when the UI is something the player looks *at* and interacts with directly: menus, HUDs, inventories, phones, dialogs. Input flows through `SetNuiFocus` and `RegisterNUICallback`. - **DUI** renders your page to an offscreen texture you then paint onto game geometry. You reach for it when the UI lives *in the world*: an in-world TV, a digital billboard, a stadium screen, a slot machine display, an advert board, a gas-station price sign. A DUI surface has no cursor and no direct click input. It is pixels on a polygon. You drive it one way, from Lua, with `SendDuiMessage`. The quick test: if the player needs to move a mouse over it, that is NUI. If it is something they walk past and see, that is DUI. > **DUI is client-only** > Everything in this lesson runs in a `client_script`. The DUI texture and the prop both live on your own game instance, not on the server. There is no `server_script` here. If you later want a server to tell every client what to show, the server triggers a client event and the client does the DUI work. The client requests and renders; the server only coordinates. The server uses `source` to know which player asked, but it never creates a DUI itself. ## Build it ### Step 1 - Make the resource folder _Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._ Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder: ```text resources/qu_dui_surfaces ``` ### Step 2 - Create the files _Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._ Create this exact file layout. The `html` folder holds the page DUI will render, exactly like a NUI page: ```text resources/qu_dui_surfaces/ fxmanifest.lua client.lua html/ index.html ``` ### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua _Goal: FiveM knows which files to load and which web files to serve._ Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this: ```lua fx_version 'cerulean' game 'gta5' client_script 'client.lua' files { 'html/index.html' } ``` Notice there is no `ui_page` line. That is the first real difference from NUI: a full-screen NUI declares `ui_page`, but a DUI page is loaded by URL from Lua, so you only need to ship the file with `files {}` so the runtime can serve it. Do not add a `lua54 'yes'` line either. As of June 2025 that directive is deprecated and ignored; Lua 5.4 is the only runtime now, so you leave it out. ### Step 4 - Write the HTML page _Goal: There is a page for DUI to render: a clock that listens for Lua messages._ Open `html/index.html` and paste this. It is a plain page with a clock that updates itself, plus a listener for messages from Lua so you can change its label live: ```html
QUASAR LIVE
00:00:00
``` The body is fixed at 512 by 256 pixels on purpose. That is the resolution you will hand `CreateDui`, and matching the page size to the texture size keeps the result crisp instead of stretched. ### Step 5 - Write the lesson code _Goal: The topic is now represented by runnable code: a DUI painted onto a prop._ Open `client.lua` and paste this: ```lua local dui -- the DUI object handle (from CreateDui) local txd -- the runtime texture dictionary (from CreateRuntimeTxd) local active = false -- The texture we will replace. NOTE: these two values are a texture -- DICTIONARY name and a texture name, NOT the prop's model name, and they -- must be looked up per-prop in CodeWalker or OpenIV. prop_billboard_03 is -- used here as an example target; if the swap appears to do nothing even -- though all three console lines print, the most likely cause is that one -- of these two strings does not match the prop's real embedded texture. -- Verify them in CodeWalker before relying on the visible result. local TARGET_TXD = 'prop_billboard_03' -- confirm in CodeWalker/OpenIV local TARGET_TXN = 'P_Billboard_03' -- confirm in CodeWalker/OpenIV local RUNTIME_TXD = 'qu_dui_txd' local RUNTIME_TXN = 'qu_dui_tex' local function startDui() if active then return end -- 1. Create the browser surface from our local page, at 512x256. local url = ('https://cfx-nui-%s/html/index.html'):format(GetCurrentResourceName()) dui = CreateDui(url, 512, 256) -- 2. Get the low-level handle the texture system understands. local duiHandle = GetDuiHandle(dui) -- 3. Make a runtime texture dictionary to hold our new texture. txd = CreateRuntimeTxd(RUNTIME_TXD) -- 4. Turn the DUI's live output into a texture inside that dictionary. CreateRuntimeTextureFromDuiHandle(txd, RUNTIME_TXN, duiHandle) -- 5. Replace the prop's baked texture with our live DUI texture. AddReplaceTexture(TARGET_TXD, TARGET_TXN, RUNTIME_TXD, RUNTIME_TXN) active = true print('[qu_dui_surfaces] DUI created and texture replaced') end local function stopDui() if not active then return end -- Undo the texture swap first, then destroy the browser surface. RemoveReplaceTexture(TARGET_TXD, TARGET_TXN) DestroyDui(dui) dui, txd, active = nil, nil, false print('[qu_dui_surfaces] DUI destroyed and texture restored') end RegisterCommand('dui_start', function() startDui() end, false) RegisterCommand('dui_stop', function() stopDui() end, false) -- Push a new label into the live page without recreating anything. RegisterCommand('dui_label', function(source, args) if not active then print('[qu_dui_surfaces] start the DUI first with /dui_start') return end local text = table.concat(args, ' ') SendDuiMessage(dui, json.encode({ type = 'setLabel', text = text })) print('[qu_dui_surfaces] sent label: ' .. text) end, false) -- Always clean up if the resource stops, or the swap leaks until map reload. AddEventHandler('onResourceStop', function(res) if res == GetCurrentResourceName() then stopDui() end end) ``` ### Step 6 - Start and test it _Goal: The expected proof appears, and the billboard shows your live clock._ Open `server.cfg` and add this line: ```text ensure qu_dui_surfaces ``` Save, then run: ```text restart qu_dui_surfaces ``` Join the server so your game world exists, then run these from the F8 console: ```text dui_start ``` Look at any `prop_billboard_03` in the world. The most reliable spot to test is to spawn the prop in front of you, but if you are near a billboard that uses this prop you will see its face change to a dark clock that ticks every second. Then push a label and stop it: ```text dui_label OPEN 24 / 7 dui_stop ``` Expected output: ``` [qu_dui_surfaces] DUI created and texture replaced [qu_dui_surfaces] sent label: OPEN 24 / 7 [qu_dui_surfaces] DUI destroyed and texture restored ``` The console lines are your proof the chain ran. The visible proof is the billboard face: a ticking clock after `dui_start`, the label changing to `OPEN 24 / 7` after `dui_label`, and the original texture back after `dui_stop`. > **Do not see the billboard? It is still working** > `prop_billboard_03` is a specific prop, and not every map area has one in view. If the console lines printed, the DUI and the texture swap succeeded. To force a visible target, spawn the prop yourself with `CreateObject` near you, or point `TARGET_TXD` and `TARGET_TXN` at a prop you can see. The exercise at the end walks you through spawning your own prop and painting the DUI onto it, so the result is always on screen. ## How it works You ran it, the console confirmed the chain, and the billboard showed a live page. Now take the chain apart so the next time you want a webpage on a surface you are deciding which native does what, not copying. DUI is a five-link chain plus a few maintenance natives. Read each link by what it produces and what consumes it. ### Link 1: CreateDui makes the browser surface ```lua local url = ('https://cfx-nui-%s/html/index.html'):format(GetCurrentResourceName()) dui = CreateDui(url, 512, 256) ``` `CreateDui(url, width, height)` spins up an offscreen browser instance and starts rendering the page at `url` into a buffer of the size you give. It returns a **DUI object**: a handle you keep and pass to every other DUI native. Here `https://cfx-nui-/html/index.html` is the documented resource-file URL scope, so it resolves to the file shipped in `files {}`. That is different from posting a NUI callback, where browser-side JavaScript uses `https://${GetParentResourceName()}/`. You build this file URL from `GetCurrentResourceName()` so the resource works if its folder is renamed. The width and height matter. They are the resolution of the offscreen buffer, and they are why the HTML body was set to 512 by 256. Match the page to the buffer and the texture is sharp. Mismatch them and the browser renders at one size while the texture samples at another, which is the usual cause of a blurry or squashed DUI. You can point DUI at a remote URL too, not just a local file, but a local page you ship is the predictable choice: it loads instantly, works offline, and you control every pixel. Security note: a remote URL renders externally-controlled web content (and whatever JavaScript it runs) inside the player's game-client browser. Only ever point DUI at a URL you control; never at a player-supplied or third-party address, and never interpolate untrusted text into the URL. ### Link 2: GetDuiHandle hands the texture system a pointer ```lua local duiHandle = GetDuiHandle(dui) ``` The DUI object from link 1 is a high-level thing you command. The texture system underneath needs a low-level reference to the actual render output. `GetDuiHandle(dui)` returns that: a **DUI handle string** the runtime texture natives understand. You do not read it or parse it. You just pass it straight to the next link. Think of it as the difference between a remote control (the DUI object you press buttons on) and the wire the picture actually travels down (the handle). ### Link 3: CreateRuntimeTxd makes a home for the texture ```lua txd = CreateRuntimeTxd(RUNTIME_TXD) ``` Textures in GTA live inside texture dictionaries (TXDs). To make a brand-new texture at runtime, you first need a runtime dictionary to put it in. `CreateRuntimeTxd(name)` creates one with the name you choose and returns it. The name is yours to pick, but it has to be unique while it exists, which is why the resource uses a tagged name like `qu_dui_txd`. This native does not touch DUI at all yet. It is just allocating an empty shelf you are about to put a texture on. ### Link 4: CreateRuntimeTextureFromDuiHandle turns pixels into a game texture ```lua CreateRuntimeTextureFromDuiHandle(txd, RUNTIME_TXN, duiHandle) ``` This is the link that fuses the two worlds. It takes the runtime dictionary from link 3, a texture name you choose (`qu_dui_tex`), and the DUI handle from link 2, and it creates a live game texture whose contents are whatever the browser is currently rendering. Because it is wired to the DUI handle, it is not a snapshot. When the page repaints, the texture updates. That is the whole reason the clock ticks in the world without you doing anything more: link 4 made a texture that mirrors the browser, frame for frame. ### Link 5: AddReplaceTexture paints it onto the world ```lua AddReplaceTexture(TARGET_TXD, TARGET_TXN, RUNTIME_TXD, RUNTIME_TXN) ``` Now you have a live texture, but nothing in the world is using it yet. `AddReplaceTexture(origTxd, origTxn, newTxd, newTxn)` tells the renderer: anywhere the game would draw the original texture (`prop_billboard_03` / `P_Billboard_03`), draw mine instead (`qu_dui_txd` / `qu_dui_tex`). From that moment, every model on screen that samples the billboard texture shows your page. A texture replacement does not automatically map the player's cursor or world aim to browser coordinates. FiveM exposes DUI mouse-move, button, and wheel natives, but an advanced script must calculate the surface point and translate it to DUI pixels. This beginner proof is display-only. There are other documented ways to send DUI output into the world: the DUI docs themselves point at drawing the runtime texture with DRAW_SPRITE in 2D, drawing onto a game **render target** that a specific model is set up to use, or rendering it through a specialized Scaleform. `AddReplaceTexture` is not the path the DUI page shows, but it is a real, widely-used texture-replacement native and the broadest, simplest one to learn first because it works on any prop without the model needing a pre-declared render target. ### Driving it after it is live: SendDuiMessage and SetDuiUrl ```lua SendDuiMessage(dui, json.encode({ type = 'setLabel', text = text })) ``` `SendDuiMessage(dui, jsonString)` is the DUI twin of `SendNUIMessage`. It posts a JSON string into the page, where it arrives on the same `window` message event you already use in NUI. In `index.html` the listener checks `event.data.type === 'setLabel'` and updates the label. This is how you change what a live DUI shows without tearing the whole chain down and rebuilding it: the browser stays up, you just message it. Note you pass a JSON *string* here, so you call `json.encode` yourself, unlike `SendNUIMessage` which encodes the table for you. When you need a different page entirely, not just new data on the same page, you call `SetDuiUrl(dui, newUrl)`. The same browser surface navigates to the new URL and the texture keeps updating from it. Reach for `SetDuiUrl` when one billboard cycles between completely different layouts; reach for `SendDuiMessage` when it is the same layout with changing content. ### Tearing it down: RemoveReplaceTexture and DestroyDui ```lua RemoveReplaceTexture(TARGET_TXD, TARGET_TXN) DestroyDui(dui) ``` Cleanup is two steps in the right order. `RemoveReplaceTexture` undoes link 5, so the prop goes back to its real texture. `DestroyDui(dui)` shuts down the browser instance from link 1 and frees its memory. The `onResourceStop` handler runs the same teardown when the resource stops, which matters: a DUI you never destroy keeps a whole browser alive in the background, and a texture replacement you never remove lingers until the map reloads. Treat every `CreateDui` as having a matching `DestroyDui`, the same way every `function` has its `end`. ### Check yourself: Why does this DUI proof ignore ordinary mouse clicks while a full-screen NUI page receives them? Full-screen NUI can receive focus through `SetNuiFocus`, so the runtime routes cursor and keyboard input to it. DUI is rendered offscreen and painted onto geometry, so there is no automatic mapping from a click to a point on that texture. Advanced code can build that mapping and forward input with the documented DUI mouse natives. For a beginner interaction, use a target or control prompt in front of the display and update the DUI with `SendDuiMessage`. ### When you reach for this pattern A live page on a texture is the foundation of every dynamic surface in a modern server, and the chain you just built is the one every one of them uses: - **In-world screens and TVs.** A clubhouse TV showing a stream, a stadium jumbotron, a security-camera monitor.
