> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds a working resource named `qu_config_demo`: a `config.lua` that holds settings, and a `server.lua` that reads them and prints them on a command. Follow the folder names, file names, code, restart command, and expected output exactly. Once it runs, the next section takes the config apart key by key so you know which lines are safe to touch and which are not.

## What a config file is

A `config.lua` is just a Lua file that builds one big table and leaves it where the rest of the script can find it. There is no magic. The whole pattern is this:

```lua
Config = {}
Config.Debug = true
Config.MaxPlayers = 32
```

`Config = {}` creates an empty **table**, a container that holds named values. Each line after it drops one more value in under a name. `Config.Debug` is the name `Debug` inside the `Config` table, and you read it back the same way you set it. That is the entire idea. A real script just has more lines.

When a script author wants you to change behavior, they pull those changeable values out of the code and into this one file. You edit the file, you do not edit the code. That separation is the point of a config: the risky logic stays sealed, and the safe knobs sit in one place with your name on them.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_config_demo
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact layout. The config lives in its own file, the way real scripts ship it:

```text
resources/qu_config_demo/
  fxmanifest.lua
  config.lua
  server-config.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM loads the config before the code that reads it._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

shared_script 'config.lua'
server_script 'server-config.lua'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

The order matters. `shared_script 'config.lua'` is listed first, so FiveM runs it before `server.lua`. A shared script is sent to clients and runs on both client and server, so it may contain only settings players are allowed to read. `server-config.lua` is server-only and loads before `server.lua`; it is where server-only configuration and secret references belong.

### Step 4 - Write config.lua
_Goal: A real, multi-key config table the script can read._

Open `config.lua` and paste this. These are the keys you will see in almost every script you download:

```lua
Config = {}

Config.Framework = 'qbox'          -- 'qbox' | 'esx' | 'standalone'
Config.Locale = 'en'               -- language file to load
Config.Debug = false               -- true prints extra logs

Config.OpenKey = 'F6'              -- key binding to open the menu

Config.Prices = {
    water = 5,
    bandage = 25,
    repairkit = 150,
}

Config.Locations = {
    { label = 'Legion Square', coords = vec3(215.0, -810.0, 30.0) },
    { label = 'Sandy Shores',  coords = vec3(1960.0, 3740.0, 32.0) },
}

```

Open `server-config.lua` and paste this. The actual webhook stays in a non-replicated convar rather than a file clients download:

```lua
ServerConfig = {}
ServerConfig.Webhook = GetConvar('qu_config_webhook', '')
```

If you need a webhook, set it in the server's private `server.cfg` and never commit the real value:

```cfg
set qu_config_webhook "https://discord.com/api/webhooks/replace-me"
```

### Step 5 - Write server.lua
_Goal: The code reads the config instead of hard coding values._

Open `server.lua` and paste this. Notice it never hard codes a price or a framework name. It reads them from `Config`:

```lua
RegisterCommand('config_demo', function()
    print('[qu_config_demo] framework: ' .. Config.Framework)
    print('[qu_config_demo] locale:    ' .. Config.Locale)
    print('[qu_config_demo] debug:     ' .. tostring(Config.Debug))
    print('[qu_config_demo] open key:  ' .. Config.OpenKey)
    print('[qu_config_demo] water price: ' .. Config.Prices.water)
    print('[qu_config_demo] locations:   ' .. #Config.Locations)
end, true)
```

### Step 6 - Start and test it
_Goal: The config values appear in the console._

Open `server.cfg` and add this line:

    ```cfg
ensure qu_config_demo
```

    Save. The line in `server.cfg` only takes effect the next time the whole server boots, so to start the resource right now in your running server, run these two commands in the txAdmin Live Console. `refresh` makes the server notice your brand-new folder; `ensure` starts it (and restarts it if it was already running):

    ```text
refresh
ensure qu_config_demo
```

    From now on, after you EDIT this resource you only need `restart qu_config_demo` (it is already started, so a plain restart is enough).

    Run this test from the txAdmin Live Console:

    ```text
config_demo
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_config_demo] framework: qbox
[qu_config_demo] locale:    en
[qu_config_demo] debug:     false
[qu_config_demo] open key:  F6
[qu_config_demo] water price: 5
[qu_config_demo] locations:   2
```

## The keys you will meet everywhere

Different authors name things slightly differently, but the same handful of keys show up in nearly every `config.lua`. Here is what each one controls and whether it is safe to change.

A few of these deserve a closer look, because the type of the value matters as much as the value itself.

### Booleans: true and false, never quoted

```lua
Config.Debug = false
```

`Config.Debug` is a **boolean**. It is either `true` or `false`, with no quotes. This is the single most common type to get wrong. If you write `Config.Debug = 'false'` with quotes, you have stored the text `"false"`, which is a string, and a string is always treated as true when the script checks it. So the debug logs you tried to turn off stay on. Booleans are bare words: `true` or `false`.

### Numbers stay bare, strings get quotes

```lua
Config.Prices = {
    water = 5,
    bandage = 25,
}
```

A price is a **number**, so it has no quotes: `water = 5`. A name like the framework is a **string**, so it sits inside quotes: `Config.Framework = 'qbox'`. Mixing these up is the second most common config break. Write `water = '5'` and the script may try to do math on text and error, or silently misbehave. The rule: numbers bare, text in quotes.

### The Discord webhook

```lua
ServerConfig.Webhook = GetConvar('qu_config_webhook', '')
```

A **webhook** is a URL the script posts to so log messages appear in a Discord channel. Treat it like a password: anyone who has it can post through it. Never put one in `config.lua` when that file is declared as a `shared_script`, because shared files are downloaded by clients. Keep the value in a private server convar and let `server-config.lua` read it with `GetConvar`. If a webhook is exposed, rotate it in Discord instead of merely deleting it from the file.

### Check yourself: Why does Config.Debug = 'false' fail to turn debugging off?

Because the quotes turn it into a string, not a boolean. When the script checks `if Config.Debug then`, Lua treats any non-empty string as true, including the text `"false"`. So the check passes and the debug logs keep printing. The fix is to remove the quotes so it is the real boolean `false`. In Lua only `false` and `nil` count as false; every other value, including `0` and the string `"false"`, counts as true. That is a Lua rule worth memorizing early, because it surprises people coming from other languages.

## Safe edits versus risky edits

Here is the mental line that keeps your server alive. There are two kinds of files in a downloaded script.

### The config is the cockpit

`config.lua` is built to be edited. The author put values there on purpose so you can tune them. Changing a documented price, key bind, coordinate, or boolean is the intended customization surface, but it is not risk-free: a wrong type, missing item name, invalid coordinate, or exposed secret can still break behavior. Back up the file, make one change, restart, and test the documented range.

### The logic is the engine

The other files, usually `server.lua`, `client.lua`, and anything in a `server/` or `client/` folder, hold the actual logic: how the script talks to the database, how it validates a player, how it moves money. Editing those is a **risky edit**. You can break things you cannot see, introduce a security hole, or make the script impossible to update later because your changes get wiped by the next version. As a rule: if a value is not exposed in `config.lua`, the author did not intend for you to change it. Touching the engine is an advanced move, not a setup step.

```text
resources/qu_config_demo/
  config.lua         <- shared settings. Clients can read every value here.
  server-config.lua  <- server-only settings and private convar reads.
  server.lua         <- the engine. Read it, learn from it, do not casually edit.
  fxmanifest.lua     <- the wiring. Only touch when adding or renaming files.
```

The whole reason a `config.lua` exists is to give you a safe surface so you never need to open the engine just to set a price.

## A real before/after edit

Let us change the water price from `5` to `12` and turn debug on. This is the most common edit you will ever make, and it is completely safe.

**Before:**

```lua
Config.Debug = false

Config.Prices = {
    water = 5,
    bandage = 25,
    repairkit = 150,
}
```

**After:**

```lua
Config.Debug = true

Config.Prices = {
    water = 12,
    bandage = 25,
    repairkit = 150,
}
```

Two values changed, nothing else. Now restart and re-test:

```text
restart qu_config_demo
config_demo
```

Expected output:

```
[qu_config_demo] debug:     true
[qu_config_demo] water price: 12
```

That is the entire loop you will repeat hundreds of times: open `config.lua`, change a value, save, restart the resource, confirm in the console. A config edit only takes effect after a restart, because the resource reads the file once at start. Saving the file alone changes nothing until you restart.

### Reskinning the UI is a config edit too

Many scripts expose their menu accent color as a config value, so a "before/after" can be visual as well as numeric. If a script ships something like `Config.AccentColor`, switching the brand color is the same safe edit: change the value, restart, done.

You did not open the engine to do that. You changed one exposed value. That is the difference between a config edit and a logic edit, even when the result is something you can see on screen.

## How a bad edit breaks the resource

This is where most beginners lose an evening. A `config.lua` is real Lua, so it follows Lua's rules to the letter. A single missing comma or quote does not produce a friendly "you made a typo here" message. It stops the whole resource from loading, and the script that depended on it does nothing.

### A missing comma

Delete the comma after `water = 12` so two entries run together:

```lua
Config.Prices = {
    water = 12
    bandage = 25,
}
```

The resource will not start. In the console you get something like:

```text
Error parsing script @qu_config_demo/config.lua in resource qu_config_demo:
config.lua: '}' expected (to close '{' ...) near 'bandage'
Failed to load script config.lua.
```

(Illustrative: the exact line number and wording depend on your server build and where the error sits in your file. What matters is the shape: `'}' expected ... near 'bandage'`.)

Read it backwards. Lua reached `bandage` while it was still expecting either another item or the `}` that closes the table, because nothing told it the `water = 12` line had ended. The comma is the separator between entries. Without it, Lua cannot tell where one value stops and the next begins, so it gives up and the resource never loads.

### A missing quote

Now break a string instead. Drop the closing quote on the framework value:

```lua
Config.Framework = 'qbox
```

```text
Error parsing script @qu_config_demo/config.lua in resource qu_config_demo:
config.lua:3: unfinished string near ''qbox'
Failed to load script config.lua.
```

A string needs a fence on both ends. With only an opening quote, Lua reads to the end of the line looking for the closing one, never finds it, and reports an unfinished string. Same outcome: the resource fails to load and everything that reads `Config` is dead until you fix the quote.

### The wrong type

The nastiest break is the one that does not error at all. Quote a number that the code does math on:

```lua
Config.Prices = {
    water = '12',
}
```

The file loads fine. No red text. But when the script later runs `playerMoney - Config.Prices.water`, it is subtracting a string from a number, and Lua throws a runtime error at that moment instead of at startup. So the resource looks healthy until a player tries to buy water, then it breaks mid-action with an error that points at the code, not at your config. This is why type matters: numbers bare, strings quoted, booleans bare. Get the type right and the value almost takes care of itself.

### Check yourself: You changed one value in config.lua and now the whole resource will not start. The error names a line near the bottom of the file. Where do you actually look?

Start at the line the error names, then scan the lines just above it. A parse error like "'}' expected" or "unfinished string" usually points at the first place Lua got confused, which is often one line past the real mistake. The missing comma or quote is almost always on the line above the one named, or on the line you just edited. Reading the error backwards, from the symbol it expected to the value it choked on, points you straight at it. And because you only changed one value, the culprit is whatever you last touched. Undo that one edit and the resource loads again, which confirms you found it.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `'}' expected (to close '{' ...) near 'bandage'` -> A comma is missing between two table entries. Every entry inside a { } table needs a comma after it, including a trailing one is fine. Add the comma after the value on the line above the one named.
- `unfinished string near ''qbox'` -> A quote is missing on one end of a string. Strings need a matching quote at the start and the end. Re-add the closing quote on the value named in the error.
- `attempt to perform arithmetic on a string value` -> You quoted a number that the code does math on, like a price. Remove the quotes so it is a real number: water = 12, not water = '12'.
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'Config' or 'ServerConfig')` -> A config file did not load first. In fxmanifest.lua, list shared_script 'config.lua', then server_script 'server-config.lua', then server_script 'server.lua'.
- `I changed a value but nothing changed in game` -> A config edit only applies after a restart. Save the file, then run restart qu_config_demo in the console and test again.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Recognize the Config = {} table pattern and read any value back with Config.Name.
- Name common config keys such as Framework, Locale, Debug, Prices, Locations, Items, and key bindings, while keeping webhooks and other secrets server-only.
- Tell a safe config edit from a risky logic edit, and know that anything not exposed in config.lua was not meant for you to change.
- Set the right type every time: numbers bare, strings quoted, booleans as bare true or false.
- Read a parse error backwards to find a missing comma or quote, and know a wrong type can break at runtime instead of at startup.
- Run the full loop: edit config.lua, save, restart the resource, confirm the change in the console.

## Try it yourself

Add a brand new setting to the config and prove the code reads it. Open `config.lua` and add a max-stock limit for the water shop, then make `server.lua` print it:

```lua
-- in config.lua, add this line:
Config.MaxStock = 50
```

```lua
-- in server.lua, add this line inside the command handler:
print('[qu_config_demo] max stock: ' .. Config.MaxStock)
```

Restart `qu_config_demo` and run `config_demo`. Confirm the new line prints `max stock: 50`. Then deliberately break it: write `Config.MaxStock = '50'` with quotes, restart, and watch the demo still load fine but notice the value is now a string. Finally, add a second location to `Config.Locations` and confirm the `locations:` count prints `3` instead of `2`. You have now added a config key, read it from code, and seen first hand how type and the trailing comma decide whether your edit is safe or a trap.

## References
- FiveM resource manifest reference: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/
- FiveM script loading (client/server/shared): https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/#scripts
- Lua 5.4 reference manual: tables: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/manual.html#2.1

title: B6 · How scripts talk to frameworks · Quasar Academy

toc.label: The one idea: your script asks, the framework answers
A side by side: the same action in three frameworks

ai:
You already know how to register a command and print a line. The missing piece is the player. A framework like ESX, QBCore, or Qbox is the thing that knows who the player is, how much cash they hold, and what job they work. Your script does not store any of that. It **asks the framework**. In this lesson you build one server script that asks all three the same two questions, give $100 and what is your job, and you watch each one answer in its own dialect.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A server script that detects whether the server runs ESX, QBCore, or Qbox, gets the player object, gives them $100, and prints their job. The same action, three frameworks, one file.
- Time: ~24 minutes
- You need: A local server running ONE framework you can restart (es_extended, qb-core, or qbx_core), the FiveM client so you can join it in-game with `connect 127.0.0.1:30120`, plus the natives toolbox and command/print habits from earlier modules.
- You'll learn: How to get the player object in each framework -> how to read money and job -> the export style vs the event style -> why real scripts detect the framework first and then branch

### Before you start
- I can make a resource folder, write an fxmanifest, and ensure it in server.cfg.
- I can register a server command and read txAdmin Live Console output.
- I know my server runs exactly one of ESX, QBCore, or Qbox, and I can restart it.

> **Build the proof, then understand it**
> This lesson builds one server script named `qu_framework_talk` that detects the framework, gets the player object, adds $100, and prints the player's job. Follow the folder names, files, code, and restart steps exactly. Once a command prints money and job for your framework, the next section takes the call apart so you can read any framework export without copying.

> **This complements the frameworks overview**
> If you have not met ESX, QBCore, and Qbox as concepts yet, read [Frameworks: ESX, QBCore, Qbox](/lessons/frameworks) first. That lesson tells you what each framework *is*. This one shows you how your code actually *talks* to it.

## The one idea: your script asks, the framework answers

A plain FiveM resource knows almost nothing about a player. It can read a ped with `PlayerPedId`, but it has no idea that ped is "John, a cop, with $4,200 in the bank." That knowledge lives in the framework. So every framework hands your script a **getter**: you pass it a server id, and it hands back a **player object**, a table that holds that player's money, job, identifier, and a set of functions to change them.

```text
your script              framework                database
-----------              ---------                --------
GetPlayer(source)  --->  look up player    --->   read row
                   <---  player object (table)

Player.addMoney(100) --> framework updates --->   write row
```

The whole skill is three moves, in this order:

1. Get a reference to the framework itself (the "core object" or its exports).
2. Use it to get the **player object** for one `source`.
3. Call functions on that player object to read or change money and job.

Every framework follows that exact shape. Only the names change. That is why, once you can read one, you can read all three.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Make the resource folder
_Goal: The server has one folder for this lesson._

Inside your server's `resources` folder, create this folder:

```text
resources/qu_framework_talk
```

### Step 2 - Create the files
_Goal: Every file named in the manifest exists._

Create this exact file layout. Everything here is server side, because money and job live on the server, never on the client.

```text
resources/qu_framework_talk/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest.lua
_Goal: FiveM loads your one server file._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and paste this:

```lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

server_script 'server.lua'
```

No `lua54` line: Lua 5.4 is the only runtime now, so that directive is deprecated and ignored. We are deliberately not adding a `dependencies { }` block yet. The detection code in the next step handles a framework that has not loaded, so the script will not crash even if it starts in the wrong order.

### Step 4 - Write the detect-and-act code
_Goal: The topic is now runnable code that works on whichever framework you run._

Open `server.lua` and paste this. It detects the framework once, then a `/fwtest` command gets the player, gives $100, and prints the job.

```lua
-- Detect the framework ONCE when the resource starts.
-- GetResourceState returns 'started' only for a resource that is actually running.
local Framework = nil
local FrameworkName = 'none'

if GetResourceState('es_extended') == 'started' then
    Framework = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()
    FrameworkName = 'esx'
elseif GetResourceState('qbx_core') == 'started' then
    FrameworkName = 'qbox'
elseif GetResourceState('qb-core') == 'started' then
    Framework = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()
    FrameworkName = 'qbcore'
end

-- `%s` is a placeholder: :format(FrameworkName) drops the value of
-- FrameworkName into the %s slot, so this prints e.g. "...framework: esx".
print(('[qu_framework_talk] detected framework: %s'):format(FrameworkName))

RegisterCommand('fwtest', function(source)
    local src = source
    if src == 0 then
        print('[qu_framework_talk] run this in-game, not from the console (it needs a real player)')
        return
    end

    if FrameworkName == 'esx' then
        local xPlayer = Framework.GetPlayerFromId(src)
        if not xPlayer then return end
        xPlayer.addAccountMoney('money', 100) -- 'money' is the cash account; reason is optional
        print(('[qu_framework_talk] ESX gave $100, job is %s'):format(xPlayer.getJob().name))

    elseif FrameworkName == 'qbcore' then
        local Player = Framework.Functions.GetPlayer(src)
        if not Player then return end
        Player.Functions.AddMoney('cash', 100, 'qu_framework_talk test')
        print(('[qu_framework_talk] QBCore gave $100, job is %s'):format(Player.PlayerData.job.name))

    elseif FrameworkName == 'qbox' then
        local Player = exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)
        if not Player then return end
        exports.qbx_core:AddMoney(src, 'cash', 100, 'qu_framework_talk test')
        print(('[qu_framework_talk] Qbox gave $100, job is %s'):format(Player.PlayerData.job.name))

    else
        print('[qu_framework_talk] no supported framework is running on this server')
    end
end, true)
```

### Step 5 - Start and test it
_Goal: The expected proof appears in txAdmin and the player's cash goes up._

Open `server.cfg` and make sure this line is **below** your framework's `ensure` line, so the framework starts first:

```cfg
ensure es_extended       # or qb-core, or qbx_core (only one of these)
ensure qu_framework_talk
add_ace group.admin command.fwtest allow
```

> **Why this command is locked down**
> `/fwtest` gives a player $100. The `true` as the last argument to `RegisterCommand` marks the command **restricted**, and `add_ace group.admin command.fwtest allow` grants it only to the admin ACE group. Without both, ANY connected player could run `/fwtest` and print/grant themselves money. Any command that changes money, items, or permissions must be restricted server-side and gated by an ACE -- never trust that a command is hidden just because players do not know its name.

Save, then in the txAdmin Live Console run:

```text
restart qu_framework_talk
```

The moment it restarts, the detection line prints. On an ESX server you see:

Expected output:

```
[qu_framework_talk] detected framework: esx
```

Now connect to your own server and spawn in as a character (you must be fully loaded in the world, not on the loading screen). Join with the same account that is a txAdmin administrator, so the command has both a real player AND the `command.fwtest` ACE permission. Then press the **F8** key to open the in-game console (a black overlay at the top of the FiveM window), click into it, and type the command and press Enter:

```text
/fwtest
```

Watch txAdmin. The exact line depends on which framework you run; on an ESX server with the default unemployed job you see (illustrative -- your framework's wording will differ):

Expected output:

```
[qu_framework_talk] ESX gave $100, job is unemployed
```

On QBCore the line reads `QBCore gave $100, job is unemployed`; on Qbox, `Qbox gave $100, job is unemployed`. `unemployed` is the default job on all three (it is the documented default in QBCore/Qbox PlayerData and ESX's default job). The real, framework-independent proof is two things together: (1) the printed line names your framework, and (2) your in-game cash goes UP by exactly $100 from whatever it was before. Note the starting balance first, run /fwtest, then re-check -- the delta is the proof, not any specific total, because each server configures its own starting cash.

## How it works

You ran it and your cash went up. Now take the calls apart so the next time you open a framework's docs you are deciding, not copying. There are only four things happening: get the core, get the player, change money, read job. The rest is the detection wrapper that lets one file serve three frameworks.

### Getting the framework: export style vs event style

The very first thing every framework script needs is a reference to the framework itself. There are two historical ways to get it, and knowing both is what lets you read old and new scripts.

```lua
-- The EXPORT style (modern, preferred everywhere in 2026)
local ESX = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()
local QBCore = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()
```

An **export** is a function one resource publishes for other resources to call. `exports['es_extended']` reaches into the running `es_extended` resource and `:getSharedObject()` calls the function it published under that name. You get back the big `ESX` table on the spot. QBCore does the identical thing with `:GetCoreObject()`. This is a direct, synchronous call: you ask, you receive, on the same line.

```lua
-- The EVENT style (older ESX scripts you will still meet in the wild)
ESX = nil
TriggerEvent('esx:getSharedObject', function(obj) ESX = obj end) -- legacy recognition only
```

You will see this event pattern in old tutorials. Current ESX Legacy code should use the export or its supported import; do not assume the old event exists on a current install. Recognize it for migration work, then replace it. Qbox uses exports rather than a shared-object event.

The mental model: **export is a phone call, event is a letter.** A phone call gives you the answer while you wait. A letter gets answered later, when the other side gets around to reading it. For grabbing the framework object, you want the phone call.

### Getting the player object

Once you hold the framework, you ask it for one player by their `source`, the server id of the connected player.

```lua
local xPlayer = Framework.GetPlayerFromId(src)   -- ESX
local Player  = Framework.Functions.GetPlayer(src) -- QBCore
local Player  = exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)    -- Qbox
```

Look at the shape. All three take the same input, a `source` number, and all three return the same kind of thing, a **player object** table. The only differences are the path you call it through:

- **ESX** exposes it flat on the shared object as `GetPlayerFromId`. The object it returns is called `xPlayer` by convention.
- **QBCore** nests its functions under a `.Functions` sub-table, so it is `GetPlayer`. The object is called `Player`.
- **Qbox** skips the core table entirely and exposes `GetPlayer` straight off the `qbx_core` export.

That `if not Player then return end` line under each call matters. `GetPlayer` returns `nil` when the framework has no loaded player for that source yet, for example a player who is connecting but has not finished loading their character. Indexing a `nil` value is the single most common framework crash, the `attempt to index a nil value` error. Guarding for it is not optional politeness; it is the difference between a script that survives a busy server and one that errors every time someone joins.

### Changing money

With the player object in hand, you call its functions to change state.

```lua
xPlayer.addAccountMoney('money', 100)  -- ESX
Player.Functions.AddMoney('cash', 100) -- QBCore
exports.qbx_core:AddMoney(src, 'cash', 100, 'example') -- Qbox native API
```

Here the dialects diverge. ESX uses named accounts such as `money` and `bank`. QBCore uses configured money types through `Player.Functions`. Qbox has a similar data model but its native API is the `qbx_core` money export, which also accepts a reason for the audit log. Stick to `cash` and `bank`, which all three frameworks define out of the box. Qbox's `AddMoney` also documents a built-in `crypto` type, but on ESX and QBCore extra accounts like `black_money` or `crypto` only exist if the server's framework configuration adds them -- so do not assume an account exists before you confirm it.

> On ESX Legacy you will still see the shorter `xPlayer.addMoney(100)` and `xPlayer.removeMoney(100)` in older scripts. Those are backwards-compatibility shortcuts; `addAccountMoney('money', ...)` and `removeAccountMoney('money', ...)` are the current API, so write those today.

### Reading job

```lua
xPlayer.getJob().name        -- ESX, returns the job name string
Player.PlayerData.job.name   -- QBCore
Player.PlayerData.job.name   -- Qbox
```

ESX hands you the job through a **function**, `getJob()`, which returns a table, and you read `.name` off it. QBCore and Qbox instead expose the job as plain **data** sitting on `Player.PlayerData.job`, so you read `.name` directly without calling anything. Both styles carry the same fields underneath: `name` (like `police`), `label` (the pretty `Police Officer`), and a `grade` table with the rank. The default job a fresh character holds is `unemployed`, which is exactly what your `/fwtest` printed.

### Why real scripts detect the framework first

Look again at the top of the file. Before any command, the script decides once which framework it is running on:

```lua
if GetResourceState('es_extended') == 'started' then
    Framework = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()
    FrameworkName = 'esx'
elseif GetResourceState('qbx_core') == 'started' then
    FrameworkName = 'qbox'
elseif GetResourceState('qb-core') == 'started' then
    Framework = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()
    FrameworkName = 'qbcore'
end
```

`GetResourceState('es_extended')` is a FiveM native that returns the string `'started'` only when that resource is actually running on this server. So this block is your script looking around the server and asking "which framework is home?" It sets `FrameworkName` once, and from then on every command just branches on that string. This is exactly how published, multi-framework scripts work: a popular robbery or job script does not ship three separate downloads, it ships one that **detects then branches**, so it drops into an ESX server or a QBCore server unchanged.

Notice the order. Qbox uses `qbx_core` as its core resource, while QBCore uses `qb-core`. Some Qbox servers also keep a compatibility `qb-core` running, so you must check for `qbx_core` **before** `qb-core`, or a Qbox server would be misread as QBCore. Order of detection is not cosmetic; it decides correctness on hybrid servers.

### Check yourself: On a Qbox server that also runs a qb-core compatibility shim, why does checking qbx_core before qb-core matter?

Because `GetResourceState` would return `'started'` for both `qbx_core` and `qb-core` on that server. If you checked `qb-core` first, the script would set `FrameworkName = 'qbcore'` and then try to call `exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()` and read job and money through QBCore's paths, when it should be using Qbox's `exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer`. The two are close cousins but not identical, and the resource that defines the *real* player data is `qbx_core`. Checking the more specific resource first makes the branch land on the framework that actually owns the player, not the shim that happens to be running beside it.

### When you reach for this pattern

Getting the player object is the opening move of nearly every server-side feature you will ever build:

- **Any shop, job, or reward.** Selling fish, paying a mechanic, a daily login bonus: all of them start with `GetPlayer(source)` and end with `AddMoney` or `RemoveMoney`.
- **Any job-gated action.** A police-only `/cuff`, a mechanic-only repair: you read `job.name` and compare it before letting the action run.
- **Any script meant to be shared or sold.** The detect-then-branch wrapper is what makes a script "framework agnostic." It is the difference between code that works on one server and code that works on thousands.

## A side by side: the same action in three frameworks

Here is the whole job, give $100 and read the job name, lined up so you can see the dialects against each other. Same shape, different words.

```lua
-- ============ ESX ============
local ESX = exports['es_extended']:getSharedObject()
local xPlayer = ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)
xPlayer.addAccountMoney('money', 100)       -- give $100 (cash account)
local job = xPlayer.getJob().name           -- read job (via function)

-- ============ QBCore ============
local QBCore = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()
local Player = QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)
Player.Functions.AddMoney('cash', 100)      -- give $100 (cash account)
local job = Player.PlayerData.job.name      -- read job (plain data)

-- ============ Qbox ============
local Player = exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)
exports.qbx_core:AddMoney(src, 'cash', 100, 'example')
local job = Player.PlayerData.job.name      -- read job (plain data)
```

Read top to bottom and the family resemblance is obvious. Every framework: get the core (or its export), get the player, change money, read job. The table below is the same comparison as a lookup you can keep open while you script.

## If something went wrong

## Common errors and fixes
- `attempt to index a nil value (global 'ESX' / 'QBCore')` -> The framework object is nil because the framework had not started when you grabbed it, or the resource name is misspelled. Confirm the framework is ensured ABOVE qu_framework_talk in server.cfg, and that the detection branch matched your framework (check the 'detected framework' print line).
- `detected framework: none` -> GetResourceState found no es_extended, qbx_core, or qb-core in the 'started' state. Run the resource list in txAdmin and confirm your framework is actually running and spelled exactly as the code checks it.
- `attempt to index a nil value (local 'Player' / 'xPlayer')` -> GetPlayer returned nil, usually because the source has no loaded character yet. Run /fwtest in-game after you are fully spawned, and keep the 'if not Player then return end' guard so a half-loaded player cannot crash the handler.
- `Job prints but money does not change on QBCore or Qbox` -> You probably added to the wrong account. AddMoney needs a money type first: AddMoney('cash', 100) moves pocket cash, AddMoney('bank', 100) moves the bank balance. Check which account you are reading in-game.
- `Qbox server prints 'detected framework: qbcore'` -> A qb-core compatibility resource is running alongside qbx_core and your detection checked qb-core first. Keep the qbx_core check ABOVE the qb-core check so the more specific framework wins.

## What you can do now

## Recap
- Explain that your script does not store money or job: it asks the framework, which owns that data.
- Get the framework object the export way (getSharedObject / GetCoreObject) and recognize the older ESX event way when you read old scripts.
- Get the player object from a source in ESX, QBCore, and Qbox, and guard against the nil it returns for an unloaded player.
- Give and take money, and read the job name, knowing ESX uses one account while QBCore and Qbox split cash and bank.
- Write a detect-then-branch wrapper with GetResourceState so one script serves all three frameworks, checking qbx_core before qb-core.

## Try it yourself

Build a restricted job-gated test command, `/paycop`, that pays a fixed server-defined amount only when the active job is `police`. Do not accept the reward amount from the player.

```lua
local PAYCOP_AMOUNT = 100

RegisterCommand('paycop', function(source)
    local src = source

    -- 1. Get the player object for your framework (reuse the branch from the lesson).
    -- 2. Read their job name into a variable called jobName.
    -- 3. if jobName ~= 'police' then print a 'not a cop' line and return.
    -- 4. Otherwise add PAYCOP_AMOUNT through the server framework API.
end, true)
```

Grant only your admin group the `command.paycop` ACE while testing. Test once as unemployed, where it refuses, and once as police. In a real payroll feature, entitlement, amount, cooldown, and duty all come from server-held state; a job name alone is not enough.

## References
- ESX commands and player functions: https://docs.esx-framework.org/en/esx_core/es_extended/commands
- QBCore server function reference: https://docs.qbcore.org/qbcore-documentation/qb-core/server-function-reference
- Qbox qbx_core exports: https://docs.qbox.re/resources/qbx_core/
- FiveM GetResourceState native: https://docs.fivem.net/natives/?_0x4039B485
- FiveM exports and the manifest: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/resource-manifest/resource-manifest/

title: Project · Player-to-player trade system · Quasar Academy

toc.label: The mental model: who is allowed to decide what
How it works — every decision, and why

ai:
A trade lets two players hand items to each other in the world: you offer 5 bandages, they accept, the items move. The hard part is not the menu — it is making the swap impossible to cheat. This lesson builds a trade where the **server** owns every decision, both players must confirm, and a lock stops the classic "accept twice and duplicate the item" exploit. ### What this lesson covers - Difficulty: Intermediate - Time: 60-90 min - Framework: QBCore / ESX / Qbox - Stack: ox_inventory + ox_lib ### Before you start - A working FiveM server you can restart from txAdmin or the server console (you reach it with `connect 127.0.0.1:30120` in the F8 console, and you type server commands in the txAdmin live console or the terminal window the server runs in). - QBCore, ESX Legacy, or Qbox installed and a test character that can spawn items. - ox_inventory and ox_lib running and listed in your server.cfg with `ensure`. - You finished a basic resource before (you know what fxmanifest.lua, client_script, and server_script do). - Two ways to connect two players: a second PC, a friend, or two game clients. A trade needs two `source` IDs. ## The mental model: who is allowed to decide what Before any code, get this picture straight, because it is the whole lesson. In FiveM there are two worlds. The **client** is each player's own game — it runs on their PC, and a cheater can rewrite anything it sends. The **server** is the one machine you control. The rule that keeps every economy script safe is one sentence: > The client may *ask*. Only the server may *decide* and *act*. A trade has two people. Call them the **initiator** (the one who runs the command) and the **target** (the one who receives the request). The dangerous version — the one the old guide taught — let the client say "give me 50 gold from player 12." The server believed it. That is a free money printer. Here is what the server must verify itself, never trusting the client's word: - **Does the initiator actually own the item and amount they are offering?** Ask the inventory, do not trust the menu's number box. - **Are the two players close enough to trade?** Measure the distance server-side so nobody trades across the map. - **Did *both* players confirm?** A trade is an agreement, not a one-sided grab. - **Is this trade already being processed?** Lock it so a double-click cannot run the swap twice (that is how duplication bugs happen). Everything below is built around those four checks. The NUI menu is just a polite way to collect a "yes" — it never moves an item. > **ox_inventory is the item authority** > This lesson uses **ox_inventory** for item operations because it is the modern standard and its functions run server-side, which is exactly the trust boundary we want. The three calls you need: > > - `exports.ox_inventory:GetItemCount(source, item)` returns how many of `item` a player holds. Server-side. > - `exports.ox_inventory:RemoveItem(source, item, count)` removes items and returns `true`/`false`. > - `exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(source, item, count)` adds items and returns `true`/`false`. > > If your server uses qb-inventory or a framework's built-in inventory, the *shape* of the lesson is identical — only those three export names change. The security logic does not. ## Build it You will create one resource called `trade`. Make a folder `resources/[local]/trade` with this layout, then paste each file exactly. ```text trade/ fxmanifest.lua config.lua server/main.lua client/main.lua html/index.html html/style.css html/app.js ``` ### Step 1 - Manifest _Goal: The server loads your resource with the right files in the right realm._ Create `fxmanifest.lua`. The `fx_version` and `game` lines are required by every modern resource. `shared_script` loads on both realms; `server_script` only prints to the **server console**; `client_script` only prints to the player's **F8 console**. ```lua fx_version 'cerulean' game 'gta5' author 'Quasar University' description 'Player-to-player trade system' version '1.0.0' shared_script 'config.lua' server_script 'server/main.lua' client_script 'client/main.lua' ui_page 'html/index.html' files { 'html/index.html', 'html/style.css', 'html/app.js', } ``` Notice there is **no `lua54 'yes'` line**. Lua 5.4 has been the only runtime since June 2025; the directive is a dead no-op. Leaving it out is correct. ### Step 2 - Config _Goal: One place to tune the trade rules without touching logic._ Create `config.lua`. We hardcode no magic numbers in the logic; they all live here. ```lua Config = {} -- How close (in game units) the two players must be to trade. Config.MaxTradeDistance = 3.0 -- How long (seconds) a pending request stays open before it auto-expires. Config.RequestTimeout = 20 -- Most items a single trade may move. A sanity cap, not a balance rule. Config.MaxItemCount = 100 ``` ### Step 3 - Server logic — the only place a trade actually happens _Goal: A request can be created, accepted, validated, and executed safely. The client cannot move items on its own._ Create `server/main.lua`. This is the heart of the system. Read it once top to bottom, then we explain every block. ```lua -- Pending requests, keyed by the TARGET's server id. -- pending[targetId] = { from, item, count, expires } local pending = {} -- Trades currently mid-swap, keyed by initiator id, to block double-execution. local locking = {} -- Resolve the framework player so we can read their world position. -- We only need GetPlayer for the ped/coords; item ops go through ox_inventory. local function getPlayerPed(src) return GetPlayerPed(src) -- server-side native: returns the player's ped end -- Server-measured distance between two players. Never trust a client distance. local function playersAreClose(a, b) local pa = GetEntityCoords(getPlayerPed(a)) local pb = GetEntityCoords(getPlayerPed(b)) return #(pa - pb) <= Config.MaxTradeDistance end -- 1) INITIATOR runs the command. We validate, then send a request to the target. RegisterCommand('trade', function(source, args) local src = source local targetId = tonumber(args[1]) local item = args[2] local count = tonumber(args[3]) -- Validate shape first. Bad input is the most common attack and the most common typo. if not targetId or not item or not count or count <= 0 then TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', src, 'usage: /trade [id] [item] [count]', 'error') return end if count > Config.MaxItemCount then TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', src, 'amount too high', 'error') return end if targetId == src then TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', src, 'you cannot trade with yourself', 'error') return end if not GetPlayerName(targetId) then TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', src, 'that player is not online', 'error') return end if not playersAreClose(src, targetId) then TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', src, 'move closer to trade', 'error') return end -- Does the INITIATOR actually own what they claim to offer? Ask the inventory. if exports.ox_inventory:GetItemCount(src, item) < count then TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', src, 'you do not have that item', 'error') return end -- Store the offer server-side. The client never holds the source of truth. pending[targetId] = { from = src, item = item, count = count, expires = os.time() + Config.RequestTimeout, } -- Ask the TARGET to confirm. We send display data only; the server keeps the real offer. TriggerClientEvent('trade:prompt', targetId, src, item, count) TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', src, ('request sent to player %d'):format(targetId), 'inform') end, false) -- 2) TARGET clicks Accept. This is a REQUEST to finish, not the finish itself. RegisterNetEvent('trade:accept', function() local targetId = source -- source is set by the engine, NOT by the client payload local offer = pending[targetId] -- No offer, or it expired: refuse. Re-reading server state means the client cannot fake it. if not offer then TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', targetId, 'no trade to accept', 'error') return end if os.time() > offer.expires then pending[targetId] = nil TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', targetId, 'trade expired', 'error') return end local initiator = offer.from -- LOCK: if this initiator's trade is already running, bail. Stops double-accept dupes. if locking[initiator] then return end locking[initiator] = true -- Re-validate EVERYTHING at execution time. The world changed since the request. local ok = GetPlayerName(initiator) and GetPlayerName(targetId) and playersAreClose(initiator, targetId) and exports.ox_inventory:GetItemCount(initiator, offer.item) >= offer.count if not ok then pending[targetId] = nil locking[initiator] = nil TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', targetId, 'trade no longer valid', 'error') TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', initiator, 'trade failed', 'error') return end -- Remove FIRST, add SECOND. If remove fails, nothing was given. No dupes possible. local removed = exports.ox_inventory:RemoveItem(initiator, offer.item, offer.count) if not removed then pending[targetId] = nil locking[initiator] = nil TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', initiator, 'trade failed', 'error') return end local added = exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(targetId, offer.item, offer.count) if not added then -- Refund: the target's inventory was full. Give the items back to the initiator. exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(initiator, offer.item, offer.count) pending[targetId] = nil locking[initiator] = nil TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', targetId, 'your inventory is full', 'error') TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', initiator, 'trade refunded', 'error') return end -- Success. Clear state and tell both players. pending[targetId] = nil locking[initiator] = nil TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', initiator, ('you gave %dx %s'):format(offer.count, offer.item), 'success') TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', targetId, ('you received %dx %s'):format(offer.count, offer.item), 'success') end) -- 3) TARGET clicks Decline. Just drop the offer. RegisterNetEvent('trade:decline', function() local targetId = source if pending[targetId] then local initiator = pending[targetId].from pending[targetId] = nil TriggerClientEvent('trade:notify', initiator, 'request declined', 'inform') end end) -- Clean up state if a player drops so locks/offers do not leak. AddEventHandler('playerDropped', function() local src = source pending[src] = nil locking[src] = nil for targetId, offer in pairs(pending) do if offer.from == src then pending[targetId] = nil end end end) ``` ### Step 4 - Client logic — collect a yes/no and show notifications _Goal: The target sees a menu, clicks a button, and the server hears about it. The client moves zero items._ Create `client/main.lua`. The client's whole job is UI plumbing. ```lua -- The server tells THIS player they were offered a trade. Open the menu. RegisterNetEvent('trade:prompt', function(fromId, item, count) SetNuiFocus(true, true) -- give the cursor to the NUI so the player can click SendNUIMessage({ action = 'show', fromId = fromId, item = item, count = count, }) end) -- The NUI 'Accept' button calls back here. We just relay intent to the server. RegisterNUICallback('accept', function(_, cb) SetNuiFocus(false, false) TriggerServerEvent('trade:accept') -- NOTE: we send NOTHING. Server already has the offer. cb('ok') end) RegisterNUICallback('decline', function(_, cb) SetNuiFocus(false, false) TriggerServerEvent('trade:decline') cb('ok') end) -- Server-driven notifications. Uses ox_lib's notify; swap for your own if needed. RegisterNetEvent('trade:notify', function(message, kind) lib.notify({ description = message, type = kind or 'inform' }) end) ``` ### Step 5 - The NUI menu — three files _Goal: A clean accept/decline card appears, populated by the server's data, and the buttons report back._ Create `html/index.html`: ```text

Trade request

Player offers you:

x

``` Create `html/style.css`: ```text * { box-sizing: border-box; font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; } body { margin: 0; background: transparent; } .hidden { display: none; } #card { width: 320px; margin: 18vh auto 0; padding: 20px; background: #16181d; color: #f2f4f8; border-radius: 10px; text-align: center; } .offer { font-size: 1.2rem; font-weight: 600; color: #4fb0ff; } .row { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-top: 16px; } button { flex: 1; padding: 10px; border: 0; border-radius: 6px; cursor: pointer; color: #fff; font-weight: 600; } #accept { background: #1f8f4e; } #decline { background: #b23b3b; } ``` Create `html/app.js`. The fetch URL is built with `GetParentResourceName()` so it works no matter what you name the folder — never hardcode the resource name here. ```text const resource = GetParentResourceName(); function post(name, body) { return fetch(`https://${resource}/${name}`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json; charset=UTF-8' }, body: JSON.stringify(body || {}), }); } const card = document.getElementById('card'); window.addEventListener('message', (e) => { const data = e.data; if (data.action === 'show') { document.getElementById('from').textContent = data.fromId; document.getElementById('item').textContent = data.item; document.getElementById('count').textContent = data.count; card.classList.remove('hidden'); } }); document.getElementById('accept').addEventListener('click', () => { card.classList.add('hidden'); post('accept'); }); document.getElementById('decline').addEventListener('click', () => { card.classList.add('hidden'); post('decline'); }); // Let Escape decline, so the player is never stuck with a locked cursor. document.addEventListener('keyup', (ev) => { if (ev.key === 'Escape' && !card.classList.contains('hidden')) { card.classList.add('hidden'); post('decline'); } }); ``` ### Step 6 - Start it _Goal: The resource is live and the command exists._ Add `ensure trade` to your `server.cfg` (after ox_lib and ox_inventory so the exports exist when `trade` loads). Then in the **server console** (the txAdmin live console or the terminal the server runs in) type: ```text ensure trade ``` `ensure` starts the resource, or restarts it if it was already running, so you can use it again after every edit. To confirm it loaded, run `status` in that same server console and look for it in the resource list. Expected output: ``` Two players stand next to each other. `/trade` is a **chat** command, not an F8 one, so Player A (id 1) presses `T` (or `/`) to open the chat box and types: ```text /trade 2 water 3 ``` Player A sees a notification "request sent to player 2". Player B's screen shows the trade card: "Player 1 offers you: 3x water" with Accept / Decline buttons. B clicks **Accept**. Both see green notifications — A: "you gave 3x water", B: "you received 3x water". Open both inventories: 3 water moved from A to B. If A only had 2 water, the command is rejected up front with "you do not have that item" and no menu ever appears for B. ``` ## How it works — every decision, and why **Why the command lives on the server, not the client.** `RegisterCommand('trade', fn, false)` registers a chat command on the **server** realm (this file is a `server_script`). The `false` is the `restricted` flag — `false` means any player may run it, which is correct for a trade since both sides are ordinary players. State-changing *admin* commands should pass `true` and gate behind ACE, but a normal trade is not an admin action. The key point: because the handler runs server-side, the `source` argument is the real player id set by the engine, not something the client typed. **Why we validate shape before anything else.** The first `if` block rejects missing or non-numeric arguments and zero/negative counts. A negative count is a classic exploit attempt ("give -5 means take 5"). Catching it here means the rest of the function can assume clean input. **Why distance is measured on the server.** `playersAreClose` reads both peds' coordinates with `GetEntityCoords` server-side and compares with `#(a - b)`, the vector-length operator. If we asked the client "are you close?" a cheater would always answer yes. The server has its own copy of where every ped is, so it cannot be lied to. **Why `GetItemCount` is the source of truth, not the menu.** The old design let the client send the item name *and the amount*. That means a cheater types `999` and the server hands over 999. Here, the menu's number box never reaches the swap. The server asks `ox_inventory:GetItemCount(src, item)` and only proceeds if the initiator genuinely holds that many. We check it **twice**: once when the request is made, and again at execution, because the player could have dropped the items in the seconds between. **Why the offer is stored server-side keyed by target id.** `pending[targetId] = { from, item, count, expires }` keeps the real offer on the machine the client cannot touch. When the target clicks Accept, the client sends `trade:accept` with **no payload at all**. The server looks up the offer it already saved. This is the single most important pattern in the lesson: if the acceptance carried the item and count, a cheater would just send `trade:accept` with `gold, 1000000`. By carrying nothing, there is nothing to forge. **Why `source` is trustworthy inside the net event.** In `RegisterNetEvent('trade:accept', ...)`, `source` is set by the FiveM engine to the id of the player who fired the event. The client cannot overwrite it. That is why `local targetId = source` is safe and why we never accept an id from the event arguments. **Why the lock exists.** `locking[initiator]` is the anti-duplication guard. Picture the target spamming Accept, or a laggy client firing the event twice. Without the lock, two executions could both pass validation before either removes the items, and the target receives the items twice while the initiator loses them once — a dupe. Setting `locking[initiator] = true` before any inventory change and clearing it on every exit path means a second event finds the lock set and returns immediately. **Why remove happens before add.** `RemoveItem` returns `true`/`false`. We remove from the initiator first and only add to the target if the removal succeeded. If we added first and the remove failed, the item would exist in two inventories — a dupe. Remove-then-add can only ever destroy or move items, never multiply them. And if the target's inventory is full so `AddItem` returns `false`, we **refund** by adding the items back to the initiator, so no one loses anything. **Why we re-validate at execution time.** Between the request and the click, players move, disconnect, and drop items. So before the swap we re-check both players are online, still close, and the initiator still owns the goods. A request is a snapshot; execution must trust the present, not the past. **Why `playerDropped` cleans up.** If a player disconnects mid-trade, their entry in `pending` and `locking` would linger. The handler wipes their offers and lock so a future player who reuses that server id is not blocked by a ghost lock. **Why `GetParentResourceName()` builds the fetch URL.** In `app.js`, NUI callbacks must POST to `https:///`. Hardcoding the folder name breaks the moment someone renames it. `GetParentResourceName()` returns the real name at runtime, so the URL is always right. > **Framework swap cheat-sheet** > The logic above never calls a framework for items — ox_inventory handles that and works under QBCore, ESX, and Qbox alike. You only touch the framework if you want to gate trades by job, or read a character name for a fancier notification. The accessors, for reference: > > - **QBCore**: `local QBCore = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()` then `QBCore.Functions.GetPlayer(src)`. Character first name is `Player.PlayerData.charinfo.firstname`; the account name is `Player.PlayerData.name` (both exist — do not confuse them). > - **Qbox**: `exports.qbx_core:GetPlayer(src)`, same `PlayerData` shape. > - **ESX Legacy**: `local xPlayer = ESX.GetPlayerFromId(src)`. > > If you wanted to trade *money* instead of items, that is where the framework matters — QBCore uses `Player.Functions.RemoveMoney('cash', amount, 'trade')` / `AddMoney`, and ESX uses `xPlayer.removeAccountMoney('money', amount)` / `addAccountMoney`. Same server-authority rules apply: validate the balance server-side, lock, remove-then-add. ## Failure modes and recovery ## Common errors and fixes - `Command does nothing, server console prints `attempt to index a nil value (field 'ox_inventory')` or `No such export`.` -> ox_inventory is not started, or `trade` starts before it. In server.cfg put `ensure ox_lib` and `ensure ox_inventory` ABOVE `ensure trade`, then run `ensure trade` again in the server console. - ``/trade` says nothing at all and no notification appears.` -> `lib.notify` is undefined because ox_lib is not loaded for the client. Confirm ox_lib is ensured, and that you did not accidentally put the notify code in the server file. client_script prints to F8; check the F8 console for a red Lua error. - `Menu opens but Accept does nothing.` -> Open the F8 console with NUI dev tools (`nui_devTools` in F8 while the server is in dev mode) and watch the Network tab. A 404 on the POST means the fetch URL is wrong — confirm `app.js` uses `GetParentResourceName()` and that `RegisterNUICallback('accept', ...)` matches the string you POST to. - `Items disappear from the initiator but never reach the target.` -> The target's inventory was full so AddItem returned false. The refund branch should have returned them — if it did not, you removed the refund block. Restore the `if not added then ... AddItem(initiator ...) end` branch. - `A player reports duplicated items after spamming Accept.` -> Your lock is missing or cleared too early. `locking[initiator] = true` must be set BEFORE the first inventory call and cleared on EVERY return path, including the failure branches. - ``you do not have that item` even though you do.` -> Item name mismatch. ox_inventory item names are the database key (e.g. `water`, not `Water` or `bottle_of_water`). Check the exact name in your ox_inventory items list and type it lowercase. - `Trade works across the whole map.` -> You are not measuring distance, or you compared client-reported positions. Confirm `playersAreClose` uses server-side `GetEntityCoords` on both peds and that you call it both at request and execution. ## Checkpoint ### Check yourself: The target clicks Accept. What data does the client send to the server, and why is that the most important security choice in the whole system? The client sends **nothing** — `TriggerServerEvent('trade:accept')` carries no item, no count, no player id. The server already stored the real offer in `pending[targetId]` when the request was made, and it reads the firing player from the engine-set `source`. This matters because anything the client sends, a cheater can forge. If acceptance carried the item and amount, a modder would simply fire `trade:accept` with `gold, 999999`. By making the acceptance an empty "yes" and keeping the offer server-side, there is nothing to tamper with. The client asks; the server decides and acts. ## Exercise Right now a trade is one-directional: the initiator gives, the target receives, and only the target confirms. Turn it into a **two-sided trade** where both players put up an item and both must accept before anything moves. Concretely, modify the code so that: 1. The `/trade` command no longer executes anything — it only opens a "trade window" between the two players and records the initiator's offered item/count in server state (e.g. `session[pairId] = { a = {...}, b = {...}, aReady = false, bReady = false }`). 2. The target gets a command or NUI input to set *their* offered item/count, which the server validates with `GetItemCount` exactly like the initiator's. 3. Each player has a **Ready** button. The swap only runs when `aReady` and `bReady` are both true. 4. The swap moves items in **both** directions, still remove-then-add, still behind a single lock, and if either `AddItem` fails the **entire** trade rolls back (refund both sides). The point of the exercise: notice that every new piece of data the target supplies (their item, their count, their ready state) must be validated server-side at execution time, and that "both ready" is just a second confirmation gate built from the same pattern you already used once. When you can roll back a two-leg swap with no dupes, you understand server-authoritative state. ## Recap - The client asks; only the server decides and acts. Every economy script lives or dies by this line. - Store the offer server-side and make acceptance an empty 'yes' — there is nothing to forge if the client carries no data. - Validate ownership, distance, and online status with server-side reads, both when the request is made and again at execution. - A per-trade lock plus remove-before-add makes duplication impossible; refund on a failed add so nobody loses items. - ox_inventory's GetItemCount/RemoveItem/AddItem run server-side and work under QBCore, ESX, and Qbox; the framework only matters if you trade money instead. - NUI is just a way to collect a click — build fetch URLs with GetParentResourceName() and never let the menu's numbers reach the swap. ## References - ox_inventory — exports (GetItemCount / AddItem / RemoveItem): https://overextended.dev/ox_inventory - ox_lib — lib.notify: https://overextended.dev/ox_lib/Modules/Notify/Client - FiveM — NUI callbacks & GetParentResourceName: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-manual/nui-development/ - FiveM — RegisterCommand & server events: https://docs.fivem.net/docs/scripting-reference/runtimes/lua/ - QBCore — player & money functions: https://docs.qbcore.org/qbcore-documentation/qbcore-resources/qb-core - ESX — xPlayer accounts: https://documentation.esx-framework.org/

title: C2 · Creating items for ox and qs inventory · Quasar Academy
ai:
An item in a FiveM inventory is not magic. It is one row in a Lua table that you write by hand. Get the shape right and the inventory draws an icon, shows a tooltip, plays an animation on use, and lets you give it with one export. Get the shape wrong and the slot is blank, the item does not stack, or `AddItem` rejects it. By the end you will have a working `water` item in **ox_inventory**, you will give it to yourself from the server console, and you will be able to read the **qs-inventory** version and name every difference. ### What this lesson covers - You'll build: A real `water` item in ox_inventory: the items.lua entry, the PNG icon, a restart to load it, and a server command that hands it to a player after a capacity check. Then the same item the qs-inventory way, side by side. - Time: ~25 minutes - You need: A local server already running ox_inventory (with ox_lib and oxmysql ensured before it), a text editor, and one PNG you can rename. If you have not installed an inventory yet, do that first, then come back. - You'll learn: The ox_inventory items.lua entry shape field by field -> where the icon file goes -> how to load a new item -> how to give it safely with AddItem after CanCarryItem -> and how the qs-inventory shape and AddItem differ ### Before you start - I can find my server resources folder and open data files in a text editor. - I can edit server.cfg and restart one resource from the console. - I know ox_lib and oxmysql are Overextended community resources my inventory depends on. - I can read txAdmin Live Console output. > **Build the proof, then understand it** > This lesson adds one real item named `water` to ox_inventory, drops its icon, restarts the resource, and gives it to a player with a server command. Follow the file paths, the table fields, the icon filename, and the restart command exactly. Once the item is in your hands in game, the next section takes the table apart key by key, and the last section shows the qs-inventory equivalent so you can work in either inventory. ## Two inventories, one idea Before you touch a file, fix one thing in your head. **ox_inventory** and **qs-inventory** are different resources made by different people, and they store items in different files with different field names. But the idea is identical in both: an item is a key in a Lua table, and the value is a config table that describes the item. - **ox_inventory** is Overextended's free, open-source, slot-based inventory. Items live in `data/items.lua`. You can read its source on GitHub. - **qs-inventory** is Quasar Store's paid, encrypted inventory (it supports ESX, QBCore, and Qbox). Items live in `shared/items.lua` in the QBCore-style shape. There is no public source to read, only the docs. This lesson builds the item in ox_inventory first because it is free and open, so you can verify every claim against the real source. Then it maps the same item onto qs-inventory. > **Which file am I editing?** > ox_inventory uses `data/items.lua`. qs-inventory uses `shared/items.lua`. They are not interchangeable. Copy-pasting an ox item config into the qs file (or the reverse) is the number one reason a new item shows up blank or refuses to stack. We cover the exact field differences in the last section. ## Build it in ox_inventory ### Step 1 - Open data/items.lua _Goal: You are looking at the real item table._ Inside your ox_inventory resource, open this file: ```text resources/[inventory]/ox_inventory/ data/ items.lua <- you edit this web/ images/ <- icons live here (filename = item key) ``` The file is one big Lua table that is `return`ed. Every item is a key in that table. The top and bottom look like this: ```lua return { -- each item is a key here, for example ['water'] = { ... } } ``` The item **key** (the string in the brackets) is the canonical name of the item. It is the exact string you will use everywhere else: in the database, in shops, and in every `AddItem` call. Choose it carefully and keep it lowercase. ### Step 2 - Add the water item _Goal: A new item entry exists in items.lua._ Add this entry inside the returned table, alongside the items already there. This is a real, minimal usable item: ```lua ['water'] = { label = 'Water', weight = 500, stack = true, close = true, description = 'A refreshing bottle of water', consume = 1, client = { status = { thirst = 200000 }, anim = { dict = 'mp_player_intdrink', clip = 'loop_bottle' }, prop = { model = `prop_ld_flow_bottle`, pos = vec3(0.03, 0.03, 0.02), rot = vec3(0.0, 0.0, -1.5) }, usetime = 2500, cancel = true } } -- Then drop the icon at web/images/water.png (filename = item key). ``` Mind the commas. Lua table entries are separated by commas, so if `water` is not the last entry in the table, it needs a comma after the closing brace. We explain every field in the next section. ### Step 3 - Drop the icon PNG _Goal: The inventory has art to draw for the slot._ The icon is a PNG file, and its filename must match the item key exactly, lowercase, with `.png`. The `water` key needs `water.png`. Put it here: ```text resources/[inventory]/ox_inventory/web/images/water.png ``` > **web/images, not data/images** > Newer ox_inventory builds read icons from `web/images/` (the NUI build folder). Some very old guides say `data/images/`. Use `web/images/`. A missing or misnamed file does not throw an error. It just draws a blank slot, which is easy to miss. You can picture the result as a single inventory slot:
water.png
Water
A refreshing bottle of water
0.50 kg
### Step 4 - Restart ox_inventory to load the item _Goal: The new item definition is live._ A running server caches item definitions. It will keep rejecting `water` until you reload. In the server console (txAdmin Live Console) run: ```text restart ox_inventory ``` For reference, ox_inventory must be ensured after its dependencies in `server.cfg`: ```cfg # Load order: ox_lib and oxmysql must start BEFORE ox_inventory ensure oxmysql ensure ox_lib ensure ox_inventory ``` ### Step 5 - Give the item to a player and check capacity first _Goal: The item lands in a player's inventory the safe way._ Now hand the item to a player. You need a tiny resource of your own to hold this command. Create one like this: 1. Make a folder `resources/[local]/qu_items/` (the `[local]` folder is just an organizational bracket folder; any subfolder works). 2. Inside it create two files: `fxmanifest.lua` and `server.lua`. 3. Put this in `fxmanifest.lua` (this is what tells FXServer the file is a SERVER script): ```lua fx_version 'cerulean' game 'gta5' server_script 'server.lua' ``` 4. Put the command below in `server.lua`. It checks capacity first, then adds, then reports failure if it failed: ```lua RegisterCommand('givewater', function(source, args) local src = source > 0 and source or tonumber(args[1]) if not src or not GetPlayerName(src) then print('Usage from console: givewater ') return end if exports.ox_inventory:CanCarryItem(src, 'water', 1) then local success, response = exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(src, 'water', 1) if success then print(('[qu_items] added water to player %s'):format(src)) else print('AddItem failed: ' .. tostring(response)) -- invalid_item | inventory_full | invalid_inventory end else print(('[qu_items] player %s cannot carry water'):format(src)) end end, true) ``` Add your new resource to `server.cfg` (anywhere AFTER `ensure ox_inventory`) so it starts: ```cfg ensure qu_items ``` Restart the server (or `ensure qu_items` from the console), join the server so you have an inventory, find your server id (type `status` in the FXServer or txAdmin Live Console — it lists every connected player with their id, name and identifier), then run `givewater 1` from the server or txAdmin Live Console, replacing `1` with your id. The `true` at the end of `RegisterCommand` makes the command RESTRICTED. This matters for safety: a command that hands out items must never be runnable by ordinary players, or anyone can spawn unlimited items and wreck your economy. The server console can always run it; an in-game player can only run it if they hold the `command.givewater` ACE permission, which you grant explicitly in server.cfg, e.g. `add_ace group.admin command.givewater allow`. Leave it restricted (`true`) until you have set that up. Expected output: ``` [qu_items] added water to player 1 ``` If the item is not registered yet, you will instead see the failure path fire: Expected output: ``` AddItem failed: invalid_item ``` ## How it works You have a real item and you gave it to yourself. Now take the table apart so the next item you write, you are deciding, not copying. Two things matter: the fields on the entry, and the export that gives it. ### The item key is the canonical name ```lua ['water'] = { -- fields } ``` The bracketed string `'water'` is the item key, and it is the single source of truth for this item's name. The database row uses it, shops use it, and every `AddItem` and `RemoveItem` call uses this exact string. The icon file is named after it too. If you ever rename it, you rename it everywhere or things silently break. Keep it lowercase with no spaces. ### The top-level fields describe the item itself ```lua label = 'Water', weight = 500, stack = true, close = true, description = 'A refreshing bottle of water', consume = 1, ``` Read these one at a time, because three of them have traps. - **label** is the display name shown in the UI. The key (`water`) is the internal name; the label (`Water`) is what the player sees. They do not have to match. - **weight** is in grams as an integer, not kilograms. `500` means half a kilogram. This is the trap that makes one item fill the whole bag: if you write `500` thinking it is grams but the rest of your items are tuned as if it were something else, the magnitude is off. Stay consistent in grams. - **stack** defaults to `true`, so you can leave it off and the item stacks. Set it to `false` for unique items that must carry their own metadata, like phones, weapons, and IDs. If you leave a phone stackable, two phones merge into one slot and lose their per-item data. - **close** (default `true`) closes the inventory UI when the item is used. For something you drink and put away, `true` feels right. - **description** is the tooltip text on hover. Optional, but a blank tooltip looks unfinished. - **consume** is the subtle one. `1` removes one whole item per use. `0` means the item is never consumed on use, which is what you want for a phone or a radio. A decimal like `0.3` does **not** remove an item. It drains durability or a percentage instead. So `consume = 1` here means each drink uses up one bottle. ### The client table is the using player's experience ```lua client = { status = { thirst = 200000 }, anim = { dict = 'mp_player_intdrink', clip = 'loop_bottle' }, prop = { model = `prop_ld_flow_bottle`, pos = vec3(0.03, 0.03, 0.02), rot = vec3(0.0, 0.0, -1.5) }, usetime = 2500, cancel = true } ``` Everything inside `client` runs on the machine of the player using the item. - **status** is an ESX-style need adjustment. `{ thirst = 200000 }` raises the thirst stat when the bottle is drunk. A burger would use `{ hunger = 200000 }` instead. - **anim** is the animation played during use. Here it is a table with a `dict` (the animation dictionary) and a `clip` (the clip name inside it). ox_inventory also accepts a string preset like `anim = 'eating'`, but the explicit table gives you control. - **prop** is the object attached to the hand during use, with a `model`, a `pos` offset, and a `rot` rotation. Notice the backticks around `` `prop_ld_flow_bottle` `` . Those backticks are a CfxLua shortcut that hashes the model name at compile time, so you do not call `GetHashKey` yourself. - **usetime** is how long the progress bar runs, in milliseconds. `2500` is two and a half seconds. - **cancel** set to `true` lets the player cancel the use mid-progress-bar. ### The export that gives the item, and why you check first ```lua if exports.ox_inventory:CanCarryItem(src, 'water', 1) then local success, response = exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(src, 'water', 1) ``` `AddItem` is the server export that puts an item into an inventory. Read its full signature from the docs: ```lua exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(inv, item, count, metadata, slot) ``` - **inv** is the target. The player `source` works as the inventory id. - **item** is the item-name string, the same key from items.lua. - **count** is how many. - **metadata** is an optional table (durability, a serial number, custom data) or string. - **slot** is an optional target slot. It returns two values: `success` (a boolean) and `response` (a string when it fails). The failure responses are worth memorizing: `invalid_item` means the name is not in items.lua, `inventory_full` means no room, and `invalid_inventory` means the target source is bad. `CanCarryItem(inv, item, count, metadata)` returns a boolean and is your pre-flight check. You call it before `AddItem` so you never try to add something the player has no room for. Skipping it is not catastrophic (`AddItem` returns `inventory_full` anyway), but checking first is the clean pattern and lets you tell the player why nothing happened. Here is the rhythm: register the item in items.lua, restart so the definition loads, check capacity, then add. That order is the whole job. ### Check yourself: You added ['juice'] to items.lua and saved, but exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(src, 'juice', 1) returns success = false with response 'invalid_item'. The spelling is correct. What did you forget? You forgot to restart the resource. ox_inventory reads item definitions when it starts and caches them. Editing and saving `data/items.lua` does nothing to a running server until you `restart ox_inventory` (or restart the server). Until then, `AddItem` does not know `juice` exists, so it returns `invalid_item`. Restart, then run the command again. This is different from a misspelled key, which also returns `invalid_item` but stays broken after a restart. So restart first, and only then suspect the spelling. ### Adding metadata or a specific slot When you do need metadata, like a serial number on a phone, you pass it as the fourth argument and the slot as the fifth: ```lua -- AddItem with metadata + specific slot exports.ox_inventory:AddItem(src, 'phone', 1, { serial = 'ABC123' }, 5) ``` Remember the order for ox_inventory: **metadata comes before slot**. Hold that thought, because qs-inventory reverses it, and that reversal is the single easiest way to break a copied snippet. ### Reading and removing You will not only add items. The matching exports read and remove: ```lua -- How many does the player have? local count = exports.ox_inventory:GetItemCount(src, 'water') -- Remove 1 water from player `src` local ok, response = exports.ox_inventory:RemoveItem(src, 'water', 1) -- response on failure: not_enough_items | invalid_item | invalid_inventory ``` `RemoveItem` takes the same first five arguments as `AddItem`.
