Go to https://runtime.fivem.net/artifacts/fivem/build_server_windows/master/

Download the **latest recommended** build (green label, not the top "Latest" which may be unstable). Extract the zip to:

```text
C:\FXServer\
```

Inside you should see `FXServer.exe`, `citizen/` folder, and other files.

Expected output:

```
C:\FXServer\FXServer.exe exists. Running it starts a bare server.
```

### Step 3 - Get your license key
_Goal: You have a Cfx.re license key._

Go to https://portal.cfx.re/ → Servers → Create Server. Give it a name. Copy the license key — it starts with `licensekey:`. Save it somewhere you can paste from. You'll need it when txAdmin asks for it.

Don't create a server.cfg yet — the txAdmin recipe will write one for you with all the right settings for QBCore.

### Step 4 - Start the server and pick the QBCore recipe
_Goal: txAdmin loads, QBCore is deployed, and the server is online._

Run `FXServer.exe`. On first boot, txAdmin opens at `http://localhost:40120`. Set a username and password. When it asks for a recipe, search for **QBCore** and select the official QBCore Framework recipe.

txAdmin downloads QBCore, creates the `server.cfg`, sets up the database, and starts all resources. Paste your license key when prompted.

Expected output:

```
txAdmin dashboard shows green status. The server is joinable at `127.0.0.1:30120`.
```

### Step 5 - Connect HeidiSQL to your database
_Goal: You can browse the QBCore database._

Download HeidiSQL from https://www.heidisql.com. Open it, click New Session:

- Host: `127.0.0.1`
- User: `root`
- Password: (leave blank unless you set one in XAMPP)
- Port: `3306`

Click Open. You should see the QBCore database with tables like `players`, `vehicles`, `items`.

Expected output:

```
HeidiSQL shows the QBCore database. You can browse player data, items, and server configuration tables.
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| MySQL won't start in XAMPP | Port 3306 already in use | Click Config → my.ini → change port, or stop the other MySQL instance |
| `FXServer.exe` crashes immediately | Missing Visual C++ Redistributable | Install VC_redist.x64.exe from Microsoft |
| txAdmin recipe fails to download | Firewall blocking or GitHub rate limit | Wait a few minutes and retry. If persistent, download QBCore manually |
| `ensure` command not found in HeidiSQL console | HeidiSQL doesn't run FiveM commands | Use txAdmin Live Console for FiveM commands, HeidiSQL for SQL only |

title: Introduction to GitHub for FiveM developers · Quasar Academy
ai:
GitHub is how the FiveM community shares resources, collaborates on scripts, and backs up work. This is the absolute minimum you need: create a repo, push your resource, clone it somewhere else, and understand the three-command loop you'll run every day.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A GitHub account with a repository containing your FiveM resource, ready to clone and push changes.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A computer with internet, Git installed, and a resource you want to version-control.
- You'll learn: What Git and GitHub are, creating a repository, cloning, committing, pushing, pulling, and the basic workflow every developer uses every day.

### Before you start
- I have a FiveM resource folder with at least one script file.
- I have installed Git (https://git-scm.com/downloads).
- I have created a GitHub account (https://github.com/signup).

> **Git vs GitHub**
> **Git** is the version control tool on your computer. **GitHub** is the website that hosts your code online and lets others see it. You use Git commands in the terminal; GitHub is where the code lives on the internet.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Create a repository on GitHub
_Goal: An empty repo is ready to receive your code._

Go to https://github.com/new. Name it `my-fivem-script`. Leave it public or private. Do NOT check "Add a README" — you already have files to push. Click **Create repository**.

GitHub shows you a page with commands. Copy the URL: `https://github.com/YOURNAME/my-fivem-script.git`

### Step 2 - Initialize Git in your resource folder
_Goal: Git is tracking your files locally._

Open a terminal in your resource folder:

```bash
cd resources/my-script
git init
git add .
git commit -m "First commit: my FiveM resource"
```

- `git init` — creates a hidden `.git` folder that tracks everything
- `git add .` — stages every file for commit
- `git commit -m "..."` — saves a snapshot with a message

Expected output:

```
Terminal prints something like `[main c1a2b3] First commit: my FiveM resource` with a list of files.
```

### Step 3 - Push to GitHub
_Goal: Your code is online, visible on github.com._

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOURNAME/my-fivem-script.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
```

- `remote add` — tells Git where GitHub is
- `branch -M main` — names your branch "main" (modern default)
- `push` — sends your commits to GitHub

Refresh your GitHub repo page. Your files are there.

Expected output:

```
github.com/YOURNAME/my-fivem-script shows all your resource files.
```

### Step 4 - The daily workflow: edit, commit, push
_Goal: You know the three commands you'll use every day._

Every time you make changes:

```bash
git add .                          # stage changes
git commit -m "Fixed the spawn bug"  # save a snapshot
git push                            # send to GitHub
```

That's it. This loop is 90% of what you'll ever do with Git.

To get your code on another computer:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/YOURNAME/my-fivem-script.git
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `fatal: not a git repository` | You're not inside the Git folder | `cd` into the correct directory first |
| `Permission denied (publickey)` | SSH key not set up | Use HTTPS URL instead: `https://github.com/...` |
| `Everything up-to-date` after push but files not on GitHub | You forgot to `git add` and `git commit` | Always `git add .` then `git commit` before `git push` |
| `failed to push — remote contains work` | Someone else pushed to the repo | `git pull` first, resolve conflicts, then push |

title: What is SQL? Your first queries · Quasar Academy
ai:
Every FiveM server uses a database — for player data, vehicles, inventory, and money. SQL is the language you talk to it with. This lesson covers the four commands you'll use every day: SELECT to read, INSERT to create, UPDATE to change, and DELETE to remove. Four commands. That's it.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: Your first SQL queries: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — the four commands every database interaction uses.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: HeidiSQL or phpMyAdmin connected to a MySQL database (see the QBCore server lesson).
- You'll learn: What a relational database is, what tables/rows/columns are, the four SQL command types (CRUD), and how to write safe parameterized queries.

### Before you start
- I have a MySQL database running (via XAMPP or standalone).
- I have HeidiSQL or phpMyAdmin open and connected.
- I have read the server setup lesson or know what a database is.

> **SQL is just asking questions**
> Think of a database as a spreadsheet. **Tables** are the sheets. **Rows** are the entries. **Columns** are the fields. SQL is how you ask "show me all players with money over 1000" — and it gives you exactly those rows.

## Build it

### Step 1 - SELECT — read data
_Goal: You can fetch any data from any table._

```sql
-- Get all players
SELECT * FROM players;

-- Get only the name and money columns
SELECT name, money FROM players;

-- Get players with money over 5000
SELECT name, money FROM players WHERE money > 5000;

-- Get the top 5 richest players
SELECT name, money FROM players ORDER BY money DESC LIMIT 5;
```

- `*` means "all columns"
- `WHERE` filters rows
- `ORDER BY` sorts (DESC = highest first)
- `LIMIT` caps the number of results

Expected output:

```
HeidiSQL shows a grid of results matching your query.
```

### Step 2 - INSERT — create new data
_Goal: You can add rows to any table._

```sql
-- Add a new player
INSERT INTO players (name, money, job)
VALUES ('NewPlayer', 500, 'unemployed');

-- Add multiple rows at once
INSERT INTO items (name, label, weight)
VALUES
    ('bread', 'Bread', 1),
    ('water', 'Water Bottle', 1),
    ('medkit', 'Medkit', 2);
```

Column names in parentheses. Values in the same order. String values need single quotes.

Expected output:

```
`SELECT * FROM players` now shows 'NewPlayer' with 500 money.
```

### Step 3 - UPDATE — change existing data
_Goal: You can modify any row._

```sql
-- Give a player more money
UPDATE players SET money = 1000 WHERE name = 'NewPlayer';

-- Give everyone 100 bonus (be careful with no WHERE!)
UPDATE players SET money = money + 100;
```

**Always use WHERE** unless you mean to update every row. Without WHERE, `UPDATE players SET money = 0` wipes everyone's money.

Expected output:

```
'NewPlayer' now has 1000 money instead of 500.
```

### Step 4 - DELETE — remove data
_Goal: You can delete rows (carefully)._

```sql
-- Remove a specific player
DELETE FROM players WHERE name = 'NewPlayer';

-- Remove all players with 0 money
DELETE FROM players WHERE money = 0;
```

**Never run DELETE without WHERE** unless you want to empty the entire table.

Expected output:

```
'NewPlayer' is gone from the players table.
```

## Security: parameterized queries

Never put user input directly into SQL. Use `?` placeholders:

```lua server.lua
-- SAFE: parameterized query
MySQL.query('SELECT * FROM players WHERE name = ?', { playerName })

-- DANGEROUS: string concatenation (SQL injection)
MySQL.query('SELECT * FROM players WHERE name = \'' .. playerName .. '\'')
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Table 'qbcore.players' doesn't exist` | Wrong table name or database not selected | Check HeidiSQL — click your database first, then run the query |
| `Syntax error near 'WHERE'` | Comma, quote, or parenthesis missing | Check every quote is closed and commas are between items |
| Deleted all rows accidentally | Forgot WHERE clause | Immediately restore from backup. Always backup before DELETE |

title: How to make your first FiveM server · Quasar Academy
ai:
You don't need XAMPP, a framework, or a database for your first server. Five steps: download, license, config, start, join. This is the fastest path to seeing your own server in the FiveM browser list. Everything else (QBCore, ESX, resources, SQL) comes after you confirm the base works.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A working FiveM server you can join from the same PC — the absolute minimum path from zero to seeing the 'ready' message.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A Windows PC with GTA V installed, a Cfx.re account, and 10GB free disk space.
- You'll learn: Download artifacts, get a license key, write a minimal server.cfg, start the server, and join it — the five steps that turn a blank folder into a joinable server.

### Before you start
- I own GTA V on Steam, Epic, or Rockstar Launcher (updated and launched at least once).
- I have a Cfx.re account at portal.cfx.re.
- I am on Windows 10 or 11.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Download FiveM server artifacts
_Goal: FXServer.exe is on your computer._

Go to https://runtime.fivem.net/artifacts/fivem/build_server_windows/master/

Download the **Recommended** build (green label, NOT the top "Latest"). Extract the zip to:

```text
C:\FXServer\
```

Expected contents: `FXServer.exe`, `citizen/` folder, `.dll` files.

Expected output:

```
Double-clicking `FXServer.exe` opens a console window, then txAdmin starts.
```

### Step 2 - Get your license key
_Goal: You have the one mandatory key._

Go to https://portal.cfx.re/ → **Servers** → **Create Server**. Enter a name. Copy the generated key.

The key looks like: `licensekey:cfxk_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx`

This is required even for local/single-player servers. Without it, the server won't start.

### Step 3 - Write a minimal server.cfg
_Goal: The server has a name, endpoints, and a license._

Create `server.cfg` in `C:\FXServer\`:

```bash server.cfg
# How players reach your server
endpoint_add_tcp "0.0.0.0:30120"
endpoint_add_udp "0.0.0.0:30120"

# Your license key from portal.cfx.re
sv_licenseKey "your_license_key_here"

# What players see in the browser list
sv_hostname "My First FiveM Server"

# Enable OneSync (required for modern servers)
onesync on

# Maximum players
sv_maxclients 32
```

Six lines. That's it. You don't need tags, Discord links, or resource lists yet.

### Step 4 - Start the server
_Goal: The server console shows it's running._

Double-click `FXServer.exe`. The console window opens. You'll see:

- "Validating license key" → "License key OK"
- txAdmin starting at `localhost:40120`
- "Server ready" message after ~30 seconds

Don't worry about txAdmin right now. Just watch the console until it says the server is ready.

Expected output:

```
Console prints: `[txAdmin] Server is ready.`
```

### Step 5 - Join your server
_Goal: You're standing in Los Santos on your own server._

Open FiveM. Press F8 to open console. Type:

```text
connect 127.0.0.1:30120
```

Or if you want friends on your local network to join:

```text
connect 192.168.x.x
```

(Replace with your PC's local IP — find it with `ipconfig` in Command Prompt.)

Expected output:

```
You load into Los Santos. The server name appears in the bottom-right corner. You did it.
```

## Next steps

Your server is running but empty — no resources, no scripts, no framework. From here:

- **Understand your server.cfg** → see [server.cfg, annotated line by line](/lessons/server-cfg-annotated)
- **Organize your folders** → see [Organize your server folders](/lessons/organize-server-folders)
- **Add QBCore or ESX** → see the QBCore + txAdmin lesson
- **Add your first resource** → see Create a basic FiveM script
- **Let friends join** → port forward 30120 on your router, give them your public IP
- **Secure it** → see the server hardening lesson

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `FXServer.exe` window opens then closes instantly | Missing VC++ Redistributable | Download and install VC_redist.x64.exe from Microsoft |
| `Invalid license key` | Key copied wrong or not activated | Re-copy from portal.cfx.re, make sure there are no extra spaces |
| `connect localhost` does nothing | FiveM client not running or server not ready | Wait for "Server is ready" in the console, then try again |
| `Failed to start resource 'txAdmin'` | Port 40120 in use | Close other programs using that port, or restart your PC |

title: Understanding and fixing FiveM errors · Quasar Academy
ai:
Errors are not random noise — they are structured reports telling you exactly what broke, which resource, which file, and which line. The skill is learning to read them. This lesson teaches the debugging method, not just a list of fixes, so you can diagnose errors you've never seen before.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A systematic debugging method: read the error → find the named resource and line → diagnose the cause → apply the fix → test.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A FiveM server with txAdmin Live Console access, and a basic understanding of server.cfg and resource structure.
- You'll learn: How to read a FiveM error (resource name, file path, line number), the difference between server and client errors, common error categories, and a step-by-step debugging workflow.

### Before you start
- I can read txAdmin Live Console or the FXServer console window.
- I have at least one resource loaded on my server.
- I can open and edit script files.

> **The #1 debugging mistake**
> Beginners see a red error line and immediately start changing code at random. Don't. Read the error first. It names the resource, the file, and the line. Fix that one spot, restart, and test. If it works, stop. If not, read the next error.

## The debugging method

Every error follows the same structure. Learn to read it:

```text
[    script:my_resource] SCRIPT ERROR: @my_resource/client.lua:42:
attempt to index a nil value (global 'MyVariable')
```

Break it down:
- `[script:my_resource]` — the resource that crashed
- `client.lua:42` — the file and line number
- `attempt to index a nil value` — what went wrong
- `(global 'MyVariable')` — the specific thing that was nil

## Build it

### Step 1 - Find where the error happened
_Goal: You know the exact resource, file, and line._

Open txAdmin Live Console (or your server console). Scroll to the red error lines. Look for the pattern:

```text
[    script:RESOURCE_NAME] SCRIPT ERROR: @RESOURCE_NAME/FILE:LINENUMBER:
```

Memorize this pattern. Every FiveM error includes it. If you see `RESOURCE_NAME` but can't find that resource folder, check:
- Is the resource actually in `resources/`?
- Is it in `server.cfg` with `ensure RESOURCE_NAME`?
- Is the folder name spelled exactly as shown?

### Step 2 - Identify the error type
_Goal: You know the category of the problem._

Common error patterns and what they mean:

| Error text | What it means | What to check |
|---|---|---|
| `attempt to index a nil value` | A variable is nil when you expected a table/object | Did you define it? Did a function return nil? |
| `attempt to call a nil value` | You're calling something as a function that isn't one | Is the function name spelled right? Is the resource that provides it running? |
| `SCRIPT ERROR: citizen:/scripting/lua/scheduler.lua` | A thread crashed in the scheduler | An infinite loop or a non-yielding thread — check your `Citizen.Wait()` calls |
| `Could not find resource` | The resource folder is missing or spelled wrong | Check `resources/[category]/your_resource` — case matters |
| `Invalid FXServer artifact` | Wrong artifact version or corrupt download | Re-download the Recommended build |

### Step 3 - Client vs server errors
_Goal: You know which console to check._

**Server errors** appear in the FXServer console or txAdmin Live Console. They always start with `[script:resource_name]`.

**Client errors** appear in the player's F8 console. They also start with `[script:resource_name]` but show on the client side only.

A client crash with no server error → the problem is in `client.lua`. A server crash with no client error → the problem is in `server.lua`. Both crashing → check `shared.lua` or the event that crosses the boundary.

To see client errors: open F8 in-game and look for red lines. To see server errors: watch the txAdmin Live Console tab.

### Step 4 - Fix one thing, test, repeat
_Goal: The error is gone and nothing new broke._

The method:

1. Read the error → find the file and line
2. Open that file at that line
3. Understand what that line does
4. Make ONE change
5. Restart the resource (`restart my_resource`)
6. Check if the error is gone
7. If yes → test the feature. If no → read the new error, go to step 1

Never change five things at once. If you fix the error but break something else, you won't know which change caused it.

Expected output:

```
The console shows no new errors. The feature that was broken works correctly.
```

## When you're truly stuck

1. **Read the error out loud** — it sounds obvious, but saying "my_resource client.lua line 42 tried to use MyVariable but it was nil" often makes the fix obvious
2. **Comment out the breaking line** — if the error stops, the problem is that line. If it moves to the next line, the problem is upstream
3. **Add a print statement** — `print('DEBUG: MyVariable is', MyVariable)` right before the crash line tells you what the value actually is
4. **Check the official docs** — docs.fivem.net has every native and event documented
5. **Check txAdmin Logs** — txData/profile/logs/ has detailed server-side logs

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `attempt to index a nil value (global 'ESX')` | ESX framework not started before your resource | Add `ensure es_extended` before your resource in server.cfg |
| `attempt to index a nil value (global 'QBCore')` | QBCore not started or using wrong export | Use `local QBCore = exports['qb-core']:GetCoreObject()` |
| Error on line 1, but file has content | File encoding is wrong (UTF-8 BOM) | Save as UTF-8 without BOM in VS Code |
| Resource runs but nothing happens, no errors | Logic error — code is valid but does the wrong thing | Add print statements to trace execution |

title: Add Discord rich presence to your server · Quasar Academy
ai:
Discord rich presence shows your server's name, player count, and a custom image right in Discord when someone is playing on your server. It's free advertising — every player's friends see it. This lesson sets up the full integration from Discord developer portal to Lua script.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A Discord rich presence integration showing player count, server name, and a custom image in players' Discord profiles.
- Time: ~25 minutes
- You need: A Discord application at discord.com/developers, a FiveM server with OneSync, and basic Lua knowledge.
- You'll learn: Creating a Discord application, uploading rich presence art, connecting FiveM to Discord via SetDiscordRichPresence, and displaying live player counts and server info.

### Before you start
- I have a Discord account and can access discord.com/developers.
- I have a local FiveM server with OneSync enabled.
- I can create and edit Lua scripts.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Create a Discord application
_Goal: You have a Client ID and uploaded art assets._

Go to https://discord.com/developers/applications → New Application. Name it after your server.

Go to **Rich Presence** → **Art Assets**. Upload images (512x512 minimum):
- `server_logo` — your server's logo
- `server_large` — a hero image or screenshot

Copy your **Client ID** (18-digit number) from the General Information page.

### Step 2 - Set up the resource
_Goal: A resource that talks to Discord._

```text
resources/qu_discord_rpc/
  fxmanifest.lua
  client.lua
```

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'
client_script 'client.lua'
```

### Step 3 - Set the Discord app ID and presence
_Goal: Discord shows the small icon and server name._

```lua client.lua
local appId = 'YOUR_DISCORD_CLIENT_ID'  -- 18-digit number

Citizen.CreateThread(function()
    -- Set the Discord application ID once
    SetDiscordAppId(appId)

    while true do
        -- Update presence every 15 seconds
        SetRichPresence('Playing on My Cool Server')

        -- Small image = your logo
        SetDiscordRichPresenceAssetSmall('server_logo')
        SetDiscordRichPresenceAssetSmallText('My Cool Server')

        Citizen.Wait(15000)
    end
end)
```

Expected output:

```
In Discord, your profile shows "Playing My Cool Server" with your logo.
```

### Step 4 - Add live player count and join button
_Goal: Discord shows how many players are online._

```lua client.lua
local appId = 'YOUR_DISCORD_CLIENT_ID'

Citizen.CreateThread(function()
    SetDiscordAppId(appId)

    while true do
        -- Get player count from the server
        local playerCount = GetNumPlayerIndices()  -- live player count on server
        -- Real count: TriggerServerEvent to get it, or use a shared variable

        SetRichPresence('Players online: ' .. playerCount)

        -- Large image = hero/screenshot
        SetDiscordRichPresenceAsset('server_large')
        SetDiscordRichPresenceAssetText('Join now at cfx.re/join/xxxxx')

        -- Add a Join button
        SetDiscordRichPresenceAction(0, 'Join', 'fivem://connect/localhost:30120')
        SetDiscordRichPresenceAction(1, 'Discord', 'https://discord.gg/yourserver')

        Citizen.Wait(15000)
    end
end)
```

For a real player count, add this server-side:
```lua server.lua
RegisterNetEvent('qu_discord:getPlayerCount', function()
    local src = source
    TriggerClientEvent('qu_discord:playerCount', src, GetNumPlayerIndices())
end)
```

Expected output:

```
Discord shows "Players online: 12" with a Join button. Friends can click to join directly.
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Rich presence doesn't appear | Client ID is wrong or app not created | Double-check the 18-digit ID at discord.com/developers |
| Image shows as gray question mark | Asset name doesn't match the uploaded name | Asset names are case-sensitive. Match exactly |
| Join button doesn't work | `fivem://` link has wrong syntax | Use `fivem://connect/ip:port` — no spaces, correct IP |
| Player count shows 0 | Not fetching count from server | Use TriggerServerEvent to get `GetNumPlayerIndices()` |

title: Add-on vehicles: the complete process · Quasar Academy
ai:
Add-on vehicles are the most common customization on FiveM servers. This is the definitive guide — not just "put files in a folder," but understanding every file type, every data_file line, and why each one matters. After this lesson you can add any vehicle from any source.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A fully working add-on vehicle resource with model files, handling, vehicles.meta, carcols, and a spawn command.
- Time: ~30 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM server, a downloaded add-on vehicle (car folder with .yft/.ytd/.meta files).
- You'll learn: The complete add-on vehicle pipeline: file types, folder structure, data_file lines in fxmanifest, handling.meta and vehicles.meta integration, spawning, and troubleshooting invisible vehicles.

### Before you start
- I have a local FiveM server with a resources folder.
- I have a downloaded add-on vehicle (a folder with .yft and .ytd files).
- I can edit fxmanifest.lua and restart resources.

> **Add-on vs Replace**
> **Add-on** vehicles keep the original GTA cars and add new ones. They need a unique spawn name and don't conflict with anything. **Replace** vehicles swap a GTA car model. They're simpler (just rename files) but break if two use the same car. Always prefer add-on.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Prepare the folder structure
_Goal: Every file is in the right place._

```text
resources/my_addon_car/
  fxmanifest.lua
  data/
    handling.meta
    vehicles.meta
    carcols.meta
    carvariations.meta
  stream/
    modelname.yft        <- vehicle 3D model
    modelname_hi.yft     <- high-detail interior
    modelname.ytd        <- textures
    modelname+hi.ytd     <- high-detail textures
```

Most add-on car downloads already have this structure. Verify before proceeding.

### Step 2 - Write the fxmanifest
_Goal: FiveM knows how to load every file._

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

description 'My Add-On Vehicle'

files {
    'data/*.meta',
    'stream/*.yft',
    'stream/*.ytd',
}

data_file 'HANDLING_FILE'         'data/handling.meta'
data_file 'VEHICLE_METADATA_FILE' 'data/vehicles.meta'
data_file 'CARCOLS_FILE'          'data/carcols.meta'
data_file 'VEHICLE_VARIATION_FILE' 'data/carvariations.meta'
```

The `data_file` lines are critical. Each one tells FiveM: "parse this XML and merge it into the game." Without them, your vehicle exists on disk but the game doesn't know about it.

### Step 3 - Verify the model name in vehicles.meta
_Goal: You know the spawn code._

Open `data/vehicles.meta`. Find `<modelName>`:

```xml vehicles.meta
<modelName>mycar</modelName>
```

This is your spawn name. It must match the stream file names (e.g., `mycar.yft`, `mycar.ytd`). If they don't match, the vehicle won't spawn.

Also check:
- `<gameName>` — the display name in trainers
- `<vehicleClass>` — VC_SPORT, VC_SEDAN, etc.
- `<handlingId>` — must match a `<handlingName>` in handling.meta

### Step 4 - Spawn and test
_Goal: The vehicle appears and drives correctly._

```lua client.lua
RegisterCommand('spawnmycar', function()
    local model = 'mycar'  -- must match <modelName> in vehicles.meta
    RequestModel(model)
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do Citizen.Wait(0) end
    local coords = GetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId())
    local veh = CreateVehicle(model, coords.x + 2, coords.y, coords.z, 0.0, true, false)
    SetVehicleNumberPlateText(veh, 'ADDON')
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
end, false)
```

Run `/spawnmycar` in chat. The vehicle appears.

Expected output:

```
The vehicle spawns, is drivable, has correct engine sound, and other players can see it.
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Vehicle invisible — no model at all | Model files not in `files {}` or wrong folder | Check every .yft/.ytd is in `stream/` and listed in `files {}` |
| Vehicle spawns but has no wheels or falls through ground | Missing `handling.meta` or wrong `<handlingId>` | Match `<handlingId>` in vehicles.meta to `<handlingName>` in handling.meta |
| `Invalid model` or `RequestModel` hangs forever | Spawn name doesn't match `<modelName>` | Case-sensitive. `mycar` ≠ `MyCar` ≠ `MYCAR` |
| Vehicle is solid white (no textures) | `.ytd` file not loaded or wrong path | Check the .ytd is in `stream/` and listed in `files {}` |

title: Map an interior and stream it in FiveM · Quasar Academy
ai:
Custom interiors turn a generic GTA building into YOUR space — a police station, a gang hideout, a player apartment. This lesson walks the full pipeline from CodeWalker to in-game, covering the file types that confuse every beginner and the exact fxmanifest lines that make it work.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A custom MLO interior mapped with CodeWalker, exported as a FiveM resource, and streamed into your server.
- Time: ~40 minutes
- You need: CodeWalker (free GTA V mapping tool), a local FiveM server, and basic understanding of YMAP/YBN files.
- You'll learn: What MLOs are, how YMAPs and YTYP files define interiors, using CodeWalker to place and export interiors, writing the correct fxmanifest, and streaming the interior in-game.

### Before you start
- I have downloaded and installed CodeWalker (https://github.com/dexyfex/CodeWalker).
- I have a local FiveM server.
- I understand basic folder structure and fxmanifest.lua.

> **MLO = Map Layer Object**
> An MLO is a custom interior mapped inside an existing GTA building. It consists of YMAP files (placement data), YTYP files (archetype definitions), and streamed textures/models. Together they tell FiveM: "inside this building, render this custom space instead."

## Build it

### Step 1 - Create the interior in CodeWalker
_Goal: Your YMAP and YTYP files are exported._

1. Open CodeWalker, load the GTA V world
2. Navigate to the building you want to map (e.g., an empty warehouse)
3. Use the object browser to place walls, floors, furniture, lighting
4. Once satisfied, **Export** → select both YMAP and YTYP formats

Export names must match. If your interior is called `my_interior`, you get:
```text
my_interior.ymap
my_interior.ytyp
```

### Step 2 - Create the resource structure
_Goal: The FiveM resource is ready._

```text
resources/my_interior/
  fxmanifest.lua
  stream/
    my_interior.ymap
    my_interior.ytyp
    my_interior.ybn       <- any bound files
    my_interior.ydd       <- any drawable dictionary
    my_interior.ydr       <- any drawable model
    my_interior.yft       <- any fragment model
    my_interior.ytd       <- any texture dictionary
```

### Step 3 - Write the fxmanifest
_Goal: FiveM streams your interior._

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

description 'My Custom Interior'

files {
    'stream/*.ymap',
    'stream/*.ytyp',
    'stream/*.ybn',
    'stream/*.ydd',
    'stream/*.ydr',
    'stream/*.yft',
    'stream/*.ytd',
}

data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST' 'stream/my_interior.ytyp'

this_is_a_map 'yes'
```

Critical lines:
- `files {}` — lists every streamed asset
- `data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST'` — loads the YTYP (archetype definitions)
- `this_is_a_map 'yes'` — tells FiveM this resource adds map data

Expected output:

```
After `ensure my_interior`, the interior appears when you enter that building.
```

### Step 4 - Verify placement and fix issues
_Goal: The interior is correctly positioned with no gaps._

Join the server, go to the building, and check:
- Do the walls appear inside the building shell?
- Is the lighting correct (not too dark/bright)?
- Can you walk through the space without falling through the floor?

Common fixes:
- **Floating objects**: adjust Z position in CodeWalker and re-export
- **No collision**: the YBN (bound) file is missing — add it to `files {}`
- **Missing textures**: the YTD is missing — add it to `files {}`

Expected output:

```
You can walk through your custom interior. Walls are solid, lighting is correct, and other players see it.
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Interior doesn't appear at all | `this_is_a_map 'yes'` is missing or YTYP not loaded | Add both: `data_file 'DLC_ITYP_REQUEST'` and `this_is_a_map 'yes'` |
| Objects visible but no collision (fall through) | YBN bound file missing | Export YBN from CodeWalker, add to `stream/` and `files {}` |
| Interior appears but is offset/rotated | Wrong coordinates in CodeWalker | Center the interior at world origin before placing, or adjust YMAP coordinates |
| `couldn't load resource` after adding interior | File size too large or too many files | Split large interiors into multiple resources |

title: Stream add-on weapons and custom loadouts · Quasar Academy
ai:
Custom weapons are second only to custom vehicles in popularity. The process is similar to vehicles but uses different metadata files. This lesson covers the full pipeline and the common mistakes that leave weapons invisible or unshootable.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: An add-on weapon resource with custom model, textures, and a give-weapon command.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM server, an add-on weapon download (folder with .yft/.ytd/weapons.meta).
- You'll learn: Weapon asset types (.yft, .ytd, .meta), weapons.meta structure, data_file lines, spawning with GiveWeaponToPed, and integrating with inventory scripts.

### Before you start
- I have read the add-on vehicles lesson — weapons follow the same pattern.
- I have a downloaded add-on weapon folder.
- I can create and restart resources.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Prepare the resource
_Goal: All files are in the right folders._

```text
resources/my_weapon/
  fxmanifest.lua
  data/
    weapons.meta
  stream/
    w_ar_myrifle.yft
    w_ar_myrifle.ytd
```

An add-on weapon needs at minimum: the model (`.yft`), the textures (`.ytd`), and the metadata (`.meta`). Some downloads include sounds (`.awc`/`.dat`) — include those if present.

### Step 2 - Check weapons.meta
_Goal: You know the weapon's spawn name._

```xml weapons.meta
<Weapon type="WEAPON_MYRIFLE">
    <modelName>w_ar_myrifle</modelName>
    <animGroup>rifle</animGroup>
    <ClipSize>30</ClipSize>
    <Damage>35.000000</Damage>
    <Recoil>0.350000</Recoil>
    ...
</Weapon>
```

The `<modelName>` is your spawn name. The `
` is a GTA weapon slot — adding to `WEAPON_ASSAULTRIFLE` means it shares ammo and animations with the assault rifle class.

### Step 3 - Write fxmanifest and spawn
_Goal: The weapon appears in hand and fires._

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

files {
    'data/weapons.meta',
    'stream/*.yft',
    'stream/*.ytd',
}

data_file 'WEAPONINFO_FILE' 'data/weapons.meta'
```

```lua client.lua
RegisterCommand('givemygun', function()
    local ped = PlayerPedId()
    local weaponHash = GetHashKey('WEAPON_MYRIFLE')
    GiveWeaponToPed(ped, weaponHash, 999, false, false)
end, false)
```

The hash name must match the `
` exactly: `GetHashKey('WEAPON_MYRIFLE')`.

Expected output:

```
`/givemygun` equips the weapon. It renders in the player's hands, fires, and uses correct ammo.
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Weapon invisible in hands | `.yft` or `.ytd` not in `files {}` | List every model/texture file in `files {}` |
| `GiveWeaponToPed` gives default weapon | Hash doesn't match `
` in weapons.meta | Match exactly: `WEAPON_MYRIFLE` must match the XML type |
| No weapon sounds | Sound files missing or not loaded | Add audio files and `data_file 'AUDIO_WAVEPACK'` line |
| Weapon clips through ground when dropped | No ground model specified | Add `<modelName2>` with the dropped/pickup model variant |

title: Stream add-on peds and custom characters · Quasar Academy
ai:
Custom peds bring your server to life — police officers, shopkeepers, gang members, unique NPCs. The streaming pattern is simpler than vehicles (fewer metadata files), but there are specific rules about model loading and ped creation that differ from objects and vehicles.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: An add-on ped resource with custom model, textures, and a spawn-ped command.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM server, an add-on ped download (.yft/.ytd files), and basic fxmanifest knowledge.
- You'll learn: Ped asset types, the ped streaming pattern, spawning peds with CreatePed, setting relationships, and integrating custom peds into jobs and NPCs.

### Before you start
- I have read the add-on vehicles lesson — peds follow a simpler pattern.
- I have an add-on ped download (folder with .yft/.ytd).
- I can create and restart resources.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Prepare the resource
_Goal: The ped files are in the right place._

```text
resources/my_ped/
  fxmanifest.lua
  stream/
    ped_model.yft
    ped_model.ytd
```

Unlike vehicles, peds usually only need two files: the model (`.yft`) and textures (`.ytd`). No metadata XML files required unless you're replacing a GTA ped or adding special properties.

### Step 2 - Write fxmanifest and spawn
_Goal: The ped appears in-game._

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'

files {
    'stream/*.yft',
    'stream/*.ytd',
}
```

```lua client.lua
RegisterCommand('spawnmyped', function()
    local model = 'ped_model'  -- must match the .yft filename (without extension)
    RequestModel(model)
    while not HasModelLoaded(model) do Citizen.Wait(0) end

    local coords = GetEntityCoords(PlayerPedId())
    local ped = CreatePed(4, model, coords.x + 2, coords.y, coords.z, 0.0, true, false)

    -- Make the ped not attack the player
    SetPedAsNoLongerNeeded(ped)
    SetModelAsNoLongerNeeded(model)
end, false)
```

`CreatePed(pedType, model, x, y, z, heading, isNetwork, bScriptHostPed)`:
- pedType `4` = civilian (won't attack)
- pedType `6` = cop (will attack criminals)
- isNetwork `true` = other players can see this ped

Expected output:

```
A custom ped spawns in front of you. It stands idle and doesn't attack.
```

### Step 3 - Make the ped do something
_Goal: The ped has behavior — animation, scenario, or combat._

```lua client.lua
-- Make the ped play a scenario (stand at a counter, lean on wall, etc.)
TaskStartScenarioInPlace(ped, 'WORLD_HUMAN_STAND_IMPATIENT', 0, true)

-- Give the ped a weapon and make them guard
GiveWeaponToPed(ped, GetHashKey('WEAPON_PISTOL'), 999, false, true)
TaskGuardCurrentPosition(ped, 10.0, 10.0, 1)

-- Make the ped a shopkeeper who stays put
FreezeEntityPosition(ped, true)
SetBlockingOfNonTemporaryEvents(ped, true)
```

Full scenario list: https://github.com/DurtyFree/gta-v-data-dumps

Expected output:

```
The ped plays an animation, guards an area, or stands in place depending on the task.
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Ped invisible or doesn't spawn | Model name doesn't match .yft file | Use exact filename without extension: `ped_model.yft` → `'ped_model'` |
| `CreatePed` returns 0 (invalid ped) | Model failed to load or doesn't exist | Check `HasModelLoaded(model)` returns true before CreatePed |
| Ped attacks player immediately | pedType is a combat type (cop, army) | Use pedType `4` (civilian) or set relationship group |
| Ped disappears when you look away | `SetPedAsNoLongerNeeded` called too early | Only call after the ped is fully spawned and positioned |

title: Custom loading screen with music · Quasar Academy
Install any FiveM resource correctly · Quasar Academy
ai:
Most "my resource doesn't work" problems come from skipping a single step during installation. This lesson gives you a checklist that catches 90% of install failures before you even restart the server. Follow this method and you'll never post "help my script won't start" again.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A systematic method for installing any FiveM resource — from downloaded zip to working script — that avoids the common mistakes.
- Time: ~15 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM server, a downloaded resource zip, and a text editor.
- You'll learn: Reading the resource's README, checking dependencies, placing the folder correctly, reading fxmanifest for requirements, adding to server.cfg, and the verify-restart-test loop.

### Before you start
- I have a FiveM server with a resources folder.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart resources.
- I have a downloaded resource (zip file).

> **The #1 install mistake**
> Putting the resource folder in the wrong place. It must go in `resources/` (or `resources/[category]/`), not in `server-data/`, not in the FXServer root, not nested inside another resource's folder.

## The install checklist

### Step 1 - Read the README first
_Goal: You know what this resource needs before you touch a file._

Before extracting anything, open the zip and look for:
- `README.md` or `README.txt` — installation instructions
- `fxmanifest.lua` or `__resource.lua` — the manifest
- A `config.lua` or `config.json` — configuration you must edit

Read the README. Note any:
- **Dependencies**: "requires ox_lib", "needs es_extended", etc.
- **Database**: "import the SQL file"
- **Configuration**: "edit config.lua and set your framework"
- **Commands**: how to use the resource after install

Skipping the README is the #2 install mistake.

### Step 2 - Place the folder correctly
_Goal: The resource is in the right location._

Extract the zip. The folder structure should look like:
```text
resources/
  your_script/
    fxmanifest.lua       <- must be at the root of the folder
    client.lua
    server.lua
    html/                <- if it has NUI
    config.lua           <- if it needs configuring
```

Common mistake: extracting a zip that creates a folder-inside-a-folder:
```text
resources/your_script/your_script-v1.2/fxmanifest.lua  <- WRONG
```

The `fxmanifest.lua` must be exactly one folder deep: `resources/your_script/fxmanifest.lua`.

### Step 3 - Check dependencies and configure
_Goal: Every requirement is met._

Open `fxmanifest.lua` and check `dependencies`:
```lua fxmanifest.lua
dependencies {
    'ox_lib',
    'oxmysql',
    'es_extended',   -- if ESX
    'qb-core',       -- if QBCore
}
```

Make sure every dependency is installed and started BEFORE this resource in `server.cfg`. If the resource needs a framework (ESX/QBCore/Qbox), make sure you have one installed.

Open `config.lua` or `config.json` and set:
- Framework type (ESX, QBCore, Qbox, standalone)
- Any API keys or webhooks
- Any feature toggles

Expected output:

```
All dependencies are in your resources folder and started in server.cfg. Config is edited.
```

### Step 4 - Add to server.cfg, restart, and test
_Goal: The resource starts without errors._

Add to `server.cfg` AFTER all dependencies:
```bash server.cfg
ensure es_extended
ensure ox_lib
ensure oxmysql
ensure your_script        # after its dependencies
```

In txAdmin Live Console or server console:
```text
ensure your_script
```

Watch the console for errors. If you see red, read the error, find the file and line, fix it, and `restart your_script`.

Expected output:

```
Console shows `[script:your_script]` entries, no red error lines. The resource's command or feature works.
```

## Common failures

| Exact error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Couldn't find resource your_script` | Folder in wrong location or misspelled | Check `resources/your_script/fxmanifest.lua` exists |
| `dependency 'ox_lib' not found` | Dependency not installed | Download and install the missing dependency first |
| Resource starts but nothing happens | Config not set for your framework | Edit config.lua to match your framework (ESX/QBCore/standalone) |
| `attempt to index a nil value (global 'ESX')` | ESX not started before this resource | Move `ensure your_script` AFTER `ensure es_extended` in server.cfg |

title: FiveM ACE permissions and principals · Quasar Academy
ai:
ACE is FiveM's built-in permission system. It decides who can use admin commands, who can restart resources, and who can ban players. It works the same on every server — vanilla, ESX, QBCore, Qbox — because it's part of FiveM itself, not any framework.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A permission system using ACE and principals — give admins, moderators, and trusted players exactly the powers they need and nothing more.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A FiveM server with server.cfg access. No resources or frameworks required — ACE is built into FiveM itself.
- You'll learn: What ACE permissions are, how principals connect players to groups, how allow and deny work together, wildcard permissions, and how to build a clean staff permission system.

### Before you start
- I can edit server.cfg and restart the server.
- I know my license identifier (find it in txAdmin → Players → your name → Identifiers).
- I understand the difference between server and client (see the Two Worlds lesson if not).

> **Three words you need to know**
> **Principal** = who gets the permission (a player or a group). **ACE** = what permission they get (allow or deny a specific command or resource). **Inheritance** = groups can pass permissions to other groups. Think of it as: **Player → Group → Permissions**.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Understand the three parts
_Goal: You can explain each piece of the permission system._

Every ACE line has three parts:

```text
add_ace <principal> <permission> <allow|deny>
```

- **Principal** — who. A group (`group.admin`), an individual (`identifier.license:abc123`), or everyone (`builtin.everyone`)
- **Permission** — what. `command.kick`, `command.ban`, `resource.myresource`, or custom like `i.am.cool`
- **Allow/Deny** — whether they can or can't

Groups are the most common principal. Instead of giving permissions to 10 different admins one by one, you give them to `group.admin` once, then add each admin to that group.

Expected output:

```
You can look at any server.cfg's ACE section and say: which group, what permission, allowed or denied.
```

### Step 2 - Add a player to a group (principals)
_Goal: Your player is in the admin group._

A principal connects a player to a group:

```bash server.cfg
add_principal identifier.license:abc123def456 group.admin
```

The identifier format is `identifier.<type>:<value>`. Common types:
- `license` — tied to your Cfx.re account (most stable, use this)
- `steam` — tied to your Steam account
- `fivem` — old format, still works but license is preferred

Find yours in txAdmin → Players → click your name → Identifiers.

Groups can also inherit from each other. Admins should get moderator powers automatically:

```bash server.cfg
add_principal group.admin group.moderator
```

Now `group.admin` inherits everything `group.moderator` can do. Admins automatically get moderator permissions — you don't need to grant them twice.

Expected output:

```
In txAdmin Players panel, your name shows `group.admin` under principals.
```

### Step 3 - Give permissions to a group (ACEs)
_Goal: Admins can kick and ban, moderators can only kick._

ACE lines grant or deny specific permissions:

```bash server.cfg
# Admin gets everything
add_ace group.admin command.kick allow
add_ace group.admin command.ban allow
add_ace group.admin command.restart allow

# Moderator gets kick only — not ban
add_ace group.moderator command.kick allow
add_ace group.moderator command.ban deny
```

By default, everything is denied. You only get what you explicitly allow.

**Deny is stronger than allow.** If a player is in two groups and one denies `command.ban`, they cannot ban — even if another group allows it. Deny always wins.

Expected output:

```
An admin can `/kick`, `/ban`, and `/restart`. A moderator can only `/kick`. A regular player gets "no permission" from all three commands.
```

### Step 4 - Use wildcards for bulk permissions
_Goal: One line can grant multiple permissions._

Wildcards give access to everything under a category:

```bash server.cfg
# Give admins ALL commands
add_ace group.admin command allow
```

`command` without a dot means "all command.* permissions." This grants `command.kick`, `command.ban`, `command.restart`, and every other command permission.

Be careful with this. Only give broad wildcards to owners. For staff, grant specific permissions one by one.

Other useful wildcards:

```bash server.cfg
# Give a resource full access
add_ace resource.my_admin_menu command allow

# Block everything from a dangerous resource
add_ace resource.suspicious_script command deny
```

Expected output:

```
A single `command allow` line gives an admin every command permission on the server.
```

## The complete starter template

Copy this into your server.cfg, replace the placeholders, and you have a working staff permission system:

```bash server.cfg
## --- Staff groups ---
# Add yourself as admin (replace with YOUR license identifier)
add_principal identifier.license:YOUR_LICENSE_HERE group.admin

# Moderators inherit from admin (optional — remove if admins and mods are separate)
# add_principal group.admin group.moderator  # admins get mod powers too

## --- Admin permissions ---
add_ace group.admin command.kick allow
add_ace group.admin command.ban allow
add_ace group.admin command.restart allow

## --- Moderator permissions (replace with your mod's license) ---
# add_principal identifier.license:MOD_LICENSE_HERE group.moderator
# add_ace group.moderator command.kick allow

## --- Check current permissions ---
# list_aces
# list_principals
```

## Useful commands

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `add_principal child parent` | Adds a player or group into another group |
| `remove_principal child parent` | Removes a player or group from a parent group |
| `add_ace principal permission allow` | Grants a permission |
| `add_ace principal permission deny` | Denies a permission |
| `remove_ace principal permission` | Removes a previously granted/denied permission |
| `list_aces` | Shows all current ACE permissions (run in server console) |
| `list_principals` | Shows all current principal assignments |

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| `/kick` says "no permission" even though you added yourself | Wrong identifier or group name typo | Check your license identifier matches exactly. `add_principal` must come BEFORE `add_ace` in server.cfg |
| Admin can't do anything after restart | ACE lines are below the admin `ensure` lines, or wrong order | Put ALL ACE lines in one section near the top of server.cfg, before any `ensure` lines |
| Moderator can still ban even though you added `deny` | They're also in a group that `allow`s it, or the deny line is misspelled | `deny` always wins IF it's on the same permission. Check the exact permission name matches |
| `builtin.everyone` gave permissions to everyone | You used `builtin.everyone` as a principal | Never use `builtin.everyone` for dangerous permissions. Remove it immediately |
| Identifier changed after Cfx.re account change | License identifiers are tied to your Cfx.re account | Update the identifier in server.cfg. Always use license identifiers — they're the most stable |

## ⚠ Safety rules

- Never give dangerous permissions to `builtin.everyone`
- Never paste random ACE lines from tutorials without understanding what they do
- Never give admin permissions to players you don't trust
- Always use `identifier.license` when possible — it's tied to Cfx.re accounts, not hardware
- Keep permission groups organized with comments in server.cfg
- Test permissions with ONE player before giving them to your whole staff team

title: How to set up a FiveM server on Linux · Quasar Academy
ai:
Most production FiveM servers run on Linux. It's cheaper, faster, and once you learn the five-command workflow you'll never go back to a Windows server eating RAM in the background. This lesson takes you from an empty Ubuntu VPS to a joinable server in under an hour.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A working FiveM server on a Linux VPS — artifacts, server.cfg, resources, and a screen session to keep it online.
- Time: ~45 minutes
- You need: A Linux VPS with Ubuntu (root access), SSH client, a Cfx.re account, and a license key from portal.cfx.re.
- You'll learn: Why Linux for production, the artifacts vs data split, server.cfg line by line, screen to keep the server alive, and the resource-install workflow.

### Before you start
- I have a Linux VPS with Ubuntu 22.04 or newer and root/sudo access.
- I can SSH into the VPS (ssh root@your-server-ip).
- I have a Cfx.re account and a license key from portal.cfx.re.

> **Why Linux for production?**
> Linux uses less RAM (no desktop GUI), costs less for VPS hosting, handles more players on the same hardware, and most hosting providers default to Ubuntu. Windows servers are fine for learning, but almost every large FiveM community runs on Linux.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Update the server
_Goal: All system packages are current._

Before installing anything, update Ubuntu:

```bash
apt update && apt upgrade -y
apt install wget git screen -y
```

This downloads the latest package lists and upgrades everything. It prevents "package not found" errors later.

Expected output:

```
The terminal scrolls through updated packages. No red error lines.
```

### Step 2 - Create the folder structure
_Goal: Two clean folders: one for binaries, one for your data._

```bash
mkdir -p /home/fxserver
mkdir -p /home/server-data
```

- `/home/fxserver/` — the FiveM server binaries (the engine). You update this folder when FiveM releases a new build.
- `/home/server-data/` — **your** content: `resources/` folder and `server.cfg`. This is what you back up and version-control.

Keeping them separate means updating the engine never touches your scripts.

Expected output:

```
Two empty folders exist at `/home/fxserver/` and `/home/server-data/`.
```

### Step 3 - Download and extract the artifacts
_Goal: FXServer is ready to run._

Go to the Linux artifacts page and copy the URL for the **Recommended** (green) build:

```text
https://runtime.fivem.net/artifacts/fivem/build_proot_linux/master/
```

Download and extract:

```bash
cd /home/fxserver
wget -O fx.tar.xz <paste-the-download-url-here>
tar -xf fx.tar.xz
rm fx.tar.xz
```

Artifacts are the actual FiveM server software. Scripts don't work without them — they're the engine everything runs on.

Expected output:

```
`/home/fxserver/run.sh` exists. Running it would start a bare server.
```

### Step 4 - Get the server data
_Goal: The resources folder and starter config are ready._

```bash
cd /home/server-data
git clone https://github.com/citizenfx/cfx-server-data.git .
```

Git is a tool that downloads code from GitHub. The `cfx-server-data` repo contains the starter resources (chat, mapmanager, spawnmanager) and an example server.cfg.

Expected output:

```
`/home/server-data/resources/` exists with starter resources inside.
```

### Step 5 - Write your server.cfg
_Goal: The server has a name, endpoints, license, and resources._

Open `/home/server-data/server.cfg` and replace everything with:

```bash server.cfg
## --- Endpoints (how players connect) ---
endpoint_add_tcp "0.0.0.0:30120"
endpoint_add_udp "0.0.0.0:30120"

## --- Server identity ---
sv_hostname "My Linux FiveM Server"
sv_maxclients 32
onesync on

## --- License key (REQUIRED — server won't boot without it) ---
sv_licenseKey "PASTE_YOUR_KEY_HERE"

## --- Built-in resources ---
ensure mapmanager
ensure chat
ensure spawnmanager
ensure sessionmanager

## --- Admin permissions ---
add_ace group.admin command allow
add_principal identifier.license:YOUR_LICENSE_HERE group.admin
```

`ensure` tells FiveM: "start this resource when the server boots." Resources listed higher start first.

Expected output:

```
The file is saved. Every line has a purpose you can explain.
```

### Step 6 - Start the server
_Goal: Players can connect._

```bash
cd /home/server-data
bash /home/fxserver/run.sh +exec server.cfg
```

This runs the FXServer binary and hands it your config. The console prints each resource starting in order, then "Server ready."

Expected output:

```
The console shows resources starting in order, then a ready message. Open FiveM, press F8, type `connect YOUR_SERVER_IP:30120`.
```

### Step 7 - Keep the server alive with screen
_Goal: The server stays online after you close SSH._

When you close your SSH terminal, any process running in it dies. `screen` fixes this:

```bash
# screen already installed in Step 1
screen -S fivem
cd /home/server-data
bash /home/fxserver/run.sh +exec server.cfg
```

Now press **Ctrl+A, then D** to detach. The server keeps running. To rejoin later:

```bash
screen -r fivem
```

Expected output:

```
You close your SSH terminal. The server is still online. Friends can still connect.
```

## Installing resources — the workflow

Every resource follows the same five steps:

1. **Download** the resource and place it in `/home/server-data/resources/`
2. **Read the README** — note dependencies, framework, and SQL files
3. **Add dependencies** first, then add `ensure resource_name` to server.cfg AFTER its dependencies
4. **Import any `.sql` files** using HeidiSQL or the mysql command line
5. **Restart the server** or run `ensure resource_name` in the console

## Recommended modern stack

| Tool | What it does |
|---|---|
| Ubuntu 22.04+ | The operating system |
| txAdmin | Web dashboard for server management (starts at `:40120`) |
| MariaDB | The database that stores player data |
| oxmysql | The connector between FiveM and MariaDB |
| HeidiSQL | Desktop tool to view and edit the database |
| GitHub | Where resources are hosted and version-controlled |
| VS Code | Code editor for editing configs and scripts |

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| Server won't start — "Invalid license key" | Key is wrong or missing | Re-copy from portal.cfx.re. No extra spaces |
| "Could not find resource X" | Resource folder missing or misspelled | Check `/home/server-data/resources/X/fxmanifest.lua` exists |
| Players can't connect — "Connection timed out" | Port 30120 blocked by firewall | Run `ufw allow 30120/tcp` and `ufw allow 30120/udp`. Check your VPS provider's firewall too |
| Server dies when you close SSH | You didn't use screen | Always start the server inside `screen -S fivem` |
| `Permission denied` when running run.sh | Script isn't executable | Run `chmod +x /home/fxserver/run.sh` |
| `apt: command not found` | You're not on Ubuntu/Debian | This guide is for Ubuntu. For CentOS/RHEL, use `yum` instead of `apt` |

title: Using JSON in FiveM · Quasar Academy
ai:
JSON is how simple data survives a server restart without setting up a database. Many scripts use it for config files, settings, whitelists, and temporary storage. This lesson teaches you to save, load, and update JSON files — the lightweight way to give your resource a memory.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A resource that saves and loads player data using JSON — name, money, and job — and survives server restarts.
- Time: ~20 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM server, one resource folder, basic Lua knowledge.
- You'll learn: What JSON is, json.encode() and json.decode(), SaveResourceFile and LoadResourceFile, real multi-player patterns, and when JSON beats a database.

### Before you start
- I can create a FiveM resource with server.lua and fxmanifest.lua.
- I know what a Lua table is (see the Tables lesson if not).
- I can read the server console output.

> **JSON is just text that remembers structure**
> Think of JSON like a sticky note. You write data down as text, stick it in a file, and read it back later. The text has a strict format that Lua can convert to a table and back. `json.encode()` writes the note. `json.decode()` reads it back.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Create the resource
_Goal: A resource folder with a server script._

```text
resources/qu_json_demo/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
```

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'
server_script 'server.lua'
```

### Step 2 - Save data to JSON
_Goal: A .json file appears with your data inside._

```lua server.lua
-- Create a Lua table with player data
local playerData = {
    name = "John",
    money = 5000,
    job = "police"
}

-- Convert to JSON and save to a file
SaveResourceFile(
    GetCurrentResourceName(),       -- which resource owns this file
    "playerdata.json",              -- the filename
    json.encode(playerData),        -- convert table → JSON text
    -1                              -- length: -1 means "all of it"
)

print("Data saved to playerdata.json")
```

Line by line:
- `GetCurrentResourceName()` — returns `qu_json_demo`, so the file lives inside this resource
- `SaveResourceFile()` — writes content to a file on disk. Only works server-side
- `json.encode()` — converts a Lua table to JSON text. Tables become `{}`, strings get quotes, numbers stay numbers
- `-1` — tells FiveM to figure out the length automatically

Expected output:

```
The server console prints "Data saved." A file `resources/qu_json_demo/playerdata.json` appears with: `{"name":"John","money":5000,"job":"police"}`
```

### Step 3 - Load data from JSON
_Goal: You can read the saved data back into Lua._

```lua server.lua
-- Load the file contents
local file = LoadResourceFile(
    GetCurrentResourceName(),
    "playerdata.json"
)

-- Convert JSON text → Lua table
local data = json.decode(file)

-- Use the data like any Lua table
print("Name: " .. data.name)
print("Money: " .. data.money)
print("Job: " .. data.job)
```

- `LoadResourceFile()` — reads a file's content as a string. Returns `nil` if the file doesn't exist
- `json.decode()` — converts JSON text back to a Lua table. After this, `data.name` works like any table field

Expected output:

```
The server console prints: `Name: John`, `Money: 5000`, `Job: police`
```

### Step 4 - Update and re-save data
_Goal: The file reflects your changes._

Loading, editing, and saving is the full loop:

```lua server.lua
-- 1. Load existing data
local file = LoadResourceFile(GetCurrentResourceName(), "playerdata.json")
local data = json.decode(file)

-- 2. Edit the values
data.money = data.money + 500   -- give the player 500 more
data.job = "detective"          -- promote them

-- 3. Save back to the file
SaveResourceFile(
    GetCurrentResourceName(),
    "playerdata.json",
    json.encode(data),
    -1
)

print("Updated: money=" .. data.money .. " job=" .. data.job)
```

JSON files are fully rewritten every time you save — you can't edit one field in place. Load the whole file, change what you need, save the whole file.

Expected output:

```
The console prints `Updated: money=5500 job=detective`. The .json file now shows the new values.
```

### Step 5 - Store data for multiple players
_Goal: Each player has their own data, keyed by license._

Real servers store data per-player using identifiers as keys:

```lua server.lua
-- Register a command so players can save their data
RegisterCommand('savemydata', function(source)
    local license = GetPlayerIdentifierByType(source, 'license')

    -- Load existing data (or start fresh)
    local file = LoadResourceFile(GetCurrentResourceName(), "players.json")
    local allData = file and json.decode(file) or {}

    -- Write this player's data
    allData[license] = {
        name = GetPlayerName(source),
        money = 5000,
        job = "unemployed"
    }

    -- Save everything back
    SaveResourceFile(GetCurrentResourceName(), "players.json", json.encode(allData), -1)
    print(GetPlayerName(source) .. " data saved")
end, false)
```

The resulting JSON looks like:

```json
{
  "license:abc123": { "name": "John", "money": 5000, "job": "police" },
  "license:def456": { "name": "Jane", "money": 2500, "job": "medic" }
}
```

Each license is a unique key, so data never mixes between players.

Expected output:

```
Type `/savemydata` in chat. A `players.json` file appears with your data.
```

## JSON vs databases — when to use which

| Use JSON for | Use MariaDB/MySQL for |
|---|---|
| Config files and settings | Player inventories |
| Whitelists | Character data |
| Small amounts of data (under 1000 entries) | Money and banking systems |
| Resource-level configuration | Anything that needs to be queried (search, filter, sort) |
| Temporary save systems | Data shared across multiple resources |
| Quick prototypes | Large amounts of data (thousands of entries) |

Rule of thumb: if you need to search, sort, or filter the data, use a database. If you need a simple config or a small key-value store, JSON is faster to set up.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| `attempt to index a nil value (local 'data')` | `json.decode()` got `nil` because the file is empty or doesn't exist | Check `if file then` before decoding. Use `or {}` as a fallback |
| `json.encode() expects a table, got nil` | You passed `nil` to `json.encode()` instead of a table | Make sure your table variable is not nil before encoding |
| File doesn't update after saving | The resource needs a restart to see the change, or you're reading from the wrong path | `SaveResourceFile` and `LoadResourceFile` use the resource folder. Check you're loading from the same resource that saved |
| JSON file has wrong data after server crash | The file was being written when the crash happened | JSON files aren't transactional. For critical data, use a database |
| `SaveResourceFile` doesn't work on client | This function only works server-side | Move your file operations to `server.lua` |

title: FiveM databases, oxmysql, and SQL importing · Quasar Academy
ai:
The server is the game world. The database is its memory. Without a database, players lose everything — money, vehicles, characters, houses — every time the server restarts. This lesson connects FiveM to MariaDB using oxmysql, the modern standard for database access.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: A MariaDB database connected to FiveM via oxmysql, with a sample SQL file imported and verified.
- Time: ~30 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM server, MariaDB installed (see the tools lesson), and HeidiSQL or phpMyAdmin.
- You'll learn: What a database is, why servers need them, the MariaDB → oxmysql → FiveM chain, connection strings, importing SQL files, and the resource-install database workflow.

### Before you start
- MariaDB is installed and running (see the tools-and-database lesson).
- HeidiSQL or phpMyAdmin is connected to MariaDB.
- I can edit server.cfg and restart the server.

> **The chain, in one picture**
> Your Lua script → oxmysql (the connector) → MariaDB (the database) → HeidiSQL (your window into it). Each piece does one job. Your script asks oxmysql to save data. oxmysql translates that into SQL. MariaDB stores it. HeidiSQL lets you see it.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Create a database
_Goal: An empty database is ready to receive tables._

Open HeidiSQL, connect to MariaDB (Host: `127.0.0.1`, User: `root`, Port: `3306`). Right-click the server → **Create new** → **Database**. Name it `fivem_db`.

A database is a container for tables. It starts empty. Scripts create tables inside it when you import their SQL files.

Expected output:

```
`fivem_db` appears in HeidiSQL's database list. It has no tables yet.
```

### Step 2 - Install oxmysql and connect
_Goal: The server console says 'Connection Successful'._

Place oxmysql in your resources folder, then add to server.cfg:

```bash server.cfg
ensure oxmysql

set mysql_connection_string "mysql://root:YOUR_PASSWORD@127.0.0.1/fivem_db?charset=utf8mb4"
set mysql_slow_query_warning 150
```

Breaking down the connection string:
- `root` — your MariaDB username
- `YOUR_PASSWORD` — your MariaDB password (leave empty if you didn't set one)
- `127.0.0.1` — where MariaDB lives (`localhost` = same machine)
- `fivem_db` — the database name you created in Step 1
- `?charset=utf8mb4` — character encoding (always include this)

Restart the server. Watch the console for the magic line:
```text
[oxmysql] Connection Successful
```

Expected output:

```
The console prints "Connection Successful." If it prints an error, check your password, database name, and that MariaDB is running.
```

### Step 3 - Understand SQL files
_Goal: You know what a .sql file does and why scripts ship them._

Most FiveM scripts include a `.sql` file. This file creates the tables the script needs.

Example `users.sql`:
```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    money INT DEFAULT 500,
    job VARCHAR(50) DEFAULT 'unemployed'
);
```

This creates a table called `users` with four columns: `id`, `name`, `money`, and `job`. The table is the skeleton — rows of data fill it later. Without importing this file, the script's database queries will fail because the table doesn't exist.

### Step 4 - Import an SQL file
_Goal: New tables appear in HeidiSQL._

In HeidiSQL:
1. Select your database (`fivem_db`) on the left
2. Click **File → Load SQL file** (or drag the `.sql` file into the query window)
3. Review the SQL — understand what tables it creates
4. Click **Execute** (the blue triangle)
5. Right-click the database → **Refresh**

New tables should appear. Common table names: `users`, `players`, `owned_vehicles`, `jobs`, `houses`, `ox_inventory`.

Expected output:

```
After refresh, the database shows the new tables with their columns.
```

### Step 5 - Verify the connection from a script
_Goal: A test query proves everything works._

Create a test resource. The `MySQL` global is provided by oxmysql — you need it in your fxmanifest:

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'
server_script 'server.lua'
shared_script '@oxmysql/lib/MySQL.lua'  -- this makes MySQL.query available
```

Now create the server script:

```lua server.lua
-- Try to fetch all users from the database
local result = MySQL.query.await('SELECT * FROM users')
print('Database query returned: ' .. json.encode(result))
```

If the table is empty, you'll see `[]`. That's fine — it means the connection works. If the table has data, you'll see the rows.

Expected output:

```
The console prints the query result without error. No "connection failed" or "table doesn't exist" messages.
```

## The database install workflow

Every time you install a script that needs a database:

1. **Read the README** — what framework? What dependencies?
2. **Find the .sql file** — usually in the script's root folder or a `sql/` subfolder
3. **Import it** — open HeidiSQL, select your database, load and execute the .sql
4. **Check the tables appeared** — refresh and verify
5. **Add ensure lines** — in server.cfg, AFTER the dependency's ensure line
6. **Restart the server** — and watch the console for errors

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| `[oxmysql] Connection Failed` | Wrong password, database name, or MariaDB not running | Check MariaDB is started. Verify the connection string matches your setup exactly |
| `Unknown database 'fivem_db'` | The database doesn't exist | Create it in HeidiSQL first: right-click → Create new → Database |
| `Table 'users' doesn't exist` | SQL file wasn't imported | Import the script's .sql file in HeidiSQL |
| `Access denied for user 'root'` | Wrong password or MariaDB authentication settings | Reset MariaDB root password or check auth plugin settings |
| SQL import fails: `Table already exists` | You already imported this file before | That's fine — the data is already there. You can skip this or drop and re-import |
| `mysql_connection_string` has no effect | oxmysql not in server.cfg before scripts that need it | `ensure oxmysql` must come BEFORE any script that queries the database |

title: State Bags in FiveM: what they are and why they matter · Quasar Academy
ai:
Before State Bags, every script that needed to know if a player was on duty had to ask the server: "Is this player on duty?" — over and over. State Bags let you attach the answer directly to the player. Any script reads it instantly, and you only hear about it when it changes. This is how modern FiveM servers share data between resources.

### What this lesson covers
- You'll build: Three working State Bags — player duty status, vehicle lock state, and a global double-XP flag — read from multiple resources.
- Time: ~25 minutes
- You need: A local FiveM server with OneSync enabled (it is by default), basic Lua knowledge.
- You'll learn: Player vs Entity vs Global State Bags, setting and reading values, replication, change handlers, and why State Bags replaced constant event spam.

### Before you start
- I can create a FiveM resource with client.lua, server.lua, and fxmanifest.lua.
- I understand the difference between client and server (see the Two Worlds lesson).
- I know what a Lua table is.

> **State Bags in one sentence**
> A State Bag is data you attach to a player, an entity, or the whole server — and it syncs automatically to everyone who needs it.

## Build it

### Step 1 - Create the resource
_Goal: A resource with client and server scripts._

```text
resources/qu_statebags/
  fxmanifest.lua
  server.lua
  client.lua
```

```lua fxmanifest.lua
fx_version 'cerulean'
game 'gta5'
server_script 'server.lua'
client_script 'client.lua'
```

### Step 2 - Set a player State Bag (server)
_Goal: Any script can read this player's duty status._

```lua server.lua
-- When a player connects, set their duty status
AddEventHandler('playerJoining', function()
    local src = source
    local playerState = Player(src).state

    -- Set initial values
    playerState:set('onduty', false, true)   -- true = replicate to all clients
    playerState:set('job', 'unemployed', true)

    print('Player ' .. src .. ' joined — state initialized')
end)
```

- `Player(src).state` — get the State Bag for this player
- `:set('key', value, replicate)` — set a value. Third arg `true` = sync to everyone
- Set `false` for server-only data that clients don't need to see

Expected output:

```
The server console prints the join message. The State Bag now holds `onduty=false` and `job='unemployed'` for this player.
```

### Step 3 - Read a player State Bag (client)
_Goal: Any script on any client can read it instantly._

```lua client.lua
-- Read your own State Bag
CreateThread(function()
    while true do
        local duty = LocalPlayer.state.onduty
        local job = LocalPlayer.state.job

        -- Show on screen
        SetTextFont(0)
        SetTextScale(0.4, 0.4)
        SetTextColour(255, 255, 255, 255)
        SetTextEntry('STRING')
        AddTextComponentString(('Duty: %s | Job: %s'):format(
            duty and 'ON' or 'OFF', job or 'none'
        ))
        DrawText(0.4, 0.05)

        Citizen.Wait(0)
    end
end)
```

`LocalPlayer.state` reads your own State Bag. No server callbacks needed. No events to listen to. The data is just... there.

Expected output:

```
The top-left of your screen shows "Duty: OFF | Job: unemployed."
```

### Step 4 - Change the state and watch it sync
_Goal: Toggle duty status from the server._

Add a command on the server to toggle duty:

```lua server.lua
RegisterCommand('toggleduty', function(source)
    local playerState = Player(source).state
    local current = playerState.onduty or false
    playerState:set('onduty', not current, true)
    print(('Player %s duty: %s'):format(source, not current and 'ON' or 'OFF'))
end, true)  -- true = restricted (only admins)
```

Type `/toggleduty` in chat. The client display updates instantly — no event, no callback, no polling.

Expected output:

```
The screen display flips from "Duty: OFF" to "Duty: ON" immediately.
```

### Step 5 - React to changes with a handler
_Goal: Your code runs ONLY when the value changes._

Instead of checking "has it changed?" every frame, use a handler:

```lua client.lua
AddStateBagChangeHandler('job', nil, function(bagName, key, value)
    local playerId = GetPlayerFromStateBagName(bagName)
    if playerId == 0 then return end  -- not a player State Bag

    -- Show a notification when someone's job changes
    print(('Player %s job changed to: %s'):format(playerId, value))
end)
```

This handler fires automatically every time ANY player's `job` key changes. No loop, no timer, no wasted CPU — it only runs when something actually happens.

Expected output:

```
When a player's job changes, the console prints the update. Nothing fires when the job stays the same.
```

### Step 6 - Use entity State Bags for vehicles
_Goal: A vehicle's lock state is readable from any script._

```lua server.lua
-- Set a vehicle's lock state when spawned
RegisterNetEvent('qu_statebags:spawnVehicle', function(model)
    local src = source
    local coords = GetEntityCoords(GetPlayerPed(src))
    local veh = CreateVehicleServerSetter(model, 'automobile', coords.x, coords.y, coords.z, 0.0)

    -- Attach state to the vehicle entity
    Entity(veh).state:set('locked', true, true)
    Entity(veh).state:set('fuel', 100, true)
    Entity(veh).state:set('owner', src, true)
end)
```

Any script can now check `Entity(veh).state.locked` to decide whether a player can enter the vehicle. No database query needed for a live state.

Expected output:

```
A spawned vehicle has `locked=true`, `fuel=100`, and an `owner` in its State Bag.
```

### Step 7 - Use Global State for server-wide events
_Goal: Every player knows about a global event instantly._

```lua server.lua
-- Set a global flag
GlobalState:set('doubleXP', true, true)

-- Read it anywhere (server or client)
print('Double XP active: ' .. tostring(GlobalState.doubleXP))
```

`GlobalState` is one State Bag shared by the entire server. Perfect for events, announcements, or configuration that every player needs.

Expected output:

```
Every client can read `GlobalState.doubleXP`. When it changes, every change handler fires.
```

## State Bags vs databases

| State Bags | Databases |
|---|---|
| Live, in-memory state | Permanent storage on disk |
| Gone when server restarts | Survives restarts |
| Duty status, vehicle lock, current activity | Money, inventory, houses, characters |
| Fast reads, no queries | Slower (disk + network), but durable |
| Example: `Player.state.onduty = true` | Example: `SELECT money FROM players WHERE id = 1` |

Modern systems use both. The database stores permanent data. State Bags keep live status so scripts don't hammer the database every second.

## Common failures

| What you see | What it means | How to fix it |
|---|---|---|
| `attempt to index a nil value (local 'playerState')` | Player ID is invalid or player disconnected | Check the player exists before accessing their state |
| State Bag value is nil when you expect it | The value was never set or the key is misspelled | `onduty` ≠ `onDuty` ≠ `on_duty` — keys are case-sensitive |
| Other clients can't see the change | `replicate` was set to `false` | Pass `true` as the third argument to `:set()` |
| Handler fires for non-player State Bags | Entity and Global State Bags also trigger handlers | Filter with `GetPlayerFromStateBagName(bagName)` — returns 0 for non-players |
| State Bag data is gone after restart | State Bags are in-memory, not saved to disk | Use a database for data that must survive restarts. Reload from DB on player join |
| Change handler fires multiple times | Every State Bag change triggers ALL matching handlers | Check `key` in your handler to make sure you're reacting to the right key |
